context: This triplet refers to a counterfactual, model-based evaluation within the paper’s estimated multi-industry general-equilibrium framework calibrated to French firm-level R&D data (13,708 firm-year observations, 2000–2006). The policy is a hypothetical type-dependent subsidy applied to private firms’ research (separate rates for basic vs. applied research), and welfare effects are reported in consumption-equivalent terms for the French economy; the model’s optimal rates are a 50% subsidy for basic research and 14% for applied research.

difficulty.justification: Assessing whether type-differentiated R&D subsidies increase welfare requires substantial domain-specific theory about R&D externalities and matching model structure to institutional detail; the sign is sensitive to modeling assumptions, general-equilibrium feedbacks, and parameter uncertainty, so opposite outcomes are plausible under alternative calibrations.

context: This study uses annual country-year gender-specific labor-market aggregates for 16 European countries from 1969–1988 to estimate the effects of parental leave legislation (paid and unpaid weeks, job-protection and eligibility rules) on employment-to-population ratios, weekly hours, and wages. The unit of observation is sex (and in some specifications age) within country-year; most leave is taken by women and is often publicly financed. Policy variation arises from staggered enactments and extensions of leave across countries and time, with comparisons between women and men and between younger (25–34) and older (45–54) women.

difficulty.justification: Evaluating whether parental leave increases total employment requires specialized knowledge of labor supply/demand, gender roles, and policy design; effects vary by institutions and time, have plausible opposing mechanisms (reduced hiring vs. increased retention), and empirical identification is moderately challenging given aggregate sex-by-age panels and potential policy endogeneity.

triplet_key: 24749|relabeling expenses as r&d|measured productivity effect of reported r&d
outcome: Measured productivity effect of reported R&D
context: This paper studies how China’s InnoCom R&D tax-notches affected firm R&D and productivity using administrative corporate tax returns (SAT) and manufacturing survey data over 2006–2011, exploiting the 2008 Enterprise Income Tax reform that created size-dependent R&D-intensity thresholds (3%, 4%, 6%) and a 15% preferential rate for certified high‑tech firms. The unit of observation is Chinese manufacturing firms; institutional features include certification requirements, audits, R&D reported under administrative expenses, and VAT third‑party reporting which shape relabeling and enforcement margins.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a negative measured productivity effect from relabeling requires fairly deep institutional knowledge (tax notches, certification, audits) and is highly context-dependent because enforcement, third‑party reporting, and firm selection determine whether R&D is genuine or just relabeled; multiple channels (measurement error, composition/selection, reallocation, audit responses) make opposite explanations plausible and demand careful identification beyond the available administrative links.

triplet_key: 16967|being affected by us financial crisis|mortgage loan acceptance rate
treatment: Being affected by US financial crisis
context: This empirical study examines German retail lending using the universe of savings‑bank consumer and mortgage loan applications from July 2006–June 2008. It compares individual loan outcomes at savings banks 'affected' by their ownership/guarantees of Landesbanken hit by US subprime losses (Sachsen LB, West LB, Bayern LB) versus 'nonaffected' banks. Units are loan applications and banks; institutional features include savings banks' regional/narrow‑banking mandate and vertical support obligations to Landesbanken after the August 2007 shock.

difficulty.justification: Understanding this triplet requires intermediate banking/market institutional knowledge (regional savings‑bank mandates and Landesbank links), and the sign is context‑dependent on German institutional arrangements. Identification is confounded by selection, simultaneous policy responses and spillovers, and requires tracing multiple channels, though the August 2007 shock and full‑application dataset give moderately good empirical leverage.

triplet_key: 18722|tradition of local democracy|real per capita gdp
context: The paper links pre-industrial local succession practices of ancestors (from the Ethnographic Atlas mapped to modern populations via language/ Landscan) to contemporary outcomes across countries worldwide. Outcomes include national democracy (Polity2 averages in three 50-year intervals between 1850–2000), institutional quality and GDP (WGI and GDP averages 1996–2005), and individual democracy attitudes (World Values Survey waves 1981–2008). Units are countries and individuals; key controls include ancestral political hierarchies, European ancestry, agricultural suitability, and survey-wave or period fixed effects.

difficulty.justification: Establishing that pre‑industrial local democratic traditions causally raise modern per‑capita GDP requires specialized institutional theory and identification strategies because colonial history, migration, geography, and measurement/mapping error create strong confounders. Multiple transmission channels, selection and GE feedbacks make opposite signs plausible and available aggregate evidence noisy, so credible identification is difficult.

context: This paper studies the short-run causal effect of a horizontal bank–trust merger on negotiated mortgage interest rates in Canada, using administrative data on government-insured 5‑year fixed‑rate mortgages issued 1992–2004 and focusing on contracts signed within one year of the merger. The unit of observation is individual mortgage transactions (households) located in local markets (FSA neighborhoods, 5 km radius). Institutional features include CMHC/Genworth insurance, nationwide posted rates, decentralized branch negotiation, and broker intermediation; the merger discretely altered local choice-sets.

difficulty.justification: Assessing whether a local bank–trust merger reduces median consumer bargaining leverage requires intermediate market knowledge (posted vs negotiated rates, broker roles) and is sensitive to institutional/contextual details; multiple plausible countervailing channels (efficiency gains, broker responses, selection) make the sign ambiguous and mediation complex, though transaction-level data and a short-run discrete change can provide reasonably informative evidence.

context: This paper studies how the ECB’s Corporate Sector Purchase Program (CSPP), announced March 10, 2016 and implemented mid‑2016, affected trade credit and real activity across euro‑area firm supply chains. Using firm‑year data (2013–2017) from ORBIS and FactSet Revere supplier‑customer links, the authors compare CSPP‑eligible (investment‑grade bond issuers) firms and their downstream customers across euro‑area core and periphery countries to assess how improved bond‑market access changes trade credit provision, investment, employment, and upstream market structure.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate knowledge of central‑bank corporate purchases and supply‑chain/trade‑credit channels; the sign plausibly varies by country, firm type and timing, and is confounded by supplier selection and demand shocks. Identifying a clean positive effect needs careful network DID/identification to rule out alternative channels and omitted heterogeneity.

triplet_key: 18865|omnibus information treatment|preferred top 1% average tax rate
context: This paper uses randomized online survey experiments conducted in the United States between January 2011 and August 2012 (with one-month follow-ups and additional rounds) to study how customized, interactive information about U.S. income inequality, historical top tax rates and economic growth correlations, and estate-tax incidence affects individual preferences for redistribution. The unit of observation is U.S. adult respondents recruited primarily via Amazon Mechanical Turk (≈10,000 participants across rounds, with some supplemental panel recruitment). The institutional background is contemporary U.S. tax and transfer policy amid rising top income/wealth shares.

difficulty.justification: Randomized online experiments give good internal identification of a short-term causal effect in the sample, so only moderate domain-specialty is needed; however results are context- and framing-sensitive (MTurk sample, specific information content/timing), and heterogeneous channels and external validity/persistence concerns make generalizing the sign moderately difficult.

context: This paper studies the decentralized rollout of municipal public pharmacies in Chile from October 2015 to April 2018 (146 of 344 counties adopted). It analyzes county- and local-market outcomes using drug-level IQVIA data (2014–2018), municipal finance records (2013–2019), avoidable hospitalizations, and an individual-level informational field experiment conducted just before the October 2016 local election. The institutional setting is a highly concentrated private retail and wholesale pharmaceutical sector; public pharmacies are single municipal stores that procure via a public intermediary and target local residents, especially chronic-patient buyers.

difficulty.justification: The net fiscal effect hinges on institutional details (procurement, pricing, subsidies), selection into adoption, and opposing channels (direct operating costs vs. procurement savings and reduced healthcare spending), so the sign is context-dependent and empirically ambiguous despite the rich administrative and market data available.

triplet_key: 19441|attending a private school|average test scores across subjects
context: This paper evaluates a two-stage randomized school voucher experiment implemented in Andhra Pradesh, India, with baseline tests in March–April 2008 and follow-up assessments roughly 2.3 and 4 years later. The study covers primary-school students (and villages/markets) across 180 rural villages in five districts. Villages were randomized to treatment/control and eligible applicants within treatment villages were randomized to receive vouchers that covered private school fees and materials; private schools participated voluntarily and were not allowed to select students. The analysis estimates individual impacts, market-level spillovers, and cost-effectiveness.

difficulty.justification: A well-designed two-stage randomized voucher experiment largely removes selection/confounding, so moderate domain knowledge suffices and the sign is straightforward to identify; remaining concerns are limited to attrition, spillovers, and external validity across settings.

triplet_key: 29905|20% decrease in social security benefits|total employment rates
context: This paper uses U.S. microdata (SIPP panels 1984–2008 and CPS panels 1979–2018) to estimate a structural life-cycle model of consumption, human-capital investment, labor supply and Social Security claiming. Focusing on male high-school graduates (individual-level units, estimation from ages ~22 up to 80), it models U.S. Social Security rules (claiming from age 62, normal retirement 65, using 2004 rules) and simulates counterfactual benefit and tax changes. Estimation employs indirect inference to match life-cycle employment and measured-wage profiles; extensions include health shocks and part-time work.

difficulty.justification: Determining the sign requires specialized life‑cycle and retirement theory (substitution vs income effects, claiming timing, human‑capital dynamics) and depends on institutional details and age composition. Structural estimation on rich microdata helps identification but results rest on modeling assumptions and unobserved heterogeneity, so moderate-to-high difficulty remains.

context: This paper analyzes Boston (Massachusetts) charter middle schools using administrative data on Boston Public School students who were in fourth grade between 2006 and 2009 (followed to eighth grade). The unit of analysis is individual students; the institutional setting is a decentralized charter application system with school-specific randomized admission lotteries, distance to charter schools, and a state charter-cap constraint. The study links charter application/lottery records, student addresses, and MCAS test scores to estimate achievement effects and selection.

difficulty.justification: Randomized charter admission lotteries and linked administrative data make identification of a positive effect relatively straightforward (IV/LATE on compliers), but noncompliance/attrition, heterogeneous mechanisms (peer composition, instruction), and limited external validity to Boston raise moderate concerns.

context: This paper studies the 2009 U.S. “Cash for Clunkers” (CARS) program (July 24–Aug 24, 2009), which paid $3,500–$4,500 dealer credits for qualifying trade-ins. Using monthly new-vehicle registration data for 957 U.S. Core-Based Statistical Areas from 2004–June 2010, the authors compare cities with differing ex-ante exposure (number of qualifying “clunkers” as of July 2008, scaled by 2004 purchases) to estimate impacts on auto purchases and local employment, house prices, and household defaults, controlling for demographics, income, unemployment, and pre-trends.

difficulty.justification: Multiple plausible channels (liquidity from trade‑ins, changed borrowing for new cars, local employment/spillovers) and heterogeneous local credit conditions make the sign context-dependent and ambiguous; however, cross‑city exposure variation and pre‑trend controls provide a reasonably good identification strategy, so establishing a credible null requires adequate power and ruling out offsetting effects.

triplet_key: 23545|minimum payment reductions|probability of having collections debt
context: Between January 2005 and August 2006 Money Management International randomized 79,739 prospective clients across U.S. states into debt-management-plan (DMP) offers. Units are distressed credit‑card borrowers; treated individuals received larger immediate minimum‑payment reductions and delayed debt write‑downs negotiated with eleven card issuers, while controls received status‑quo DMP terms. The study links counselor records to PACER bankruptcy data, TransUnion credit reports, and SSA W‑2 tax records to measure repayment, bankruptcy, credit, employment, and 401(k) outcomes over roughly five years.

difficulty.justification: A large randomized trial with administrative linkages gives strong identification and abundant evidence (low evidence difficulty), but understanding the sign still requires some domain knowledge and tracing multiple mediating channels (liquidity vs. moral hazard) and depends on DMP institutional details across settings.

context: This paper analyzes U.S. variable annuity markets from 2005–2020 (focus 2013–2020 for policy analysis), using product-quarter observations (individual annuity share-classes), insurer and broker-level data on commissions, expenses, sales, and FINRA complaints. It exploits the 2015–2017 Department of Labor fiduciary rule proposal/implementation (issued 2016, vacated 2018) and cross-sectional and time-series variation in commissions, expenses, and product availability to estimate how broker incentives and the fiduciary rule affected annuity sales and investor welfare.

difficulty.justification: Understanding this link needs substantial market and regulatory knowledge plus econometric identification skills; commissions are endogenous and subject to insurer/broker selection, concurrent product changes, and anticipation effects, though the DOL fiduciary-rule episode provides a plausible quasi-experiment to help identification.

context: Using Danish matched employer–employee administrative data over 2003–2008, the paper studies firms and individual employees around a legal threshold (firms with ≥35 employees) after Denmark’s 2006 Act No. 562 requiring firms above the cutoff to produce gender-disaggregated wage statistics (effective 2007). The analysis focuses on firms with 20–50 employees (treated: 35–50; controls: 20–34) to estimate how mandated pay-transparency affected wages, hiring, promotions, productivity, and profits in the private sector.

difficulty.justification: An RD using rich Danish administrative data gives reasonably strong identification, so only intermediate domain expertise is needed, but the sign is context-dependent (institutional enforcement, small‑firm dynamics) and plausible alternative channels (hiring, composition, anticipatory responses, spillovers, measurement of promotions) mean attribution is moderately ambiguous and requires careful checking.

context: This paper studies the effects of the 1970 and 1977 U.S. Clean Air Act Amendments on manufacturing using plant-level Census of Manufactures data from 1967–1987. It exploits county-level, pollutant-specific attainment/nonattainment designations for four criteria pollutants across 3,070 counties and 1.75 million plant observations; polluting plants in nonattainment counties faced stricter plant-specific regulation. The unit of analysis is individual manufacturing plants; identification leverages cross-sectional and temporal variation in county nonattainment status and industry emitting status to estimate regulation impacts on employment, capital, and shipments.

difficulty.justification: Intermediate regulatory and industrial knowledge is needed; the sign hinges on opposing channels (capital-augmenting abatement investments vs. contraction/exit/reduced investment) and on local institutional/enforcement heterogeneity, and while the paper's diff-in-diff/pollutant-specific design helps identification, selection and compositional/feedback effects leave substantial ambiguity.

context: Using administrative records for Chicago public housing and Chicago Public Schools from the 1990s (student semesters 1991–92 through 2001–02), the paper studies how high-rise CHA building closures and demolitions in Chicago affected individual students and households. Units are individual students matched to building addresses; affected families were offered Section 8 vouchers or CHA transfers (forced relocations, limited counseling) amid CHA redevelopment and crisis-driven closures, altering housing, neighborhood poverty, and school assignments.

difficulty.justification: Determining the sign requires specialized knowledge of housing policy, school assignment rules, and peer effects; outcomes depend heavily on local institutions and heterogeneous relocations. Strong confounding, selection into who was displaced and where they moved, and multiple mediating channels (neighborhood, school composition, resources, take-up of vouchers) make the sign highly ambiguous despite rich administrative data.

triplet_key: 29905|20% decrease in social security benefits|measured average yearly wages
context: This paper uses U.S. microdata (SIPP panels 1984–2008 and CPS panels 1979–2018) to estimate a structural life-cycle model of consumption, human-capital investment, labor supply and Social Security claiming. Focusing on male high-school graduates (individual-level units, estimation from ages ~22 up to 80), it models U.S. Social Security rules (claiming from age 62, normal retirement 65, using 2004 rules) and simulates counterfactual benefit and tax changes. Estimation employs indirect inference to match life-cycle employment and measured-wage profiles; extensions include health shocks and part-time work.

difficulty.justification: Determining a positive effect requires detailed lifecycle theory and structural identification: benefit cuts trigger opposing substitution/income effects, alter retirement timing, human-capital investment, and sample composition, and can have general-equilibrium wage feedbacks; the paper's structural/indirect-inference approach helps but identification remains sensitive to functional form, unobserved heterogeneity, and institutional specifics.

triplet_key: 28021|fintech lender|interest rate premium for thin files
outcome: Interest rate premium for thin files
context: Using U.S. individual-level monthly credit-bureau data (2012–2017) on unsecured personal loans, this result compares interest rates set by online fintech versus traditional lenders specifically for borrowers with "thin credit files" (credit history <48 months). The unit is the loan/borrower (monthly panel spanning 3 months pre- to 15 months post-origination) across U.S. zip codes and states; estimations use the paper's baseline pricing regression with controls (credit score, loan size, maturity) and zip-code-by-month fixed effects.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate market knowledge but hinges on institutional details (fintech underwriting, funding, and competition) and borrower selection into channels; unobserved heterogeneity and multiple mediating channels (screening, costs, alternative data) make the sign plausible but not unambiguous without stronger identification.

context: This triplet examines how early-childhood Medicaid eligibility from the original U.S. Medicaid rollout (state implementation 1966–1970) affected adult disability transfer receipt for birth cohorts roughly 1936–1975. Outcomes are measured for native-born individuals aggregated by birth state, birth year, and race, using Census/ACS disability and program-participation data (primarily 2000–2007). Identification exploits the federal categorical-eligibility linkage to AFDC and cross-state differences in pre‑Medicaid AFDC rates to construct migration‑adjusted cumulative childhood exposure; effects on disability transfers are driven primarily by whites.

difficulty.justification: Understanding this effect requires specialized knowledge of Medicaid/AFDC institutional history and life‑cycle health economics; cohort migration, long‑run mediators (education, labor, mortality selection), and state policy heterogeneity make a negative sign plausible but difficult to identify cleanly from aggregated, decades‑later outcomes even with a rollout-based strategy.

context: This paper studies U.S. county-level effects of Clean Air Act regulation on air pollution and housing values across 1970–1990, focusing on 1970–80 and 1980–90 decades. It uses county TSP monitor data, attainment/non‑attainment designations (mid‑decade: 1974–75 and 1984–85), and county housing values (Census/CCDB and PUMS). The institutional setting is the 1970 and 1977 Clean Air Act Amendments and EPA enforcement, with important background shocks such as the 1981–82 recession and regional industrial declines (e.g., Rust Belt).

difficulty.justification: Regulation plausibly reduces particulate matter, but credible identification requires intermediate institutional knowledge and careful handling of nonrandom attainment designations, monitor placement/measurement issues, and contemporaneous economic shocks (recession, regional industrial decline), so confounding and multiple channels make establishing the sign moderately difficult.

context: Using a newly constructed dataset of 642 new chemical entities and their launch dates across up to 76 countries from 1983–2002, the paper studies drug-country launch timing. It exploits cross-country and over-time variation in patent regimes (product vs. process, patent duration), pharmaceutical price‑control regimes, and health‑policy institutions (e.g., national formularies, WHO essential medicines lists) to estimate how these institutional and market factors affect the speed and geographic diffusion of new drugs.

difficulty.justification: Assessing whether process patents speed drug launches requires specialized knowledge of IP law and pharma market strategy, and the sign varies with institutional details (enforcement, price controls, market size). Many confounders and strategic sequencing/selection effects create high causal complexity, and while the described dataset is rich it does not eliminate important identification challenges.

context: This paper studies US Medicaid eligibility expansions for pregnant women across 49 states (plus DC) during 1979–1990. Units include state-year aggregates (Vital Statistics infant outcomes) and individual women aged 15–44 (CPS, NLSY). It exploits cross-state, over-time variation from targeted AFDC-linked expansions in the early 1980s and broader income-based expansions (1987–1990, e.g. 133%–185% FPL) to assess effects on low birthweight, infant mortality, Medicaid take-up, service use, and program expenditures.

difficulty.justification: Assessing whether Medicaid eligibility expansions reduced low birthweight requires substantial health‑policy and maternal health knowledge and application of identification strategies; effects vary by state context, take‑up, and baseline care access. Multiple mediating and selection channels (fertility/compositional changes, prenatal care use, measurement issues) make the sign plausible but not straightforward, and the available quasi‑experimental variation supports reasonable inference though important threats remain.

triplet_key: 7422|elimination of student benefit program|college attendance probability
context: This empirical study exploits the 1981–82 U.S. policy shock that eliminated the Social Security Student Benefit Program to estimate effects of grant-like student aid on college attendance and completed schooling. Using NLSY cohorts (high-school seniors 1979–81 versus 1982–83) and follow-up through 1996, the unit of observation is individuals (youth/households), comparing children of Social Security beneficiaries (proxied by deceased fathers) to others in the United States to identify causal impacts of generous monthly survivor benefits (avg ≈$5,400).

difficulty.justification: Standard human-capital and credit-constraint theory suffices for baseline predictions, but the sign depends on context (benefit magnitude, college costs, other aid, cohort/institutional factors). The 1981–82 shock provides plausible identification, yet selection into beneficiary families, cohort trends and multiple mediating channels leave nontrivial ambiguity and moderate evidence gaps.

triplet_key: 18493|increase subsidy to early investment|early investment by high-education parents' children
outcome: Early investment by high-education parents' children
context: Using U.S. data (Children of the NLSY79, NLSY79 cohort and CPS benchmarks, samples through ~2010 and 2006), the paper studies children and households to assess how parental resources and credit constraints shape early (ages 0–11) versus late (ages 12–23) human capital investments and later earnings. The institutional setting emphasizes U.S. schooling (public K–12, subsidized postsecondary education and student loans), young parents’ labor income profiles, and imperfect credit markets. The authors combine reduced-form regressions with a calibrated dynastic lifecycle model to evaluate policy interventions (subsidies, loans, transfers).

difficulty.justification: Mechanically, subsidies should raise early investment but the effect for children of high-education parents depends on income/substitution effects, crowd-out, timing complementarities, parental labor supply and credit constraints; longitudinal NLSY data plus a calibrated model supports inference but important identification and generalizability challenges remain.

triplet_key: 15321|recession during impressionable years|belief that luck drives success
context: This triplet is estimated using the NLS72 panel (the US High School Class of 1972), observing the same individuals from 1972–1986. The treatment is state-level unemployment during respondents' impressionable years, operationalized as the state unemployment rate interacted with a 1972–1979 dummy (when respondents were ages 18–25); the outcome is the individual's belief that luck drives economic success. Estimation uses individual-level panel regressions with state and year fixed effects (errors clustered by state).

difficulty.justification: There is established theory and empirical work linking formative labor-market shocks to belief formation (so moderate domain knowledge suffices), but the sign depends on cohort/institutional context and is sensitive to migration/selection and unobserved state-level confounders. Multiple mediating channels (personal experience, peer effects, local policy) and limited identifying variation in a short cohort window mean plausible alternative explanations remain without stronger identification.

context: The paper studies causes of nutritional inequality in the United States using 2004–2016 household and store scanner data. It analyzes ~61,000 Nielsen Homescan households and ~35,000 RMS stores, matches to TDLinx supermarket openings (6,721 entries) and zip-/county-level retail data, and examines household-level grocery purchases, supermarket entry events, and thousands of household moves across zip codes/counties. The setting is U.S. grocery markets and policy debates about “food deserts” and SNAP; analyses combine panel event studies, within-location comparisons, and a structural demand model to decompose supply versus demand drivers.

difficulty.justification: Identifying the effect of moving to a ‘healthier’ area on household health is conceptually and empirically challenging due to strong mover selection, heterogeneous local institutions/prices, multiple mediating channels (access, prices, preferences, time use) and measurement error in purchase‑based health indices; the rich panel, event‑study and structural approaches help but do not eliminate these identification hurdles.

context: Between January 2005 and August 2006 Money Management International randomized 79,739 prospective clients across U.S. states into debt-management-plan (DMP) offers. Units are distressed credit‑card borrowers; treated individuals received larger immediate minimum‑payment reductions and delayed debt write‑downs negotiated with eleven card issuers, while controls received status‑quo DMP terms. The study links counselor records to PACER bankruptcy data, TransUnion credit reports, and SSA W‑2 tax records to measure repayment, bankruptcy, credit, employment, and 401(k) outcomes over roughly five years.

difficulty.justification: Randomized assignment and administrative SSA W‑2 follow‑up give strong internal identification of an ITT effect on wage earnings, so establishing a null average effect is relatively straightforward; however limits from external validity (specific borrower population and pre‑crisis timing), W‑2 measurement gaps (e.g., self‑employment/cash earnings), and possible heterogeneous or delayed channels create some residual uncertainty.

triplet_key: 12478|reform of joint-and-several liability rule|preventable complications of labor and delivery
outcome: Preventable complications of labor and delivery
context: This study examines how state-level tort reforms in the United States from 1989–2001 affected obstetric practice and birth outcomes. Using individual Vital Statistics natality records on millions of births (mothers and infants), the authors exploit within-state changes in laws (caps on non-economic/punitive damages, joint-and-several liability, collateral-source rules) across counties to estimate effects on C-sections, labor induction, complications, and APGAR scores. Many reforms were enacted or overturned in the 1990s, providing staggered policy variation across states.

difficulty.justification: Evaluating how changes to joint-and-several liability affect preventable obstetric complications requires specialized knowledge of malpractice law and obstetric practice, and effects vary with institutional details (liability backstops, insurance, baseline practices). Multiple opposing channels (reduced defensive C‑sections vs. reduced precautionary care), selection and spillovers make the sign highly ambiguous and identification challenging despite large vital‑stats data and staggered reforms.

context: This empirical study examines U.S. security analysts’ recommendations and annual earnings forecasts using IBES data (primarily 1994–2008) and NYSE TAQ trading data (1993–2002). The units are analyst–stock–day observations and investor trades (small vs. large) for U.S.-listed firms (NYSE, Amex, NASDAQ). The institutional setting emphasizes incentives from underwriter affiliation, investment-banking pressure, reputational capital, and the post-2001 analyst conflicts-of-interest spotlight, exploiting earnings-announcement and recommendation/forecast update windows.

difficulty.justification: Requires specialized knowledge of analyst incentives, underwriting ties, and reputation models plus identification strategies; institutional features (regulation, market structure, time) strongly shape the sign. Multiple confounders, reverse causality, selection and market-feedback channels make sign identification conceptually and empirically challenging despite rich IBES/TAQ data.

context: This paper analyzes French matched employer–employee administrative data (DADS) merged with the French Labor Force Survey for 1997–2007, with robustness checks using the CPS in the United States. Units include individual male workers (age 30–52), establishments/firms and 328 commuting-zone local labor markets. The setting emphasizes housing constraints, search frictions, and place-based policies (e.g., French Zones Franches Urbaines). The author combines panel flow decompositions with structural equilibrium estimation to identify employers’ role in spatial unemployment and to simulate policy counterfactuals.

difficulty.justification: Evaluating whether French enterprise zones raise aggregate welfare requires specialized knowledge of place-based policy, search-and-matching frictions, housing constraints, and structural equilibrium methods; results depend heavily on local institutions and general-equilibrium feedbacks. Rich matched employer–employee data plus structural estimation improve identification, but strong modeling assumptions, fiscal externalities, and alternative counterfactuals leave substantial ambiguity.

triplet_key: 29947|limited background check (no arrest in past 1 year)|willingness to hire workers with criminal record
treatment: Limited background check (no arrest in past 1 year)
outcome: Willingness to hire workers with criminal record
context: This paper uses a randomized field experiment conducted March–April 2020 on a large U.S. nationwide staffing platform to measure employer demand for workers with criminal records. The unit of analysis is hiring managers (1,095 responses from 913 businesses) posting short-term, entry-level jobs. The study randomizes wage subsidies, crime-and-safety insurance caps, performance-history and criminal-record recency screens (including a treatment allowing only applicants with no arrest/conviction in the past year), and objective performance information; the reported 21-percentage-point increase refers to the subsample of managers randomized to a 0% wage subsidy.

difficulty.justification: Random assignment gives strong internal identification and the mechanism is straightforward, so domain knowledge and confounding concerns are low. But the result is context-dependent (platform, short-term/entry-level jobs, timing and subsidy condition), limiting external validity and raising overall difficulty.

triplet_key: 7710|learning by doing|price relative to static marginal cost
outcome: Price relative to static marginal cost
context: This paper studies the global market for wide-bodied commercial jet aircraft from the late 1960s through the 1990s (primarily 1972–1994), using firm- and model-level production, price, cost, and delivery data. Units of observation include aircraft models, manufacturers/firms and annual market size; the setting is an oligopolistic, capacity-constrained commercial aircraft industry with learning-by-doing (organizational forgetting), large development costs, and entry/exit. The paper combines structural estimation and dynamic simulation to evaluate pricing, industry dynamics, and counterfactual antitrust/industrial policies.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether learning-by-doing lowers price relative to static marginal cost in the wide‑body jet market requires specialized dynamic oligopoly theory and structural identification to disentangle cost learning from demand shifts, strategic pricing, capacity constraints and entry/exit; the sign is highly context‑dependent and opposite outcomes are readily plausible without strong identifying assumptions.

triplet_key: 25245|initial allowance of an ex-ante valuable patent|firm productivity
treatment: Initial allowance of an ex-ante valuable patent
context: This paper studies US firms and their workers using linked USPTO patent applications (published 2001–2011, analysis up to 2010 filings) and US Treasury business and W2/1099 tax records (1997–2014). The unit of analysis is patent-applying firms (EINs), typically small newly-innovative firms (median ~17 employees), and their employees. It exploits USPTO initial allowance versus rejection decisions (within art-unit/year) and predicted ex-ante patent value to identify how patent-induced productivity and rents affect firm outcomes, wages, hiring, and retention in the U.S. patent institutional setting.

difficulty.justification: Understanding this link requires intermediate knowledge of patent institutions and firm productivity mechanisms; identification is plausible here because of within-art-unit/year allowance variation and predicted ex-ante value, but confounding (firm/inventor quality, examiner heterogeneity) and multiple mediating channels (rents, hiring, investment, retention) make the sign moderately ambiguous absent careful empirical controls.

triplet_key: 30779|entry of public pharmacies|avoidable hospitalizations and related health outcomes
outcome: Avoidable hospitalizations and related health outcomes
context: This paper studies the decentralized rollout of municipal public pharmacies in Chile from October 2015 to April 2018 (146 of 344 counties adopted). It analyzes county- and local-market outcomes using drug-level IQVIA data (2014–2018), municipal finance records (2013–2019), avoidable hospitalizations, and an individual-level informational field experiment conducted just before the October 2016 local election. The institutional setting is a highly concentrated private retail and wholesale pharmaceutical sector; public pharmacies are single municipal stores that procure via a public intermediary and target local residents, especially chronic-patient buyers.

difficulty.justification: Identifying the sign requires specialized knowledge of pharmaceutical markets, health behavior, and local politics; multiple mediating and general-equilibrium channels plus selection on municipal adoption make opposite effects plausible, although the paper's rich rollout, administrative and experimental data improve identification but do not eliminate all confounding.

context: Using the paper's calibrated spatial equilibrium model, validated to Thailand's 1986–1996 period with GIS-defined local spatial markets and village/household data, the authors simulate counterfactual policies that subsidize branch profits in markets distant from existing branches. The simulations trace effects on local deposit-cash ratios, wealth accumulation, endogenous branch openings, and aggregate income inequality across local markets and heterogeneous households.

difficulty.justification: Requires specialized spatial-equilibrium and banking knowledge and careful identification of multiple countervailing channels (access to finance, wealth accumulation, entry effects). The calibrated model and rich GIS/household data improve inference but strong structural assumptions and context-specific institutions leave nontrivial ambiguity.

context: The paper studies equity-market liberalizations in emerging-market countries roughly from the late 1970s through September 2000, using country-level S&P EMDB total-return indices for liberalizing countries (about 15 with 5-year pre/post windows around liberalization months from Bekaert & Harvey and Henry). Units are national stock-market indices; empirical analyses focus on returns around policy events that opened markets to foreign investors. The setting emphasizes institutional features—liquidity/turnover, investor protection, concentrated controlling-shareholder ownership, and cross-country variation in liberalization dates and credibility.

difficulty.justification: Identifying the sign requires advanced knowledge of emerging-market institutions and international-finance channels; confounding from anticipation, global shocks, index inclusion, corporate sales, and credibility means positive or negative effects are both plausible and empirically hard to pin down.

triplet_key: 28582|subsidizing household portfolio adjustment costs|overall income inequality
context: This causal claim comes from the paper’s calibrated and validated spatial-equilibrium structural model of Thailand (validated to the 1986–1996 branching-expansion period). The authors simulate a counterfactual policy that subsidizes household portfolio-adjustment costs for households in markets distant from existing bank branches and report model-implied effects on household- and market-level outcomes, including overall income inequality.

difficulty.justification: Interpreting this sign requires advanced knowledge of spatial-equilibrium banking, portfolio-choice frictions, and distributional mechanisms under branch expansion; results are sensitive to institutional details of 1986–96 Thailand. Multiple plausible countervailing channels (who benefits from subsidies, entry responses, labor/asset reallocation) and reliance on model assumptions mean the sign is credible but not unambiguous without strong validation.

context: This paper studies India’s dismantling of small-scale industry (SSI) product reservations (major de-reservation 1997–2008) and its effects on manufacturing establishments and districts using the Annual Survey of Industries panel (primarily 2000–2007) and NSS unorganized-manufacturing rounds (2000, 2005). Units are establishments (product-level incumbents and entrants), products, and districts. The institutional setting is the national SSI reservation regime (eligibility tied to a plant-and-machinery cap around Rs.10 million) that restricted certain product lines to small firms; de-reservation varied by product timing, generating differential local exposure.

difficulty.justification: Identifying whether de-reservation raises entrant employment requires substantial knowledge of the SSI institutional rules and heterogeneity across products/locations; the sign can vary with incumbent responses, scale economies, and local market structure. The staggered product-level reform provides useful quasi-experimental variation, but multiple mediating channels and potential confounders make clean identification moderately difficult.

context: This paper reports a large-scale field experiment in Punjab, Pakistan (pilot 2014–15; main year 2015–16) that randomized roughly 600 procurement officers/offices across 26 districts into autonomy, performance-pay, combined, or control arms. Units are procurement officers and their procurement transactions (≈21,000 purchases of 25 homogeneous goods). The institutional setting is public procurement subject to pre-audit by district Accountant General offices (notably end-of-year budget lapsing and approval delays); treatments altered AG discretion (petty cash, checklist) and/or introduced rank-order bonuses; outcomes include quality-adjusted unit prices, approval delays, and officer time use.

difficulty.justification: Randomized design gives strong identification (low evidence concerns), but understanding whether bonuses increase time requires intermediate public-sector incentive knowledge and depends on institutional details (audit, approval delays) with multiple mediating channels and plausible countervailing behaviors (gaming, reallocation).

context: This paper studies low-income families randomized in the U.S. Moving to Opportunity (MTO) experiment (random assignment 1994–1998) across five cities (Baltimore, Boston, Chicago, Los Angeles, New York), linking HUD participant records to annual Experian credit reports (2001–2017) and Clarity payday-loan data. Units are MTO households, adult participants, and children who age into credit markets. Treatments compare Experimental vouchers (required move to <10% poverty tracts with counseling), unrestricted Section 8 vouchers, and control public-housing residents, exploiting random assignment to identify neighborhood effects on credit outcomes.

difficulty.justification: Randomized assignment in MTO greatly aids identification, so domain knowledge need not be highly specialized, but the distinction between assignment and actual voucher use, heterogeneity across cities and time, multiple mediating channels (income, employment, access to financial services, local court practices), and measurement/attrition issues mean moderate-to-high contextual and causal complexity and only moderate evidence sufficiency.

triplet_key: 20835|medicaid spending on children|government recoup fraction by age 60
context: Using U.S. administrative tax and Medicaid data, the paper studies long-term effects of Medicaid and SCHIP expansions in the 1980s–1990s on individuals born 1981–1984 across U.S. states. It links children to parents in IRS records (1996–2012) to compute simulated childhood (birth–age 18) Medicaid eligibility by cohort, state, and birth month, and follows adult outcomes (tax payments, EITC, wages, mortality, college attendance) through ages 19–31. Identification leverages state policy variation and federal birth-month eligibility changes; MSIS provides spending and take-up data.

difficulty.justification: Estimating that childhood Medicaid increases government recoupment by age 60 requires advanced life‑cycle reasoning and strong assumptions to extrapolate from outcomes observed only through age 31, faces substantial state‑policy heterogeneity and selection/confounding risks, and thus is highly complex and data‑limited.

context: This paper studies U.S. temporary bonus-depreciation tax incentives enacted in the 2002 (JCWAA) and 2003 (JGTRRA) laws—30% then 50% first-year bonus (retroactive to Sept 11, 2001; largely expiring end-2004)—and concurrent Section 179 expensing changes. Using quarterly BEA investment and price series matched to IRS/MACRS schedules for 36 capital-good types (panel 1959–2005, focus 2001–2005), it exploits cross-sectional eligibility (MACRS recovery ≤20 years) and time variation to identify effects on investment spending and observed investment prices in the U.S.

difficulty.justification: Understanding the sign requires intermediate public‑finance and investment knowledge; the temporary, policy‑specific setting and concurrent Section 179 changes make context and confounding moderately important, and identification must handle price, anticipation and general‑equilibrium channels—but the eligibility cutoff and rich BEA/IRS panel provide reasonably strong empirical leverage.

triplet_key: 28175|universal or partial loan forgiveness|benefits to high earners
context: This paper uses the 2019 Survey of Consumer Finances to analyze U.S. households (individuals aged 22–60) and their federal student loans, projecting repayment under federal programs (FFEL/DL) and Income-Driven Repayment plans (IBR, PAYE, REPAYE) that tie payments to 10–15% of discretionary income above 150% of the federal poverty line and forgive remaining balances after 20–25 years. The authors simulate earnings growth and discount future payments to compute present values and evaluate distributional impacts of universal, capped ($10k/$50k) and expanded-IDR forgiveness policies.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether forgiveness benefits high earners requires intermediate policy knowledge of IDR rules and lifetime-income modeling and is sensitive to institutional details (timing, caps, tax treatment). SCF-based simulations are useful but rely on strong assumptions about earnings growth, selection into borrowing, behavioral responses and discounting, leaving substantial uncertainty.

triplet_key: 18445|family labor supply|consumption smoothing to wage shocks
context: Using U.S. Panel Study of Income Dynamics (PSID) panel data covering the late 1990s through 2009 (consumption-rich waves from 1999–2009, sample period 1997–2009), the paper analyzes married two‑earner households (household heads roughly age 30–65) to trace how idiosyncratic permanent and transitory wage shocks map into household consumption, earnings, hours and asset accumulation. It estimates a life‑cycle structural/unitary household model (risk‑free asset, permanent/transitory wage shocks, possible non‑separability) to quantify self‑insurance via assets and family labor supply versus external insurance.

difficulty.justification: Identifying that family labor supply causally increases consumption smoothing requires specialized life‑cycle/household modeling and careful identification of exogenous permanent vs transitory wage shocks; results are highly sensitive to institutional/contextual factors (taxes, childcare, insurance), unobserved heterogeneity, measurement error and intrahousehold bargaining, and thus demand strong structural assumptions despite useful PSID data.

context: This paper studies eurozone bank-level interventions during the Global Financial Crisis and subsequent sovereign-debt shock, using hand-collected State Aid Register data on interventions from 2007–2009 and balance-sheet and loan-level outcomes through 2012. The unit of analysis is ultimate-parent banks (N=830) and bank–firm loan relationships, across eurozone countries differing in fiscal capacity and access to ECB facilities (including LTRO). The institutional setting includes national government recapitalizations, guarantees, liquidity measures, and EU Commission approvals, with fiscal constraints proxied by revenue/GDP, debt/GDP and maturing debt.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether forbearance increases ECB liquidity reliance requires intermediate knowledge of state-aid, central-bank collateral/eligibility and crisis policy interactions; the sign depends on institutional context (fiscal space, eligibility rules) and opposing channels (government support can substitute for or complement central-bank liquidity). Endogeneity and selection (sicker banks both more likely to receive forbearance and to tap ECB facilities) make causal identification nontrivial despite rich hand-collected data.

context: Using the universe of U.S. business and worker tax records for 2001–2015, the paper studies matched employer–employee relationships across commuting-zone × industry markets in the United States. Units are individual workers (W-2/1099) and firms (EINs). The authors exploit variation in firm- and market-level value added, worker mobility, procurement-auction shocks, and shift-share industry shocks to estimate a structural equilibrium model with worker heterogeneity over non-wage amenities, firm productivity, and progressive labor-income taxation (amenities treated as fixed during estimation).

difficulty.justification: Determining whether a firm-level wage rise increases employment requires specialized theory and identification because competing channels (wage cost vs productivity/retention/selection effects), sorting and turnover, tax/GE feedbacks, and endogeneity of wages make the sign context-dependent and potentially ambiguous; rich matched tax data and a structural equilibrium model help but rely on strong assumptions, so credible identification remains difficult.

context: Using commercial claims from a large insurer (covering tens of millions) over 2011–2015 across U.S. hospital emergency departments, the paper analyzes ED episodes and hospitals to study out-of-network physician billing. It exploits the entry timing of large ED outsourcing firms (EmCare, TeamHealth) into specific hospitals (2011–2015) and a 2014 New York arbitration law to estimate causal effects. Institutional context: hospitals outsource ED staffing, physicians and hospitals contract separately with insurers, producing surprise balance bills and a bargaining outside option for physicians.

difficulty.justification: Understanding requires intermediate knowledge of ED outsourcing, separate contracting and surprise billing; the sign depends strongly on institutional context (state laws, insurer/hospital market power) and alternative mechanisms (selection, standardized contracts, scale effects) make the opposite sign plausible, so credible identification is nontrivial though the entry timing and NY arbitration variation provide useful quasi-experimental leverage.

context: This paper examines banks in New England from 1867–1880 (including the Panic of 1873 and the Long Depression), using individual national banks and their presidents as units and county-level manufacturers for real-effects tests. It exploits exogenous variation from state-level Married Women’s Property Acts and bankers’ marriage/remarriage timing to alter presidents’ personal downside exposure. Institutional setting: national banks with double shareholder liability, no deposit insurance, annual OCC examiners, reserve and lending restrictions. Outcomes include leverage, loan rollovers, regulatory violations, and bank losses.

difficulty.justification: Requires specialized institutional and legal-historical knowledge to interpret liability effects; the sign is context-dependent (unique 19th-century institutions) and subject to plausible confounders (marriage timing, enforcement, macro shocks). Multiple mediating channels (moral hazard, monitoring, enforcement) and potential measurement limits mean identification is challenging though the law/timing variation provides reasonably strong leverage.

context: This paper analyzes French matched employer–employee administrative data (DADS) merged with the French Labor Force Survey for 1997–2007, with robustness checks using the CPS in the United States. Units include individual male workers (age 30–52), establishments/firms and 328 commuting-zone local labor markets. The setting emphasizes housing constraints, search frictions, and place-based policies (e.g., French Zones Franches Urbaines). The author combines panel flow decompositions with structural equilibrium estimation to identify employers’ role in spatial unemployment and to simulate policy counterfactuals.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether a corporate profit subsidy raises aggregate welfare in this spatial labor-market setting requires specialized theory and identification (incidence, place-based externalities), is highly sensitive to institutional and local conditions, and has nearly equally plausible opposing mechanisms (efficiency gains vs. fiscal distortions and misallocation); the rich matched data and structural GE estimation help but rely on strong modeling assumptions, so evidence is informative but not definitive.

triplet_key: 11551|reallocating world capital to equalize mpks|world output
treatment: Reallocating world capital to equalize MPKs
context: Cross-country analysis using a cross-section of about 53 countries circa 1996–2000 (Penn World Tables 6.1 for GDP/capital; World Bank wealth data ~2000; Bernanke & Gurkaynak capital shares). Unit of observation is the country. The authors assume competitive domestic goods and factor markets and Cobb–Douglas production, and they adjust for reproducible vs. natural capital and for domestic relative prices of investment goods. They perform structural counterfactual reallocations of the world reproducible-capital stock to assess deadweight losses from non‑equalized returns.

difficulty.justification: Neoclassical theory (diminishing returns, Cobb–Douglas) predicts a positive effect of reallocating capital toward higher MPKs, but in cross-country data identification hinges on strong structural assumptions (TFP differences, measurement of capital and prices, complementarities with human/human-capital, distortions and GE price effects), so the sign is moderately to highly sensitive and requires substantial domain-specific assumptions and corrections.

triplet_key: 27077|has supplier with cspp-eligible bonds|other external finance (debt, loans, cash)
context: This paper studies how the ECB’s Corporate Sector Purchase Program (CSPP), announced March 10, 2016 and implemented mid‑2016, affected trade credit and real activity across euro‑area firm supply chains. Using firm‑year data (2013–2017) from ORBIS and FactSet Revere supplier‑customer links, the authors compare CSPP‑eligible (investment‑grade bond issuers) firms and their downstream customers across euro‑area core and periphery countries to assess how improved bond‑market access changes trade credit provision, investment, employment, and upstream market structure.

difficulty.justification: Multiple plausible, opposite channels (supplier liquidity could raise trade credit and reduce customers' external borrowing, or suppliers could tighten terms raising customers' borrowing), strong heterogeneity across countries/sectors, and endogeneity of CSPP eligibility mean specialized knowledge and careful identification are required despite rich firm‑link data.

triplet_key: 33427|placement in predominantly white neighborhoods|later-life neighborhood black share
context: This paper analyzes long-run causal effects of the Gautreaux Assisted Housing Program (program operated 1976–1998; main sample 1982–1994) in the Chicago metropolitan area, linking historical placement records to administrative data through 2019/2020. Units are Black public-housing/voucher recipient households and their children. The program—created by a class-action settlement and a 1981 consent decree—placed families into “General” (<30% Black) or “Revitalizing” tracts based largely on registration order and unit availability. Authors use linked LEHD, MAF-ARF, Decennial Census and Numident data to estimate placement effects.

difficulty.justification: The program's quasi-random placement and rich administrative follow-up give strong identification leverage, but long-run intergenerational channels, Chicago-specific institutional/historical context, and potential selection/return-migration make the sign moderately context-dependent and require nontrivial causal reasoning.

context: This paper analyzes 25,541 IRS corporate audits of foreign-controlled U.S. corporations closed between 1996 and 2007, linking audit-determined tax deficiencies to the foreign owner’s home-country corruption (Transparency International CPI). The unit is the firm-year of U.S. corporations with at least 25% foreign ownership. The setting exploits common U.S. tax rules and IRS audit procedures (including the DIF selection score) and examines how enforcement changes—Sarbanes-Oxley (2002), Schedule M-3 (2004), and audit-rate increases—affect evasion.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether SOX reduced corporate tax evasion requires specialized knowledge of corporate governance, tax reporting, and IRS audit selection; effects are highly context-dependent (U.S. institutions, foreign owners, time) and confounded by detection versus true evasion, endogenous audit selection, and heterogeneous firm responses, so strong identification is needed.

triplet_key: 25230|unemployment insurance benefit increase|wages of low-wage workers
context: This paper studies four large unemployment insurance (UI) benefit reforms in Austria (1976, 1985, 1989, 2001) using administrative social-security and UI registry data covering workers and firms from 1972 onward. The unit is individual employees (age 25–54) and their employers; reforms differentially changed UI benefit levels across earnings-based reference wages, in a setting with broad UI eligibility, high take-up (quitters eligible), no experience rating, and linked unemployment assistance. The authors compare treated and nearby control earnings percentiles to estimate wage responses to exogenous UI benefit shifts.

difficulty.justification: Understanding the wage response to higher UI requires intermediate labor-economics theory (search, reservation wages, monopsony/bargaining) and is sensitive to institutional details (take-up, eligibility, linked assistance). Multiple plausible channels and potential selection/spillover effects make opposite signs credible, though the rich administrative reforms-based design improves empirical identification relative to cross-sectional inference.

triplet_key: 19890|increase in number of clearing members|total collateral demand
treatment: Increase in number of clearing members
context: This paper empirically analyzes how expanded margin requirements and central clearing affect collateral demand in the single-name CDS market using a global DTCC Trade Information Warehouse snapshot as of December 30, 2011. The unit of observation is bilateral CDS exposures across 184 reference entities (primarily European and American financial and sovereign names) and 885 anonymized counterparties (distinguishing 14 dealers and customers). It evaluates counterfactual clearing and margining arrangements—including dealer-to-dealer initial margins, CCP proliferation, and client clearing—under post‑Lehman G20 regulatory reforms.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether adding clearing members raises or lowers total collateral requires specialized knowledge of CCP margining, netting and default‑fund rules and is highly sensitive to market structure, participant composition, and regulatory detail; multiple opposing channels (multilateral netting vs. higher initial margins and default‑fund contributions), network effects, and counterfactual identification make the sign ambiguous and empirically challenging given typical data limitations.

context: This estimate uses MDRC’s randomized Jobs First waiver experiment in Connecticut (New Haven and Manchester), with random assignment April 1996–February 1997 and follow‑up through December 2000, for 4,803 welfare cases (women). The triplet refers specifically to the pre‑time‑limit period—the first seven post‑assignment quarters (before the 21‑month time limit can bind)—and estimates causal effects of Jobs First (including the expanded earnings disregard) on quarterly transfer income (cash welfare plus Food Stamps) using administrative UI and welfare records.

difficulty.justification: Randomized assignment and admin records give strong internal identification, but the net sign is theoretically ambiguous because expanded earnings disregards and increased work incentives pull transfer income in opposite directions (cash welfare up for some with earnings disregard, but overall welfare receipt and Food Stamps may fall with higher earnings), and effects can vary by subgroup and local conditions.

context: Using U.S. administrative data from USCIS H-1B lotteries (mainly FY2006–FY2007, with additional FY2008 and 2003–2008 analyses) matched to IRS firm-level tax records and USPTO patent data, the paper studies causal impacts of marginal H-1B visa allocations on U.S. firms (EINs). The institutional setting is the capped H-1B program (Regular and Advanced Degree Exemption) with lotteries when caps bind; outcomes include quarterly employment, patents, R&E tax credit claims, payroll per employee, and firm profits.

difficulty.justification: The H‑1B lottery gives credible exogenous variation (reducing classic confounding), so intermediate domain knowledge suffices to interpret the LATE; however the sign on payroll per employee hinges on composition (more lower‑paid H‑1Bs), complementarities or displacement of incumbents, and firm/type/time heterogeneity, making opposite mechanisms plausibly operative and generalization nontrivial.

context: This paper evaluates causal effects of charter school “takeovers” on middle-school students (mostly grades 4–8) in New Orleans’ Recovery School District (post‑Katrina conversions, takeovers opening 2008–2013, outcomes 2011–2014 LEAP/iLEAP) and in Boston (Gavin→UP takeover, Fall 2011, MCAS outcomes 2012–2014). The institutional setting is incumbency-based seat “grandfathering” into reopened charter schools (and, for Boston, concurrent oversubscribed lotteries), with analyses exploiting administrative enrollment, demographic, and standardized-test records under federal/state restart and charter rules.

difficulty.justification: Lotteries provide strong identification so domain knowledge needn't be highly specialized, but results depend on institutional details (post‑Katrina takeovers, grandfathering, city differences) and there are plausible complications (noncompliance, attrition, peer/spillover effects, heterogeneous treatment) that make the sign moderately challenging to pin down and to generalize.

triplet_key: 19473|higher public basic research investment|private applied research investment
context: This paper combines a theoretical multi-industry general-equilibrium model with structural estimation using French firm-level R&D data (basic vs. applied) for 2000–2006 (13,708 firm-year observations), matched balance-sheet and multi-industry ownership data (EAE, LIFI), and U.S. patent citation data (1974–2006). Units are firms (product lines, industries); the institutional setting is France’s R&D policy environment (public basic-research funding ≈0.5% of GDP and existing corporate R&D subsidies ≈10%). The analysis targets spillovers, technology diffusion, and innovation policy counterfactuals.

difficulty.justification: Establishing a positive effect requires specialized theory and identification to separate knowledge spillovers from substitution/crowding-out, account for GE feedbacks and industry heterogeneity, and validate patent-citation links; the paper's rich data and structural approach materially help but residual confounding and institutional specificity leave nontrivial uncertainty.

context: This paper studies the welfare cost of consumption fluctuations in the U.S. economy using annual data (primarily 1889–1997 and 1954–1997). It analyzes representative-agent consumption and dividend series, CRSP equity returns, and the real term structure to infer a pricing kernel and value the marginal and total costs of business-cycle (defined as cycles ≤8 years) and broader consumption uncertainty. The approach combines theoretical asset-pricing identities with VAR/cointegration models, band-pass filtering, and asset-pricing kernel estimation.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether removing consumption uncertainty raises long-run growth requires deep asset-pricing and macro growth theory, is highly context- and model-dependent, has credible opposing channels (e.g., risk-taking/innovation vs. precautionary saving effects), involves general-equilibrium and selection feedbacks, and rests on an essentially unobservable counterfactual so available evidence is weak without strong identifying assumptions.

triplet_key: 21530|public audit oversight (pcaob inspection regime)|earnings response coefficient (erc)
context: This paper examines U.S. public firms’ market responses to earnings news around the rollout of the PCAOB under SOX, exploiting inspections from 2003–2007 (limited Big Four inspections in 2003, annual inspections for large auditors in 2004, and a triennial rollout for small auditors). The analysis uses U.S. issuers (and U.S.-traded cross-listed foreign firms as controls) at the earnings-announcement level (3-day CARs and unexpected earnings) to infer changes in reporting credibility from the institutional shift from peer review to public audit oversight.

difficulty.justification: Understanding the sign requires applied knowledge of audit regulation and market pricing; the staggered PCAOB rollout aids identification but effects vary by auditor size, firm type, and concurrent SOX-era changes, creating important confounders. Multiple channels (auditor behavior, reporting quality, market perceptions) and potential selection/validation issues make a positive ERC effect plausible but nontrivial to establish convincingly.

triplet_key: 21562|casino payments (per-capita tribal cash transfers)|enjoyable relationship with parent
context: This triplet is estimated using the Great Smoky Mountains Study (1993‑onward) of children in 11 western North Carolina counties (oversampling the Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians). It exploits exogenous per‑capita tribal casino transfers (~$4,000/year, disbursed mid‑1990s to enrolled adult tribal members) and compares younger American Indian cohorts exposed before age 16 with older cohorts and non‑Indian families. The outcome "enjoyable relationship with parent" is reported by the child in the GSMS (measured in annual interviews, reported at age 16) and estimated with individual fixed‑effects panel regressions.

difficulty.justification: Understanding the sign requires intermediate household-policy knowledge; effects likely vary with local institutions and culture and are plausibly confounded by community-level casino effects and parental behavior changes. Multiple mediating channels (income, stress, time use, substance use) and possible spillovers make identification nontrivial, though the GSMS cohort/exposure design and fixed effects provide moderately credible evidence.

triplet_key: 11756|effective tax rate on equity securities|asset returns
treatment: Effective tax rate on equity securities
context: This paper examines whether personal taxes on equity are capitalized into U.S. stock valuations using annual aggregate data for the U.S. stock market from 1917–2004. The unit of analysis is aggregate equity valuations and S&P returns (price-earnings, price-dividend, Tobin’s q), and the author constructs effective dividend and capital-gains tax rates accounting for statutory rates, payout behavior, and tax‑qualified accounts. The setting spans major tax reforms (including wartime hikes and the 2003 cuts) and controls for macroeconomic covariates in time‑series regressions.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate asset‑pricing and tax policy knowledge; the sign depends on institutional details (payout behavior, tax‑deferred accounts, international investor composition) and multiple competing channels, and long‑run aggregate time‑series evidence is informative but still vulnerable to confounders and identification challenges.

triplet_key: 23288|closure of office of thrift supervision|shadow bank market share
treatment: Closure of Office of Thrift Supervision
context: This paper studies U.S. residential mortgage origination from 2007–2015 using HMDA, Fannie/Freddie, and FHA loan data. Units of analysis are individual loans, borrowers, lenders (banks, non‑bank shadow lenders, and online fintech originators), and counties. The institutional setting is the post‑crisis regulatory environment—Basel III capital rules, changes to mortgage servicing rights (MSR) treatment, mortgage‑related lawsuit settlements, and the 2011 OTS closure—together with GSE/FHA financing dynamics and the rise of online origination technology; the authors exploit geographic and temporal variation in regulatory exposure to identify effects.

difficulty.justification: Pinning down the effect of the OTS closure on shadow‑bank mortgage share requires specialized knowledge of post‑crisis bank regulation, GSE/FHA financing and origination technology, and is highly context dependent given concurrent reforms and local market structure. Geographic/temporal variation in rich HMDA/GSE data aids identification, but multiple plausible confounders, regulatory spillovers and feedbacks make the sign moderately ambiguous and empirically challenging.

triplet_key: 29116|removal of explicit gender requests|total applications to previously-female jobs
context: The paper analyzes the causal effects of an unexpected removal of employers’ standardized ‘preferred gender’ field from all job ads on XMRC.com (a Xiamen, China job board) on March 1, 2019. Using internal ad-, application-, resume- and call-back records for roughly one year surrounding the ban, the authors study ad- and application-level outcomes (jobs, workers, firms), exploiting within-ad pre/post changes, high-frequency (daily/weekly) time variation, and a 2018–2019 lunar-calendar comparison. Only the encoded gender label was removed; embedded text preferences remained.

difficulty.justification: Reasoning requires intermediate labor-market and discrimination knowledge; the net sign depends on offsetting channels (women deterred vs. men newly attracted) and on remaining embedded text, so context and selection matter. The authors’ within-ad, high-frequency pre/post design is strong, but institutional specifics and plausible alternative channels leave nontrivial ambiguity.

context: The CAL effect is estimated from a randomized experiment in Vadodara municipal primary schools (implemented in 2002–03 and continued in 2003–04) that provided grade‑4 students two hours per week of shared computer-assisted math games using existing school computers. The unit of observation is fourth‑grade students in municipal schools; schools were randomized (stratified) to CAL vs. comparison and outcomes were measured with repeated pre/post academic tests.

difficulty.justification: A stratified randomized school‑level trial with repeated pre/post tests delivers strong internal validity, so identifying the positive sign is straightforward. Remaining issues are mainly external validity, implementation fidelity, and small spillovers rather than confounding.

context: This paper studies the macroeconomic effects of federal housing agencies' mortgage portfolio purchases in the United States from the postwar era with emphasis on January 1967–December 2006 (and extensions through the 2007–08 crisis). Units are monthly aggregate mortgage markets and agency balance sheets (Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac, Ginnie Mae), mortgage originations, rates, housing starts and homeownership. The institutional setting is US GSE policy (capital requirements, portfolio caps, conforming loan limits, Treasury/Fed interventions); identification exploits ex‑ante narrative policy events and GSE stock‑market news.

difficulty.justification: Requires substantial institutional and mortgage-market knowledge; the sign is context-sensitive and confounded by macro cycles, monetary policy, and reverse causation. Multiple channels (rates, underwriting, house prices) and general-equilibrium feedbacks make identification nontrivial, though narrative shocks/market-news strategies can help.

triplet_key: 20470|increase in ma capitation payments|plan benefits actuarial value
context: Using U.S. county-level data from 1997–2003, the paper studies how the 2000 Benefits Improvement and Protection Act (BIPA), implemented March 2001, raised Medicare Advantage (MA) county capitation payments via urban/rural payment floors. The analysis uses counties (and county-year averages of premiums, benefits, enrollment, and Traditional Medicare costs) and plan-level data to estimate how insurers adjusted premiums and benefits for Medicare beneficiaries in the pre-2004 demographic risk-adjustment regime.

difficulty.justification: Understanding this link requires intermediate knowledge of Medicare Advantage incentives and plan behavior; BIPA provides plausibly exogenous county payment variation, but selection, plan entry/exit, and insurers’ choice between raising premiums vs benefits (and limited risk adjustment pre-2004) make the sign moderately ambiguous and require careful identification.

triplet_key: 26458|borrowing from a nonbank lender|initial interest rate
context: Using hand-collected credit agreements for U.S. publicly traded middle-market firms (annual revenues $10M–$1B) during 2010–2015, the paper studies why these firms borrow directly from nonbank financial intermediaries. The analysis exploits variation across local banking markets (MSA/non-MSA counties) and bank regulator leniency (OCC vs. state supervision) and regulatory guidance that treats cash-flow loans to firms with negative EBITDA as “substandard.” Units are individual loan contracts and firms; loan pricing, covenant structure, and lender identity are analyzed using SEC-filed agreements.

difficulty.justification: Selection of riskier or distressed firms into nonbank lending and local/regulatory supply shocks create substantial endogeneity and heterogeneity concerns; the rich contract-level data and variation in local banking markets and regulator leniency improve identification but do not fully eliminate alternative explanations for higher initial rates.

context: This paper reports a two‑year (FY2011–12 to FY2012–13) randomized field experiment in Punjab, Pakistan, allocating 482 urban property-tax circles (three-person tax teams, ≈550 staff) to three performance-pay schemes or control. Using circle-level administrative quarterly revenue records and a survey of ~16,000 properties (≈12,000 random, ≈4,000 re-assessed), the study evaluates impacts on revenue, assessments, taxpayer satisfaction, and bribe incidence in a context of formulaic GARV property taxation, weak enforcement, and pervasive collusion between inspectors and taxpayers.

difficulty.justification: An RCT with administrative revenue outcomes gives strong identification of a positive effect, so domain knowledge needed is moderate and evidence generally sufficient; however, setting-specific factors (weak enforcement, collusion), potential gaming/timing effects, and spillovers make the sign somewhat context-dependent and require careful interpretation.

context: The paper analyzes U.S. state corporate tax changes (statutory rates and apportionment rules) over roughly 1980–2012 using a panel of ~490 county-groups (aggregated from over 3,000 counties) and matched firm–establishment data. Units include establishments, local populations, wages and housing rents. Identification exploits state-level variation in corporate and personal tax rules — including external tax changes via apportionment — to estimate impacts on establishment entry, wages, rents and profits within a spatial-equilibrium framework with heterogeneous firms.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether state corporate tax cuts raise worker welfare requires specialized incidence and spatial-equilibrium knowledge and is sensitive to local industry structure, mobility, and fiscal offsets, making the sign context-dependent and confoundable. The paper's rich panel and firm–establishment data plus apportionment variation materially improve identification, but multiple mediating channels and potential policy endogeneity keep the task substantially challenging.

context: This paper studies India’s dismantling of small-scale industry (SSI) product reservations (major de-reservation 1997–2008) and its effects on manufacturing establishments and districts using the Annual Survey of Industries panel (primarily 2000–2007) and NSS unorganized-manufacturing rounds (2000, 2005). Units are establishments (product-level incumbents and entrants), products, and districts. The institutional setting is the national SSI reservation regime (eligibility tied to a plant-and-machinery cap around Rs.10 million) that restricted certain product lines to small firms; de-reservation varied by product timing, generating differential local exposure.
