context: Using the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development’s Moving to Opportunity randomized experiment (1994–98 enrollment, 10–15 year follow-up surveys in 2008–10), the paper studies low-income public-housing families with children in five U.S. cities (Baltimore, Boston, Chicago, Los Angeles, New York). Families were randomly assigned to an Experimental voucher (restricted to low-poverty tracts plus counseling), a regular Section 8 voucher, or control; outcomes for adults and youth are analyzed using survey, administrative, and neighborhood-tract data.

difficulty.justification: Randomization of MTO assignment gives strong internal identification, so establishing the sign is relatively straightforward; however, differential take-up, attrition, heterogeneous effects across cities/ages, and multiple mediating channels (neighborhood environment, diet/activity, stress) create moderate context dependence and complexity.

triplet_key: 20767|reducing urban residency positions|quality of rural residents
context: Analyzes the U.S. family medicine residency market (approximately 430 programs and ~3,000 residents per year) over 2003–2010 (with 2011–12 out-of-sample validation). Units are individual residents and residency programs participating in the centralized NRMP clearinghouse (pairwise-stable, many-to-one matching) where salaries are pre-set. The paper studies antitrust concerns about low resident wages and policy interventions to increase rural training, leveraging program and resident characteristics, Medicare-based reimbursement variation, and final-match data to estimate preferences and run counterfactual simulations.

difficulty.justification: The effect depends critically on NRMP matching behavior, applicant and program selection, Medicare funding rules and strategic responses, so specialized knowledge and structural identification are needed; multiple channels could plausibly reverse the sign, and although rich match and reimbursement data improve identification, strong assumptions are required for credible causal inference.

triplet_key: 14610|decline in bilateral trade barriers|total french sales
context: This paper studies French manufacturing firms using firm-level sales in 1986 for 229,900 firms (34,035 exporters) across 113 destinations (including France). It examines export entry, sales distributions, and how firm efficiency, fixed entry costs, and bilateral trade barriers shape participation and sales, combining customs and administrative manufacturing data. The authors estimate a heterogeneous‑firms trade model (Melitz-style) by the method of simulated moments and run general‑equilibrium counterfactuals (e.g., lower trade barriers or entry costs) to quantify effects on firm sales, exits, and welfare.

difficulty.justification: Assessing this link requires specialized heterogeneous‑firm trade theory and GE reasoning; multiple opposing channels (selection, reallocation, export diversion vs scale) make the sign context‑dependent and ambiguous, though the rich firm‑level data and structural estimation give reasonable identification conditional on model assumptions.

context: This paper evaluates the causal impact of improving implementation of India’s National Rural Employment Guarantee Scheme (NREGS) by randomizing the 2010–2012 staggered rollout of biometric “Smartcard” payment reforms across sub-districts (mandals) in rural Andhra Pradesh. It uses an RCT at mandal-scale with household surveys of ~5,000 NREGS-registered jobcard holders and independent government censuses (SECC, Economic and Livestock Censuses) to measure effects on wages, employment, incomes, prices, and poverty in imperfectly competitive local labor markets.

difficulty.justification: Interpreting how improved NREGS implementation affects private wages requires intermediate policy and local labor-market knowledge; the sign plausibly flips with local institutions and channels (reservation wage, labor supply, income-demand and bargaining), and identifying those GE and spillover effects is moderately complex. The mandal-level RCT gives strong internal leverage, but potential spillovers, selection into NREGS and measurement limits mean empirical identification is feasible but not trivial.

context: This study evaluates early impacts of the Moving to Opportunity (MTO) randomized housing mobility demonstration at the Boston site. It uses families with children living in public housing in high-poverty (1990 tract poverty ≥40%) areas who enrolled between October 1994 and May 1996, with follow-up surveys on average 2.2 years after random assignment (1997–1998) and administrative quarterly earnings/welfare data through 1998. The program randomly assigned households to an Experimental restricted low-poverty voucher with counseling, an unrestricted Section 8 voucher, or a Control group.

difficulty.justification: Random assignment gives strong identification of the ITT, so moderate domain knowledge suffices, but heterogeneous take-up, measurement of behavior, mediating channels (peers, school, family stress), and site-specific effects make the sign for boys context-dependent and nontrivial to pin down.

context: Using repeated cross-sections of the US General Social Survey (1974–1994) matched to MSA/PMSA characteristics from the 1970–1990 Censuses, the paper studies how local income inequality and racial/ethnic fragmentation influence individual membership in voluntary groups. Units are individual GSS respondents nested in metropolitan areas; key community covariates are interpolated Gini coefficients and 1990 racial/ethnic fragmentation indices. Empirical analysis controls for state and year effects and instruments local inequality to support causal interpretation.

difficulty.justification: Moderate domain knowledge suffices but the sign is context-dependent (US metros 1974–94) and plausible countervailing mechanisms exist; unobserved community traits, selection/migration and measurement error make confounding likely, and IV strategies help but their validity is often debatable, so establishing the negative causal sign is fairly difficult.

context: This paper studies U.S. households (primarily 1996–2005 survey waves) across states to assess how state-level bankruptcy asset exemption laws (Chapter 7) and hospital credit provision (EMTALA/charity care practices) create implicit “bankruptcy” health insurance. Using household-level data (MEPS, SIPP, PSID) and a web survey of uninsured adults, the author exploits cross-state and within-state variation in exemption rules to analyze uninsured households’ out-of-pocket medical payments and insurance take-up, and calibrates a 2005 microsimulation to evaluate policy welfare implications.

difficulty.justification: Determining that more seizable assets raise insurance take-up requires intermediate knowledge of bankruptcy exemptions, insurance incentives, and hospital/credit institutions; the sign depends strongly on institutional/contextual details and there are plausible opposing mechanisms and confounders (income, selection, charity care), so identification is moderately complex though the described cross-state and within-state variation can provide reasonably informative evidence.

context: This paper studies the causal effect of the ACA Medicaid eligibility expansions (primarily 2014, staggered 2014–2017) on mortality in the United States by linking 2008–2013 American Community Survey respondents (panel tracked 2008–2017) to administrative Medicaid enrollment (CMS) and death records (Census Numident and MDAC). The unit is near-elderly low‑socioeconomic adults—primarily individuals ages 55–64 in 2014 who were citizens with family income ≤138% FPL or less than high‑school education—comparing residents of expansion versus non‑expansion states under the optional ACA expansion.

difficulty.justification: Interpreting ACA Medicaid expansion effects on mortality requires intermediate policy and health-economics knowledge; the sign is plausible (reduced mortality) but depends on state context, baseline coverage, and timing. Identification is helped by administrative enrollment and death links and staggered rollout, but challenges remain from selection, migration, treatment take-up, rare outcome/power, and heterogeneous timing/effects, so a careful quasi-experimental strategy is needed.

context: This paper studies the impacts of large dam construction in India over roughly 1970–1999 (with crop analyses 1971–1987 and disease and rainfall panels through the 1990s). The analysis uses Indian districts as units, GIS-derived river and dam locations to classify upstream/catchment and downstream/command areas, and district-level panels on agricultural production, irrigation, rural consumption, poverty and wages. Context includes state-led dam planning/financing, the Land Acquisition Act (1894), documented displacement, and environmental impacts of reservoirs.

difficulty.justification: Assessing whether large dams raise poverty requires specialized knowledge of infrastructure, resettlement, irrigation and ecological channels; effects vary strongly with institutions (compensation, relocation, irrigation delivery) and are vulnerable to selection, spillovers and measurement confounding, so pinning down the sign is quite difficult even with district panels and GIS.

triplet_key: 21581|increase in household debt to gdp|subsequent gdp growth
treatment: Increase in household debt to GDP
context: Using an unbalanced annual panel of 30 countries from 1960–2012, this paper studies country‑year variation in household and non‑financial firm debt (debt/GDP), GDP, unemployment, trade and mortgage spreads. Units are countries; analyses exploit cross‑country and within‑country dynamics including exchange‑rate regimes, proximity to the zero lower bound, and external borrowing to assess how expansions in household debt (especially three‑year changes) predict subsequent GDP growth, unemployment, consumption and trade dynamics under varying monetary and external constraints.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether household debt expansions cause lower subsequent GDP growth requires more than common-sense macro knowledge because effects vary by regime (monetary/external constraints) and timing, and there are strong risks of reverse causation and omitted credit‑supply shocks. Multiple mediating channels and general-equilibrium feedbacks make sign identification highly ambiguous without quasi-experimental identification or very rich controls.

context: This paper studies U.S. life insurers and their affiliated reinsurers (captives) over 2002–2012 using company- and reinsurance-agreement Schedule S data. The units are operating companies, affiliated (unauthorized, unrated) reinsurers, and reinsurance contracts, set in the institutional context of post-2000 Regulation XXX/AXXX statutory reserve changes and new captive laws (post‑2002) and concentrated domiciles (South Carolina, Vermont, Bermuda, Barbados, Cayman). The authors evaluate effects on marginal cost, pricing, issuance, and risk-based capital.

difficulty.justification: Requires detailed institutional knowledge of statutory reserving, captives, and RBC rules; sign plausibly negative but is context-dependent (domicile, regulation) and subject to strong endogeneity/selection and multiple channels, though the Schedule S data and post‑2002 regulatory changes provide useful identifying variation.

triplet_key: 29406|pooling small group and individual markets|extent of adverse selection in individual market
treatment: Pooling small group and individual markets
outcome: Extent of adverse selection in individual market
context: This paper empirically analyzes U.S. health‑insurance market segmentation using Oregon all‑payer claims and plan files (2014–2016), comparing small‑group (employer) and individual marketplace enrollment and premiums for households. Units are households and plans; authors exploit “forced switchers” who lose employer coverage, insurer premium and MLR data, and ACA institutional features (guaranteed issue, metal tiers, risk adjustment, risk corridors, reinsurance). They structurally estimate demand and insurer pricing to simulate counterfactual pooling (e.g., ICHRA employer shifts) and validate results using multi‑state premium/MLR data.

difficulty.justification: Determining that pooling small‑group and individual markets reduces adverse selection requires specialized knowledge of insurance pricing, ACA institutions and risk‑adjustment mechanics; the sign depends on institutional details and sorting behavior, multiple mediating channels (pricing responses, composition, risk adjustment) create plausible opposite predictions, and the authors’ forced‑switcher design plus structural estimation provide decent but assumption‑dependent evidence.

context: Using the German Socio-Economic Panel (1984–1989) for West Germany, this estimate focuses on Arbeiter (hourly workers) in manufacturing (person–job spells). The paper exploits within-job temporal variation from industry-level union‑negotiated reductions in standard weekly hours (mid‑1980s onward) and estimates person–job fixed-effects panel regressions (with IV robustness) to relate reductions in agreed standard hours to changes in actual weekly hours.

difficulty.justification: Understanding the sign requires intermediate labor‑economics and institutional knowledge (collective bargaining, overtime rules). The effect is context dependent (institutions, overtime use) and operates through multiple mediating channels (overtime substitution, intensity, composition), so alternative signs are moderately plausible, but the person–job fixed effects plus IV strategy provide reasonably strong evidence for a negative effect in this specific setting.

context: This paper studies the macroeconomic effects of federal housing agencies' mortgage portfolio purchases in the United States from the postwar era with emphasis on January 1967–December 2006 (and extensions through the 2007–08 crisis). Units are monthly aggregate mortgage markets and agency balance sheets (Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac, Ginnie Mae), mortgage originations, rates, housing starts and homeownership. The institutional setting is US GSE policy (capital requirements, portfolio caps, conforming loan limits, Treasury/Fed interventions); identification exploits ex‑ante narrative policy events and GSE stock‑market news.

difficulty.justification: Identifying how agency mortgage purchases affect aggregate unemployment requires specialized macro‑and housing finance knowledge and varies strongly with institutional era, monetary/fiscal context, and countercyclical interventions; multiple opposing channels (stimulus via mortgage rates and construction vs. risk/exposure, selection and reverse causality) and GE feedbacks make the sign highly ambiguous and hard to pin down even with narrative-event identification.

triplet_key: 15362|refinancing-facilitated home-equity extraction|systemic risk of mortgage market
context: This triplet is studied for the U.S. residential housing market using a calibrated, reduced-form simulation of individual homes/mortgages (vintages) from 1919–2008, with emphasis on the refinancing boom and price decline in 1999–2008 (notably June 2006–Dec 2008). The unit is homeowner mortgages nationally under institutional features (widespread cash‑out and rate/term refinancing; 30‑year fixed, often non‑recourse mortgages). The simulation uses simple heuristics, does not model lenders or supply constraints, and assumes households can refinance freely at prevailing historical rates.

difficulty.justification: Assessing whether cash‑out refinancing raises systemic mortgage risk requires specialized knowledge of mortgage contracts, securitization and macro‑financial feedbacks; the sign is highly sensitive to institutional details (non‑recourse rules, lender behavior) and multiple confounding channels exist, while the described simulation omits key lender/supply interactions that are crucial for establishing the systemic link.

context: The paper examines how heterogeneity in U.S. state tax rates affected the spatial allocation of workers and firms using annual state-level data from 1980–2010. It analyzes workers, establishments, trade flows and state governments across U.S. states, focusing on personal income, corporate (sales‑apportioned) and general sales taxes, federal transfers, and public‑good provision within a spatial general‑equilibrium framework. The authors exploit over 350 actual state tax changes and observed worker/firm relocations to estimate mobility elasticities and to simulate counterfactual, harmonized tax distributions.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether higher government spending raises a state's employment share requires spatial general‑equilibrium theory and careful identification: spending can create public‑sector jobs and local demand but is often financed by taxes or transfers that crowd out private employment, while migration, spillovers, and reverse causality make the sign highly context‑dependent and empirically challenging.

context: Using the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development’s Moving to Opportunity randomized experiment (1994–98 enrollment, 10–15 year follow-up surveys in 2008–10), the paper studies low-income public-housing families with children in five U.S. cities (Baltimore, Boston, Chicago, Los Angeles, New York). Families were randomly assigned to an Experimental voucher (restricted to low-poverty tracts plus counseling), a regular Section 8 voucher, or control; outcomes for adults and youth are analyzed using survey, administrative, and neighborhood-tract data.

difficulty.justification: Random assignment gives clear leverage, so medium domain knowledge suffices, but partial take-up, attrition, power limits, heterogeneous effects across cities/gender, and multiple mediating channels make confidently concluding a null effect context-dependent and moderately difficult.

triplet_key: 27258|bank capital requirement|monetary policy transmission to lending
context: Using U.S. commercial bank balance-sheet and branch data (Call Reports and FDIC Summary of Deposits) over 1994–2017, the paper structurally estimates how federal-funds-rate movements transmit through banks to borrowers. The unit of analysis is bank-level deposits, loans, spreads, and market shares in the U.S. market; the model incorporates deposit and loan market power, reserve and capital requirements, maturity mismatch, and costly non-deposit funding in the U.S. regulatory environment.

difficulty.justification: Requires specialized knowledge of bank regulatory and balance-sheet channels; the sign depends on institutional details and there are multiple, opposing mediating channels, and although rich bank-level data and a structural model help, identification remains challenging and model-dependent.

context: The paper analyzes the Canadian National Breast Screening Study (CNBSS), a randomized trial that enrolled about 90,000 women aged 40–59 between 1980–1985 in Canada, focusing on women aged 40–49. Participants were randomized to an intervention offering annual mammography and clinical breast exams during an active period (enrollment plus 3–4 years) or to usual care. Using individual-level mammography uptake and 20-year follow-up via cancer registries, it examines overdiagnosis (breast cancer incidence) and all-cause mortality across compliance groups.

difficulty.justification: Randomization gives strong leverage, but noncompliance, low expected effect on all‑cause mortality, potential treatment harms from overdiagnosis, competing risks and period-specific care mean the sign (no effect) is plausible but not trivial to establish with precision without careful IV/per‑protocol analyses and power considerations.

context: The paper analyzes monthly bank‑level data on 1,264 listed financial institutions across 17 countries (Australia, Belgium, Canada, Denmark, Finland, France, Germany, Italy, Japan, Netherlands, Norway, Portugal, Spain, Sweden, Switzerland, the UK, and the US) over roughly 1987–2015 (with price series back to the 1970s). It links stock‑market and real‑estate bubble episodes (identified via BSADF and a trend‑deviation approach) to bank‑level systemic risk (∆CoVaR and MES), distinguishing boom and bust phases and heterogeneity by bank size, loan growth, leverage, and maturity mismatch.

difficulty.justification: Economic theory gives clear channels both raising and lowering short‑term bank risk during asset price booms (higher collateral and profits vs. greater risk‑taking, maturity/liquidity mismatches, and common exposures), and empirical identification is complicated by reverse causality, common shocks, cross‑country institutional heterogeneity, and measurement issues—so credible identification is moderately to highly challenging but feasible with rich panel methods.

context: This paper analyzes quarterly data from 1920–2012 for 20 developed countries, using country-level bank-sector observations (past three-year change in bank credit-to-GDP, bank equity indices, dividend yields) to study how credit expansions predict subsequent bank equity crash risk and mean excess returns. The institutional setting focuses on bank lending to households and non-financial firms (BIS and Schularick & Taylor loan series), spans major historical episodes (Great Depression, 1990s crises, 2007–08), and uses country-quarter observations aggregated to non-overlapping 1–3 year horizons with standard market controls.

difficulty.justification: Assessing a negative effect of credit expansions on subsequent bank equity returns requires specialized macro‑finance/banking knowledge; the sign depends on institutional and cyclical context and many alternative mechanisms (growth vs. boom‑bust, anticipation, reverse causality) are plausible, while the long multi‑country historical panel helps identification but does not eliminate complex GE, selection, and confounding challenges.

context: This analysis examines whether U.S. states that implemented laws requiring Amazon to collect sales tax (19 treated states with staggered adoptions in 2012–2015) experienced different GDP growth. The unit of observation is state-quarter: the authors collect five-year GDP growth data in a window around each state’s implementation year and regress state-level GDP growth on indicators for treated states after implementation, including state and time fixed effects and weights, comparing treated states to control states that did not change Amazon tax status.

difficulty.justification: Multiple plausible opposing channels (tax incidence, substitution of online/offline purchases, cross‑border sales, and revenue‑driven public spending) and variation across states make the sign highly context dependent; staggered adoption, endogeneity of policy timing, measurement noise in state GDP, and likely small effects relative to GDP make correct identification difficult without strong identification strategies.

context: The paper analyzes early COVID-19 dynamics using daily confirmed-case time series pooled from 16 high-risk countries/regions (including China, Japan, South Korea, Singapore, Taiwan, Thailand, UAE, Vietnam, Malaysia, Australia, Canada, France, Germany, Italy, UK, and US) during January–February 2020 to estimate stochastic transmission parameters under an SIS framework (γ set to ≈1/14 days). Those estimates are embedded in a partial-equilibrium asset-pricing model with firm-level mitigation and a Poisson vaccine-arrival process (calibrated around 1/year) to study optimal mitigation and effects on firm valuations.

difficulty.justification: Assessing how transmission volatility alters the optimal dynamic mitigation rule requires specialized epidemiological and asset-pricing expertise, is highly sensitive to institutional/timing/calibration choices (vaccine arrival, γ, firm heterogeneity), admits competing mechanisms that can flip the sign, involves complex stochastic dynamic optimisation and expectation feedbacks, and early pooled COVID data provide limited identification.

triplet_key: 22057|end of the professor's privilege|university patenting rate
context: The paper analyzes Norway’s January 1, 2003 reform that ended the “professor’s privilege,” transferring majority commercialization rights to universities and creating TTOs. Using linked Norwegian administrative data on university-employed PhDs, national firm incorporation records (startup analysis 2000–2007), and patent applications (1995–2010), the authors compare individual-level entrepreneurship and patenting before and after the reform against various control groups to estimate the policy’s causal effects on startup and patenting rates and quality.

difficulty.justification: Understanding the sign requires intermediate institutional knowledge of academic IP regimes and TTO incentives; effects vary with organizational design, discipline, and enforcement, so context matters. Multiple plausible channels (faculty incentives vs. university commercialization capacity), selection and timing issues make the sign ambiguous, though the rich individual-level Norwegian data and pre/post controls make a credible inference feasible with careful identification.

context: This paper evaluates the causal impact of improving implementation of India’s National Rural Employment Guarantee Scheme (NREGS) by randomizing the 2010–2012 staggered rollout of biometric “Smartcard” payment reforms across sub-districts (mandals) in rural Andhra Pradesh. It uses an RCT at mandal-scale with household surveys of ~5,000 NREGS-registered jobcard holders and independent government censuses (SECC, Economic and Livestock Censuses) to measure effects on wages, employment, incomes, prices, and poverty in imperfectly competitive local labor markets.

difficulty.justification: Randomized staggered rollout gives strong identification so empirical evidence is fairly sufficient, but sign can be driven by multiple competing channels (demand stimulus vs. substitution/reservation-wage effects), local market structure and scale matter, and GE/selection feedbacks make the sign moderately complex to reason about.

triplet_key: 24441|earlier childhood move to better areas|adult income
treatment: Earlier childhood move to better areas
context: Using de-identified linked Census short forms, federal tax records, and the ACS covering roughly the U.S. population from 1989–2015, the paper studies intergenerational income mobility for the 1978–1983 birth cohorts in the United States. Units are children linked to parents (parent income measured 1994–2000; children’s incomes 2014–15), analyzed across geographies (Census tracts, blocks, commuting zones). The design exploits variation in parental characteristics, childhood neighborhood exposure, and timing of moves to assess drivers of racial gaps in adult earnings and related outcomes.

difficulty.justification: Assessing a positive effect of earlier moves to better neighborhoods requires intermediate-to-advanced knowledge of neighborhood effects and identification strategies; effects vary by timing, race, and local institutions. Rich linked administrative data and variation in timing help identification, but strong selection, heterogeneous channels, and general equilibrium/selection concerns keep the sign nontrivial to establish.

context: This paper studies the causal effects of the UK Regional Selective Assistance (RSA) investment-subsidy program on manufacturing plants and firms in Britain over 1986–2004. Using administrative panel data on plants, firms and program applicants (SAMIS, IDBR, ARD), and area aggregates at ward and Travel-to-Work levels, it exploits exogenous EU-driven changes to area eligibility (maps in 1993 and 2000) and Net Grant Equivalent rates to estimate impacts on employment, investment, productivity, firm entry/exit, and local unemployment.

difficulty.justification: Exploiting EU-driven eligibility and grant-rate variation provides fairly strong identification, so common policy/market knowledge suffices, but multiple channels (entry/exit, spillovers, labor supply and GE effects) and institutional/time-specific features mean moderate ambiguity and complexity despite good administrative data.

context: The paper studies how U.S. trade policy affected production relocation and retail prices for large residential washing machines (and complementary dryers) in the United States across three episodes: the 2012 antidumping duties on Korea/Mexico, the 2016 antidumping duties on China, and the 2018 Section 201 global safeguard tariffs. It combines monthly trade flows, Korean parts exports, CPI, and weekly retailer-model price microdata (brands: LG, Samsung, Whirlpool, Maytag, G.E.) over roughly 2010–2019 to examine firm sourcing, onshoring, and consumer price incidence.

difficulty.justification: Reasonable intermediate policy and market knowledge is needed; supply‑chain adjustments are context‑dependent and admit plausible alternative stories (contemporaneous tariffs, exchange rates, firm strategy), and the causal link runs through multi‑step sourcing and parts flows. However, the paper's rich, firm‑ and model‑level trade and price microdata across multiple policy episodes substantially improves identification, making the task moderately difficult rather than extremely so.

context: This triplet refers to a model-based counterfactual computed for year 2015 using the paper's estimated structural search-and-learning model (estimated on CRSP and Morningstar data). The experiment sets all active funds' marketing expenses to zero (bj=0) and resolves the equilibrium (funds reoptimize fees; index fund price held fixed). Units are heterogeneous retail investors who sequentially search (with a distribution of search costs); the outcome is the model-implied aggregate search costs across investors in that no-marketing equilibrium.

difficulty.justification: Determining the sign needs specialized structural-search-and-learning theory and industry knowledge; it depends on institutional details and equilibrium fee adjustments, and opposite outcomes are plausibly consistent absent careful modeling and credible estimation.

context: This paper uses postwar quarterly macro data for the US (1948–1994) and other G7 countries (samples vary, typically 1960s–1994) at the country level, using aggregate GDP, employment (or hours), and labor productivity to decompose fluctuations into technology versus demand/monetary shocks under structural VAR identification with long‑run restrictions; the setting compares predictions of RBC versus sticky‑price imperfect‑competition models and evaluates how identified shocks drive employment and productivity.

difficulty.justification: Distinguishing demand from technology shocks and sign of employment response requires specialized macro theory and credible identification (long‑run SVAR restrictions) rather than common sense; institutional differences and measurement/identification concerns make opposite explanations plausible and entail multi‑step GE reasoning, so evidence is informative but not definitive.

context: In this paper the authors study U.S. temporary bonus-depreciation incentives from the 2002 and 2003 laws (30% then 50%, retroactive to Sept. 11, 2001, largely expiring end-2004), using quarterly BEA investment and price series matched to IRS/MACRS schedules for 36 capital-good types (panel 1959–2005; focus 2001–2005) and cross-sectional eligibility (MACRS recovery ≤20 years) and time variation to identify effects on investment and prices. They combine estimated supply elasticities with a general-equilibrium model to infer aggregate U.S. GDP and employment effects (≈0.1–0.2% GDP; $10–20 billion).

difficulty.justification: Identifying a positive GDP effect from bonus depreciation requires specialized knowledge of tax depreciation, investment timing and price responses and is sensitive to the policy's temporary nature and concurrent shocks (context matters). The sign is plausible but confounded by intertemporal substitution, demand shocks, and general-equilibrium feedbacks, and inference to aggregate GDP depends on modeling assumptions despite fairly detailed micro data.

triplet_key: 18487|assigned to a school with more minority students|arrest and incarceration probability
treatment: Assigned to a school with more minority students
context: This paper examines the causal effects of the end of race-based busing in Charlotte-Mecklenburg Schools following court actions that led to contiguous neighborhood-based school zones implemented in fall 2002. Using CMS administrative data (1995–2011) linked to county arrest/incarceration records and National Student Clearinghouse college data, the authors follow student cohorts (rising 6th graders 1996–2002 and adjacent cohorts) in Mecklenburg County, NC, comparing students on opposite sides of newly drawn school boundaries to estimate impacts on achievement, attainment, and young-adult crime.

difficulty.justification: An RD/natural experiment framework helps, so intermediate domain knowledge suffices, but effects operate through multiple mediating channels (peer effects, school resources, policing, selection) and depend strongly on local institutions and timing; alternative signs are plausible without careful identification and heterogeneity analysis.

context: This analysis uses an unbalanced panel of U.S. publicly traded bank holding companies (FR Y-9C filers). The z-score regressions cover 1996–2011 at the BHC-year level and regress distance-to-default (z-score, computed as (ROA + market-capitalized equity/asset ratio) divided by the six-year quarterly SD of ROA) on TPS / Tier 1 and standard bank controls.

difficulty.justification: Requires specialized banking-capital knowledge and understanding of how capital instruments affect measured solvency. High risk of reverse causality, omitted heterogeneity, regulatory/time variation, and measurement feedbacks make the sign hard to identify from standard panel regressions without strong identification.

triplet_key: 21819|private information about future job loss|private ui market profitability
treatment: Private information about future job loss
context: Uses U.S. panel data to study private information about impending job loss and implications for unemployment insurance. Main analyses exploit HRS subjective probability elicitations (1992–2013) for individuals under 65 and their spouses and PSID household expenditure panels (working-age heads, ~25–65) to track involuntary job loss/unemployment over the next 12 months. The institutional setting is the U.S. labor market with existing government UI; key assumptions include private beliefs about job-loss risk, observable underwriting covariates, and dynamic household responses (consumption, spousal labor supply).

difficulty.justification: Requires specialized knowledge of insurance selection/moral-hazard and dynamic household responses; sign depends on institutional details (public UI, underwriting) and is confounded by private beliefs, measurement error, and selection, while panel subjective probabilities help but do not fully identify the causal sign without strong assumptions or exogenous variation.

context: This triplet refers to a model-based counterfactual for northern Bangladesh: using parameters estimated from a village-level RCT (the one-time 600 Taka conditional migration subsidy offered in Aug–Sep 2008) among eligible poor rural households, the authors simulate a permanent conditional subsidy policy in their structural model and report welfare impacts measured as consumption-equivalent gains, using survey rounds (2008 baseline, 2008–09, and 2011 follow-up) to estimate the model.

difficulty.justification: Moving from a one‑time RCT to a structural, permanent policy requires substantial migration/labor-market theory and strong identifying assumptions; results are context dependent (local labor markets, networks), multiple dynamic and general‑equilibrium channels make opposite outcomes plausible without careful modeling, and the experimental data provide limited long‑run evidence.

context: This paper studies coastal U.S. housing markets, combining a theoretical heterogeneous-beliefs housing model with empirical evidence from a door-to-door household survey (n=187) conducted in Rhode Island in Feb–July 2017 and property-level LiDAR/STORMTOOLS inundation estimates. Units are households/homeowners and individual properties; analysis contrasts coastal (within 400 feet) and non-coastal residents, links belief heterogeneity about 10-year flood risk to sorting, willingness-to-pay, and price dynamics, and calibrates structural simulations using sea-level-rise projections and US flood-insurance (NFIP/FEMA) institutions.

difficulty.justification: Intuition supports a positive effect, but credible identification requires intermediate housing-market and coastal-policy knowledge; the sign depends on local institutions, mobility constraints, heterogeneous beliefs, sorting, and reverse causality, and the small observational survey limits definitive evidence.

triplet_key: 21562|casino payments (per-capita tribal cash transfers)|emotional disorder symptoms
context: This empirical study uses the Great Smoky Mountains Study (annual waves from 1993) of 1,420 children in 11 counties in western North Carolina (oversampling Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians) to estimate effects of exogenous per‑capita casino transfers (~$4,000/year, disbursed mid‑1990s to enrolled adult tribal members) on adolescent personality, emotional, and behavioral outcomes. It compares younger American Indian cohorts exposed to payments before age 16 with older cohorts and non‑Indian families, exploiting panel data on children, parents, household characteristics, and census‑tract location.

difficulty.justification: The quasi-experimental cohort design gives credible leverage, but understanding the sign requires intermediate policy/microeconomics knowledge because multiple channels (household income, parental behavior, local labor/conditions) and possible concurrent community changes can reverse or attenuate effects; with rich panel data inference is feasible but not trivial.

context: Analyzes summer 1999 (June–Sept) hourly wholesale electricity markets in three restructured U.S. regions—California, New England, and PJM—using firm-hour observations of generation, retail obligations, and prices. Context: early post-restructuring regime with retail rate freezes and varied vertical arrangements (divestiture vs long-term contracts vs retained generation), ISOs operating spot markets, and price caps; authors reconstruct firm cost functions and simulate Cournot and competitive equilibria to estimate impact of vertical commitments on wholesale prices.

difficulty.justification: Requires specialized IO and electricity‑market knowledge; the sign depends critically on institutional details (rate freezes, price caps, contracts) and is theoretically ambiguous (vertical integration can both mitigate or enable market power), so identification needs complex structural modeling and rich data despite the authors' firm‑hour observations.

triplet_key: 11516|strengthening of intellectual property rights|affiliate r&d expenditures
context: This paper examines whether strengthening patent-based intellectual property rights increases international technology transfer by analyzing 16 country patent-reform episodes during 1982–1999. It uses affiliate-level BEA data on U.S. multinational parent-affiliate pairs (royalty payments, affiliate R&D) linked to NBER/WIPO patent records to compare pre- and post-reform outcomes. The institutional setting comprises substantive patent-law reforms (scope, term, enforcement, administration); specifications control for taxes, trade openness, country-specific trends, and parent/affiliate fixed effects.

difficulty.justification: Understanding this link requires intermediate knowledge of patent policy and multinational R&D incentives; effects vary by country, industry, and initial IPR strength, and are vulnerable to selection, anticipation, and omitted‑variable confounding. The described panel, fixed‑effects, and patent‑affiliate linkage make inference plausible but not straightforward given multiple mediating channels and potential endogeneity.

context: The paper examines how heterogeneity in U.S. state tax rates affected the spatial allocation of workers and firms using annual state-level data from 1980–2010. It analyzes workers, establishments, trade flows and state governments across U.S. states, focusing on personal income, corporate (sales‑apportioned) and general sales taxes, federal transfers, and public‑good provision within a spatial general‑equilibrium framework. The authors exploit over 350 actual state tax changes and observed worker/firm relocations to estimate mobility elasticities and to simulate counterfactual, harmonized tax distributions.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate public‑economics and spatial GE knowledge; the sign depends on spending type and financing and is confounded by endogenous location choices and tax–spend tradeoffs, and while many tax changes provide useful variation, identification remains challenging.

context: This paper studies U.S. business bankruptcy filings from 1992–2005 (tracked up to five years after filing) across bankruptcy court divisions, using 129,000 establishments (28,000 firms) in the Census LBD. It compares Chapter 11 reorganizations to cases converted to Chapter 7 liquidation, exploiting random assignment of judges within divisions. The unit of analysis is real‑estate plant locations (occupancy and employment); the institutional setting is the U.S. federal bankruptcy code and local markets, with heterogeneity by local market thickness and county small‑business lending (CRA) access.

difficulty.justification: Knowledge of bankruptcy law and local real‑estate/credit markets is needed but not specialized; random judge assignment and rich LBD data give reasonably strong identification, yet heterogeneity across local markets, firm/establishment selection and multiple mediating channels mean the sign is context-dependent and not trivial to pin down.

triplet_key: 22001|announcement of anti-corruption reforms|nonsoe returns for high tfp, q, and efd firms
outcome: NonSOE returns for high TFP, Q, and EFD firms
context: The paper analyzes stock-market reactions to China’s December 4, 2012 “Eight‑Point Policy” anti‑corruption announcement. Using 2012–2013 event windows (3- and 5-day) for 2,260 firms listed on the Shanghai and Shenzhen exchanges and 81 mainland H‑shares in Hong Kong, the authors relate abnormal returns to firm-level 2011 characteristics (SOE status, entertainment & travel costs) and province-level Marketization indices to infer how curbing corruption differentially affects firms across institutional environments.

difficulty.justification: Determining that anti-corruption news systematically raises non-SOE returns for high-TFP/Q/EFD firms requires substantive knowledge of Chinese institutional politics and firm rent-seeking channels, is highly context- and institution-dependent, faces plausible alternative explanations and heterogeneous channels, and rests on event-study evidence that can be noisy and confounded.

context: Using their estimated structural demand-and-supply model for the U.S. Medicare Part D stand-alone PDP market (34 geographic markets), the authors simulate counterfactual flat voucher subsidies in 2010 with the MA–PD outside option held fixed. They compute equilibrium outcomes and total welfare for vouchers ranging $0–$1,500 (in $100 increments), reporting results such as total surplus peaking at an $800 voucher and remaining positive until $1,300.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether a flat voucher raises total welfare here requires substantial knowledge of Medicare Part D market structure and structural estimation assumptions; the sign depends on institutional details (outside options, selection, plan responses) and is confounded by insurer pricing and selection effects, while equilibrium feedbacks make identification complex though the authors' structural simulations provide informative but assumption-sensitive evidence.

context: Analyzes the U.S. family medicine residency market (approximately 430 programs and ~3,000 residents per year) over 2003–2010 (with 2011–12 out-of-sample validation). Units are individual residents and residency programs participating in the centralized NRMP clearinghouse (pairwise-stable, many-to-one matching) where salaries are pre-set. The paper studies antitrust concerns about low resident wages and policy interventions to increase rural training, leveraging program and resident characteristics, Medicare-based reimbursement variation, and final-match data to estimate preferences and run counterfactual simulations.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether centralized matching affects resident salaries requires specialized matching-theory and medical labor-market knowledge, and the sign depends on institutional details (norms, reimbursement rules). Multiple plausible channels (sorting, monopsony/bargaining, program budget constraints and GE feedbacks) make the sign ambiguous despite decent data and structural tools, so identification is challenging but tractable with strong assumptions.

context: The paper studies state-level angel investor tax credit programs across 31 U.S. states from 1988–2018, exploiting staggered introductions and terminations. It analyzes state-year outcomes (firm entry, jobs) and deal-, firm-, and investor-level data (Crunchbase, Venture databases, Form D, AngelList) as well as beneficiary vs failed-applicant firms and a survey of angels. The institutional setting is state personal income tax credits for accredited investors (certification required), with variation in credit rates, eligibility restrictions, and program budgets, intended to spur high-tech entrepreneurial entry and job creation.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate knowledge of venture finance and tax-policy design; the sign is context-sensitive (program design, market conditions) and subject to plausible opposing mechanisms (crowding out vs. ecosystem spillovers) and selection/confounding, although the described multi-level data and staggered variation make a credible empirical identification feasible with careful design.

context: This estimate assesses the long-run impact of the county-level Head Start rollout in the United States (1965–1980) on cohorts born 1950–1980, using linked 2000 Census and 2001–2018 ACS data merged to the SSA Numident. The unit is individuals observed at ages 25–54; identification exploits county Head Start launch dates combined with school-entry age eligibility (ages 3–5 vs 6+), with county fixed effects and state-by-birth-year trends. The incarceration outcome here is measured as current incarceration at the time of the Census/ACS observation (point-in-time), not ever-arrested or ever-convicted.

difficulty.justification: Assessing a causal null for Head Start → current incarceration requires specialist knowledge of early-childhood program evaluation, cohort rollout instruments, and criminal justice measurement; threats include rollout endogeneity, migration/composition and point-in-time incarceration measurement, policy heterogeneity, and multiple mediating channels, so credible identification is challenging but feasible with careful design.

context: Using a 20% sample of U.S. Medicare Part B claims from 2000–2009 for ED visits, the paper studies emergency-department physicians’ chest CT ordering for pulmonary embolism (units: ED visits and ≈6,600 physicians, linked to hospital and HRR characteristics). For this triplet, the authors use their estimated distribution of physician testing thresholds from a structural selection model and, under calibrated assumptions about test costs, treatment benefits, and false positives, simulate a counterfactual world with no overtesting (physician thresholds set to the social optimum) to compute changes in total social benefits from chest CTs.;

difficulty.justification: Assessing whether eliminating overtesting raises net social benefits requires specialized health-economics and structural-identification knowledge (threshold models, test accuracy, downstream treatment effects). The sign is context-dependent and plausibly reversed under different prevalence, accuracy, or cost assumptions, the analysis must handle selection/composition and equilibrium effects, and calibration of benefits/costs leaves substantive uncertainty.

triplet_key: 18890|idiosyncratic granular bank-supply shocks|aggregate loan and investment fluctuations
context: This paper analyzes matched bank–firm loan and firm balance-sheet data for listed Japanese nonfinancial firms and their lenders over 1990–2010. Using annual loan records across hundreds of banks and 870–1,633 listed firms, the authors exploit Japan’s concentrated banking sector and episodes of bank distress, mergers, and capital injections to separate bank-specific supply shocks from firm borrowing shocks and estimate impacts on firm-level capital investment and aggregate investment.

difficulty.justification: Correctly signing granular bank-supply → aggregate investment requires specialized knowledge of bank-firm lending channels and identification strategies and is sensitive to Japan’s institutional setting (bank concentration, alternative finance). While rich matched data and quasi-experiments help, substantial confounding (simultaneous demand shocks, selection, GE feedbacks and reallocation) make the causal sign nontrivial to pin down without careful design.

treatment: Relying only on carbon tax (no subsidies)
context: This paper combines a micro-founded endogenous-growth model with firm-level estimation using U.S. energy-sector microdata from 1975–2004. It analyzes 1,576 innovative firms (6,228 firm-period observations) using Census LBD and Economic Census records, NSF R&D survey data, and USPTO patents to study firm R&D, patents, production, employment, entry/exit, and emissions. The structural model contrasts clean versus fossil-fuel (dirty) technologies, is calibrated to carbon-cycle dynamics, and evaluates policy counterfactuals (carbon taxes and clean-research subsidies) for production, innovation direction, and welfare.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether a carbon tax alone reduces consumption requires specialized growth-and-innovation theory and careful identification because effects hinge on dynamic general-equilibrium feedbacks, induced-innovation (directional R&D), revenue recycling, and transition/selection effects; although the paper has rich firm-level estimation and calibration, counterfactual welfare signs remain sensitive to key modeling assumptions and institutional details.

context: This paper studies the causal effect of reductions in collectively-bargained standard weekly hours in (West) Germany, primarily 1984–1989 with additional analysis for 1990–1994, exploiting monthly industry‑level panel data on 201 manufacturing industries and individual-level German Socio‑Economic Panel data on full‑time workers. The institutional setting is industry-level union bargaining (notably IG Metall) that implemented stepwise cuts in standard hours, overtime premia, and greater hours flexibility; outcomes examined include employment, hours per worker, worker‑hours, and wage bills.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate labour-economics and institutional knowledge (collective bargaining effects). Multiple mediating channels (hours per worker, overtime premia, wage bills, substitution/composition and demand responses) and plausible opposing mechanisms make the sign context-dependent though industry‑panel and microdata provide reasonably good but not definitive identification.

context: Using an annual panel of 157 countries from roughly 1970–2001 (2,228 country-year observations), this paper studies how abrupt stops in capital inflows (“sudden stops”) and large current-account reversals affect national real GDP growth. Units are countries observed annually across world regions. Key institutional features include measures of financial openness (Quinn index), trade openness (exports+imports/GDP), reserve levels, external debt, short-term debt shares, and de-facto exchange-rate regimes; the analysis evaluates how capital-account restrictions and exchange-rate flexibility mediate crisis incidence and growth impacts.

difficulty.justification: Requires specialized understanding of capital-flow dynamics and policy interactions; the sign is highly context-dependent with strong endogeneity/selection and multiple mediating channels, and the available observational panel evidence is unlikely to rule out plausible opposite explanations.

context: This empirical study examines 115 highly leveraged Austrian ski hotels that underwent debt restructurings between 1998 and 2005 (with a control sample of 2,095 hotels). The Austrian Hotel- and Tourism Bank (AHTB) mediated restructurings under eligibility rules for “structurally important” hotels; data include pre- and post-restructuring accounting and local weather. The authors use two-year Unexpected Snow (relative to a ten-year average) to distinguish liquidity versus strategic distress and to instrument reductions in leverage, estimating effects on ROA, margins, costs, and sales.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a positive effect of reduced leverage on margins requires moderate finance/restructuring knowledge and is sensitive to institutional and industry context (AHTB rules, ski-hotel seasonality). Although the authors use a plausibly exogenous weather instrument and pre/post controls, multiple competing channels (interest savings, creditor monitoring, selection into restructuring, liquidity vs strategic distress) make the sign moderately ambiguous and empirically challenging to pin down definitively.

context: This paper studies Norway's Disability Insurance (DI) system using linked administrative registers (1967–2010) and appeals data (1989–2005) for parent–child pairs. It focuses on parents (≤55) who appealed an initial DI denial and their adult children (18+), exploiting randomized assignment of Administrative Law Judges in Oslo to the written appeal process. The institutional setting features centralized, non-specialized judges whose leniency variation exogenously shifts parental DI awards, enabling causal estimates of parental DI receipt on children’s DI application, receipt, labor, and education outcomes.

difficulty.justification: Randomized judge assignment provides strong exogenous variation so empirical identification is feasible with the administrative data, but multiple mediating channels (income effect, care substitution, transfers, child DI application) and Norway-specific institutions make the sign moderately context-dependent and require intermediate policy knowledge to interpret.

triplet_key: 21498|decline in trade costs since the 1970s|gdp volatility
treatment: Decline in trade costs since the 1970s
context: This paper studies how declining trade costs since the early 1970s through 2007 affected GDP volatility across a multi-country sample (24 core countries plus a rest-of-world aggregate). The authors combine sector-level data (24 sectors), bilateral trade flows, and country-year observations with a calibrated multi-sector stochastic trade model that features country- and sector-specific productivity shocks and ex-ante labor allocation. Using the model and data, they back out trade costs and shocks and run counterfactuals to quantify trade’s causal impact on aggregate volatility.

difficulty.justification: Understanding this link requires advanced trade and macro knowledge plus multi-sector modeling; the sign depends heavily on shock correlations, sectoral composition, and general-equilibrium reallocation. Calibration choices and measurement of trade costs mean opposite signs remain plausible without strong identifying assumptions.

triplet_key: 25581|larger payment amount|concerns about fairness to recipients
context: This paper analyzes responses to a randomized online survey and incentivized donation experiment fielded in the United States in Nov–Dec 2017 with 2,666 adult respondents matched to US demographics. Respondents evaluated hypothetical paid-kidney-donor procurement and allocation systems (varying payer: public agency vs. recipient; payment type: cash vs. noncash; amount: $30,000 vs. $100,000) across five assumed transplant-supply scenarios, set against the US kidney shortage and the 1984 National Organ Transplant Act prohibiting donor compensation.

difficulty.justification: Fairness judgments about larger payments hinge on framing, payer, and institutional context (so intermediate domain knowledge and high context dependence); multiple plausible mechanisms could push perceptions either way, making the sign ambiguous and moderately complex to reason through, although the randomized survey gives reasonably strong internal evidence for the sample.

context: This randomized field experiment was conducted in rural Malawi (Dedza, Mchinji, Dowa, Kasungu) with smallholder farmers applying for in-kind paprika input loans through MRFC and Cheetah Paprika. The intervention fingerprinted applicants to strengthen identification and lenders’ dynamic incentives; loans were in-kind and repayment could be garnished from paprika sales. The paid-inputs outcome is measured in the August 2008 follow-up among loan recipients (N=520) and shows heterogeneous effects concentrated among borrowers with low predicted repayment (lowest quintile).

difficulty.justification: Understanding the sign needs intermediate knowledge of credit incentives, input markets, and local enforcement; effects are highly context-dependent (institutions, crop markets, repayment enforcement) and multiple channels (liquidity, deterrence, selection, reputational dynamics) can produce opposite signs. The RCT provides credible identification for the sample but heterogeneous impacts and external validity limits make definitive sign attribution fairly difficult.

context: This triplet refers specifically to IMF‑sponsored attempts to stabilize high inflation within the paper’s sample of 81 inflation‑stabilization episodes across 21 emerging‑market countries (late 1970s–early 1990s). The unit of observation is monthly country‑level dividend‑inclusive real stock‑price indices (IFC/IFS), and the stock‑market response is measured as the cumulative real‑dollar return over the two‑month implementation window [0,+1]; IMFHIGH equals one for IMF‑sponsored high‑inflation (12‑month inflation >40% for the prior 24 months) stabilization attempts.

difficulty.justification: Inferring a positive IMF→stock effect requires substantial macro‑financial knowledge because IMF involvement is endogenous to crisis severity and interacts with credibility, fiscal/monetary tightening, exchange rates and capital flows; the sign can plausibly go either way across settings and disentangling channels requires strong identification beyond the panel of 81 episodes.

context: This paper reports a two-year (2009–2010) field experiment in Gujarat, India (Ahmedabad and Surat regions) on third‑party environmental audits of industrial Schedule II plants. The unit of observation is audit‑eligible industrial plants (N=473) and their auditors; outcomes include auditors' pollution reports, independent backchecks, and an endline pollution survey (2011). The intervention restructured the audit market—random assignment of auditors, centralized fixed pay, random backchecks (20%), and bonus pay in year two—within the Gujarat Pollution Control Board regulatory framework.

difficulty.justification: The randomized market‑level design gives credible identification within the study (hence evidence sufficiency is reasonably good), but multiple opposing channels (incentives, collusion, deterrence), strong dependence on local enforcement and firm–auditor relationships, and potential measurement/selection subtleties make the sign nontrivial to predict without empirical results.

context: The paper studies how ECB liquidity affected corporate deposit and loan spreads in the euro area during Jan 2, 2006–June 30, 2010, with emphasis on the 2007–2010 financial crisis and the ECB’s Oct 8, 2008 shift to fixed-rate full-allotment. It uses bank–firm weekly deposit transactions from a European trading platform and loan-level DealScan facilities, exploiting cross-sectional bank risk (CDS) and time variation in aggregate ECB liquidity, reserve maintenance timing, and firms’ cash holdings across European banks and corporate borrowers.

difficulty.justification: Assessing whether ECB liquidity reduces loan spreads for low‑risk banks requires intermediate knowledge of central‑bank operations and bank funding channels and is sensitive to crisis timing and institutional details; multiple mediating channels and plausible confounders make the sign nontrivial, though the paper's high‑frequency bank–firm data and timing-based identification materially improve identification.

triplet_key: 16977|applying hcc risk-adjustment to pre-period|differential payments per enrollee
context: This paper studies how introducing Medicare Advantage risk adjustment altered selection into private plans and government payments in the U.S. Medicare program. It uses individual-level MCBS claims and survey data for roughly 55,000 beneficiaries (1994–2006, with county-level checks through 2008) to analyze transitions between traditional fee-for-service and Medicare Advantage around the phased HCC risk-adjustment reform (2004–2007), concurrent benchmark and budget-neutrality changes, and outcomes including risk scores, Medicare expenditures, and enrollee satisfaction.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate knowledge of Medicare payment rules and strategic coding/selection incentives; the sign depends on institutional details and concurrent reforms, with plausible alternative mechanisms (benchmark changes, coding intensity, selection) and moderate empirical identification challenges despite rich data.

context: This paper reports a large-scale randomized controlled trial in Tanzania (2013–2014) across a nationally representative sample of 350 public primary schools in 10 districts, covering over 120,000 students in grades 1–3. It evaluates causal effects of two interventions — unconditional capitation grants (TZS 10,000 per student) and teacher performance pay tied to externally administered tests — implemented by Twaweza against a background of limited school resources, large class sizes, and prevalent teacher absence.

difficulty.justification: A large, nationally representative RCT gives strong identification so the causal sign can be estimated reliably in this setting; however, parental responses (crowding-out versus complementarity) and other mediating channels plus context-specific school finance norms introduce moderate complexity and require some domain knowledge.

triplet_key: 27444|exposure to earned income tax credit expansion|earnings conditional on working
treatment: Exposure to Earned Income Tax Credit expansion
context: This paper analyzes U.S. mothers using linked SSA administrative earnings (1978–2015) and CPS (1991–2016) data to estimate effects of the 1993 Earned Income Tax Credit expansion (effective 1994). It compares first‑birth cohorts exposed at birth (1993–1996) versus 3–6 years later (1988–1991), focusing on never‑married (high‑impact) and married (comparison) mothers to identify how temporary post‑birth work incentives affect employment, accumulated experience, and long‑run earnings.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate policy and labor-market knowledge to map EITC incentives onto mothers' experience accumulation; identification is sensitive to cohort-specific shocks, concurrent policy changes, selection/composition and dynamic channels, and while rich administrative data help, nontrivial threats to sign inference remain.

context: This paper analyzes an 18-month randomized early childhood intervention in central Colombia (started early 2010) targeting disadvantaged children aged 12–24 months at baseline drawn from beneficiaries of the Familias en Acción CCT program. The cluster RCT randomized 96 municipalities into psychosocial stimulation, micronutrient, combined, or control arms; home visits were delivered by local community “Madre Líderes.” Outcomes are child cognitive and socio-emotional development and parental material and time investments measured at baseline and immediately after the intervention (children ≈30–42 months).

difficulty.justification: A well-implemented cluster RCT substantially reduces confounding so identifying a positive effect on cognition is relatively straightforward; nonetheless, variation by context, delivery fidelity, and multiple mediating channels (parental time/material investments, direct stimulation) mean moderate domain knowledge and some remaining uncertainty about external validity and implementation-related biases.

context: This paper develops a model of trading under asymmetric information and empirically estimates the marginal social versus private value of financial expertise using two main settings: US venture capital and the US junk‑bond underwriting market. For venture capital it draws on published time‑series estimates of fund inflows, valuations, and venture success rates; for junk bonds it analyzes US dollar‑denominated high‑yield bond issues and defaults around 1987–1990, exploiting the Drexel Burnham collapse. Units are venture‑backed firms, venture funds, issuing firms and bond issues; key features include underwriting fees and capital‑flow variation.

difficulty.justification: The sign is theoretically ambiguous — expertise can raise allocative efficiency or instead enable rent extraction and worsen informational frictions — and its identification requires specialized asymmetric‑information theory plus quasi‑experimental strategies; institutional specifics and persistent selection/GE confounders make credible inference difficult.

context: This paper studies local mortgage advertising by subprime lenders in U.S. designated market areas (DMAs) during 2002–2007, linking lender–DMA–quarter advertising expenditures (newspapers, spot TV, outdoor) to loan-level originations in the LoanPerformance database. Units include individual mortgages and lender–market–quarter aggregates in the nonprime mortgage market prior to the 2007–08 crisis; the institutional background emphasizes ARM teaser/reset features, regulatory scrutiny of deceptive ads, and the rise of online classifieds. The analysis exploits within‑lender, within‑market variation and staggered Craigslist market entry.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether local advertising causally raises reset rates requires substantial market and product knowledge because advertising can both signal/induce higher‑priced products and attract different borrower mixes; confounding (targeted advertising, reverse causality, concurrent market shocks) and competing mechanisms (competition lowering prices versus predatory targeting raising them) are highly plausible. Proper identification therefore demands strong quasi‑experimental variation and fine-grained controls to rule out selection, composition and general equilibrium feedbacks, making the sign hard to pin down.

triplet_key: 30161|investors with government ties|gp interest in investor
context: This study examines preferences and matching in China’s venture capital and private equity (VCPE) market using administrative investment data (2015–2019), a large non-deceptive field experiment run with Zero2IPO in 2019, and a follow-up qualitative survey in 2021. Units are fund managers (General Partners, GPs) and capital providers (Limited Partners, LPs) observed as firms and senior respondents. The institutional setting is China, where central/provincial/local governments and SOEs play major LP roles; the paper studies how government ties affect GP–LP matching, firm preferences, and equilibrium allocation.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether government ties lower GP interest requires specialized knowledge of Chinese LP incentives and identification strategies because such ties can signal both benefits (stable capital, access) and costs (political constraints, red tape), and the net effect varies strongly by government level, fund type, and institutional context.

triplet_key: 21120|tax changes induced by close elections|star scientist migration
treatment: Tax changes induced by close elections
context: This paper studies the effect of state-level personal and corporate tax changes on migration of “star scientists” in the United States using the universe of U.S. patent applications from 1976–2010. The unit is individual inventors (top 5% by patents) aggregated into annual bilateral origin→destination state flows. Identification exploits within-state over-time variation in marginal income tax rates, corporate tax rates, R&D and investment tax credits, and apportionment rules, with robustness from close-election–driven policy shifts and timing/event-study tests.

difficulty.justification: Close-election driven tax variation and event-study tests provide credible identification, but understanding star-scientist migration requires moderate policy and agglomeration knowledge, is context-dependent (industry/academic hubs, time), has plausible alternative channels/confounders (concurrent policies, firm moves, network effects), and involves multiple mediating and general-equilibrium mechanisms.

triplet_key: 20256|mandatory board gender quota|gender earnings gap within boards
context: This paper uses linked Norwegian administrative data (population ages 16–64) from the late 1990s through 2014 to evaluate the causal effects of Norway’s 2003 board‑gender quota law (made compulsory by 2006, enforced by 2008) requiring roughly 40% representation of each gender on public limited companies’ (ASA) boards. Analyses examine firms, business groups and individual workers (board appointees, highly qualified cohorts, recent graduates), exploiting cross‑firm differences in pre‑2003 female board shares and cohort timing to study labor‑market and top‑echelon outcomes.

difficulty.justification: Understanding whether Norway’s quota reduced the gender earnings gap on boards requires intermediate policy and corporate‑governance knowledge; the effect is moderately context‑dependent and multiple plausible mechanisms (selection into board seats, composition, negotiation, and equilibrium signaling) could produce either sign, so empirical identification is feasible with rich administrative data but still nontrivial due to potential differential trends and composition/selection effects.

context: This analysis estimates the effect of the CARD Act fee regulations on total income (fees + interest + interchange) measured as a percentage of average daily balances for low-FICO accounts (origination FICO < 660). It uses monthly account-level data (Mar 2008–Dec 2011) from eight large U.S. banks and a difference-in-differences comparison of consumer (treated) versus small-business (control) cards, focusing on the Phase 3 (post-August 2010) implementation period.

difficulty.justification: Requires specialized knowledge of card economics and regulatory responses; multiple revenue-shifting channels, selection and differential-trend threats in a volatile 2008–2011 period make sign identification nontrivial, though account-level DiD provides moderately informative evidence.

context: This empirical study examines individuals who played the Massachusetts Megabucks lottery (prizes won during 1984–1988) using a 1995–96 survey linked to Social Security earnings for six years before and after winning. Units are individual lottery players (major annuitized winners and season‑ticket small‑prize winners). The institutional setting is the Massachusetts State Lottery where major prizes were randomly assigned and paid annually over 20 years; analyses adjust for ticket‑buying, season‑ticket selection, and differential nonresponse.

difficulty.justification: Random assignment of large annuitized prizes and linkage to Social Security earnings provide strong internal identification of income effects on labor supply; remaining difficulties are heterogeneity in baseline incomes and mediating choices (retirement, leisure, informal work) that limit external generalizability and create some variation in the sign across subgroups.

context: Using U.S. establishment-level data, the paper studies firms filing Chapter 11 between 1992 and 2005 (with five-year follow-up) across U.S. bankruptcy districts. The unit of analysis is bankrupt firm establishments and neighboring Census-block establishments matched from LexisNexis filings and the Census LBD. The institutional setting is the U.S. federal bankruptcy system—comparison of Chapter 11 reorganization versus Chapter 7 liquidation—with blind, within-division random assignment of cases to judges that creates exogenous variation in conversion propensity; outcomes are local employment and establishment entry/exit by sector.

difficulty.justification: Blind within-division judge assignment gives strong quasi-experimental variation (reducing endogeneity), but multiple competing channels (direct job loss at bankrupt establishments versus reallocation to nearby establishments, demand and price spillovers), potential SUTVA violations, and local institutional/context heterogeneity leave the sign moderately ambiguous and require careful identification and interpretation.

context: This paper studies the U.S. First-Time Homebuyer Credit during the Great Recession (2008–2010, focusing on the Feb 2009–Jul 2010 expansions), exploiting ZIP-code–level monthly housing transactions and de-identified individual tax records for ≈9,000 ZIPs (≈69% of the 2007 U.S. population). Units include individual homebuyers, tax filers, and ZIP-level housing markets and prices. The policy was a refundable $8,000 credit (first-time buyer and income eligibility rules) enacted to spur demand amid housing inventory overhang, coinciding with expanded FHA low-down-payment lending and high foreclosure activity.

difficulty.justification: Determining a positive effect is nontrivial because it requires specialized knowledge of housing-market mechanics, eligibility and timing rules, and interacting policies (e.g. FHA expansion) and because opposing mechanisms (absorption of distressed inventory vs. compositional/selection effects and concurrent foreclosure dynamics) make the sign context-dependent and confoundable; rich data help but do not eliminate these identification challenges.

context: This paper analyzes an 18-month randomized early childhood intervention in central Colombia (started early 2010) targeting disadvantaged children aged 12–24 months at baseline drawn from beneficiaries of the Familias en Acción CCT program. The cluster RCT randomized 96 municipalities into psychosocial stimulation, micronutrient, combined, or control arms; home visits were delivered by local community “Madre Líderes.” Outcomes are child cognitive and socio-emotional development and parental material and time investments measured at baseline and immediately after the intervention (children ≈30–42 months).

difficulty.justification: A cluster RCT with measured baseline/endline gives strong identification making the sign relatively easy to establish; however, effects can vary by local institutions (CCT exposure, norms) and operate through multiple channels (knowledge, preferences, time/material substitution), producing moderate contextual and causal complexity.

context: This paper studies welfare effects of Austrian disability insurance reforms using linked administrative social-security data and the universe of DI applications. It exploits two quasi-experiments: the 2003 pension reform that changed DI benefit formulas (variation 2004–2017) and the 2012–2017 stepwise increase in the relaxed screening age (RSA) from 57 to 60. The unit of analysis is (predominantly male) private-sector workers aged roughly 30–62 (focus on 54–61), observed at quarterly/annual frequency; institutional setting: payroll‑funded DI with RSA-based eligibility rules.

difficulty.justification: Basic policy and incentive knowledge suffices to expect a positive effect, and the paper's administrative data plus two quasi‑experiments make sign identification relatively feasible. However, institutional details (eligibility rules, screening practices), selection/composition and other channels (employer behavior, macro trends) mean moderate care is required to rule out alternative explanations.

context: The paper uses annual country-year data on about 150 countries (main analyses 1960–2000; extended 1850–2000 for expectation analysis) to study effects of regime changes on log per-capita income and investment. It exploits episodes of democratization, autocracy, and economic liberalization (Polity2; Sachs–Warner liberalization), variation in forms of government and electoral rules (presidential vs parliamentary; majoritarian vs proportional), and controls for wars and former Soviet-bloc transitions; expected regime-change hazards (democratic capital) are modeled.

difficulty.justification: The sign is theoretically plausible but depends on institutional details (electoral rules, veto players), historical/international context, and selection of regimes; multiple channels and endogeneity (selection, reverse causality, measurement issues) make identification nontrivial despite rich panel data and controls.

triplet_key: 26942|access to food stamps in early childhood|longevity
treatment: Access to Food Stamps in early childhood
context: This study exploits the county-level rollout of the U.S. Food Stamps program (1961–1975) to estimate effects of access from conception through age five for individuals born 1950–1980. Outcomes are measured for adults observed in linked 2000 Census and 2001–2013 ACS data merged to the SSA NUMIDENT; the longevity outcome is measured as survival to 2012 using the full NUMIDENT (not limited to the Census/ACS subsample). Analysis is at the individual/cohort-by-birth-county level across U.S. counties.

difficulty.justification: Understanding and credibly estimating long-run effects of early-life Food Stamp access requires substantial program and life-course health knowledge; staggered county rollout helps identification but threats remain (endogenous adoption, migration, co-occurring policies, measurement error) and the pathways from early nutrition to adult mortality are complex and heterogeneous.

context: Using U.S. financial-market and survey data around scheduled FOMC meetings (primarily 1995–2014, with many Treasury/TIPS analyses from 2000–2014), the paper studies high-frequency (30-minute) surprises in federal-funds/eurodollar futures and the simultaneous responses of nominal and real yields, break-even inflation, and Blue Chip/Greenbook forecasted output growth. Units include tick-by-tick futures, daily zero-coupon yields and forwards, and monthly survey forecasts. The institutional setting is U.S. monetary policy announcements—including forward guidance and ZLB-era episodes—exploiting abrupt FOMC surprises to identify monetary news.
