It exploits episodes of democratization, autocracy, and economic liberalization (Polity2; Sachs–Warner liberalization), variation in forms of government and electoral rules (presidential vs parliamentary; majoritarian vs proportional), and controls for wars and former Soviet-bloc transitions; expected regime-change hazards (democratic capital) are modeled.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a positive effect of economic liberalization on growth requires more than common-sense—there are strong endogeneity concerns (reverse causality, selection into reform), measurement and policy-bundle issues, and large heterogeneity across countries/time, so opposite or null signs are plausible despite a long panel and controls.

triplet_key: 30257|more programs joining the platform|on-time graduation rate
context: This paper studies Chile’s centralized higher-education assignment system using administrative data on PSU test-takers and ranked applications across 2010–2012 cohorts. It exploits the 2012 expansion of the on‑platform set (G8 universities joining the long-running G25 platform) and the 2011 BVP score‑based scholarship to estimate effects of on- vs off‑platform options on enrollment, waitlists, dropout and seven‑year graduation for individual applicants. The institutional setting features a deferred‑acceptance centralized match, widespread off‑platform offers, and decentralized aftermarket waitlists.

difficulty.justification: Understanding this effect requires specialized knowledge of centralized deferred‑acceptance matching, aftermarket offers and waitlists; the institutional details (who joins, capacity, scholarship eligibility) matter for the sign. There are multiple countervailing channels (selection/composition, match quality, capacity/peer effects and GE feedbacks) so credible identification is feasible with the administrative quasi‑experiment but not trivial.

context: This paper uses 14,159 U.S. commercial property transactions from January 1, 1992 to March 30, 1999 across 11 states and the District of Columbia to study how property liquidation value—proxied by property-specific zoning flexibility—affects non-recourse commercial loan contracts. The unit of analysis is individual property transactions/loans in local real estate markets. Authors link COMPS.com transaction data to census-tract controls, the Wharton Land Use Control Survey, crime and broker-market indicators, and estimate fixed-effect regressions of loan outcomes (rates, LTV, maturity, duration, number of lenders).

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate real-estate and contract theory knowledge; the sign is context-dependent and confounded by borrower/property selection, market conditions and omitted variables, and establishing causality needs careful identification and robust controls.

triplet_key: 23810|in utero medicaid exposure|second generation average birthweight
context: This paper studies U.S. cohorts born 1975–1988 (first generation) and their children born 1990–2017 (second generation), exploiting 1980s state-level expansions in prenatal Medicaid eligibility. Using restricted Vital Statistics natality files linked to mothers’ state and year of birth and simulated eligibility from CPS and state Medicaid rules collected back to 1975, the authors compare individuals across U.S. states and cohorts (by state/year of birth) to estimate how in utero access to Medicaid affected infant health across generations.

difficulty.justification: Plausible positive effect but identification requires intermediate-to-specialized knowledge of Medicaid rules, cohort/state variation, and intergenerational channels; results are sensitive to migration, concurrent state policies, measurement of simulated eligibility, and multiple mediating/selection channels, so credible inference is challenging though feasible with rich data and careful design.

context: This empirical study analyzes Medicare inpatient admissions for patients age 66+ in the United States, focusing on four conditions (AMI, heart failure, pneumonia, hip/knee replacement). Main analyses use 2006–2008 (quality measurement) and 2008 baseline patient counts with growth 2008–2010, and a longer 1996–2008 panel for survival decomposition. Hospital-level performance measures (risk‑adjusted 30‑day survival/readmission, process adherence, HCAHPS) from CMS are linked to hospital market shares within HRRs to assess how patient choice and market allocation respond to quality in a largely insured Medicare setting.

difficulty.justification: Assessing whether patient satisfaction causally affects hospital market share requires intermediate health‑economics knowledge; the sign varies by local referral patterns, insurer networks, and patient sorting. Strong concerns about reverse causality, omitted variables (physician referrals, capacity, reputation), and multiple mediating channels make identification difficult despite rich Medicare data.

triplet_key: 18535|food stamp program exposure in early childhood|metabolic syndrome
treatment: Food Stamp Program exposure in early childhood
context: This paper studies long-run effects of childhood exposure to the U.S. Food Stamp Program, exploiting county-by-county rollout from 1961–1975. Using individuals born 1956–1981 observed in the PSID, it links county-of-birth month/year to adult health and economic outcomes measured through 2009, focusing on disadvantaged families (parent < high school). The institutional setting is the federal Food Stamp/SNAP expansion, with treatment defined as the share of months exposed between conception and age five.

difficulty.justification: Intermediate knowledge of developmental origins and program evaluation is needed; the sign depends on context (baseline nutrition, food environments, program generosity) and is confounded by selection, migration, measurement error and competing mechanisms (improved prenatal nutrition versus excess early calories), so a strong quasi-experimental design and robustness checks are required to credibly pin down the negative effect.

context: This paper constructs a continuous national series of white-to-Black per-capita wealth ratios in the United States from 1860 to 2020, using historical complete-count censuses, southern state tax records, Monroe Work’s Negro Year Book, and the Survey of Consumer Finances. The unit of analysis is racial groups’ per-capita (and household-level) wealth nationally. The analysis situates wealth dynamics in institutional contexts—slavery, Emancipation, Reconstruction, Jim Crow, New Deal, the Civil Rights era, and post-1980 asset-price booms—and examines how initial conditions, income growth, savings behavior, and capital gains shaped long-run convergence and divergence.

difficulty.justification: Determining that large reparations would reduce the white–Black wealth gap requires specialized knowledge of wealth accumulation, capital gains, and intergenerational transmission; the sign depends sensitively on timing, size, targeting, general-equilibrium asset-price and fiscal responses, and behavioral channels, and while rich historical data help, credible causal identification remains challenging.

context: This paper studies Pennsylvania’s state-run retail monopoly for wine and spirits (the Pennsylvania Liquor Control Board) using store-level daily and weekly sales, wholesale costs, and geocoded store locations for 2005, linked to 2000 Census tract demographics. Units of observation include PLCB stores, Census tracts (consumers over 21), and store-week/day sales. The PLCB sets uniform retail prices via markup rules and controls entry and store locations; the authors estimate a structural spatial demand model and simulate profit- and welfare-maximizing store networks and counterfactual private-entry equilibria.

difficulty.justification: Identifying whether unregulated entry reduces total welfare here requires specialized spatial-competition theory and strong structural assumptions because outcomes depend critically on institutional details (uniform pricing, markup rules, wholesale costs, cross-border effects) and multiple interacting channels; alternative signs are equally plausible without a well-specified model and rich data, making the inference quite difficult.

context: This paper studies the causal effect of the ACA Medicaid eligibility expansions (primarily 2014, staggered 2014–2017) on mortality in the United States by linking 2008–2013 American Community Survey respondents (panel tracked 2008–2017) to administrative Medicaid enrollment (CMS) and death records (Census Numident and MDAC). The unit is near-elderly low‑socioeconomic adults—primarily individuals ages 55–64 in 2014 who were citizens with family income ≤138% FPL or less than high‑school education—comparing residents of expansion versus non‑expansion states under the optional ACA expansion.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate policy and health-economics knowledge; state-level heterogeneity, timing of expansions, selection into enrollment, and lagged/rare nature of mortality make opposite or attenuated effects plausible, though linked administrative enrollment/death data and staggered expansion quasi-experiments provide reasonably strong but not definitive identification.

context: This paper studies Los Angeles Unified School District’s Zones of Choice (ZOC) reform—implemented starting around 2012–2013—using administrative data from roughly 2008–2019. It evaluates effects on high-school students (chiefly 11th graders and graduating cohorts) and market-level school outcomes across ZOC versus non-ZOC neighborhoods in Los Angeles. ZOC combined catchment areas, expanded within-district choice with centralized rank-ordered (immediate-acceptance) assignment and lotteries for oversubscription, thereby changing competitive incentives among attendance-zone schools.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a positive effect of ZOC on value‑added requires specialized knowledge of assignment rules, sorting and peer mechanisms and is sensitive to LAUSD institutional details; alternative channels (selection, spillovers, concurrent policies) and equilibrium school responses make the sign plausible but not obvious without rigorous identification.

triplet_key: 10223|state street decision|elasticity of innovation with respect to size
outcome: elasticity of innovation with respect to size
context: This paper analyzes U.S. financial-sector innovations from 1990–2002 using firm-year observations (Compustat-listed firms) identified via Wall Street Journal articles, U.S. patents, and stock returns (CRSP). It studies which firms (banks, brokers, IT vendors, etc.) innovated and how characteristics (size, profitability, academic ties, leverage, local spillovers) predict innovation, and examines how the late-1990s State Street court decision—which changed perceived patentability of financial methods—affected appropriability and the firm-size elasticity of innovation.

difficulty.justification: Assessing whether the State Street decision raised the size-elasticity of financial-sector innovation requires specialized knowledge of patent law, appropriability, and firm-level innovation incentives, and hinges on institutional specifics (patentability, enforcement, contemporaneous market/tech shocks). Multiple plausible countervailing mechanisms (larger firms better able to patent or litigate, shifts to secrecy, selection of innovators) and measurement/identification challenges make the sign hard to pin down without careful empirical strategies.

context: This paper studies Iraq during 2004–2008 using district-level panel data (104 districts) on attacks against Coalition and Iraqi security forces, geo-located reconstruction spending—particularly small-scale CERP projects—and local characteristics (sectarian status, socio-economic indicators, and oil infrastructure). It evaluates how U.S. reconstruction and counterinsurgency policies, including the 2007 troop surge and the Commander’s Emergency Response Program, affected insurgent violence by exploiting variation in project timing, size, and district-level spending over time.

difficulty.justification: Identifying whether small CERP-style projects causally reduce insurgent attacks requires specialized counterinsurgency and programmatic knowledge, and the sign is highly context-dependent and confounded (targeting, reverse causality, surge timing, local politics). Multiple mediating channels (security provision, grievance, provocation, displacement) and likely incomplete district-level controls make credible identification difficult without strong quasi-experimental strategies.

context: This paper studies eurozone bank-level interventions during the Global Financial Crisis and subsequent sovereign-debt shock, using hand-collected State Aid Register data on interventions from 2007–2009 and balance-sheet and loan-level outcomes through 2012. The unit of analysis is ultimate-parent banks (N=830) and bank–firm loan relationships, across eurozone countries differing in fiscal capacity and access to ECB facilities (including LTRO). The institutional setting includes national government recapitalizations, guarantees, liquidity measures, and EU Commission approvals, with fiscal constraints proxied by revenue/GDP, debt/GDP and maturing debt.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate banking and policy knowledge; the negative sign is plausible but depends strongly on institutional context (fiscal capacity, ECB access) and is confounded by selection, simultaneous shocks, and multiple mediating channels—rich bank- and loan-level data help but identification remains challenging.

context: Empirical study of U.S. cities from 1900–1937 (348 cities) estimating how the 1921 and 1924 national immigration quota acts changed city-level mortality. The authors construct city-level “missing immigrants” (quota exposure) from pre‑WWI migration trends and 1910 settlement patterns, and use annual cause-of-death counts (infectious, non-infectious, external) to assess spillovers of reduced immigration on urban public health, controlling for age structure, pandemic years, city and year fixed effects, and state-by-year shocks.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate historical migration and public‑health knowledge; the sign depends on city institutions and timing (e.g., pandemics) and is subject to multiple mediating channels and confounders, and although the authors' exposure construction and fixed effects help identification, heterogeneous effects and residual confounding make establishing the sign nontrivial.

context: This paper studies the impacts of large dam construction in India over roughly 1970–1999 (with crop analyses 1971–1987 and disease and rainfall panels through the 1990s). The analysis uses Indian districts as units, GIS-derived river and dam locations to classify upstream/catchment and downstream/command areas, and district-level panels on agricultural production, irrigation, rural consumption, poverty and wages. Context includes state-led dam planning/financing, the Land Acquisition Act (1894), documented displacement, and environmental impacts of reservoirs.

difficulty.justification: Dam impacts involve specialized mechanisms (irrigation, hydropower, displacement, disease, land acquisition) and require careful identification. Endogenous dam siting, opposing channels and district-level aggregation make the sign highly ambiguous without strong quasi-experimental strategies.

context: This triplet refers specifically to U.S. joint (married) income taxation raising secondary earners’ marginal tax rates and thereby discouraging their labor supply. The paper illustrates this using PSID-based effective tax-rate estimates for 1988 (when the 1945 birth cohort is age 45) in an environment with the EITC, and more generally studies cohorts 1941–45 and 1951–55 via an estimated life-cycle model (MSM) with PSID and HRS data to simulate counterfactuals for married women’s participation.

difficulty.justification: Determining that joint filing raises secondary-earner marginal rates and reduces their participation requires intermediate tax-policy knowledge (EITC, filing rules) and is sensitive to cohort/institutional details; household bargaining, income vs substitution effects, assortative matching and selection create plausible opposing mechanisms and demand life‑cycle structural assumptions, so credible identification is moderately to highly challenging.

context: This triplet is estimated for Eurozone countries during 2007–2010 using country-level sovereign CDS (and bank CDS) data; the unit of observation is the sovereign/country. The institutional setting is public bank bailouts and government guarantees—notably Ireland’s Sept 30, 2008 guarantee and the Fall 2008 bailouts—and the analysis examines announcement effects (immediate widening of sovereign CDS and narrowing of bank CDS), controlling for banks’ equity performance.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate knowledge of bank–sovereign linkages in a crisis; the sign is highly context-dependent (fiscal space, guarantee vs capital injection) and there are multiple countervailing channels and simultaneity concerns, while announcement event-study data help identification but do not fully eliminate confounding.

triplet_key: 24133|directed entry of a new public nursing home|entrant fixed-cost recovery
treatment: Directed entry of a new public nursing home
context: This paper studies Pennsylvania’s nursing home industry using resident-level MDS admissions and facility surveys and cost reports, primarily for 2000–2002 (with robustness to earlier years). Units are nursing homes and newly admitted residents; outcomes are skilled nurse staffing per resident and private/Medicaid pricing. The institutional setting is Pennsylvania’s cost-based, peer-group Medicaid reimbursement rule (rates set from 3–5 year lagged peer costs across counties), high Medicaid coverage among residents, and variation in local market structure (county/MSA competition, no CON law). The analysis evaluates causal effects of Medicaid rates and entry on staffing and welfare.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether a state-directed public entrant lowers that entrant’s fixed-cost recovery hinges on institutional details (cost-based, lagged peer reimbursement, subsidies, occupancy/selection) and multiple opposing channels (subsidies/support vs. lower market revenues/peer-rate effects), creating high context dependence and complex identification challenges despite available administrative data.

context: This empirical study examines individuals who played the Massachusetts Megabucks lottery (prizes won during 1984–1988) using a 1995–96 survey linked to Social Security earnings for six years before and after winning. Units are individual lottery players (major annuitized winners and season‑ticket small‑prize winners). The institutional setting is the Massachusetts State Lottery where major prizes were randomly assigned and paid annually over 20 years; analyses adjust for ticket‑buying, season‑ticket selection, and differential nonresponse.

difficulty.justification: Random assignment of major annuitized prizes and linked administrative earnings data give relatively strong identification, lowering ambiguity and evidence needs; however the sign depends on institutional details (annuity vs lump sum, sample of lottery players, timing) and multiple mediating channels (consumption, debt repayment, labor supply, gifting) so moderate expertise and care are required.

triplet_key: 29406|pooling small group and individual markets|individual market premiums (most chosen plan type)
treatment: Pooling small group and individual markets
outcome: Individual market premiums (most chosen plan type)
context: This paper empirically analyzes U.S. health‑insurance market segmentation using Oregon all‑payer claims and plan files (2014–2016), comparing small‑group (employer) and individual marketplace enrollment and premiums for households. Units are households and plans; authors exploit “forced switchers” who lose employer coverage, insurer premium and MLR data, and ACA institutional features (guaranteed issue, metal tiers, risk adjustment, risk corridors, reinsurance). They structurally estimate demand and insurer pricing to simulate counterfactual pooling (e.g., ICHRA employer shifts) and validate results using multi‑state premium/MLR data.

difficulty.justification: Requires specialized knowledge of insurance design, ACA institutions, and pricing/risk‑adjustment mechanics; the sign hinges on relative risk distributions and insurer strategic responses so opposite outcomes are plausible, but the paper's forced‑switcher quasi‑experiment and structural/validation work provide reasonably strong (though not definitive) evidence.

context: This triplet refers to U.S. states that implemented Industrial Development Bonds (IDB) in the 1950s–1960s; the estimation sample is the 28 states that upheld IDB in state supreme courts before an early-1970s IRS reform. The unit of observation is U.S. states, and identification compares short-run changes around the court upholding relative to the prior legislative vote-in. The paper reports heterogeneous effects on state personal income tax collection: a significant positive impact of the court upholding is found only in states that had not signed an Exchange-of-Information (EoI) agreement with the IRS.

difficulty.justification: Identifying the sign requires specialized knowledge of tax law, IDB mechanics, and IRS–state information-sharing institutions; the effect is highly institution- and timing-dependent (EoI status) and is confounded by selection into early court rulings and concurrent state fiscal/policy changes, so heterogeneous and opposite-signed effects are plausible without strong identification.

context: Using the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development’s Moving to Opportunity randomized experiment (1994–98 enrollment, 10–15 year follow-up surveys in 2008–10), the paper studies low-income public-housing families with children in five U.S. cities (Baltimore, Boston, Chicago, Los Angeles, New York). Families were randomly assigned to an Experimental voucher (restricted to low-poverty tracts plus counseling), a regular Section 8 voucher, or control; outcomes for adults and youth are analyzed using survey, administrative, and neighborhood-tract data.

difficulty.justification: Although the MTO random assignment gives strong causal leverage, youth physical health is determined by many mediating channels (environmental exposure, stress, social networks), heterogeneous effects by age/gender/site, compliance/attrition and measurement/power issues, so the sign is not straightforward to predict and empirical evidence can be mixed.

triplet_key: 19226|exposure to import competition from china|cumulative earnings for low-wage workers
treatment: Exposure to import competition from China
context: This estimate comes from the paper’s 1991–2007 analysis using the 1% Social Security longitudinal sample of individual earnings and employer SIC codes, focusing on U.S. manufacturing workers. The triplet specifically concerns low-wage workers defined as the bottom tercile of average pre-exposure annual earnings (1988–1991) within age cohorts; the outcome is cumulative labor earnings over 1992–2007 measured relative to initial earnings. Industry-level variation in U.S. import penetration from China (1991–2007) is instrumented using import growth in other high-income countries.

difficulty.justification: The ADH-style China shock setting and IV strategy require substantial empirical/trade knowledge but are not purely theoretical; the negative sign is plausible and often found, yet identification is complicated by sorting, local demand shocks, occupational reallocation, measurement and GE feedbacks, so empirical evidence is informative but not definitive.

context: This paper uses U.S. annual national macro data (roughly 1912–2006, with main samples starting 1917/1930/1939/1950 and ending 2006) to estimate causal effects of government purchases—especially federal defense spending tied to WWI, WWII and the Korean War—and changes in average marginal income‑tax rates on per‑capita real GDP and its components. Units are national annual observations (per‑capita GDP, defense and non‑defense purchases, AMTR). Identification leverages war-driven exogenous variation, Ramey’s defense‑news, Romer‑Romer narrative tax shocks, and time‑series IV regressions.

difficulty.justification: Identifying the sign of tax‑to‑GDP effects requires substantial macro/fiscal knowledge and depends heavily on institutional context (war vs peacetime, monetary regime, expectation formation). Narrative and IV strategies mitigate endogeneity but important channels (Ricardian responses, anticipation, monetary offsets, composition and general equilibrium feedbacks) and external‑validity concerns make the sign nontrivial to pin down.

context: This paper analyzes U.S. aggregate annual data from 1866–2010 (and 1870–2008 for some macro series), using value-weighted nonfinancial corporate bond default rates, measures of bank loans and corporate bond market size, GDP, industrial production, stock and housing returns. It compares episodes of corporate bond-market default crises (identified when annual default rates exceed ~2.5%) to banking crises drawn from historical sources, studying national-level credit flows, collateral effects, and macroeconomic responses in the United States.

difficulty.justification: Determining that banking crises lower inflation requires substantial macro and banking knowledge because effects depend on monetary/fiscal responses, exchange-rate and commodity shocks, and institutional change across 1866–2010. Identification is complicated by concurrent shocks, selection of episodes, and multiple offsetting channels (credit supply, nominal rigidities, policy reaction), so the sign is plausible but not unambiguous without careful empirical design.

triplet_key: 21760|historical increase in state tax rates and sales reliance|aggregate output
treatment: Historical increase in state tax rates and sales reliance
context: The paper examines how heterogeneity in U.S. state tax rates affected the spatial allocation of workers and firms using annual state-level data from 1980–2010. It analyzes workers, establishments, trade flows and state governments across U.S. states, focusing on personal income, corporate (sales‑apportioned) and general sales taxes, federal transfers, and public‑good provision within a spatial general‑equilibrium framework. The authors exploit over 350 actual state tax changes and observed worker/firm relocations to estimate mobility elasticities and to simulate counterfactual, harmonized tax distributions.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a positive effect requires specialized spatial-GE and fiscal-incidence knowledge because tax changes simultaneously alter public-good provision, federal transfers, migration, firm location and trade; the endogeneity of tax changes and offsetting channels make the sign ambiguous despite rich quasi-experimental variation.

triplet_key: 13507|retail price commitments|incentive to raise wholesale prices
context: Analyzes summer 1999 (June–Sept) hourly wholesale electricity markets in three restructured U.S. regions—California, New England, and PJM—using firm-hour observations of generation, retail obligations, and prices. Context: early post-restructuring regime with retail rate freezes and varied vertical arrangements (divestiture vs long-term contracts vs retained generation), ISOs operating spot markets, and price caps; authors reconstruct firm cost functions and simulate Cournot and competitive equilibria to estimate impact of vertical commitments on wholesale prices.

difficulty.justification: Requires specialized electricity-market and industrial-organization knowledge; the sign hinges on institutional details (vertical integration, rate freezes, price caps) and multiple strategic channels so the opposite sign is plausibly without strong identification, and empirical inference relies heavily on model assumptions despite rich firm-hour data.

context: This study examines how state-level tort reforms in the United States from 1989–2001 affected obstetric practice and birth outcomes. Using individual Vital Statistics natality records on millions of births (mothers and infants), the authors exploit within-state changes in laws (caps on non-economic/punitive damages, joint-and-several liability, collateral-source rules) across counties to estimate effects on C-sections, labor induction, complications, and APGAR scores. Many reforms were enacted or overturned in the 1990s, providing staggered policy variation across states.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate knowledge of malpractice law and obstetric practice; the sign can vary with institutional details (hospital behavior, enforcement, baseline practice) and is plausibly confounded by other contemporaneous policies and selection. Large vital‑stats panels and staggered reforms give useful variation, but multiple channels (defensive medicine, patient selection, provider responses) make identification nontrivial.

context: For this triplet the analysis uses U.S. county-year averages of Medicare Advantage plan benefits (physician and specialist copayments) constructed from plan-level data available 2000–2003 — with 2001 treated as the first post-reform year after BIPA (March 2001). The difference-in-differences estimates evaluate how BIPA's urban/rural payment floors, which raised county MA capitation payments, affected mean copayments by 2003 under the pre-2004 demographic risk-adjustment regime.

difficulty.justification: Economic theory suggests higher MA capitation should permit lower physician copayments, but the sign depends on market competition, plan benefit-design choices, and pre-2004 risk-adjustment incentives. Empirical identification via county-level DiD on BIPA payment floors is plausible but aggregation, enrollment selection, and alternative responses (premiums, other benefits, or profits) leave meaningful uncertainty.

context: This paper reports a randomized trial of READI Chicago (N=2,456 men) conducted with rolling referrals from August 2017–March 2020 and 20-month follow-ups through late 2021. It evaluates an 18-month bundled intervention (subsidized supported work, CBT, and outreach referrals) in five high-violence Chicago neighborhoods (e.g., Austin/West Garfield Park, North Lawndale, Englewood). Units are individual men identified by algorithmic, outreach, or re-entry pathways; outcomes come from Chicago police and administrative records.

difficulty.justification: Although the randomized design reduces confounding (so only intermediate domain expertise is required), the bundled intervention, rare/count outcomes (shootings/homicides), local policing/measurement issues, potential spillovers/displacement, and strong setting dependence make the sign difficult to pin down with high confidence.

context: The paper constructs cross-country measures of legal protection against corporate self-dealing based on Lex Mundi law-firm responses about laws in force in May 2003 for 72 countries (covering most global market capitalization). It codes a hypothetical related-party transaction to build ex-ante/ex-post private enforcement and public-sanction indices and links these country-level legal indices to stock-market outcomes (market capitalization, IPOs, listed firms, block premiums, ownership concentration) using financial data from the late 1990s–early 2000s.

difficulty.justification: Determining that stronger anti-self-dealing rules causally raise firms-per-capita requires intermediate legal/economics knowledge and is sensitive to country-specific enforcement, complementary institutions, and timing; substantial confounding and endogeneity (wealth, financial development, reverse causation, measurement error) mean multiple channels and identification strategies are needed, so inference is moderately hard.

triplet_key: 9704|bank integration|employment growth sensitivity to bank capital
outcome: Employment growth sensitivity to bank capital
context: The paper analyzes U.S. state-level business volatility over 1976–1994 using annual state-year observations (total and small-firm employment). It studies how banking integration—the share of state bank assets owned by multi-state bank holding companies—changed with staggered state deregulation of interstate banking (late 1970s through the 1994 Riegle-Neal Act). The institutional setting is state-level banking deregulation and holding-company entry rules; identification exploits cross-state, over-time variation in deregulation while controlling for industry composition and common time effects.

difficulty.justification: Requires specialized banking and small‑firm credit knowledge; the sign depends on institutions (local relationship lending vs. scale advantages of multistate banks) and rival mechanisms are plausible, while staggered deregulation helps identification but selection and policy-timing confounders make inference nontrivial.

context: Using cross-country annual data (primarily 1961–2000, sample of ~58 countries aggregated into non‑overlapping decades), the paper links systemic financial risk—proxied by skewness of real bank‑credit growth and multiple crisis indexes—to per‑capita GDP growth and investment. The unit of analysis is countries (and country‑decades); key institutional contrasts are financial liberalization (de facto and de jure indexes) and contract enforceability (1984 law‑and‑order index). The empirical tests exploit cross‑country and decade variation to evaluate a theoretical model where liberalization plus moderate enforceability and bailout guarantees induce systemic risk‑taking.

difficulty.justification: The negative effect is plausible but requires specialized theoretical and identification work because skewness is a noisy proxy for systemic risk, is highly confounded with reverse causality, shocks, and omitted institutional factors, and its impact depends strongly on liberalization/enforcement contexts and general equilibrium feedbacks.

context: The paper studies Chilean manufacturing plants (census of plants with ≥10 employees, ~4,379 units) over 1979–1986, a period encompassing the 1974–79 trade liberalization (tariffs cut to a uniform 10%), subsequent short-lived tariff hikes during the 1982–85 recession, and substantial plant exit. It compares import-competing, export-oriented and nontraded sectors to assess how exposure to foreign competition affected plant-level productivity, entry/exit, and reallocation dynamics using plant-level panel data.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a positive effect requires specialized knowledge of heterogeneous‑firm trade theory and careful empirical strategies to deal with selection, dynamic entry/exit, and general equilibrium feedbacks; contemporaneous macro shocks (the 1982–85 recession) and sectoral heterogeneity make opposite signs plausible in different groups, so credible identification is challenging but feasible with rich panel variation and robustness checks.

context: Using IRS Statistics of Income corporate tax returns for U.S. firms 1996–2008, the paper studies the causal effect of the 2003 Jobs and Growth Tax Relief Act (large federal dividend tax cut applied in 2003) on firm behavior. It compares private C-corporations (subject to dividend taxation) to otherwise-similar S-corporations (pass-through, not subject to dividend tax) as controls, focusing on firms with $1M–$1B assets and $0.5M–$1.5B revenue, and accounts for contemporaneous tax provisions and industry–size shocks.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a null effect requires specialized knowledge of corporate tax incidence, dividend policy, and labor rent-sharing, and careful identification choices (C vs S comparisons, controls for contemporaneous provisions). Multiple channels (payouts, investment, bargaining) plus selection and potential general-equilibrium feedbacks make the sign ambiguous and require strong empirical strategies to be convincing.

context: This paper analyzes U.S. local labor markets using Census microdata (Longitudinal Business Database) spanning 1977–2013 (concentration trends) and firm-level panels through 2011/2014 for estimation and calibration. Markets are defined as commuting-zone × 3-digit NAICS; units are firms (firm×market×year employment and payroll) and aggregated workers. The core empirical strategy exploits state corporate tax rate changes as quasi-exogenous shocks to firm labor demand—focusing on tradeable sectors and C‑corporations—combined with firm×market and year fixed effects to estimate size-dependent employment and wage responses.

difficulty.justification: Determining that state corporate tax increases reduce firm wages requires substantial public‑finance and labor‑market knowledge because incidence depends on capital mobility, firm organization, and market power; the sign is context‑dependent and confounded by state shocks, multistate profit shifting, and composition/reallocation channels, though well‑specified firm×market panels and focus on tradeable C‑corps make credible identification reasonably attainable.

triplet_key: 22436|removal of negative credit information|credit inquiries by lenders
context: This paper studies Swedish individuals (pawn‑loan borrowers) who defaulted in 2000–2001 and links credit‑registry snapshots to tax/employment records (2000–2005). It exploits a 2003 policy/IT change that shifted arrear deletions from year‑end removal to exact three‑year deletion dates, creating exogenous variation in retention time of nonpayment flags. The institutional setting includes the Swedish credit registry and differing information access for banks versus non‑contributing users (e.g., employers), enabling tests of employer screening versus credit‑supply channels.

difficulty.justification: The 2003 deletion‑timing change provides credible exogenous variation so identifying a positive effect on lender inquiries is reasonably tractable, but institutional details (who sees what data) and competing channels (employer screening, bank vs non‑contributor behavior) make the sign context‑dependent and leave moderate room for alternative mechanisms.

context: The paper studies U.S. private businesses using national accounts, IRS microdata, the 2007 Survey of Business Owners, and a sample of 6,855 brokered private-business sales (1994–2017). Units of observation are private business owners and firms (pass-throughs and privately held C corporations). The authors calibrate a dynamic lifecycle model to 2007 moments, use it to value transferable and nontransferable “sweat” capital, and simulate tax-policy counterfactuals (including reforms comparable to the 2017 corporate tax cut) in the U.S. tax and institutional setting.

difficulty.justification: Assessing whether lower employee tax rates reduce self-employment rates requires specialized knowledge of tax code, business form choice, and valuation of sweat capital; the sign is institution-dependent and many alternative mechanisms and general-equilibrium/selection channels could plausibly overturn it, and although the authors use rich microdata and a calibrated lifecycle model, identification still hinges on strong modeling assumptions.

context: This paper studies U.S. macroeconomic performance across presidential administrations, primarily using quarterly NIPA data from 1947:Q1 through 2013:Q1 (with historical extensions to 1875–1947 and various subperiods). The unit of observation is quarterly GDP growth and four-year presidential terms; the analysis links partisan (Democrat vs. Republican) control of the presidency to macro outcomes (GDP, employment, TFP, oil shocks, financial indicators) while accounting for fiscal, monetary, oil, productivity, defense, and international shocks and forecasters’ expectations.

difficulty.justification: The sign hinges on wartime vs peacetime, financing, monetary responses, and open-economy/resource constraints, so substantive policy and historical knowledge is needed; defense spending can raise GDP via demand multipliers or lower it via crowding-out and resource reallocation, and isolating exogenous defense shocks across administrations is empirically challenging.

context: This paper exploits San Francisco’s 1994 ballot initiative that extended rent control to small (≤4‑unit) multifamily buildings built before 1980. Using address‑level panel data on nearly all adults (Infutor), DataQuick parcel records, permits and condo conversions, the authors track renters and landlords from the early 1990s through 2016 and analyze impacts mainly during 1995–2012. The unit of analysis includes individual tenants, parcels/landlords and neighborhoods (zipcodes); institutional details include within‑tenancy rent caps, eviction rules and condo/TIC conversion channels.

difficulty.justification: Requires substantial institutional knowledge of rent‑control rules, conversion and permitting channels; the sign is context‑sensitive with multiple plausible competing mechanisms (conversions, maintenance, turnover) though the described rich address‑level panel data materially improves identification.

context: This empirical study examines individuals who played the Massachusetts Megabucks lottery (prizes won during 1984–1988) using a 1995–96 survey linked to Social Security earnings for six years before and after winning. Units are individual lottery players (major annuitized winners and season‑ticket small‑prize winners). The institutional setting is the Massachusetts State Lottery where major prizes were randomly assigned and paid annually over 20 years; analyses adjust for ticket‑buying, season‑ticket selection, and differential nonresponse.

difficulty.justification: Random assignment of major prizes and linked administrative earnings make identification substantially easier than observational settings, but heterogeneity in responses (retirement, household substitution), limited follow-up relative to 20-year annuity, and selection into ticket buying leave moderate ambiguity and contextual sensitivity.

context: Structural life‑cycle analysis of US male workers (ages 22–61) using the 1993 SIPP panel for wage/job transitions and PSID 1988–1996 for employment/unemployment durations. The authors stratify by education (high school or less vs some college+), model quarterly consumption, labor supply and job mobility, and embed US institutions (Unemployment Insurance, Disability Insurance, Food Stamps, Social Security) to estimate wage processes with match effects and run calibrated counterfactual welfare simulations.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether higher employment risk lowers aggregate output requires specialized structural theory and calibration because effects operate through precautionary saving, labor supply/search responses, match quality and human-capital accumulation and can be amplified or offset by institutions (UI, disability, liquidity constraints) and general-equilibrium feedbacks; empirical confounders and strong context dependence make the sign nontrivial to pin down without careful identification.

context: Using U.S. Medicare claims and AHA hospital data from 1987–1994, the paper studies the short-run causal effects of mandatory public CABG surgery report cards introduced in New York (early 1990s, effective ~1991) and Pennsylvania (effective ~1993). The analysis examines both hospital- and patient-level outcomes for elderly Medicare beneficiaries (AMI cohorts and CABG recipients), exploiting treated (NY, PA) versus control states to evaluate how public disclosure of risk-adjusted surgeon/hospital CABG mortality altered selection, treatment incidence, resource use, and health outcomes.

difficulty.justification: Identifying whether public CABG report cards raised recurrent AMI among sicker Medicare patients requires substantial clinical and health-economics knowledge and is sensitive to institutional details (how reporting changed referral and treatment). Multiple plausible mechanisms (selection away from high-risk cases, quality improvement, coding changes), contemporaneous trends, and unobserved patient/provider sorting make the sign moderately to highly ambiguous and demand careful identification beyond basic diff‑in‑diff.

context: Using the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development’s Moving to Opportunity randomized experiment (1994–98 enrollment, 10–15 year follow-up surveys in 2008–10), the paper studies low-income public-housing families with children in five U.S. cities (Baltimore, Boston, Chicago, Los Angeles, New York). Families were randomly assigned to an Experimental voucher (restricted to low-poverty tracts plus counseling), a regular Section 8 voucher, or control; outcomes for adults and youth are analyzed using survey, administrative, and neighborhood-tract data.

difficulty.justification: Randomized assignment of MTO vouchers gives strong identification of an ITT effect on adult mental health, so domain knowledge needs only intermediate policy familiarity; however noncompliance, attrition, measurement and heterogeneous/context-specific effects (cities, gender, take‑up, mechanisms) raise complexity and limit evidence sufficiency, making the sign moderately straightforward but not trivial.

triplet_key: 12478|caps on non-economic damages|preventable complications of labor and delivery
outcome: Preventable complications of labor and delivery
context: This study examines how state-level tort reforms in the United States from 1989–2001 affected obstetric practice and birth outcomes. Using individual Vital Statistics natality records on millions of births (mothers and infants), the authors exploit within-state changes in laws (caps on non-economic/punitive damages, joint-and-several liability, collateral-source rules) across counties to estimate effects on C-sections, labor induction, complications, and APGAR scores. Many reforms were enacted or overturned in the 1990s, providing staggered policy variation across states.

difficulty.justification: Requires substantive knowledge of malpractice incentives and obstetric practice; effects depend on institutional details and can work through multiple channels (defensive medicine, provider supply, selection), so opposite signs are plausible, although rich natality data and staggered law changes improve identification potential.

context: This paper studies a village-level randomized subsidy experiment in northern Bangladesh that offered 600 Taka (~USD 8.50) transport subsidies in August–September 2008 for temporary seasonal migration during the Monga lean season. The analysis uses eligible poor rural households (low landholdings, reported skipping meals) surveyed pre-intervention (2008), during 2008–09, and in a longer-run follow-up in 2011 (and some 2013 data). The setting examines spillovers of migration incentives on informal village risk-sharing and welfare, and combines RCT variation with a structural model estimation.

difficulty.justification: Randomized village-level subsidies give strong local identification, so moderate domain knowledge suffices, but village spillovers, heterogeneous compliance, remittance timing, labor‑market and risk‑sharing feedbacks make the sign context-dependent and causally complex; the RCT plus structural model provides reasonably sufficient evidence locally but leaves externalities and long‑run channels that could alter the sign.

triplet_key: 18970|privatized service delivery (psp program)|ngo cost of providing services
context: This paper evaluates, via a randomized field experiment conducted c.2009–2011, a delivery innovation to Catholic Relief Services' SILC self-help group microfinance program in Kenya, Tanzania, and Uganda. The intervention randomized trained local agents (≈276) across multiple districts to remain NGO‑paid Field Agents (FAs) or become privatized Private Service Providers (PSPs) who charge membership fees. Data include eight quarters of group accounting for >5,700 SILC groups, semiannual agent surveys, and household panel surveys in 192 villages (~1,910 households), measuring membership, savings, credit, business activity, and consumption.

difficulty.justification: An RCT directly manipulating agent payment regime makes the primary channel (lower NGO wage outlays) straightforward to identify, but contextual factors (willingness to pay, agent attrition, quality changes, NGO oversight/transition costs and spillovers) mean moderate domain knowledge and causal reasoning are needed and leave some plausible alternative pathways.

triplet_key: 30257|more programs joining the platform|start college sooner
context: This paper studies Chile’s centralized higher-education assignment system using administrative data on PSU test-takers and ranked applications across 2010–2012 cohorts. It exploits the 2012 expansion of the on‑platform set (G8 universities joining the long-running G25 platform) and the 2011 BVP score‑based scholarship to estimate effects of on- vs off‑platform options on enrollment, waitlists, dropout and seven‑year graduation for individual applicants. The institutional setting features a deferred‑acceptance centralized match, widespread off‑platform offers, and decentralized aftermarket waitlists.

difficulty.justification: Understanding this requires specialized knowledge of centralized matching (deferred acceptance), aftermarket waitlists, and scholarship interactions. The sign depends strongly on institutional details (prevalence of off‑platform offers, timing of offers, selection of programs that joined), making opposite or null effects plausible though the expansion plus scholarship offers plausible quasi‑experimental leverage.

triplet_key: 19581|offer of admission to promise academy|reading achievement
treatment: Offer of admission to Promise Academy
context: This triplet follows individual students who entered the Promise Academy sixth‑grade admissions lotteries (2005–2006 cohorts) in the Harlem Children’s Zone, New York City, and were surveyed in 2012 (about six years after the lotteries). Reading achievement is measured by the Woodcock‑Johnson Broad Reading battery administered in the follow‑up. Identification exploits the randomized admissions lotteries (reporting ITT effects) and an IV/2SLS LATE from using lottery offers as an instrument for attendance.

difficulty.justification: The randomized admissions lotteries (ITT and IV/LATE) give strong internal identification, so domain expertise needs are modest; remaining difficulties are noncompliance, differential attrition, spillovers/peer effects and external validity to other contexts, which create moderate ambiguity and context dependence.

context: Combines a theoretical two-country model with panel evidence (1960–1990) for 14 OECD countries at the country-year level. Studies how redistributive fiscal policies—labor income, social security and payroll taxes financing transfers to retirees and the unemployed—affect competitiveness (manufacturing unit labor costs), relative nontradable prices, and employment. Exploits cross-country/time variation in tax rates, Calmfors–Driffill labor‑market centralization, productivity and controls under an open‑economy setting with unionized wage setting.

difficulty.justification: Assessing a negative effect of labor taxes on profit margins requires specialized open‑economy and wage‑bargaining knowledge; the sign depends on institutions (openness, centralization, demand effects) and is confounded by many alternative channels, GE feedbacks and endogeneity concerns despite multi‑country panel evidence.

triplet_key: 20767|increasing rural residency positions|quality of rural residents
context: Analyzes the U.S. family medicine residency market (approximately 430 programs and ~3,000 residents per year) over 2003–2010 (with 2011–12 out-of-sample validation). Units are individual residents and residency programs participating in the centralized NRMP clearinghouse (pairwise-stable, many-to-one matching) where salaries are pre-set. The paper studies antitrust concerns about low resident wages and policy interventions to increase rural training, leveraging program and resident characteristics, Medicare-based reimbursement variation, and final-match data to estimate preferences and run counterfactual simulations.

difficulty.justification: Identifying whether expanding rural slots raises resident quality requires specialized knowledge of centralized matching, program incentives, and Medicare reimbursement mechanics; results depend on institutional context and selection/placement dynamics. Multiple plausible opposing channels (selection vs dilution, program response, local labor market feedback) make a structural approach and strong assumptions necessary, though rich match-and-reimbursement data permit informative counterfactuals.

triplet_key: 5758|credit to public sector|growth of financially dependent industries
context: This study examines whether financial development in 1980 affects industry-level real value-added growth over 1980–1990 across 43 countries (the U.S. is excluded), using manufacturing industry–country pairs as units. For this triplet the treatment is the 1980 ratio of domestic credit to the public sector (public-sector credit/GDP), interacted with an industry’s financial dependence (proxied from U.S. Compustat firm financing patterns); the outcome is the industry’s real value-added growth 1980–1990. Key assumptions and instruments (legal origin, judicial quality) follow the paper.

difficulty.justification: The sign hinges on institutional detail (whether public credit crowds out or complements private finance), involves multiple interacting channels (crowding out, fiscal effects, allocation/selection) and plausible opposing mechanisms, and identification is challenged by measurement and instrument validity in cross-country industry-level data.

context: The paper develops a sovereign-default model with nominal wage rigidities, calibrates it to Spain (emphasizing the 2007–2013 Great Recession/Eurozone episode), and empirically analyzes a panel of 70 countries over 1980–2016. The empirical unit is countries; the analysis examines government consumption dynamics during recession episodes and how they vary with sovereign default risk (credit ratings, historical default rates) and initial net foreign liability positions. The institutional setting emphasizes fixed-exchange-rate / currency-union environments where fiscal policy is the primary stabilization tool.

difficulty.justification: Understanding whether higher sovereign risk causes greater fiscal procyclicality requires specialized knowledge of sovereign default mechanics, fiscal constraints, and nominal rigidities; the sign depends heavily on institutional setting (e.g. currency union vs flexible rate) and initial balance-sheet positions. The relationship is highly confounded (reverse causality from recessions, omitted institutional factors), involves GE feedbacks and selection, and thus needs strong identification beyond calibration and panel correlations to be convincing.

context: Using Trucost emissions matched to FactSet, this paper analyzes U.S.-listed firms from 2005–2017 to test whether firm-level carbon emissions (scope 1–3 levels and changes, and emission intensity) affect monthly stock returns and annual institutional holdings. The unit of observation is firms (monthly returns; firm-year ownership by investor type). Analyses exploit cross-sectional variation with firm controls, industry and time fixed effects, and asset-pricing factor adjustments in the backdrop of rising climate-policy salience (including the 2015 Paris Agreement) and heterogeneous corporate disclosure and exclusionary investor screening.

difficulty.justification: Determining the sign requires intermediate finance and climate-policy knowledge because multiple opposing channels (regulatory/transition risk, operating costs, investor preferences, stranded-asset risk, and disclosure quality) operate and vary by industry and time; cross-sectional confounding (size, profitability, industry composition, screening) and limited identification strategies make a definitive causal sign difficult without stronger quasi-experimental variation.

context: Using U.S. postwar annual data from 1946–2012, the paper studies tax-unit–level reported market incomes across the income distribution (top 1%, top 5%, bottom 90%, etc.) and aggregate macro outcomes. It constructs average marginal tax rate series (income and payroll taxes) and narrative scores of exogenous federal tax reforms (Romer–Romer style) to isolate surprise marginal-rate innovations, and estimates dynamic effects of these shocks on reported incomes, GDP, unemployment, and income sources.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether marginal tax-rate cuts raise reported non-wage income requires substantial tax-policy and income-reporting expertise and careful identification of realization/timing incentives; the sign is plausible but highly context- and mechanism-dependent (realization, shifting, GE effects) and while Romer–Romer narrative shocks materially help identification, important ambiguities and measurement issues remain.

context: Using Austrian administrative social-security data on 650,922 private-sector job-loss spells (1981–2001) for individuals aged ~20–50, the paper exploits a discontinuous UI maximum duration (20 vs 30 weeks) based on months worked in the past five years (36-month cutoff) to estimate the causal effect of extended UI benefits on the job-finding rate in the first 20 weeks of search.

difficulty.justification: This is a well-studied incentive question where intermediate economic knowledge (search theory, RD identification) suffices; the discontinuity design with rich administrative data gives fairly strong identification, but anticipation, heterogeneity, possible sorting/manipulation at the cutoff and institutional specifics leave moderate ambiguity and context dependence.

triplet_key: 24520|lowering business tax rates|measured tfps and markups
context: The paper studies U.S. private businesses using national accounts, IRS microdata, the 2007 Survey of Business Owners, and a sample of 6,855 brokered private-business sales (1994–2017). Units of observation are private business owners and firms (pass-throughs and privately held C corporations). The authors calibrate a dynamic lifecycle model to 2007 moments, use it to value transferable and nontransferable “sweat” capital, and simulate tax-policy counterfactuals (including reforms comparable to the 2017 corporate tax cut) in the U.S. tax and institutional setting.

difficulty.justification: Establishing that tax cuts raise measured TFPs and markups requires specialized knowledge of tax incidence, firm lifecycle and valuation of sweat equity, plus careful treatment of measurement and selection; institutional details and general-equilibrium/selection channels materially affect the sign, and while rich microdata plus calibrated lifecycle models help, substantial identification and measurement challenges remain.

context: Using a newly constructed dataset of 642 new chemical entities and their launch dates across up to 76 countries from 1983–2002, the paper studies drug-country launch timing. It exploits cross-country and over-time variation in patent regimes (product vs. process, patent duration), pharmaceutical price‑control regimes, and health‑policy institutions (e.g., national formularies, WHO essential medicines lists) to estimate how these institutional and market factors affect the speed and geographic diffusion of new drugs.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a positive effect of stronger patent protection on drug launch timing requires specialized knowledge of IP law, pharma market incentives and regulatory behavior; effects vary by country institutions and interact with price controls and enforcement, confounding and selection are plausible, and while the described panel policy variation is informative, endogeneity of adoption and enforcement measurement leave nontrivial identification challenges.

context: This paper studies diagnosis-based “upcoding” in Medicare from 2006–2011 across US county markets and individual beneficiaries. It compares private Medicare Advantage (MA) plans to traditional fee‑for‑service (FFS) Medicare, exploiting large county-level increases in MA penetration after the 2006 Medicare Modernization Act and the institutional rule that payment risk scores in year t are based on diagnoses in year t−1. Units are county‑year market averages of risk scores and individual beneficiaries (Massachusetts APCD) around the age‑65 Medicare enrollment threshold; analyses consider plan types and insurer–provider integration.

difficulty.justification: Understanding this effect requires specialized knowledge of Medicare risk adjustment and coding incentives; the institutional lag and quasi-experimental variation strengthen identification, but selection, documentation/intensity changes, and market spillovers make the opposite or mixed explanations plausibly without careful design.

triplet_key: 11413|equity market liberalization|volatility of returns and liquidity
context: This paper examines monthly excess equity returns and firm-level daily returns across 19 emerging equity markets (primarily January 1993–December 2003), using S&P/IFC indices and Datastream firm prices, to study whether liquidity (proportion of zero daily returns and price-impact measures) and equity market liberalization (an investability ratio capturing foreign access) affect expected returns. Units of observation are country equity indices and aggregated firms; analyses contrast segmented versus integrated regimes, validate liquidity proxies against limited bid-ask/turnover data and compare results to U.S. evidence.

difficulty.justification: Identifying the sign requires substantial asset-pricing and market-microstructure knowledge and is highly context-dependent; liberalization has multiple, opposing channels (liquidity provision vs. volatile capital flows), strong endogeneity/selection concerns, and the available proxies/observational variation are insufficient to pin down the causal sign without strong identification.

triplet_key: 28780|adding an early childhood education facilitator|total staff health and nutrition time
treatment: Adding an early childhood education facilitator
outcome: Total staff health and nutrition time
context: This paper reports a large-scale randomized evaluation (Sep 2016–Apr/Jun 2018) in Tamil Nadu, India, studying 320 government anganwadi centers (AWCs) serving children aged roughly 3–6. The Government of Tamil Nadu hired half-time early childhood education (ECE) facilitators for randomly selected centers; outcomes include center time-use, child test scores (math, language, executive function), and anthropometrics. Data come from baseline (Sept–Nov 2016), unannounced classroom observations, AWC endline testing (Mar–Apr 2018), and household follow-up (May–Jun 2018). The intervention was implemented by the state within existing ICDS institutional routines.

difficulty.justification: Random assignment gives strong identification, and unannounced observations improve measurement, so empirical evidence is fairly sufficient; however the sign depends on institutional role and task substitution/complementarity (facilitator could free staff to do more or instead reallocate tasks), so context and moderate ambiguity raise difficulty.

context: This paper studies the macroeconomic effects of federal housing agencies' mortgage portfolio purchases in the United States from the postwar era with emphasis on January 1967–December 2006 (and extensions through the 2007–08 crisis). Units are monthly aggregate mortgage markets and agency balance sheets (Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac, Ginnie Mae), mortgage originations, rates, housing starts and homeownership. The institutional setting is US GSE policy (capital requirements, portfolio caps, conforming loan limits, Treasury/Fed interventions); identification exploits ex‑ante narrative policy events and GSE stock‑market news.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a positive effect of GSE mortgage purchases on home-purchase originations requires intermediate institutional knowledge and faces substantial context dependence (policy regime, crisis vs normal times). Endogeneity, concurrent macro shocks, and multiple channels (rates, credit standards, expectations) make the sign plausibly confounded, though narrative policy events and stock‑market news provide moderately strong empirical leverage.

context: This paper exploits San Francisco’s 1994 ballot initiative that extended rent control to small (≤4‑unit) multifamily buildings built before 1980. Using address‑level panel data on nearly all adults (Infutor), DataQuick parcel records, permits and condo conversions, the authors track renters and landlords from the early 1990s through 2016 and analyze impacts mainly during 1995–2012. The unit of analysis includes individual tenants, parcels/landlords and neighborhoods (zipcodes); institutional details include within‑tenancy rent caps, eviction rules and condo/TIC conversion channels.

difficulty.justification: Predicting a positive effect requires intermediate-to-advanced knowledge of landlord incentives and local housing law; institutional details (eviction rules, conversion channels) and market conditions materially affect the sign. The 1994 ballot provides a plausible quasi‑experiment and rich administrative data, which helps identification, but alternative mechanisms (gentrification, renovation costs, selection of buildings/owners) and GE feedbacks make clean attribution moderately difficult.

triplet_key: 12893|eligibility for 30 weeks of ui benefits|average exit rate from unemployment
treatment: Eligibility for 30 weeks of UI benefits
context: Using Austrian social security administrative records for involuntary job losses from 1981–2001, the paper analyzes about 92,969 individuals aged 20–50 to study exit hazards around unemployment insurance (UI) benefit exhaustion. Austria’s UI rules provide 20 or 30 weeks of benefits depending on months worked in the prior five years (36-month threshold), alongside severance pay rules and means-tested post-exhaustion assistance. The analysis contrasts registered-unemployment duration and time-to-next-job (including recalls) to assess whether spikes at benefit expiration reflect true re-employment or exits from the register.

difficulty.justification: Interpreting a negative effect of longer UI entitlement on exit rates requires intermediate policy knowledge (UI design, benefit exhaustion behavior) and is sensitive to Austrian-specific institutions (severance, means-tested assistance, recall rules). Identification is moderately challenging due to duration dependence, registration versus true exit measurement, and potential selection/heterogeneity, but rich administrative data and the eligibility threshold make a clear inference reasonably attainable.

triplet_key: 28615|tick size pilot constraints on routing|execution quality
treatment: Tick Size Pilot constraints on routing
context: This introduction synthesizes six empirical studies of U.S. financial markets and firms in the machine-learning/big-data era. The papers analyze regulatory and commercial datasets—FINRA OATS and the SEC Tick Size Pilot, earnings-call transcripts for S&P firms, high-frequency trades and cross-asset microstructure data, Bitly clickstream around macro announcements, and hedge-fund returns—using units such as firms, directors, trades/orders, securities, and funds across mainly 2000s–2010s institutional settings in U.S. equity and Treasury markets and corporate governance contexts.

difficulty.justification: Assessing whether routing constraints improve execution quality requires specialized market-microstructure knowledge and familiarity with the Tick Size Pilot details; effects vary strongly by liquidity, participant type, and asset, so context matters. Multiple channels (spread, depth, adverse selection, algorithmic behavior) and competing plausible opposite effects make the sign highly ambiguous, and credible identification needs careful quasi-experimental designs despite usable regulatory datasets.

context: This paper analyzes U.S. county- and plant-level responses to oil and gas booms and busts across 1969–2014 (with long-differences reaching 1972–1997), exploiting geological county oil and gas endowments (1960 baseline and post-2000 fracking-adjusted reserves). The units are U.S. counties and manufacturing plants (Census of Manufactures microdata). Using national oil and gas employment and price cycles, the study estimates how local wages, population, employment, and manufacturing productivity evolved through multiple boom–bust episodes under varying extraction technologies and spatial spillovers.

difficulty.justification: Multiple strong, opposing channels (Dutch disease, labor cost/selection, agglomeration and input-price effects) and heavy dependence on institutional details make the sign highly context-dependent and confoundable; however, the paper's geological instruments and plant-level panel data provide reasonably good but not definitive leverage to identify a null effect.

context: This empirical study uses US Census Bureau Longitudinal Business Database establishment-level employment data (annual, 1976 onward, focusing on entrants roughly 1977–1996) to examine how state-level intrastate and interstate branch-banking deregulations (1970s–mid-1990s), enacted at different times across US states, affected entry sizes and early growth of new private‑sector establishments (startups). The paper exploits staggered reform timing across states, conditions on four-year survival for some analyses, and controls for state and year effects to study financing-constraint effects on initial and near‑term firm size.

difficulty.justification: Assessing the effect of interstate banking deregulation on entrant mean size requires specialized knowledge of bank–firm finance links, local banking competition, and policy endogeneity; the theoretical sign is highly ambiguous (positive, negative, or zero are all plausible) and identification must contend with staggered timing, endogenous reform adoption, treatment-heterogeneity, GE and selection/conditioning-on-survival issues.

context: This paper studies U.S. equity returns and German government “twin” bonds amid rising environmental concerns. For stocks it analyzes firm- and portfolio-level monthly data (MSCI environmental scores merged to CRSP/I/B/E/S) from November 2012–December 2020, exploiting variation across firms and industries in greenness and shocks to climate concerns (Ardia et al. media index) and earnings. It also examines German 10-year twin bonds (Sept 2020–Nov 2021) to illustrate yield/price responses to growing climate tastes. Units are individual stocks, value-weighted portfolios, and sovereign bonds.

difficulty.justification: Identification requires intermediate asset-pricing and ESG knowledge; the sign is context-dependent (investor tastes, sector exposures, time period) and readily confounded by compositional and risk/fundamental differences. Multiple channels (tastes, risk premia, earnings exposure) make causal reasoning nontrivial, and while the media-index shock and MSCI scores provide plausible variation, evidence is suggestive rather than definitive.

triplet_key: 29406|pooling small group and individual markets|taxpayer costs
treatment: Pooling small group and individual markets
context: This paper empirically analyzes U.S. health‑insurance market segmentation using Oregon all‑payer claims and plan files (2014–2016), comparing small‑group (employer) and individual marketplace enrollment and premiums for households. Units are households and plans; authors exploit “forced switchers” who lose employer coverage, insurer premium and MLR data, and ACA institutional features (guaranteed issue, metal tiers, risk adjustment, risk corridors, reinsurance). They structurally estimate demand and insurer pricing to simulate counterfactual pooling (e.g., ICHRA employer shifts) and validate results using multi‑state premium/MLR data.

difficulty.justification: Determining the sign requires specialized health‑insurance and ACA policy knowledge because pooling affects premiums, premium tax credits, risk‑adjustment transfers, employer behavior, and selection in opposing ways; although the paper's forced‑switcher design and structural estimates help, substantial context dependence and interacting general‑equilibrium channels leave the sign highly ambiguous without strong empirical identification.

context: Using annual data from 1970–2019 for 37 emerging-market sovereigns, the paper documents patterns of partial sovereign default and uses country-year observations of public debt service, arrears, debt levels (World Bank IDS/DRS) and EMBI+ spreads to study default episodes. The setting is emerging-market sovereign debt markets with long-term bonds, restructuring and accumulation of arrears. The authors build a structural long-term-bond sovereign model with partial default, estimate parameters by minimum-distance matching to panel moments, and run counterfactual policy experiments (pari passu, no-dilution, debt relief).

difficulty.justification: Determining that pari passu clauses shorten default episodes requires specialized sovereign-debt and legal knowledge, is highly sensitive to institutional and creditor-composition context, and is easily confounded by selection, litigation, and negotiation dynamics; it therefore involves multiple strategic and GE channels and cannot be cleanly pinned down by panel moments alone without strong identifying assumptions.

triplet_key: 29364|automation of loan origination process|share of ppp loans to black-owned firms
outcome: share of PPP loans to Black-owned firms
context: This triplet examines the effect of banks' automation of loan origination—specifically small and medium-sized U.S. banks that outsourced back-end PPP processing to third-party software—on the share of first-draw PPP loans to Black-owned firms. The analysis uses U.S. Paycheck Protection Program first-draw loans during April 2020–Feb 24, 2021, where loan amounts were formulaic, loans were fully government-guaranteed (no lender credit risk), and lenders faced severe processing capacity constraints.

difficulty.justification: Identifying whether automation raised the share of PPP loans to Black-owned firms requires specialized knowledge of bank operations, third‑party software rules, and algorithmic allocation which are highly context dependent for PPP (formulaic loans but variable outreach/processing). Multiple plausible channels and endogenous selection of banks into automation make the opposite sign nearly as credible and demand careful empirical strategies, so evidence is suggestive but not definitive without strong design.

context: This paper studies U.S. NYSE stocks from 1926–1933 using monthly stock-level data (CRSP) and daily loan rates published in the Wall Street Journal’s centralized NYSE “loan crowd” list. Units are individual stocks (211 ever-listed loan-crowd firms and the broader CRSP universe). The institutional setting features a public, centralized stock-loan market where loan/rebate rates reflect shorting costs; notable regime shifts (October 1930 anti-shorting pressure, March 1932 lending restrictions) affected supply and demand for lendable shares. Analyses link shorting costs and first appearances on the loan list to valuation and subsequent returns.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a negative effect of high shorting costs on subsequent returns requires intermediate expertise on short‑sale mechanics and historical institutions; the sign is sensitive to institutional/regime context and is plausibly confounded by reverse causation, selection onto the loan list, and supply shocks. The presence of well‑documented loan rates and regime shifts helps identification, but multiple channels and remaining omitted‑variable concerns keep this substantively difficult.

context: This paper evaluates Colombia’s PACES secondary‑school voucher program (implemented 1991–97), focusing on the Bogotá cohort that applied in 1994 for 1995 entry to 6th grade and whose ICFES college‑entrance outcomes are observed in 1999–2001. The unit of analysis is individual students who applied for means‑targeted vouchers that covered about half of private secondary tuition and were renewable conditional on grade promotion. Vouchers were often allocated by lottery, enabling randomized comparisons using administrative ICFES registration and test‑score records.

difficulty.justification: Lottery-based randomization and rich administrative ICFES records provide strong internal identification, so establishing a positive effect is relatively straightforward. Remaining complications are limited to take-up/compliance, attrition, and external generalizability rather than core causal identification.

context: This empirical study examines 115 highly leveraged Austrian ski hotels that underwent debt restructurings between 1998 and 2005 (with a control sample of 2,095 hotels). The Austrian Hotel- and Tourism Bank (AHTB) mediated restructurings under eligibility rules for “structurally important” hotels; data include pre- and post-restructuring accounting and local weather. The authors use two-year Unexpected Snow (relative to a ten-year average) to distinguish liquidity versus strategic distress and to instrument reductions in leverage, estimating effects on ROA, margins, costs, and sales.

difficulty.justification: Moderately difficult: basic corporate finance intuition predicts lower leverage raises ROA via reduced interest and distress costs, but institutional specifics (AHTB eligibility, ski-season demand) and multiple channels (liquidity relief vs strategic restructuring, selection of ‘structurally important’ hotels) matter. The authors’ snow-based instrument and panel data help identification, but exclusion restriction and external validity concerns leave nontrivial ambiguity.

context: This paper exploits a discontinuity generated by how the U.S. Office of Economic Opportunity (OEO) launched Head Start in 1965 by providing grant-writing assistance to the 300 poorest 1960 counties. Using county- and individual-level data on cohorts born in the 1960s–70s (mortality 1973–83, 1990 Census cohorts aged 18–24 born 1966–72, and NELS 1988 students), the authors compare outcomes across counties near the OEO poverty cutoff to estimate Head Start's impacts on child health and educational attainment.

difficulty.justification: Moderate domain knowledge and standard RD identification are sufficient to assess the sign, but local institutional details, migration/selection around the county poverty cutoff, and other concurrent programs make the sign somewhat context-dependent. The discontinuity design provides fairly strong evidence locally, though external validity and potential confounders (implementation heterogeneity, cohort attrition) prevent this from being straightforwardly obvious.

context: Using the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development’s Moving to Opportunity randomized experiment (1994–98 enrollment, 10–15 year follow-up surveys in 2008–10), the paper studies low-income public-housing families with children in five U.S. cities (Baltimore, Boston, Chicago, Los Angeles, New York).
