difficulty.justification: Identifying a negative effect of FX macroprudential rules on bank FX borrowing requires specialized knowledge of policy design/enforcement and careful identification because policies are endogenous to financial conditions, effects vary by institutional setting (exchange rate regime, capital controls, reserve currency status), and substitution/GE channels (to corporates, nonbanks, cross-border shifts) make the sign ambiguous without strong empirical strategies.

triplet_key: 29116|removal of explicit gender requests|per-application call-back rates in previously gendered jobs
outcome: Per-application call-back rates in previously gendered jobs
context: The paper analyzes the causal effects of an unexpected removal of employers’ standardized ‘preferred gender’ field from all job ads on XMRC.com (a Xiamen, China job board) on March 1, 2019. Using internal ad-, application-, resume- and call-back records for roughly one year surrounding the ban, the authors study ad- and application-level outcomes (jobs, workers, firms), exploiting within-ad pre/post changes, high-frequency (daily/weekly) time variation, and a 2018–2019 lunar-calendar comparison. Only the encoded gender label was removed; embedded text preferences remained.

difficulty.justification: Determining the sign hinges on selection/composition and signaling mechanisms (employer screening, applicant sorting) that require intermediate labor-market knowledge; institutional specifics (job-board rules, embedded text preferences, local hiring practices) matter a lot and make opposite signs plausible. The authors' rich within-ad, high-frequency administrative data materially help identification but remaining circumvention via ad text and composition dynamics keep the problem moderately difficult.

triplet_key: 26942|food stamps exposure in early childhood|residence in single-family home
treatment: Food Stamps exposure in early childhood
context: This paper studies long-term causal effects of childhood access to the U.S. Food Stamps (SNAP) program that rolled out across counties from 1961–1975. Using linked 2000 Census and 2001–2013 ACS data merged to the SSA NUMIDENT for individuals born 1950–1980 (observed as adults, roughly ages 25–54), the authors exploit county-by-county staggered adoption under the War on Poverty to estimate how in utero and early-childhood exposure (especially conception to age five) affected adult education, earnings, health, incarceration, and neighborhood outcomes across U.S. counties.

difficulty.justification: Long-run effects of early childhood Food Stamp exposure on adult housing choice require intermediate policy and labor-market knowledge but hinge on strong context (urban/rural, local housing markets, migration). Multiple mediating channels (income, education, family formation, incarceration) and potential selection/migration/confounding make the sign plausible but not obvious; staggered county rollout helps identification but does not eliminate important threats.

context: This paper analyzes the effects of macroprudential foreign-exchange (FX) regulations on bank and corporate FX borrowing across 48 countries (excluding reserve issuers) over 1995–2014. Using a country-quarter panel of policy changes compiled from four sources and BIS measures of cross-border bank loans and international debt issuance, the authors examine how asset- and liability‑side FX measures aimed at banks shift FX exposure between banks, corporates and investors, controlling for country and time fixed effects and macro/global covariates.

difficulty.justification: Determining that FX macroprudential rules reduce aggregate FX borrowing requires specialized knowledge of policy instruments and cross-border credit channels; effects vary with country institutions and permit strong substitution/confounding (banks → corporates/offshore), so identification demands careful quasi‑experimental design beyond fixed effects.

triplet_key: 19581|offer of admission to promise academy|college enrollment
treatment: Offer of admission to Promise Academy
context: This triplet uses the 2005 Promise Academy sixth-grade admissions lottery in the Harlem Children’s Zone (New York City). The treatment is the randomized offer of admission; the college-enrollment outcome is measured for the 2005 cohort only, defined as enrollment in the fall after their senior year and observed via National Student Clearinghouse matching roughly six years after the lottery.

difficulty.justification: Randomized lottery assignment gives strong causal identification and low confounding, so little specialist theory is needed; however findings are specific to the HCZ 2005 cohort and measured via NSC matching (single cohort, potential measurement/power limits), creating moderate context and evidence concerns.

triplet_key: 28780|extra early childhood education facilitator|language test scores
context: This paper reports a large-scale randomized evaluation (Sep 2016–Apr/Jun 2018) in Tamil Nadu, India, studying 320 government anganwadi centers (AWCs) serving children aged roughly 3–6. The Government of Tamil Nadu hired half-time early childhood education (ECE) facilitators for randomly selected centers; outcomes include center time-use, child test scores (math, language, executive function), and anthropometrics. Data come from baseline (Sept–Nov 2016), unannounced classroom observations, AWC endline testing (Mar–Apr 2018), and household follow-up (May–Jun 2018). The intervention was implemented by the state within existing ICDS institutional routines.

difficulty.justification: A large-scale randomized rollout within government ICDS strongly identifies the sign, so only modest domain expertise is needed; remaining concerns are context dependence (curriculum, home environment, fidelity) and potential spillovers/measurement issues that mildly complicate inference.

context: This paper analyzes U.S. commercial banks in the 1980s (roughly 1980–1989/1981–1990) using a panel of 147 publicly traded banks across 36 states and DC. It exploits staggered state-level interstate banking deregulation (changes in state laws permitting out-of-state entry) to study how increased corporate-control market competitiveness affected CEO pay levels, pay structure (equity/options), and turnover. Units of observation are banks and bank CEOs; identification leverages cross-state and over-time variation in regulatory regimes with bank- and CEO-fixed effects and year controls.

difficulty.justification: Understanding this link requires intermediate knowledge of corporate governance and banking deregulation; the sign depends on institutional details and has multiple competing channels (competition, risk-taking, size/composition effects) so opposite outcomes are plausible, and empirical identification—while feasible with staggered reforms and fixed effects—is threatened by endogenous timing, selection, and measurement issues.

context: Using firm-level Worldscope data primarily for 1997 (with robustness checks to 1995), this paper compares thousands of non‑U.S. firms (from 40 countries) that are listed in U.S. markets (exchange listings, OTC/ADR, Rule 144a) to domestic peers to explain valuation differences (Tobin’s q). The institutional setting emphasizes U.S. SEC disclosure and investor protection versus home‑country investor rights (LLSV), accounting standards, market liquidity and capital‑access differences, and the role of controlling shareholders in extracting private benefits.

difficulty.justification: Evaluating whether U.S. listing raises valuation of growth opportunities requires intermediate corporate‑finance/international listing knowledge and is sensitive to institutional heterogeneity (investor protection, accounting, ownership) and period choice. Major identification problems (firm selection into listing, reverse causality, unobserved firm quality) and multiple mediating channels (disclosure, liquidity, capital access, agency) make the sign plausible but not unambiguous from the described cross‑sectional data without stronger identification.

triplet_key: 21046|coordinated assignment mechanism|test scores and graduation rates
context: This paper evaluates the welfare effects of replacing New York City’s uncoordinated high‑school assignment system (2002–03) with a centralized, student‑proposing deferred‑acceptance single‑offer mechanism implemented in Fall 2003 (2003–04). Using administrative microdata on applications, offers, assignments, and enrollments for rising public middle‑school students across NYC, the authors study how offer coordination, short rank lists, and administrative placements affected assignment outcomes, travel distance, and downstream outcomes. They show test scores and graduation rates increased after the reform, with the largest gains concentrated among students who were most likely to be administratively assigned under the old system.

difficulty.justification: Identifying this sign needs intermediate knowledge of matching mechanisms and education policy; the effect varies with institutional details (short rank lists, administrative placements) and is plausibly confounded by selection, peer effects, and school responses. Rich administrative data help identification, but disentangling multiple mediating channels and ruling out concurrent changes remains challenging.

context: Randomized controlled trial in Chipata District, Eastern Province, Zambia during the 2013–2015 agricultural cycles. Sample: 3,139 small-scale farming households in 175 rural villages. Intervention: subsidized hungry-season cash or maize loans offered in January with repayment at harvest (July), with village-level random assignment and rotation across two years. The study estimates impacts on labor allocation, local wages, consumption and agricultural output in a rain-fed, single-harvest setting with limited formal credit and pronounced seasonal liquidity constraints.

difficulty.justification: A village-randomized, rotating RCT in a liquidity-constrained, single-harvest setting gives strong exogenous variation and generally sufficient data to estimate the sign. However, the net impact on aggregate labor supply runs through multiple opposing channels (household labor substitution, hiring, migration, local wage general-equilibrium and spillovers) and may be context-specific, so careful analysis is required.

triplet_key: 26177|slower mean reversion of debt-to-output|expected long-run gdp growth
context: This paper links time‑varying debt‑to‑GDP persistence to the distribution of aggregate consumption and asset‑return risk using U.S. quarterly macro data (1947:Q2–2016:Q4) and an annual panel of 15 developed countries (1978–2014). The analysis operates at the national aggregate level (debt‑to‑GDP, consumption and GDP growth, consumption‑claim excess returns) within a fiscal policy environment characterized by debt‑management rules, tax dynamics, and stochastic government expenditure; results are complemented by an endogenous‑growth, recursive‑preferences general equilibrium model.

difficulty.justification: Establishing that slower mean reversion in debt-to-GDP causally lowers long-run GDP requires advanced macro/asset-pricing knowledge and hinges on institutional and sample-specific features; multiple plausible reverse and confounding channels plus general-equilibrium feedbacks make the sign highly ambiguous and hard to pin down with aggregate panel/time-series evidence.

context: This paper studies first-time U.S. consumer bankruptcy filers who filed between 1992 and 2005 (analysis draws on court dockets 1992–2009 but requires 5+ years post‑filing), linking individual Chapter 13 filings across federal bankruptcy offices to Social Security Administration tax records and county-level DataQuick foreclosure data. The setting exploits blind rotation judge assignment within bankruptcy offices (pre‑2005 reform) that produces cross‑judge variation in confirmation rates; outcomes analyzed at the individual level include earnings, employment, mortality, and home foreclosure over the subsequent five years.

difficulty.justification: Random judge assignment offers a credible quasi-experimental source of variation, so empirical evidence can be strong; however, local foreclosure law, mortgage contract heterogeneity, judge-specific practices, and mediating channels (plan completion, cure vs. dismissal) make the sign context-dependent and moderately ambiguous.

context: This paper studies transmission of a 2008–09 credit‑supply shock—Commerzbank’s lending cut during the global financial crisis—across German multinational parents and their international affiliates using firm‑level data (MiDi, Ustan) from roughly 2002–2015. Units are German parent firms and their foreign affiliates located worldwide. The identification exploits pre‑crisis parent reliance on Commerzbank within Germany’s relationship‑banking system to isolate an exogenous credit shock to parents and examines internal capital flows, affiliate sales and employment, and a contrasting non‑financial 2013 flood shock.

difficulty.justification: Requires specialized knowledge of relationship banking and multinational internal finance; sign depends on institutional setting and crisis timing and is plausibly confounded by contemporaneous shocks, though firm‑level data and reliance‑based identification make a credible inference feasible.

context: The estimate refers to the cumulative effect of two years of Medicare Advantage (MA) enrollment on diagnosis-based risk scores using 2006–2011 U.S. county markets and an individual Massachusetts APCD sample around the age‑65 Medicare entry. Identification exploits county‑year changes in MA penetration with dynamic panel regressions that include a second lag of MA share; institutional rules imply risk scores in year t use prior‑year diagnoses and newly eligible beneficiaries may receive diagnosis‑based scores only after two calendar years, yielding a cumulative upcoding estimate of 8.7% after two years.

difficulty.justification: Properly attributing a + effect to two years of MA enrollment requires specialized knowledge of Medicare risk-adjustment rules and coding incentives; the identification leverages a useful institutional lag but is sensitive to time‑period rules and market-level confounders. Remaining concerns (selection into MA, concurrent changes in coding practices, HCC specification/policy changes and dynamic panel assumptions) make the sign plausible but nontrivial to establish convincingly.

context: This paper studies the causal impact of the U.S. Training Within Industry (TWI) program—a government-funded in-plant management training initiative delivered largely 1940–1945—on firm performance and spillovers. It uses a newly assembled panel of 11,575 U.S. war-contractor applicant firms (1935–1955 financials), primarily large manufacturers (covering ~40% of 1939 manufacturing employment), exploiting voluntary application windows, decentralized subdistrict implementation, and variation in instructor assignment. Eligibility required an active war contract; outcomes are firm-level sales, TFP, profitability, managerial practices, and supply-chain links.

difficulty.justification: Interpreting TWI’s effect requires intermediate management/industrial organization knowledge; the wartime contractor setting and potential selection, spillovers, and instructor-implementation heterogeneity make the sign context-dependent and plausibly ambiguous without careful identification. The paper’s quasi-experimental variation helps, but multiple mediating channels and general-equilibrium/selection concerns keep the task moderately difficult.

context: This paper studies French firms using administrative firm panel data (FICUS) matched to patents (PATSTAT) over 1994–2007, exploiting size-contingent labor regulations that sharply increase at 50 employees. Units are firms (manufacturing exporters for dynamic tests). Identification uses discontinuities near the 50-worker threshold and firm-level export demand shocks constructed from product–destination changes (shift-share). The institutional setting is France’s labor law (works councils, firing/consultation requirements) and the authors combine reduced-form regressions with a structural Schumpeterian growth model and calibrated counterfactuals.

difficulty.justification: Determining that size-contingent regulation causally raises labor‑saving patents requires intermediate policy/firm-level theory and is sensitive to French institutional detail; plausible alternative mechanisms (sorting, resource constraints, patenting behavior) and GE/selection channels make identification nontrivial, though the RD + shift‑share design and structural calibration provide reasonably informative but imperfect evidence.

triplet_key: 26345|removing promotion incentives|gender gap in subjective evaluations
context: This triplet comes from randomized online experiments (2018–2021) in the U.S. among adult workers recruited from MTurk and Prolific who completed an ASVAB-based math-and-science test. Subjects were randomly assigned to the “Private” treatment, which removed employers and thus eliminated incentives to promote: self-evaluation answers were non-strategic and non-incentivized (fixed bonus regardless of responses). The outcome compares subjective self-evaluations of equally performing men and women under these conditions.

difficulty.justification: Randomized assignment of incentive removal yields fairly strong internal identification of effects on strategic reporting, so domain knowledge needed is moderate; however intrinsic confidence differences, sample selection (MTurk/Prolific), power to detect nulls, and institutional/stake heterogeneity make the sign highly context-dependent and plausibly ambiguous in other settings.

context: Using IRS Statistics of Income corporate tax returns for U.S. firms 1996–2008, the paper studies the causal effect of the 2003 Jobs and Growth Tax Relief Act (large federal dividend tax cut applied in 2003) on firm behavior. It compares private C-corporations (subject to dividend taxation) to otherwise-similar S-corporations (pass-through, not subject to dividend tax) as controls, focusing on firms with $1M–$1B assets and $0.5M–$1.5B revenue, and accounts for contemporaneous tax provisions and industry–size shocks.

difficulty.justification: Evaluating a null effect of the 2003 dividend tax cut on corporate investment demands substantial corporate finance and tax-incidence knowledge and is highly context-dependent (treatment interacts with payout form, firm type, and concurrent tax changes). Selection into C vs S status, conversion, simultaneous capital-gains changes, payout vs repurchase responses, and dynamic/internal-finance channels make opposite signs plausible and identification hard despite rich IRS return data.

triplet_key: 25775|bank size|increase in systemic risk during busts
outcome: Increase in systemic risk during busts
context: The paper analyzes monthly bank‑level data on 1,264 listed financial institutions across 17 countries (Australia, Belgium, Canada, Denmark, Finland, France, Germany, Italy, Japan, Netherlands, Norway, Portugal, Spain, Sweden, Switzerland, the UK, and the US) over roughly 1987–2015 (with price series back to the 1970s). It links stock‑market and real‑estate bubble episodes (identified via BSADF and a trend‑deviation approach) to bank‑level systemic risk (∆CoVaR and MES), distinguishing boom and bust phases and heterogeneity by bank size, loan growth, leverage, and maturity mismatch.

difficulty.justification: Establishing that larger bank size causally increases systemic risk in busts requires intermediate-to-specialized knowledge of banking networks, regulation and risk measures and is sensitive to country/regime heterogeneity. Size is highly confounded (business model, leverage, connectedness, endogenous growth), requires addressing feedbacks and selection, and the panel data help but do not eliminate identification challenges without strong strategies (IVs, quasi-experiments, or network controls).

context: This paper studies U.S. working‑age households (panel data from the PSID, calibrated to circa 1999) to evaluate policies that ban employer wage discrimination by health status (ADA/ADAAA) and prohibit insurer pricing on prior conditions (HIPAA/PPACA, with 2014 implementation). Using a life‑cycle, dynamic structural model of health, medical spending, and labor earnings for cohorts aged roughly 24–65, the authors estimate health transition and expenditure processes and simulate short‑run insurance gains versus long‑run incentive costs of these regulatory reforms.

difficulty.justification: Determining that anti‑discrimination and anti‑priceing rules raise consumption insurance requires specialized labor‑economics and insurance theory and depends strongly on institutional enforcement and timing; employer sorting, hiring responses, insurance market equilibrium and life‑cycle dynamics make the opposite sign quite plausible, and empirical identification hinges on strong structural assumptions despite rich panel data.

context: This paper studies India’s November 8, 2016 demonetization — the sudden invalidation of 500 and 1000 rupee notes (≈86% of cash) and the uneven multi‑month rollout of new notes — using district‑level units (≈600) over late‑2016 and 2017. It links district variation in local cash availability (from RBI currency‑chest records) to monthly/quarterly outcomes (ATM/POS withdrawals, e‑wallet use, bank deposits and credit, satellite nightlights, and household employment) to estimate causal effects of a large, unexpected cash shock in a setting where aggregate RBI liabilities and policy rates were essentially unchanged.

difficulty.justification: The November 2016 shock provides plausibly exogenous, district-level variation in cash availability, so intermediate domain knowledge suffices, but the sign critically depends on local cash intensity, urban/rural structure and spillovers. Identification is complicated by measurement limits of nightlights, cross-district leakage, heterogeneous substitution to digital payments, and potential confounders, making a clear causal sign moderately difficult to establish.

triplet_key: 10262|capital taxation|medium-/long-run deviations between consumption growth and pre-tax return
outcome: Medium-/long-run deviations between consumption growth and pre-tax return
context: This paper uses U.S. aggregate annual data (primarily 1947–1997) to study how capital income taxation affects the wedge between expected pre-tax returns on the capital stock and consumption growth. Units are national aggregates—BEA measures of pre-tax and after-tax capital returns, per-capita nondurable and services consumption, and capital-income tax revenue (federal, state, local). The analysis relies on consumption Euler equations, VARs to form expectations, and TSLS estimates to infer capital supply elasticity and tax incidence in the mid-to-late 20th century United States.

difficulty.justification: Pinning down how capital taxes change the long-run gap between pre-tax returns and consumption growth relies on nontrivial theory (Euler equations, incidence, capital supply elasticities) and demanding identification; the sign is sensitive to institutional setting, macro shocks, and measurement/endogeneity concerns, and aggregate TSLS/VAR evidence is unlikely to definitively rule out alternative signs without strong instruments and additional micro or cross-sectional variation.

context: The paper studies U.S. private businesses using national accounts, IRS microdata, the 2007 Survey of Business Owners, and a sample of 6,855 brokered private-business sales (1994–2017). Units of observation are private business owners and firms (pass-throughs and privately held C corporations). The authors calibrate a dynamic lifecycle model to 2007 moments, use it to value transferable and nontransferable “sweat” capital, and simulate tax-policy counterfactuals (including reforms comparable to the 2017 corporate tax cut) in the U.S. tax and institutional setting.

difficulty.justification: Identifying whether lower employee-side taxes increase firm size requires specialized knowledge of tax incidence, entrepreneurship selection, and lifecycle valuation (sweat capital), because multiple offsetting channels (wage adjustment, labor supply responses, selection into self‑employment, and general-equilibrium effects) make the sign context-dependent; the rich microdata and calibrated model help but do not eliminate plausible opposing explanations.

context: The paper studies nonlinear pricing of basic staples (rice, kidney beans, sugar) in rural Mexico using household-level purchase and price data collected for the Progresa evaluation across waves from 1997–2003. The setting is small, often isolated localities/municipalities where a phased, randomized rollout of the Progresa conditional cash transfer program (1998–1999 initial waves) generated exogenous variation in household incomes and eligibility. Units are households (consumers) and single local sellers; the paper combines a structural nonlinear-pricing model with village-level empirical estimation and program-induced income shocks.

difficulty.justification: Theory of nonlinear pricing is specialized and gives ambiguous predictions for consumer/social surplus depending on demand heterogeneity, market power, and contracting; the Progresa income shocks and rich household data help identification but substantial structural modeling and context-specific assumptions are required to pin down the sign.

triplet_key: 26149|increase in foreign employment share|domestic firm wage bill
context: This paper studies the effects of foreign-owned multinationals on U.S. workers and local firms using linked employer–employee tax records (W-2 and corporate tax filings including Form 5472) for 1999–2017, with main direct-effect estimates focused on 2010–2015. The setting is U.S. commuting zones across industries; units are individual workers (moves between firms) and firms (value added, employment, wage bill). It exploits country-of-origin spatial clustering and worker movers to identify causal direct and indirect wage and productivity spillovers of foreign multinationals.

difficulty.justification: Understanding whether foreign employment raises domestic firms' wage bills requires advanced knowledge of multinational spillovers and labor markets; the sign is highly context-dependent (industry, skill, local labor market) and is confounded by firm and worker selection and opposing channels (poaching vs productivity complementarities), though linked employer–employee data and mover-based identification provide reasonably strong (but not definitive) evidence.

context: Using the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development’s Moving to Opportunity randomized experiment (1994–98 enrollment, 10–15 year follow-up surveys in 2008–10), the paper studies low-income public-housing families with children in five U.S. cities (Baltimore, Boston, Chicago, Los Angeles, New York). Families were randomly assigned to an Experimental voucher (restricted to low-poverty tracts plus counseling), a regular Section 8 voucher, or control; outcomes for adults and youth are analyzed using survey, administrative, and neighborhood-tract data.

difficulty.justification: Randomized assignment in MTO gives strong identification of the intent-to-treat effect on adult subjective well-being, so only modest domain knowledge is needed; however noncompliance, attrition, measurement of subjective well-being, heterogeneous effects across cities/populations, and mediating channels (neighborhood, stress, networks) introduce moderate context-dependence and ambiguity.

context: This study evaluates early impacts of the Moving to Opportunity (MTO) randomized housing mobility demonstration at the Boston site. It uses families with children living in public housing in high-poverty (1990 tract poverty ≥40%) areas who enrolled between October 1994 and May 1996, with follow-up surveys on average 2.2 years after random assignment (1997–1998) and administrative quarterly earnings/welfare data through 1998. The program randomly assigned households to an Experimental restricted low-poverty voucher with counseling, an unrestricted Section 8 voucher, or a Control group.

difficulty.justification: Although random assignment helps identification, correctly concluding a true null for girls' behavior problems requires attention to noncompliance, attrition, measurement error, heterogeneous impacts (site, age, gender), and limited follow‑up/power in the Boston sample, so intermediate-to-high difficulty remains.

triplet_key: 21658|head start offer|cognitive test scores by year three
outcome: Cognitive test scores by year three
context: The paper analyzes the Head Start Impact Study (HSIS) randomized lotteries conducted in Fall 2002 for two cohorts of 3‑ and 4‑year‑old applicants with follow-up into early elementary school across hundreds of U.S. Head Start centers. The unit of analysis is individual children/households applying for Head Start. The institutional setting is the federally funded Head Start preschool program operating alongside close public and private substitutes (state preschool, CCDF/TANF‑funded care); the study examines enrollment substitution, test‑score impacts, selection heterogeneity, and fiscal cost–benefit tradeoffs.

difficulty.justification: Randomized offers greatly aid identification (so common confounding is reduced), but noncompliance, substitution into other preschool options, attrition, measurement/power limitations, heterogeneous impacts and potential spillovers make confidently concluding a true null effect fairly challenging in this institutional setting.

context: Using an international firm-level panel from 1990–2008 (7,661 non-financial firms, 81,222 firm‑year observations) across 25 countries, the paper exploits extensive dividend and capital‑gains payout tax variation (including ~15 major reforms and dozens of discrete rate changes) to study how payout taxation affects firms’ investment allocation. Firms are the unit of observation; the setting compares cash‑rich versus cash‑poor firms within country‑years under varying tax regimes (dividend/capital gains, imputation systems) to infer how taxes alter internal vs. external equity costs and investment decisions.

difficulty.justification: Requires advanced corporate-tax and corporate-finance knowledge; institutional details (imputation systems, investor taxation) and alternative channels (repurchases, signaling, clientele effects) make the sign context-dependent and confoundable. The rich panel and multiple reforms provide good variation, but multiple mediating channels and potential endogeneity mean the sign is not straightforward to pin down.

context: This paper studies French firms using administrative firm panel data (FICUS) matched to patents (PATSTAT) over 1994–2007, exploiting size-contingent labor regulations that sharply increase at 50 employees. Units are firms (manufacturing exporters for dynamic tests). Identification uses discontinuities near the 50-worker threshold and firm-level export demand shocks constructed from product–destination changes (shift-share). The institutional setting is France’s labor law (works councils, firing/consultation requirements) and the authors combine reduced-form regressions with a structural Schumpeterian growth model and calibrated counterfactuals.

difficulty.justification: Understanding this effect requires intermediate-to-advanced knowledge of labor regulation, firm dynamics, and identification methods (RD, shift-share, structural calibration). Although the administrative RD and structural approach provide strong leverage, manipulation around the threshold, selection and composition dynamics, spillovers/GE feedbacks and model calibration assumptions leave the sign reasonably plausible but nontrivial to establish conclusively.

context: This estimate concerns first-time U.S. consumer Chapter 7 bankruptcy filers who filed between 1992 and 2005, matched to Social Security Administration tax records and county-level DataQuick foreclosure data, with five-year post-filing outcomes. Because there is little cross-judge variation in Chapter 7 treatment, the paper estimates Chapter 7 effects using an event-study (granted vs. dismissed) design that includes individual fixed effects and decile-of-predicted-propensity-by-event-time controls.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a negative effect is plausible but nontrivial: Chapter 7 affects unsecured obligations and provides an automatic stay but does not remove mortgage liens, so net effects on five-year foreclosure depend on liquidity, creditor behavior, and selection into dismissal. The study's event‑study with individual fixed effects and propensity controls helps, but limited instrument variation and plausible residual confounding and time/institutional heterogeneity make definitive sign identification challenging.

context: Using quarterly CRSP data from 1980–2004 Q1 on U.S. publicly traded firms (excluding utilities and financials), the paper examines how the 2003 dividend tax cut (Jobs and Growth Tax Relief Reconciliation Act of 2003, which reduced top individual dividend tax rates to 15% and applied retroactively to 2003) affected corporate payout behavior — regular vs. special dividends, initiations, terminations, and payment amounts — comparing pre- and post-2003 firm-level outcomes and contrasting responses with the 1986 tax reform.

difficulty.justification: Identifying this effect requires intermediate knowledge of dividend tax incidence and corporate payout behavior; responses vary with firm liquidity, anticipation, and substitution toward repurchases. Pre/post comparisons and the 1986 contrast help, but heterogeneous treatment timing, macro shocks, and selection make a clean causal sign moderately difficult to establish.

triplet_key: 9196|expected increase in government spending|consumption growth due to precautionary saving
outcome: Consumption growth due to precautionary saving
context: Using U.S. household-level expenditure data from the Consumer Expenditure Survey (December 1981–February 1998, focused on 1982–1997), the paper analyzes average consumption growth across (unconstrained and all) households. It studies how incomplete markets, idiosyncratic income risk, liquidity constraints and preference shifts drive predictable consumption growth, exploiting within-household panel variation and aggregate forecasts (expected unemployment, real interest rates, government spending, and monetary policy shocks) in the U.S., including episodes around the 1982 and 1991 recessions.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a positive precautionary-saving response to expected government spending requires specialized macro and consumption theory because the same expectation can generate income effects, Ricardian offsets, and be correlated with other macro shocks; measurement of expectations and isolating the precautionary channel in CE panel data is difficult and highly confounded by heterogeneity and concurrent policy/interest-rate movements.

triplet_key: 16717|chinese import competition|employment allocation to innovative firms
context: This paper uses firm- and plant-level panels (up to half a million firms; IT establishment sample ≈160,000) across 12 European countries over 1996–2007 to estimate how rising Chinese import competition — especially after China’s 2001 WTO accession and the phased abolition of textile/apparel quotas (MFA reforms in 2002 and 2005) — affected firm innovation (patents, R&D), IT adoption, TFP, employment and survival. Identification exploits differential industry exposure to quota removal and pre-existing Chinese export shares, with firm and industry fixed effects and trend controls.

difficulty.justification: Moderate-to-high difficulty: understanding this sign requires intermediate trade/firm-level knowledge and careful attention to heterogeneous, context-specific channels (entry/exit, reallocation, demand shifts). The panel and quota-shock design help, but selection, measurement (patents/R&D), and competing mechanisms mean the opposite sign or null is plausibly supported without strong identification.

context: This empirical study examines individuals who played the Massachusetts Megabucks lottery (prizes won during 1984–1988) using a 1995–96 survey linked to Social Security earnings for six years before and after winning. Units are individual lottery players (major annuitized winners and season‑ticket small‑prize winners). The institutional setting is the Massachusetts State Lottery where major prizes were randomly assigned and paid annually over 20 years; analyses adjust for ticket‑buying, season‑ticket selection, and differential nonresponse.

difficulty.justification: Randomized assignment of large annuitized prizes and linked Social Security earnings gives strong identification, so inferring the sign is feasible; however heterogeneity by age, baseline income, annuity timing, retirement decisions and sample selection/attrition make the sign context-dependent and moderately ambiguous.

context: This paper analyzes U.S. aggregate annual data from 1866–2010 (and 1870–2008 for some macro series), using value-weighted nonfinancial corporate bond default rates, measures of bank loans and corporate bond market size, GDP, industrial production, stock and housing returns. It compares episodes of corporate bond-market default crises (identified when annual default rates exceed ~2.5%) to banking crises drawn from historical sources, studying national-level credit flows, collateral effects, and macroeconomic responses in the United States.

difficulty.justification: Understanding whether corporate-default crises reduce inflation requires substantial macro and financial-history knowledge and depends on monetary regime and policy responses. Multiple opposing channels (demand collapse and credit supply contraction pushing prices down versus monetary/fiscal easing or currency regime shifts pushing prices up), plus endogeneity and historical confounders, make sign identification quite difficult despite long-run data.

context: This paper evaluates the social costs of large-scale solar PV in the Tucson, Arizona electricity grid (Tucson Electric Power) using hourly and 15-minute data from May 2011–April 2012. Units are hours (system operator decisions), generation units, and 58 geographically dispersed PV sites. The context includes Arizona’s Renewable Portfolio Standard and exogenous solar capacity scenarios (10–20% penetration). The authors use day‑ahead weather forecasts, generator cost and outage data, and a structural welfare-maximizing model to quantify intermittency, reserve operations, and CO2 impacts.

difficulty.justification: Multiple offsetting channels (fuel-cost savings, increased reserve/curtailment costs, transmission/operational constraints, CO2 externalities) and strong dependence on grid mix and institutional details make the sign context-sensitive and plausibly ambiguous; the authors' rich data and structural model help identification but substantive operational and valuation assumptions remain important.

context: This paper estimates long-run effects of early-childhood Medicaid eligibility from the original U.S. rollout (state implementation 1966–1970) by comparing birth cohorts (roughly 1936–1975) across states. The unit is individuals aggregated by birth state, birth year, and race; the earnings results reported here are primarily for white adults and use 2000–2014 Census/ACS data. Identification leverages the federal categorical-eligibility link to AFDC, cross‑state differences in pre-existing AFDC rates, EPSDT/migration‑adjusted cumulative childhood exposure.

difficulty.justification: Interpreting a long‑run positive effect of early Medicaid on earnings requires intermediate policy and health‑economics knowledge; the sign depends on historical/institutional context (era, state policy variation, race); plausible confounders (state trends, migration, selection) exist; the causal chain involves multiple mediators (health → schooling → labor market) and composition effects; the paper's institutional variation provides reasonably strong identification but residual threats leave nontrivial uncertainty.

context: Using U.S. postwar annual data from 1946–2012, the paper studies tax-unit–level reported market incomes across the income distribution (top 1%, top 5%, bottom 90%, etc.) and aggregate macro outcomes. It constructs average marginal tax rate series (income and payroll taxes) and narrative scores of exogenous federal tax reforms (Romer–Romer style) to isolate surprise marginal-rate innovations, and estimates dynamic effects of these shocks on reported incomes, GDP, unemployment, and income sources.

difficulty.justification: Assessing whether top-1% marginal-rate cuts raise pre-tax income inequality requires specialized tax-policy and identification knowledge because of multiple behavioral and reporting channels (realization/timing, avoidance, composition) and important general-equilibrium feedbacks. The Romer–Romer narrative approach and tax-unit data strengthen identification, but context-specific institutional details and remaining confounders leave nontrivial ambiguity.

triplet_key: 24560|downward nominal wage rigidity|welfare gains from commodity super-cycle
context: This causal estimate comes from the paper’s calibrated dynamic two‑region quantitative model (heterogeneous firms and workers) calibrated to Brazil’s 1999–2013 experience (using price paths for São Paulo and Minas Gerais). The authors impose downward nominal wage rigidity (Schmitt‑Grohé & Uribe style) under a managed‑float exchange‑rate rule and simulate a commodity super‑cycle, comparing aggregate welfare (economy‑level welfare gains, unemployment, and real GDP) under rigidity versus a fully flexible labor‑market counterfactual.

difficulty.justification: Answering whether downward nominal wage rigidity reduces welfare gains from a commodity super‑cycle requires specialized open‑economy macro and labor‑market theory and is highly sensitive to institutional details (exchange‑rate regime, labor institutions, openness). Multiple general‑equilibrium channels and calibration/modeling choices can plausibly reverse or attenuate the sign, so empirical/model validation is needed beyond a single calibrated exercise.

context: This paper analyzes annual country-year panel data for 18 OECD countries (maximum span 1960–1996) using business-sector aggregates (investment, profits, sales, capital) and cyclically-adjusted fiscal variables (primary spending, public wages, transfers, and tax components). It studies how changes in fiscal policy and episodes of large fiscal adjustments affect profits and private business investment, emphasizing labor-market channels (public employment/wages and labor taxes) and the composition of spending versus taxation in advanced-economy macro settings.

difficulty.justification: Determining a negative causal effect of public wages on business investment requires substantial macro/public‑finance knowledge because multiple channels (demand stimulus, crowding‑out via rates, labor‑cost changes, composition effects) and country‑specific institutions matter; cyclical adjustment and panel data help but endogeneity and omitted‑variable/confounding concerns make the sign nontrivial to identify empirically.

context: This paper studies firm bidding in Texas’s restructured wholesale electricity balancing (day-of) market in ERCOT during the early market years (sample spanning the first half of year 2: Aug 2002–Jan 2003). The unit of observation is hourly balancing auctions and firm-level bids (12 focal generating firms, covering ~66% capacity) with observed plant-level marginal costs. The institutional setting is a multi-unit, uniform-price balancing auction following the 2001 market restructuring; the study exploits a mid-sample two-month nuclear outage as a demand/cost shock and uses structural behavioral models to analyze efficiency and merger counterfactuals.

difficulty.justification: Determining that larger firms bid more strategically in ERCOT’s uniform-price, multi-unit day‑of market relies on specialized auction/market-power theory and is sensitive to institutional details (demand shocks, concentration, vertical structure), with plausible alternative explanations (cost asymmetries, learning, selection) and nontrivial strategic interactions; the paper’s observed costs, outage shock, and structural estimation help identification but the short sample and early-market setting limit definitive inference.

triplet_key: 18972|integration of china|advanced countries' specialization in innovation
context: The paper studies international specialization in innovation versus production using firm- and country-level manufacturing data circa the late 1990s/early 2000s (benchmarked to 1999 and averaged over ~1996–2001). It focuses on 26 countries (OECD and major trading partners), exploiting BEA firm-level U.S. multinational parent–affiliate sales and WIOD bilateral manufacturing trade flows. Units include countries, bilateral production–trade flows, and multinational firm sales. The setting is a calibrated general-equilibrium model of trade and multinational production, evaluated with counterfactuals (MP cost declines, China’s integration, U.K.–EU frictions, U.S. outward-MP taxes).

difficulty.justification: Establishing that China’s integration causally raised advanced countries’ innovation specialization requires specialized trade‑and‑multinational firm theory and GE reasoning; the sign depends on era, sector mix, and firm selection and faces plausible opposing channels (offshoring of R&D, demand effects), and the calibrated counterfactuals leave nontrivial identification uncertainty.

triplet_key: 28831|minimum wage increase|employment reallocation to higher-productivity firms
context: This finding pertains to Brazil and specifically to the observed rise in the federal minimum wage between 1996 and 2012. The authors use administrative linked employer–employee RAIS data for male workers aged 18–54 and an estimated structural equilibrium (Burdett–Mortensen–style) model with heterogeneous firm productivity. The employment reallocation result comes from simulations of that estimated model: lower-productivity firms cut vacancy creation while higher-productivity firms expand hiring, reallocating employment toward higher-productivity formal-sector firms.

difficulty.justification: Establishing a positive reallocation effect requires specialized search-and-matching/heterogeneous-firm theory and structural estimation (domain_knowledge=4) and depends strongly on Brazilian institutional details, formal-sector measurement, and the sample (context_dependence=4). Alternative mechanisms (informality, reduced hiring across the board, capital substitution) make the sign contestable without the model (ambiguity=4), and identification hinges on complex GE, selection, and matching channels captured by the structural simulations (causal_complexity=5). Administrative data plus a calibrated structural model provide substantial but model-dependent evidence, so sufficiency is limited but not absent (evidence_sufficiency=4), yielding an overall difficulty of 4.

triplet_key: 25403|alleviating debt relief frictions|speed of house price recovery
context: Using a representative Equifax 10% panel of roughly 13.5 million active U.S. consumers (2007:Q2–2017:Q4) and zip‑/county‑level outcomes, the paper studies household and regional recovery after the Great Recession (post‑Lehman). It compares pre‑(2007–2009) versus post‑intervention (2010–2016) periods across U.S. zip codes to link delinquency, foreclosures, house prices, consumption, and unemployment to spatial exposure to mortgage frictions (ARM share, HARP eligibility, servicer capacity) amid policy responses (QE, HARP, HAMP, near‑zero rates). Units are individual borrowers and zip‑code/county regions in the United States.

difficulty.justification: Identifying that easing debt‑relief frictions speeds house price recovery requires substantial sectoral knowledge (mortgage contracts, servicer behavior, HAMP/HARP) and careful empirical design. Concurrent macro policies, local shocks, reverse causality, spillovers, and selection make the sign highly confoundable and demand complex identification despite the large panel data.

context: This estimate uses BEA affiliate-level data on U.S. multinational firms, measuring affiliate initial size (assets at first appearance) for affiliates that first appear in the BEA data between 1983 and 1995 (each affiliate included once). It compares affiliates located in host countries with and without capital-account restrictions (IMF and Shatz measures), controlling for multinational parent, affiliate-industry, and year dummies, powers of host-country GDP, and median employee compensation.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate knowledge of multinational finance and internal capital-market channels; the negative sign is plausible but highly context-dependent and confounded by correlated host-country policies, selection of affiliate entry types, enforcement heterogeneity, and measurement of controls, and the included controls reduce but do not eliminate these concerns.

context: This paper studies the impacts of large dam construction in India over roughly 1970–1999 (with crop analyses 1971–1987 and disease and rainfall panels through the 1990s). The analysis uses Indian districts as units, GIS-derived river and dam locations to classify upstream/catchment and downstream/command areas, and district-level panels on agricultural production, irrigation, rural consumption, poverty and wages. Context includes state-led dam planning/financing, the Land Acquisition Act (1894), documented displacement, and environmental impacts of reservoirs.

difficulty.justification: Hydrological, political‑economy and engineering mechanisms are fairly specialized but interpretable with intermediate policy knowledge; effects depend strongly on local institutions, hydrology and timing. Multiple confounders and substitution/complementarity channels (budget reallocation, resettlement, groundwater impacts, selection of dam sites) make sign identification nontrivial despite useful GIS‑panel data, so credible identification requires careful spatial and temporal strategies.

triplet_key: 17136|pre-bailout financial sector distress|change in government debt-to-gdp
context: Analyzes the 2007–2010 global financial crisis with a focus on Western Europe (Eurozone and other developed countries). Units are sovereigns and large banks (bank-level and country-level observations); main data are daily/weekly credit default swap (CDS) spreads, equity returns, and country debt ratios. The institutional setting examines public bank bailouts and government guarantees (notably Ireland’s Sept 2008 guarantee and subsequent Fall 2008 bailouts), and how bailout financing (debt issuance/taxation) affected sovereign creditworthiness and fed back to bank credit risk.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a positive effect of pre-bailout bank distress on sovereign debt requires specialized knowledge of bailouts, guarantees, and sovereign–bank feedbacks; institutional arrangements (fiscal capacity, EU rules, bailout design) strongly affect the sign, and reverse causality/common shocks and contingent liabilities make alternative signs plausible. High-frequency CDS and fiscal data help, but credible identification demands complex strategies to untangle simultaneous feedbacks and selection, so inference is moderately-to-highly difficult.

context: This paper uses postwar quarterly macro data for the US (1948–1994) and other G7 countries (samples vary, typically 1960s–1994) at the country level, using aggregate GDP, employment (or hours), and labor productivity to decompose fluctuations into technology versus demand/monetary shocks under structural VAR identification with long‑run restrictions; the setting compares predictions of RBC versus sticky‑price imperfect‑competition models and evaluates how identified shocks drive employment and productivity.

difficulty.justification: Identifying demand shocks and their effect on output–employment comovement requires specialized macro identification (SVAR long‑run restrictions) and interpretation, and the sign can vary with country/period and measurement choices. Multiple confounders and general‑equilibrium channels make opposite or mixed findings plausible, and empirical conclusions depend heavily on strong identifying assumptions and limited samples.

context: This paper studies international reserve accumulation in emerging market countries over roughly 1990–2015, combining a sovereign-default model with empirical analysis on a panel of 22 emerging economies (country-year units). Empirical tests relate reserves/GDP, debt/GDP, sovereign spreads and a 3‑year rolling measure of exchange‑rate variability, and examine crisis episodes (notably 2008). The institutional setting contrasts fixed vs. flexible exchange‑rate regimes, long‑term foreign currency debt, downward nominal wage rigidities, and global risk‑premium shocks as drivers of portfolio and stabilization policies.

difficulty.justification: Evaluating whether higher reserves reduce depreciation requires substantial policy/theoretical knowledge (sovereign default, interventions) and is highly sensitive to institutional features (exchange-rate regime, liability structure, capital controls). Endogeneity and multiple mediating/GE channels make the sign plausible but confounded, so empirical identification is challenging though feasible with careful models and panel strategies.

context: This paper analyzes microsecond‑timestamped exchange message data from the London Stock Exchange for all FTSE‑350 stocks over Aug 17–Oct 16, 2015 (43 trading days), obtained via an FCA Section 165 request. Units are inbound/outbound messages and reconstructed symbol‑day events; the authors identify microsecond “latency‑arbitrage” races, firm (UserID/FirmID) participation, race frequency/duration, and mark‑to‑market profits (primarily using a 10‑second horizon). Institutional setting: continuous electronic LSE limit‑order book with outer‑wall timestamps and fee incentives affecting IOC vs limit order usage.

difficulty.justification: Answering whether removing latency arbitrage lowers liquidity costs requires specialized market‑microstructure knowledge and depends heavily on LSE institutional details; multiple plausible channels (reduced adverse selection vs. lost high‑frequency liquidity provision), strategic and general‑equilibrium responses, and limited counterfactual variation make the sign nontrivial to identify from the available data.

context: This triplet refers to country‑level counterfactual simulations for euro‑area member states (particularly Greece, Ireland and Spain) over 2000–2012, assessing employment outcomes during the 2008–2012 bust under a “conservative fiscal policy” implemented by shutting down a modeled political‑economy spending bias in the paper’s structural heterogeneous‑household macro model. Simulations compare actual versus counterfactual trajectories for employment, public debt and spreads within the euro monetary union under sudden‑stop and credit‑cycle scenarios.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether 'conservative' fiscal policy raised employment in euro‑area sudden‑stop episodes requires specialized macro theory (monetary‑union constraints, financial frictions, heterogeneous agents) and is highly context‑dependent; competing demand‑contraction versus confidence/spread‑reduction channels and heavy reliance on structural assumptions make the sign empirically and theoretically ambiguous.

context: This paper studies U.S. working‑age households (panel data from the PSID, calibrated to circa 1999) to evaluate policies that ban employer wage discrimination by health status (ADA/ADAAA) and prohibit insurer pricing on prior conditions (HIPAA/PPACA, with 2014 implementation). Using a life‑cycle, dynamic structural model of health, medical spending, and labor earnings for cohorts aged roughly 24–65, the authors estimate health transition and expenditure processes and simulate short‑run insurance gains versus long‑run incentive costs of these regulatory reforms.

difficulty.justification: Multiple countervailing mechanisms (adverse selection and moral hazard raising utilization and premiums vs improved access and preventive care lowering some costs) and strong dependence on institutional details (other regulations, employer coverage, mandates, subsidies) make sign determination nontrivial and require structural, life‑cycle identification and assumptions.

context: This paper studies the effects of regional government spending in a monetary union using U.S. state- and Census-division-level data on military procurement (1966–2006). Units are states and ten contiguous regions; outcomes include per-capita GDP, employment, and prices. The authors exploit exogenous national military build-ups and cross-state heterogeneity in procurement exposure (e.g., Vietnam, Soviet-Afghan war, post‑9/11) in a federal setting with common monetary and tax policy to estimate an "open economy relative multiplier."

difficulty.justification: National military buil-ups provide plausibly exogenous variation, so intermediate domain knowledge suffices, but identification is sensitive to context (openness, labor mobility, local price effects) and to allocation/endogeneity of procurement locations. Multiple channels (demand, crowding, migration, spillovers) and potential confounders mean the positive sign is plausible but not trivial to pin down empirically.

context: This paper exploits a 1994 German reform that abolished the one‑third worker‑representative mandate on supervisory boards for stock corporations incorporated on/after August 10, 1994 (grandfathering older cohorts). Using matched IAB‑Orbis employer–employee data, firm financials, and incorporation cohorts (units: firms and workers) for 1989–1999 entrants, it estimates effects on wages, firm pay premia (AKM), productivity, capital and the labor share within Germany’s two‑tier board and collective‑bargaining institutional context, noting a 500‑employee exemption.

difficulty.justification: Codetermination effects on wage structure require specialized knowledge of corporate governance, bargaining, and German institutions; the sign is highly context-dependent (two‑tier boards, 500‑worker exemption, collective bargaining) and multiple opposing channels (bargaining power, managerial selection, sorting, productivity responses) make the causal sign ambiguous despite the reform-based design.

context: This paper uses a randomized field experiment run Jan–Mar 2019 in 41 storefronts of a large payday lender in Indiana, surveying 1,205 payday-borrower customers and matching responses to administrative loan records (including the state-wide Veritec database) to study repeat borrowing, present-bias, and demand for commitment. It also analyzes the lender’s nationwide 2017 data and elicits expert beliefs. Indiana’s regulatory environment (interest and loan-size caps, reporting rules) and common two-week loan maturities frame the institutional setting for structural estimation and welfare simulations of policy changes.

difficulty.justification: The randomized experiment and administrative data give strong internal validity for short-run behavioral effects, but the welfare sign hinges on borrower heterogeneity (present bias vs patient), substitution to other credit sources, enforcement and market structure, and structural-model assumptions used for counterfactual welfare simulations.

context: This paper reports a month-long field RCT in Chennai, India, with 452 low-income urban adults employed in a one-month paid data-entry job (8-day baseline then ~3-week treatment) to study causal effects of increased sleep. Interventions—cross-randomized—included night-sleep treatments (information, sleep-improving devices, and sometimes financial incentives) and workplace half-hour afternoon naps in a comfortable office; outcomes include actigraphy-measured night and nap sleep, labor supply, productivity, cognition, well-being, and economic preferences.

difficulty.justification: Randomized design with objective sleep measures makes causal identification tractable, but effects operate through multiple mediating channels (sleep architecture, night-sleep substitution, productivity, mood) and are sensitive to population, job type, and short intervention duration, leaving moderate ambiguity and external-validity concerns.

triplet_key: 23976|payroll tax cut for young workers|employment rate of young workers
treatment: Payroll tax cut for young workers
context: This paper studies Sweden’s 2007–2009 employer payroll‑tax reform that halved employer payroll taxes for young birth‑cohorts (eligibility up to age 26) using population administrative employer–employee records and firm accounting data spanning the 1990s–2013. Exploiting cohort eligibility rules, timing of the two reform steps, and cross‑firm variation in pre‑reform shares of young workers, it examines causal effects on individual net/gross wages, employment and transition rates, and firm outcomes (employment, sales, capital, profits); take‑up was automatic via payroll software.

difficulty.justification: The study's strong design (cohort eligibility cutoffs, staggered timing, cross‑firm exposure, rich admin employer–employee and accounting data, automatic take‑up) gives credible identification, but possible offsets (displacement of older workers, wage pass‑through, selection and some general‑equilibrium effects) and institutional specificity leave moderate remaining uncertainty.

context: This paper studies the effects of the 1970 and 1977 U.S. Clean Air Act Amendments on manufacturing using plant-level Census of Manufactures data from 1967–1987. It exploits county-level, pollutant-specific attainment/nonattainment designations for four criteria pollutants across 3,070 counties and 1.75 million plant observations; polluting plants in nonattainment counties faced stricter plant-specific regulation. The unit of analysis is individual manufacturing plants; identification leverages cross-sectional and temporal variation in county nonattainment status and industry emitting status to estimate regulation impacts on employment, capital, and shipments.

difficulty.justification: Identifying the negative effect requires intermediate regulatory and industrial knowledge and is sensitive to local industry composition, enforcement, and plant mobility; multiple channels (closure, compliance investment, reallocation) and plausible confounders (pre-existing trends, selection into nonattainment) make the sign nontrivial, though the pollutant‑specific, cross‑sectional and temporal variation in the data provides reasonably good identification potential.

context: This paper studies U.S. life insurers and their affiliated reinsurers (captives) over 2002–2012 using company- and reinsurance-agreement Schedule S data. The units are operating companies, affiliated (unauthorized, unrated) reinsurers, and reinsurance contracts, set in the institutional context of post-2000 Regulation XXX/AXXX statutory reserve changes and new captive laws (post‑2002) and concentrated domiciles (South Carolina, Vermont, Bermuda, Barbados, Cayman). The authors evaluate effects on marginal cost, pricing, issuance, and risk-based capital.

difficulty.justification: Correctly inferring that affiliated (shadow) reinsurance lowers life insurance prices requires specialized regulatory and insurance-market knowledge and depends heavily on institutional details (domiciles, Reg XXX timing). Identification is complicated by endogeneity/selection, multiple mediating channels (capital, risk-based capital, pricing strategy) and potential feedbacks, though rich Schedule S data and regulatory shocks make credible identification feasible but not straightforward.

context: This paper studies French firms using administrative firm panel data (FICUS) matched to patents (PATSTAT) over 1994–2007, exploiting size-contingent labor regulations that sharply increase at 50 employees. Units are firms (manufacturing exporters for dynamic tests). Identification uses discontinuities near the 50-worker threshold and firm-level export demand shocks constructed from product–destination changes (shift-share). The institutional setting is France’s labor law (works councils, firing/consultation requirements) and the authors combine reduced-form regressions with a structural Schumpeterian growth model and calibrated counterfactuals.

difficulty.justification: Reasoning requires specialized knowledge of labor-law institution effects on firm behavior, RDD and dynamic selection identification; the sign is context-dependent (institutional detail, enforcement, time) and several plausible alternative channels and sorting/selection issues mean the negative effect is not trivial to pin down, although the RD + shift-share + structural approach provides reasonably strong but not definitive evidence.

context: This study analyzes US household wealth distribution and intergenerational mobility using microdata (SCF 2007 wealth distribution; Kennickell & Starr‑McCluer 1983–1989 six‑year wealth transitions; PSID/Chetty et al. 1980–82 cohorts for earnings transitions). Units are households/dynasties across generations. The authors estimate a life‑cycle structural model with finite working lives, no borrowing, bequest motives, stochastic lifetime earnings and stochastic returns, matching wealth fractiles and generational transition matrices via method-of-simulated-moments to quantify drivers of wealth inequality.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether greater capital‑income risk speeds downward mobility requires specialized life‑cycle and asset‑return knowledge and is sensitive to institutional and cohort context. The effect is confounded by wealth‑dependent portfolio choice, endogeneity of returns, bequest motives and selection, so sign identification relies heavily on structural assumptions and limited microdata.

context: This study examines hourly bidding, scheduled quantities, and production by firms in the Iberian wholesale electricity market (Spain and Portugal) using plant- and bid-level data from OMIE and REE for January 2010–December 2012. Units of observation are firm/plant-hour-day bids and positions (with emphasis on wind farms and four dominant firms holding ~68% market share). Institutional features include a centralized day-ahead auction plus up to seven intra-day markets, no virtual bidding, capacity limits and regulatory constraints that restrict arbitrage. Analyses combine reduced-form and structural approaches.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether allowing full arbitrage reduces productive efficiency in the Iberian power market requires specialized electricity‑market knowledge and is highly sensitive to institutional details (market design, capacity limits, renewables, and dominant incumbents). Multiple plausible countervailing mechanisms (arbitrage raising competition and efficiency vs. enabling strategic withholding, congestion and re-dispatch costs), plus endogeneity of bids and plant availability, make sign identification hard despite rich bid- and plant-level data and structural modeling.

context: This paper studies the 2006 Massachusetts health reform — notably the individual mandate, Commonwealth Choice exchange, and existing community-rating and guaranteed-issue rules — and its effect on the individual health insurance market. Using insurer- and state-level data from 2004–2011 (pre:2004–05; implementation:2006–07; post:2008–11), the authors compare Massachusetts to other U.S. states to estimate impacts on coverage, premiums, and average claims for non-elderly adults (18–64) in the non-poor individual market (>$300% FPL), combining SNL insurer-year data and NHIS coverage rates.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether Massachusetts’s individual mandate reduced average claim expenditures requires specialized knowledge of insurance selection, community rating/guaranteed-issue effects, and market responses; the sign is context-dependent and subject to multiple competing channels (selection, moral hazard, insurer sorting, policy bundle effects), and while the available insurer/state panel can support credible DD-style identification, substantial confounding and equilibrium responses make definitive inference challenging.

triplet_key: 5724|reduction in standard hours|probability of involuntary separation (arbeiter)
context: This triplet uses individual-level German Socio-Economic Panel (GSOEP) data on full-time hourly workers (Arbeiter) in (West) Germany, primarily 1984–1989, and estimates the effect of reductions in collectively-bargained standard weekly hours on the probability of involuntary separation (job loss) using a fixed‑effects conditional logit specification; the sample covers manufacturing and services, excludes part-timers (<35 hours) and outliers, and includes expected standard hours as a covariate.

difficulty.justification: Identifying whether reductions in collectively‑bargained standard hours causally lower involuntary separations requires intermediate institutional knowledge (German bargaining/short‑time practices), and the sign is context‑dependent with plausible opposing mechanisms (hours cuts as layoff substitutes versus as responses to demand shocks). Panel FE and controls help, but selection, reverse causality and heterogeneous firm responses leave substantial identification challenges.

context: Using the National Supported Work (NSW) Demonstration randomized experiment (volunteers randomized in 1976–77) across seven U.S. sites (Atlanta, Chicago, Hartford, Newark, New York City, Oakland, and Wisconsin locations), the paper analyzes post-baseline employment and unemployment spell durations for AFDC recipient women over roughly 26 months of semi-monthly follow-up. The program provided 9–18 months of subsidized work; eligibility required recent unemployment and volunteer enrollment, and random assignment ensures exogenous treatment variation.

difficulty.justification: Random assignment in the NSW RCT gives strong internal identification of the treatment effect for this volunteer AFDC sample, so basic causal inference is straightforward; however multiple channels (short-term subsidized employment vs post-program lock-in or displacement), site/time-specific labor market conditions, and volunteer selection make the sign context-dependent and nontrivial to generalize.

triplet_key: 16096|empowerment zone designation|total employment in zone neighborhoods
context: This paper evaluates Round I (1993) federal urban Empowerment Zones in six U.S. cities (Atlanta, Baltimore, Chicago, Detroit, New York City, Philadelphia/Camden) over the 1990s using tract-level microdata (1990 and 2000 Decennial Census) and the Longitudinal Business Database. Units are census tracts, firms, and workers (using Journey-to-Work to separate resident vs. commuter employment). The policy combined employment tax credits (from 1994) and $100M SSBG block grants. Identification compares treated tracts to rejected and later-round applicant tracts with DiD and reweighting/matching-based checks.

difficulty.justification: Determining a positive effect requires intermediate policy and labor-market knowledge; credible identification is challenged by nonrandom placement, local spillovers/GE feedbacks, and worker/firm relocation even though DiD with rejected applicants, tract-level Census and LBD (with commuting separations) and matching substantially strengthen inference. Residual time-varying confounders and heterogeneous implementation keep the problem fairly difficult but not intractable.

context: This paper studies aircraft-level data worldwide (489,916 aircraft-year observations, 5,987 operators) from 1978–2003 across 129 countries to estimate how country-level creditor protection (Djankov et al. creditor rights index) affects airlines’ use of vintage capital. Units are aircraft-years and airline operators in commercial and military sectors; outcomes are aircraft age, technological age, and fleet size (owned vs leased). The empirical setting exploits panel variation in creditor-rights reforms, country/operator/year fixed effects, and cross-sample comparisons (military vs commercial, leased vs owned, financially constrained vs not) to identify financing effects on investment.

difficulty.justification: The negative effect is economically plausible via asset-based finance and leasing channels, but identification is complicated by endogenous country‑level reforms, heterogeneity across leasing/ownership and military vs commercial operators, and multiple mediating channels so alternative explanations remain plausible without strong quasi‑experimental variation.

context: This paper evaluates the long-run general-equilibrium effects of a universal basic income (UBI) for the United States by estimating and simulating an overlapping-generations life-cycle model calibrated to U.S. microdata (PSID, CDS, NLSY79, SCF, CEX) spanning cohorts and outcomes largely in the late 20th–early 21st century and using 2000-dollar prices. Units are two-adult households/agents. Institutional features include progressive labor and capital taxation, federal student loans, Social Security pensions, and wage/employment shocks (including prolonged out-of-work events). The model embeds intergenerational skill formation, parental transfers, college choice, and savings.

difficulty.justification: Assessing a long‑run GE effect of UBI requires specialized theory about labor supply, savings, taxation, and intergenerational human‑capital formation; results depend on institutional parameters and calibration choices, and multiple plausible channels could reverse the sign, so even rich microdata and a calibrated OLG model leave substantial identification challenges.

triplet_key: 26942|food stamps exposure in early childhood|adult human capital
treatment: Food Stamps exposure in early childhood
context: This paper studies long-term causal effects of childhood access to the U.S. Food Stamps (SNAP) program that rolled out across counties from 1961–1975. Using linked 2000 Census and 2001–2013 ACS data merged to the SSA NUMIDENT for individuals born 1950–1980 (observed as adults, roughly ages 25–54), the authors exploit county-by-county staggered adoption under the War on Poverty to estimate how in utero and early-childhood exposure (especially conception to age five) affected adult education, earnings, health, incarceration, and neighborhood outcomes across U.S. counties.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate policy and life-cycle human-capital knowledge and careful attention to timing and migration. Staggered county rollout provides a credible empirical strategy, but confounding from concurrent programs, nonrandom adoption, selective migration, and multiple mediating channels make clean identification fairly challenging.

context: This triplet analyzes how the 2010 California pay-disclosure mandate affected the 50th, 75th and 90th percentiles of city wage distributions, using CalPERS-enrolled municipal employees’ earnings (CalPERS administrative data, 2001–2012). The unit of observation is the city-level percentile of municipal earnings in California, comparing cities that had previously disclosed manager pay to those newly compelled to disclose (with additional comparisons to Arizona). The analysis tests whether disclosure compressed lower parts of municipal wage distributions versus declines concentrated at the top.

difficulty.justification: Intermediate knowledge of public-sector pay-setting and transparency effects is needed; the sign varies with institutional features (unions, hiring flexibility, pre-existing disclosure) and multiple channels (compression, top-level reductions, recruitment/retention). Rich CalPERS data help, but identification is complicated by selection, parallel-trends concerns, dynamic responses, and possible concurrent policies.

triplet_key: 20256|mandatory board gender quota|outcomes for highly qualified non-board women
outcome: Outcomes for highly qualified non-board women
context: This analysis uses linked Norwegian administrative data to study highly qualified non-board women around Norway's 2003 board‑gender quota (made compulsory in 2006 and enforced by 2008). It defines four cohorts (1993, 1998, 2003, 2008) of individuals aged 35–55 selected either by a board‑membership propensity (board‑specific human capital) index or by holding a business degree and being in the top 98th earnings percentile; each cohort is followed for five years. Outcomes are individual earnings and the probability of occupying top positions (e.g., top‑five earners) in Norwegian businesses, excluding women who became board members.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a true null effect is hard here because the quota can generate heterogeneous firm responses, anticipation and timing effects, and spillovers (promotion pipelines, wage adjustments) while excluding women who became board members creates selection bias; convincingly ruling out positive or negative effects requires strong institutional knowledge and sophisticated identification strategies.

context: This triplet uses Compustat quarterly accounting data on sales for publicly traded firms with NLRB representation elections, restricted to 1973–1999 because earlier Compustat data are incomplete. The unit of analysis is firm × event‑time (quarterly observations around the NLRB case‑closure). The institutional setting is NLRA secret‑ballot certification elections; identification compares union victory versus union loss using an election fixed‑effects difference‑in‑differences specification with a post indicator for quarters on/after NLRB case closure.

difficulty.justification: Predicting a negative effect of union victory on firm sales requires specialized labor/IO knowledge because multiple channels (wage costs, productivity, bargaining, price responses, closures) and heterogeneous industry/institutional settings matter; selection into elections, dynamic firm responses, and measurement/sample limitations make the sign highly ambiguous and hard to identify convincingly with the described design.

context: This empirical study uses newly constructed quarterly national data for the U.S. (1890–2010) and Canada (1921–2011) on GDP, the GDP deflator, government spending and unemployment to study fiscal multipliers across major wars and the Great Depression. The unit of observation is country-quarter, and identification exploits a narrative “military news” series (changes in the expected present value of military spending) as exogenous shocks to government purchases. The paper compares responses across slack versus non-slack states defined by unemployment thresholds (6.5% U.S., 7% Canada) using local-projection impulse responses.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate knowledge of fiscal multipliers and wartime dynamics. The sign plausibly flips or attenuates depending on labor-market slack, monetary offset, open-economy and supply-side effects, and although narrative "military news" shocks strengthen identification, anticipation, spending composition, and correlated shocks leave nontrivial ambiguity.
