triplet_key: 16240|provision of value-added reports|probability of job separation for low-performing teachers
outcome: Probability of job separation for low-performing teachers
context: This study evaluates a randomized pilot by the New York City Department of Education during the 2007–2008 school year in which volunteer principals of schools serving grades 4–8 were randomly assigned to receive teacher value-added reports and training. Units are principals, teachers, and students; outcomes include principals’ evaluations (baseline and follow‑up in 2008), teacher turnover through 2008–09, and student test scores (spring 2009). Context includes NYC accountability reforms and union opposition; reports were not to be used for formal evaluation during the pilot.

difficulty.justification: Randomized assignment of reports gives solid internal identification, so moderate domain knowledge and evidence suffice to infer a sign; however, results are institution- and time-specific (union rules, pilot restrictions), multiple mediating channels (principal behavior, support vs removal, voluntary vs involuntary exits), and opposite outcomes are plausibly realized in different settings, raising context dependence and causal complexity.

context: This paper analyzes 92 countries worldwide, using country-level measures of government ownership of the top-10 commercial and development banks (measured in the mid-1990s and pre-privatization circa 1985) and long-run outcomes over 1960–1995 and 1970–1995. The unit of observation is the country (aggregating bank-level equity shares to assets), and the institutional setting exploits cross-country variation in legal origins, privatization histories, property-rights protection, government intervention, and banking system development to study effects on subsequent financial development, productivity growth, and per-capita income growth.

difficulty.justification: Determining a negative causal effect requires intermediate domain knowledge but is highly context-dependent (institutional quality, privatization history, legal origin) and plausibly ambiguous because state banks can both impede or promote productivity depending on setting. Identification is complex (multiple mediating channels, selection and GE feedbacks) and cross-country observational data make conclusive inference difficult without strong instruments or natural experiments.

triplet_key: 26942|food stamps exposure in early childhood|home ownership
treatment: Food Stamps exposure in early childhood
context: This paper studies long-term causal effects of childhood access to the U.S. Food Stamps (SNAP) program that rolled out across counties from 1961–1975. Using linked 2000 Census and 2001–2013 ACS data merged to the SSA NUMIDENT for individuals born 1950–1980 (observed as adults, roughly ages 25–54), the authors exploit county-by-county staggered adoption under the War on Poverty to estimate how in utero and early-childhood exposure (especially conception to age five) affected adult education, earnings, health, incarceration, and neighborhood outcomes across U.S. counties.

difficulty.justification: The positive sign is plausible via early-life nutrition → human capital → higher earnings → greater homeownership, so intermediate policy knowledge suffices, but local labor markets, cohort heterogeneity, migration, and nonrandom county adoption create strong context dependence and confounding risks. The staggered rollout and linked admin/Census data improve identification, but uptake measurement, compositional/selection effects, and multiple mediating channels mean establishing a clean causal sign is fairly difficult.

context: This paper studies Texas’s voluntary workers’ compensation insurance market using administrative policy and claims data from 2006–2011. It analyzes employer-level and industry-occupation classification–year aggregates to estimate demand and welfare, exploiting Texas Department of Insurance regulatory updates to classification base rates in 2007–2011. The units are workers’ compensation industry-occupation classifications and covered employers/payroll; Texas is the only state without a mandate, with regulated base rates, experience-rating modifiers, insurer multipliers, and a +/-25% cap on updates. The authors link premiums, coverage, and claim costs to evaluate price elasticity and selection.

difficulty.justification: Identifying the sign requires specialized insurance-selection and regulation knowledge because Texas’s unique voluntary market, experience-rating, insurer multipliers, and caps create context-specific incentives; competing mechanisms (adverse selection, moral hazard, reporting/manipulation, insurer sorting, aggregation effects) make the opposite sign plausibly without strong identification, though rich administrative data and regulatory shocks provide a feasible but challenging identification path.

triplet_key: 12807|extensive appeals and suspension during appeals|debt enforcement efficiency
treatment: Extensive appeals and suspension during appeals
context: This paper empirically studies debt enforcement for an identical hypothetical insolvent hotel ("Mirage") using detailed survey responses from 344 insolvency practitioners and judges across 88 countries (sampled and benchmarked to January 2006; surveys 2003–2006). The unit of analysis is the country-procedure outcome (time, cost, and whether the firm is preserved as a going concern) under two case versions; institutional features (legal origin, foreclosure rules, appeals, floating-charge availability) and per-capita income are the central contextual variables driving cross-country variation.

difficulty.justification: Assessment requires intermediate institutional knowledge; the sign depends critically on procedural details (whether stays preserve value or simply delay enforcement), is confounded by country-level rule-of-law and institutional differences, and involves multiple mediating channels while the survey-based vignette evidence provides informative but imperfect identification.

context: For the specific causal link between minimum-wage changes and lower-tail wage inequality, the paper analyzes U.S. workers using CPS hourly wage data across 1973–2004 (with emphasis on 1979–1987 versus 1988–2004) and concludes that minimum-wage movements plausibly help explain lower-tail inequality trends only during 1979–1987, but do not account for the evolution of bottom-half wage inequality after 1987.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a null effect of minimum-wage changes on lower-tail inequality requires substantial labor-economics knowledge (measurement, effective state/federal variation, enforcement) and is highly sensitive to institutional era and sample composition; multiple confounding channels (employment, hours, composition, nonwage pay) and data limitations make the sign ambiguous without careful identification.

context: This paper studies the causal effect of reductions in collectively-bargained standard weekly hours in (West) Germany, primarily 1984–1989 with additional analysis for 1990–1994, exploiting monthly industry‑level panel data on 201 manufacturing industries and individual-level German Socio‑Economic Panel data on full‑time workers. The institutional setting is industry-level union bargaining (notably IG Metall) that implemented stepwise cuts in standard hours, overtime premia, and greater hours flexibility; outcomes examined include employment, hours per worker, worker‑hours, and wage bills.

difficulty.justification: The sign hinges on institutional details (union bargaining, overtime rules) and multiple competing channels (work‑sharing vs. cost/price effects, selection and composition), so moderate specialist knowledge and careful identification are required; industry and micro panel data help but endogeneity and alternative mechanisms remain plausible.

context: This empirical study examines stock price revaluations around country-wide stock market liberalizations occurring after Dec 1988 across 11 emerging-market countries (Argentina, Brazil, Chile, Colombia, India, Korea, Mexico, Pakistan, Taiwan, Turkey, Venezuela). Using monthly firm-level IFC total-return indices for 429 publicly traded firms (248 classified as investible under IFC rules and 181 non-investible), the authors exploit liberalization implementation dates as natural experiments, comparing unexpected firm-level returns in month 0 (and months 0-1) while controlling for country fixed effects, earnings growth, size, and liquidity.

difficulty.justification: Intermediate market/policy knowledge is needed: liberalization plausibly raises investible-firm prices via foreign demand and liquidity, but effects depend on country institutions, capital controls, and timing. Event-study design helps identification, yet anticipation, concurrent macro/policy shocks, and selection into 'investible' status leave moderate ambiguity.

triplet_key: 20760|interstate bank deregulation|abnormal accruals of loan loss provisions
outcome: Abnormal accruals of loan loss provisions
context: This paper studies U.S. commercial banks from 1986–2006 (quarterly), focusing on 911 bank holding companies and ~2,879 subsidiaries across 48 states plus DC (excl. Delaware and South Dakota). It exploits staggered state-level regulatory reforms—intrastate branching liberalizations, interstate banking deregulation (culminating in the 1994 Riegle‑Neal Act), and interstate branching (1994–1997)—to assess how increased competition affected disclosure quality (discretionary loan-loss provisions and SEC restatements). The setting leverages cross-state variation, state-time fixed effects, and gravity-weighted measures of competitive exposure.

difficulty.justification: Assessing whether interstate deregulation reduces discretionary loan-loss accruals requires intermediate banking/accounting knowledge and is highly context-dependent; multiple opposing channels (competition reducing opacity via monitoring vs. incentivizing income‑smoothing or risk‑taking) make the sign highly ambiguous and require reasoning through selection, composition and feedbacks, though the staggered state reforms provide reasonably good empirical leverage.

context: This introduction synthesizes six empirical studies of U.S. financial markets and firms in the machine-learning/big-data era. The papers analyze regulatory and commercial datasets—FINRA OATS and the SEC Tick Size Pilot, earnings-call transcripts for S&P firms, high-frequency trades and cross-asset microstructure data, Bitly clickstream around macro announcements, and hedge-fund returns—using units such as firms, directors, trades/orders, securities, and funds across mainly 2000s–2010s institutional settings in U.S. equity and Treasury markets and corporate governance contexts.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether routing to affiliated ATSs reduces execution quality requires specialized market‑microstructure and regulatory knowledge and is highly sensitive to institutional details (fees, order types, client selection). Selection and strategic routing/internalization channels make the sign highly ambiguous without careful identification, though rich regulatory and high‑frequency datasets can partially address those gaps.

context: This paper studies Brazil’s labor market and wage inequality over 1985–2014 with a focus on 1996–2012, using administrative linked employer–employee data (RAIS) and household surveys (PNAD, PME). Units include male workers aged 18–54, firms, and state-year aggregates. The institutional setting is Brazil’s federal minimum wage, which rose sharply from 1996 to 2012 and applied uniformly across states but varied in effective bindingness by local wage distributions. Analyses combine firm-worker decompositions, cross-state panel regressions, and a structural equilibrium model.

difficulty.justification: Multiple plausible mechanisms (cost-driven contraction, monopsony-driven expansion, compositional entry/exit) and strong context dependence make the sign highly ambiguous and require sophisticated identification and equilibrium reasoning; the rich administrative data and structural model help but do not eliminate these challenges.

triplet_key: 20520|chapter 13 protection|probability of working in same job
outcome: Probability of working in same job
context: This estimate measures the effect of Chapter 13 protection on the probability that an individual filer works in his or her pre‑filing job for first‑time U.S. consumer Chapter 13 filers (1992–2005) matched to Social Security W‑2 records, evaluated over the five years after filing. Identification targets the marginal recipient of Chapter 13 using random assignment of cases to bankruptcy judges within federal offices (leave‑one‑out judge‑leniency IV); the unit is the individual filer and the parameter is a LATE for marginal filers.

difficulty.justification: Random assignment to judges provides a credible LATE estimate and strong outcome measurement, lowering confounding, but plausible competing mechanisms (stigma, wage garnishment, plan terms), potential exclusion restriction concerns, and institutional/time heterogeneity make the sign moderately hard to pin down and limit external validity to marginal filers.

triplet_key: 16096|empowerment zone designation|wages of zone resident workers
context: Focuses on Round I (1993) Empowerment Zones in six U.S. cities over the 1990s, using 1990 and 2000 Decennial Census microdata (Journey-to-Work) at the census-tract and individual-worker level. For this triplet the outcome is individual hourly wages of zone-resident workers who work inside the zone (log hourly wages, with composition-adjusted and unadjusted estimates). Treatment is EZ designation (employment tax credits from 1994 plus SSBG block grants); causal estimates compare treated tracts to rejected/later-round applicant tracts using DiD with propensity-score trimming and regression reweighting.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a positive wage effect requires intermediate policy and labor-market knowledge; the sign depends on local institutional and temporal context and is vulnerable to sorting, spillovers, and simultaneous shocks. The DiD with applicant controls and reweighting helps, but residual confounding, composition changes, and GE/selection channels make convincing identification fairly difficult.

context: This paper studies a village-level randomized subsidy experiment in northern Bangladesh that offered 600 Taka (~USD 8.50) transport subsidies in August–September 2008 for temporary seasonal migration during the Monga lean season. The analysis uses eligible poor rural households (low landholdings, reported skipping meals) surveyed pre-intervention (2008), during 2008–09, and in a longer-run follow-up in 2011 (and some 2013 data). The setting examines spillovers of migration incentives on informal village risk-sharing and welfare, and combines RCT variation with a structural model estimation.

difficulty.justification: Understanding whether migration subsidies increase village-level risk sharing requires intermediate migration and informal insurance knowledge, is sensitive to local labor markets and social networks, and admits plausible opposing mechanisms (erosion vs. strengthening of transfers); multiple mediating and spillover channels raise complexity, though the RCT plus structural work gives reasonably good—if not definitive—empirical leverage.

context: This paper studies the causal impact of expanding Medicaid via Oregon’s 2008 Medicaid lottery. From Jan–Sep 2008 the state randomly selected low-income adults (ages 19–64) on a waiting list for the opportunity to apply for OHP Standard; outcomes are measured through Sept 2009 (~16 months). The units are uninsured low-income adults/households (below ~100% FPL) who signed up for the lottery. The institutional setting includes Medicaid eligibility rules (citizens/legal immigrants, six months uninsured, income/assets limits) and uses administrative and survey data.

difficulty.justification: Although the Oregon lottery provides random assignment (so basic identification is good), inferring a null effect on mortality is context-dependent (short follow-up, low baseline death rates) and hampered by power limitations, LATE interpretation (noncompliance) and multiple mediating channels, making a definitive null conclusion moderately difficult.

context: This analysis examines the effect of public transportation provision—measured as peak‑service large bus counts from Section 15 reports (circa 1984, 1994, 2004)—on vehicle‑kilometers traveled (VKT) across US metropolitan statistical areas (MSAs) and their urbanized areas over the 1983–2003 period (with travel data from NPTS 1995 and 2001). For transit, the authors instrument bus supply (alongside historical road instruments) using the 1972 share of the Democratic vote to identify causal effects.

difficulty.justification: Inferring a 'no effect' is difficult because transit–VKT links involve multiple opposing channels (mode substitution, induced demand, land‑use responses) and strong endogeneity; the historical political instrument and sparse travel data raise substantial identification and external‑validity concerns, so alternative signs remain highly plausible.

context: The reported effect comes from a quantitative general‑equilibrium counterfactual calibrated to the 2007 Exiobase multi‑region input‑output data (aggregated for computation to 10 regions × 21 industries). In that global counterfactual—complete elimination of all import tariffs and ad‑valorem NTB equivalents—simulated global CO2 emissions increase (while simulated global real income also rises).

difficulty.justification: Determining the sign requires specialized trade–environment CGE/IO knowledge and depends strongly on country-level carbon intensities, elasticities, and model closures; multiple offsetting channels (scale, composition, technique, embodied trade) and structural uncertainty make the opposite sign plausibly supported under different assumptions.

triplet_key: 10276|degree of dollarization|severity of reversal impact on growth
outcome: Severity of reversal impact on growth
context: Using annual country-year data for 157 countries over 1970–2001, this paper studies current-account imbalances, large deficit “reversals,” and “sudden stops.” The unit of observation is the country-year across six regions (industrial, Latin America/Caribbean, Asia, Africa, MENA, Eastern Europe). Analyses relate lagged external debt, reserves, short-term debt, and credit growth to reversal probability and estimate reversal impacts on GDP growth, contextualized by 1970s oil shocks, the 1980s debt crisis, 1990s currency crises, IMF programs, and varying exchange-rate/dollarization regimes.

difficulty.justification: The effect of dollarization on reversal severity hinges on competing channels (balance-sheet currency mismatch, loss of monetary policy/lender-of-last-resort vs. reduced exchange-rate volatility and credibility) and varies sharply by institutions, crisis type, and period. Observational country-year data are subject to strong endogeneity, selection, and heterogeneity, so the sign is highly contested and hard to identify without strong identification strategies.

triplet_key: 28175|earlier loan forgiveness (10 years)|forgiveness amounts to high earners
context: This paper uses the 2019 Survey of Consumer Finances to analyze U.S. households (individuals aged 22–60) and their federal student loans, projecting repayment under federal programs (FFEL/DL) and Income-Driven Repayment plans (IBR, PAYE, REPAYE) that tie payments to 10–15% of discretionary income above 150% of the federal poverty line and forgive remaining balances after 20–25 years. The authors simulate earnings growth and discount future payments to compute present values and evaluate distributional impacts of universal, capped ($10k/$50k) and expanded-IDR forgiveness policies.

difficulty.justification: Whether earlier (10‑year) forgiveness disproportionately benefits high earners depends on heterogeneity in initial balances, earnings growth, and program repayment rules; competing mechanisms (high balances of high earners vs faster repayment by high earners) make the sign plausible but not obvious, requiring detailed simulation and identification of earnings and balance dynamics.

context: Analyzes the 2007–2010 global financial crisis with a focus on Western Europe (Eurozone and other developed countries). Units are sovereigns and large banks (bank-level and country-level observations); main data are daily/weekly credit default swap (CDS) spreads, equity returns, and country debt ratios. The institutional setting examines public bank bailouts and government guarantees (notably Ireland’s Sept 2008 guarantee and subsequent Fall 2008 bailouts), and how bailout financing (debt issuance/taxation) affected sovereign creditworthiness and fed back to bank credit risk.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether bailout announcements reduce bank CDS spreads requires specialized knowledge of the sovereign–bank nexus, market pricing and crisis-era policy instruments; the sign is highly context-dependent (financing, credibility, scope) and multiple confounding and feedback channels make the opposite sign plausibly without strong identification, so even with high-frequency CDS data credible causal identification is very difficult.

context: This paper studies PPP lending in the United States during April 2020–May 2021 (analysis focused on first-draw loans before Feb 24, 2021). Units are small-business PPP borrowers (millions of loans) and originating lenders (banks, credit unions, CDFIs, and fintechs). The institutional setting is the federally guaranteed Paycheck Protection Program where loan sizes were formulaic (2.5× payroll), lenders faced no credit risk and received fixed fees, and many lenders faced severe capacity constraints—permitting cross-lender comparisons and within-bank automation events to assess causal effects on lending to minority-owned firms.

difficulty.justification: PPP's formulaic design and available within-bank automation events make identification easier than in general lending, but automation can improve or worsen minority access via multiple channels (selection, outreach, encoded bias, capacity reallocation) and plausible opposite signs remain without careful design and controls.

triplet_key: 21748|replacing the lowest-ranking district school with a charter school|student test scores
treatment: Replacing the lowest-ranking district school with a charter school
context: Using administrative data on roughly 28,000 Boston public school students (mostly sixth graders) across 51 traditional, pilot, and charter schools during 2006–07 through 2013–14, the paper estimates a hierarchical model (combining lottery-based IV and OLS VAM evidence) and uses Monte Carlo simulations calibrated to those estimates to predict policy effects. The reported triplet — replacing the lowest-ranking district (traditional or pilot) school with a charter — refers to simulated impacts on affected students' standardized MCAS test scores (grade 6, with multi-grade analyses to grade 8).

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate education-policy knowledge (charter effects, sorting, VAM/IV) and is highly context-dependent (Boston-specific institutions and cohorts). Lottery IV provides credible local identification, but substantial ambiguity remains because of selection, spillovers, student reassignments, and simulation assumptions needed to generalize replacement effects.

triplet_key: 26890|increase in import competition|welfare across income distribution
context: Using repeated cross-sections of India’s National Sample Survey, the paper estimates income-specific consumer price indices and welfare changes for rural households across districts between 1987/88 and 1999/2000 and re-evaluates district-level effects of India’s 1991 trade reforms. Units are rural households and districts; the setting features substantial tariff reductions, rapid expansion of services and manufacturing quality/variety, and incomplete price measurement (reliable unit-value prices mainly for foods and fuels). Identification exploits variation in relative expenditure shares, within-G price changes, and cross-district exposure to import competition.

difficulty.justification: Requires substantial trade and distributional theory plus knowledge of Indian rural markets; effects depend heavily on local production structure, labor mobility, quality/variety and concurrent reforms. Measurement limits (unit-values for limited goods), potential confounders (technology, demand shifts, selection) and multiple GE channels make the sign highly ambiguous and hard to pin down empirically.

triplet_key: 12623|diagnosis by an interventional cardiologist|mortality of non-surgical patients
context: The paper studies a 20% random sample of Medicare fee-for-service beneficiaries who received diagnostic cardiac catheterization in 1998 in the United States. Units are elderly patients observed 365 days before and after diagnosis; the analysis compares those diagnosed by interventional (angioplasty-capable) versus non-interventional cardiologists. Identification leverages cross-area (three-digit ZIP) variation in the local density of interventional cardiologists. Outcomes include one-year Medicare spending, AMI/HF readmissions, and mortality, within the institutional setting of U.S. Medicare reimbursement rules and restrictions on explicit referral payments.

difficulty.justification: Assessing whether diagnosis by an interventional cardiologist increases one-year mortality requires intermediate clinical and econometric knowledge; results are highly context-dependent (Medicare, 1998) and easily confounded by patient selection, hospital quality, and referral patterns. The sign is ambiguous a priori (aggressive procedures could raise or lower mortality), identification relies on a potentially weak/excludable instrument (local density) and multiple mediating channels, so credible inference is fairly difficult.

triplet_key: 21632|switch to high-deductible plan|spending by sickest quartile
context: This paper studies a natural experiment at a large anonymized U.S. self-insured firm over six consecutive years (four pre, two post; policy change occurred in a t0 year within 2011–2014). The firm required employees and their dependents to move from a generous zero cost-sharing PPO to a non-linear high-deductible health plan with an HSA subsidy. Using individual-level administrative claims, diagnoses, and employment/demographic data (including ex ante ACG risk scores), the authors analyze how price signals and the contract’s non-linearities causally affect utilization, prices, and spending dynamics.

difficulty.justification: Requires specialized knowledge of non-linear insurance design, HSAs, and sick-patient utilization responses; the sign is context-dependent and ambiguous (sicker patients may be relatively inelastic or may cut discretionary care), but the firm-level natural experiment with rich claims and ex ante risk scores provides reasonably strong empirical leverage.

triplet_key: 10277|restrictions on capital mobility|probability of current account reversal
context: Using an annual panel of 157 countries from roughly 1970–2001 (2,228 country-year observations), this paper studies how abrupt stops in capital inflows (“sudden stops”) and large current-account reversals affect national real GDP growth. Units are countries observed annually across world regions. Key institutional features include measures of financial openness (Quinn index), trade openness (exports+imports/GDP), reserve levels, external debt, short-term debt shares, and de-facto exchange-rate regimes; the analysis evaluates how capital-account restrictions and exchange-rate flexibility mediate crisis incidence and growth impacts.

difficulty.justification: Whether capital‑account restrictions lower or raise the probability of current‑account reversals depends heavily on the type of controls, reserves, debt composition and exchange‑rate regime; strong endogeneity (policy responds to vulnerability), measurement (de jure vs de facto openness), and multiple GE/selection channels make the sign highly ambiguous and require sophisticated identification.

context: This paper studies the causal effect of reductions in collectively-bargained standard weekly hours in (West) Germany, primarily 1984–1989 with additional analysis for 1990–1994, exploiting monthly industry‑level panel data on 201 manufacturing industries and individual-level German Socio‑Economic Panel data on full‑time workers. The institutional setting is industry-level union bargaining (notably IG Metall) that implemented stepwise cuts in standard hours, overtime premia, and greater hours flexibility; outcomes examined include employment, hours per worker, worker‑hours, and wage bills.

difficulty.justification: The sign hinges on institutional details (overtime premia, bargaining rules, coverage) and multiple offsetting channels (hours reduction, wage adjustments, employment headcount, composition), so domain knowledge is moderate but context-specific and confounding is substantial; industry panel + individual data help identification but do not make it trivial.

triplet_key: 21024|top marginal income tax rate|number of domestic superstar inventors
context: The paper studies how top personal income tax rates affect international location choices of inventors—with emphasis on “superstar” top-1% patentees—using inventor-level patent panels (USPTO, EPO/PCT) spanning roughly 1977–2000 and case studies around the 1991 Soviet collapse, the U.S. 1986 Tax Reform, and Denmark’s 1992 researchers’ tax. Units are individual inventors (mostly firm employees) across eight main OECD countries (US, Canada, Great Britain, Germany, France, Italy, Japan, Switzerland). It exploits variation in effective top marginal tax rates and patent-based quality measures to identify tax-driven migration.

difficulty.justification: Identifying how top tax rates affect superstar-inventor location requires specialized knowledge of inventor migration, patent-quality measurement, and tax incidence across countries, and the sign depends on institutional and time-varying context. Confounding from concurrent economic/policy shifts, firm- and network-level sorting, and general-equilibrium and selection channels makes causal identification challenging even with rich panel and case-study variation.

triplet_key: 18572|both no-wage and no-prior laws|expected discounted lifetime utility
context: This paper studies U.S. working‑age households (panel data from the PSID, calibrated to circa 1999) to evaluate policies that ban employer wage discrimination by health status (ADA/ADAAA) and prohibit insurer pricing on prior conditions (HIPAA/PPACA, with 2014 implementation). Using a life‑cycle, dynamic structural model of health, medical spending, and labor earnings for cohorts aged roughly 24–65, the authors estimate health transition and expenditure processes and simulate short‑run insurance gains versus long‑run incentive costs of these regulatory reforms.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether bans on wage discrimination and prior-condition pricing reduce expected lifetime utility requires specialized life‑cycle and insurance theory because multiple offsetting channels (insurance gains, wage adjustments, selection, moral hazard, labor‑supply responses, dynamic health investment) operate simultaneously. The sign is highly context‑ and parameter‑dependent and sensitive to unobserved selection and modeling assumptions, so empirical identification without strong structural assumptions is very difficult.

context: This triplet refers to a structural counterfactual simulation for four euro‑area periphery countries (Spain, Greece, Ireland, Portugal) over 2000–2012. The experiment assumes macro‑prudential policies prevent the private leverage boom (predicted private debt held constant), uses a cross‑sectional regression to map reduced private debt into lower bank recapitalization needs (0.25€ per €), and reports effects on country‑level employment (partial stabilization, notably in Ireland).

difficulty.justification: Determining this sign requires specialized macro‑financial knowledge and structural identification because there are strong, countervailing channels (short‑run credit supply effects vs crisis‑prevention benefits) and country‑specific institutions/timing matter a great deal. The exercise also relies on a tenuous cross‑sectional mapping and strong simulation assumptions, so empirical evidence is insufficient to pin the sign down with confidence.

context: This study evaluates early impacts of the Moving to Opportunity (MTO) randomized housing mobility demonstration at the Boston site. It uses families with children living in public housing in high-poverty (1990 tract poverty ≥40%) areas who enrolled between October 1994 and May 1996, with follow-up surveys on average 2.2 years after random assignment (1997–1998) and administrative quarterly earnings/welfare data through 1998. The program randomly assigned households to an Experimental restricted low-poverty voucher with counseling, an unrestricted Section 8 voucher, or a Control group.

difficulty.justification: Random assignment greatly reduces confounding so basic inference is easier (low domain-knowledge requirement), but translating household-level moves into school-average test impacts requires reasoning about compliance, school choice, composition and peer effects in a specific Boston context with short follow-up and modest sample, creating moderate ambiguity and complexity.

triplet_key: 18972|introduction of multinational production|aggregate real income and innovation wages
outcome: Aggregate real income and innovation wages
context: This triplet comes from theoretical comparative statics in the papers multi-country general-equilibrium model of trade and multinational production. It examines the counterfactual switch from frictionless trade but no multinational production (MP) to frictionless trade with MP under the limiting assumptions of no worker mobility across sectors (\u03ba \u2192 1) and perfectly correlated multilateral productivities (\u03c1 \u2192 1). The unit of analysis is the country; outcomes are aggregate real income and sectoral real wages (innovation and production). These are analytical model results, not direct empirical estimates.

difficulty.justification: This is a highly technical, model-dependent comparative‑statics result that requires specialized multi‑country GE and multinational production theory; the sign is sensitive to limiting assumptions and multiple interacting channels (selection, reallocation, wage effects), so opposite outcomes are readily plausible without the specific model structure.

context: This paper studies eurozone bank-level interventions during the Global Financial Crisis and subsequent sovereign-debt shock, using hand-collected State Aid Register data on interventions from 2007–2009 and balance-sheet and loan-level outcomes through 2012. The unit of analysis is ultimate-parent banks (N=830) and bank–firm loan relationships, across eurozone countries differing in fiscal capacity and access to ECB facilities (including LTRO). The institutional setting includes national government recapitalizations, guarantees, liquidity measures, and EU Commission approvals, with fiscal constraints proxied by revenue/GDP, debt/GDP and maturing debt.

difficulty.justification: Identifying that undercapitalized banks borrowed more from LTROs requires specialized knowledge of ECB eligibility and state-aid constraints and is highly context-dependent across countries and crisis phases; plausible confounders (selection into recapitalization, country access to ECB facilities, simultaneous liquidity shocks) and complex feedbacks mean moderately rich data help but strong quasi-experimental variation is needed for a credible causal sign.

triplet_key: 12623|diagnosis by an interventional cardiologist|mortality of bypass patients
context: This triplet is estimated using a 20% random sample of U.S. Medicare fee-for-service beneficiaries who received diagnostic cardiac catheterization in 1998. Units are elderly patients observed 365 days before and after diagnosis. The reported effect is one-year mortality specifically among the subgroup of patients who underwent bypass surgery, comparing those initially diagnosed by an interventional (angioplasty-capable) versus a non-interventional cardiologist; identification leverages three-digit-ZIP variation in local density of interventional cardiologists.

difficulty.justification: Determining the sign requires substantial specialist knowledge because diagnosis-by-interventional cardiologists is endogenous and operates through selection into surgery, referral to different hospitals/surgeons, and pre-op management; local density (3-digit ZIP) likely correlates with hospital quality and practice style, so opposite signs are plausible. The subgroup analysis (only those who underwent CABG) and 1998 Medicare institutional context make identification and external validity especially fragile.

context: This triplet concerns county-level nonfarm median contract rents in U.S. counties measured across 1930–1940, examining the effect of HOLC loans per capita (mortgage purchases/refinancings made 1933–1936). The paper analyzes roughly 2,854 counties, but the rental-market findings emphasized here pertain especially to counties with population under 50,000 in 1930, where more HOLC funding was associated with a small, statistically insignificant decline in median rents (elasticity ≈ -0.023) alongside a rise in the number of nonfarm renters.

difficulty.justification: Understanding the likely sign requires intermediate historical housing-policy knowledge (HOLC operations and 1930s rental/homeowner dynamics) and is highly context-dependent across counties and time. Multiple plausible opposing channels (credit relief preventing foreclosures vs. altered owner/renter composition), selection of counties into HOLC activity, and historical data/identification challenges make the sign hard to pin down without strong quasi-experimental evidence.

context: This paper studies Brazil’s labor market and wage inequality over 1985–2014 with a focus on 1996–2012, using administrative linked employer–employee data (RAIS) and household surveys (PNAD, PME). Units include male workers aged 18–54, firms, and state-year aggregates. The institutional setting is Brazil’s federal minimum wage, which rose sharply from 1996 to 2012 and applied uniformly across states but varied in effective bindingness by local wage distributions. Analyses combine firm-worker decompositions, cross-state panel regressions, and a structural equilibrium model.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate labor-economics knowledge (minimum-wage theory, monopsony, sorting). Sign depends on institutional detail (bindingness, unionization, firm heterogeneity); alternative explanations (productivity trends, compositional sorting, contemporaneous policies) are plausible and multiple mediating and general-equilibrium channels operate, but rich linked employer–employee data and cross-state variation in effective bindingness make credible identification feasible though still challenging.

context: This paper empirically analyzes U.S. health‑insurance market segmentation using Oregon all‑payer claims and plan files (2014–2016), comparing small‑group (employer) and individual marketplace enrollment and premiums for households. Units are households and plans; authors exploit “forced switchers” who lose employer coverage, insurer premium and MLR data, and ACA institutional features (guaranteed issue, metal tiers, risk adjustment, risk corridors, reinsurance). They structurally estimate demand and insurer pricing to simulate counterfactual pooling (e.g., ICHRA employer shifts) and validate results using multi‑state premium/MLR data.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether ICHRA adoption raises average household welfare requires substantial health‑insurance market knowledge and depends strongly on ACA institutional details and local market structure; selection, sorting, insurer pricing responses and GE feedbacks make the sign nontrivial, and while the paper's rich data and structural model help, key identification rests on modeling assumptions and external validity.

triplet_key: 13995|tax deductions for housing and local taxes|indexation of taxes to local cost-of-living
treatment: Tax deductions for housing and local taxes
outcome: Indexation of taxes to local cost-of-living
context: This paper studies spatial effects of U.S. federal taxation circa 2000 across 241 metropolitan and non-metropolitan areas, using 5% IPUMS (Census 2000) microdata for wages and housing costs and Consolidated Federal Funds Report data for federal spending. Units are representative workers/households and metropolitan areas. The author calibrates a general-equilibrium Rosen–Roback style model, incorporating federal and state income/payroll taxes, mortgage/local-tax deductions, and transfers to simulate impacts on local wages, housing prices, land values, employment, and welfare under the U.S. tax code.

difficulty.justification: Requires advanced spatial public‑finance and tax‑incidence knowledge and a Rosen–Roback GE framework; institutional details (federal vs. state rules, mortgage/SALT treatment, housing market frictions) strongly affect the sign. Empirical identification demands modeling migration, housing capitalization and general equilibrium adjustments, so observational patterns alone can be ambiguous.

triplet_key: 14652|decline in routine occupation employment|low-skill non-routine employment
context: This paper analyzes U.S. occupational and local labor-market changes from 1980–2005 using 1980, 1990, 2000 IPUMS Censuses and the 2005 ACS, aggregating individual workers (age 16–64) into 330 occupations and 722 commuting zones. It studies how rising computerization and offshoring since 1980 displaced routine middle-skill jobs and altered occupational age structure, exploiting initial commuting-zone specialization in routine occupations to infer how workers of different ages and education levels reallocated across high- and low-skill non-routine jobs.

difficulty.justification: Interpreting whether routine-job declines cause increases in low-skill non-routine work requires intermediate labor-market knowledge and is sensitive to local institutions, worker heterogeneity, and time period. Multiple plausible channels (upskilling, downgrading, retirement, migration, demand shifts) and GE/confounding risks make identification nontrivial despite the useful CZ exposure strategy.

triplet_key: 26152|government bond purchases by the eurosystem|government bond yields
treatment: Government bond purchases by the Eurosystem
context: This study examines portfolio rebalancing, risk exposures, and yield effects of the ECB’s expanded asset purchase (quantitative easing) program in the euro area using security-level holdings and Eurosystem transactions from 2013Q4–2017Q4, focusing on purchases from March 2015–Dec 2017. Units are securities and investor sectors (banks, insurance/pension funds, mutual funds, other investors, and foreign investors) across issuer countries. The institutional setting is the ECB public sector purchase program, which buys eligible government bonds proportional to country-specific capital keys under eligibility and issuer-share limits.

difficulty.justification: Intermediate knowledge of monetary economics is needed; QE is expected to lower yields via portfolio rebalancing but the effect depends on market liquidity, eligibility rules and concurrent policy/signaling. Security‑level holdings and Eurosystem transactions provide strong identifying variation, yet confounders (macro surprises, supply shifts) and multiple channels make causal attribution nontrivial.

context: This empirical study exploits the 1698 Dai Viet–Khmer boundary in southern Vietnam to estimate long-run effects of historical state institutions. It compares nearby villages/hamlets and households using historical maps (1878–1926), South Vietnamese-era hamlet and public-opinion surveys (1969–1973), income data (1970–72), and modern household consumption surveys (VHLSS 2002–2012). The setting contrasts centralized, village-based Dai Viet administration with patron-client Khmer governance, across episodes including French colonialism, the Vietnam War, and postwar communist rule.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a positive long-run effect of historical state strength on household consumption requires specialized institutional and economic-history knowledge, and is highly context-dependent with many plausible confounders (geography, colonial investment, war damage, selective migration). The causal pathways span centuries with multiple mediators and GE/selection effects, and while the multi-period historical and modern data are informative, they are imperfect for fully ruling out alternative explanations.

context: This paper analyzes cross-country variation in labor-market regulation using detailed indices of employment law, collective-bargaining law, and social‑security law measured around 1997 for 85 countries. Units are countries; political controls use the fraction of years that executives and legislatures were left/center between 1975–1995 (and robustness to 1928–1995). The institutional context includes legal origin (common, French, German, Scandinavian, socialist), trade openness and political constraints. Main outcomes include unofficial-economy size, labor-force participation, unemployment (especially ages 20–24), and relative wages.

difficulty.justification: Labor-market theory gives guidance but interpreting cross-country EPL indices requires substantial institutional knowledge and is highly context-dependent (enforcement, informality, age composition). Reverse causality, omitted institutional confounders, heterogeneous channels (hiring/firing, informal sector, selection) and weak observational identification make the sign hard to pin down without strong quasi-experimental variation.

context: The paper analyzes U.S. state corporate tax changes (statutory rates and apportionment rules) over roughly 1980–2012 using a panel of ~490 county-groups (aggregated from over 3,000 counties) and matched firm–establishment data. Units include establishments, local populations, wages and housing rents. Identification exploits state-level variation in corporate and personal tax rules — including external tax changes via apportionment — to estimate impacts on establishment entry, wages, rents and profits within a spatial-equilibrium framework with heterogeneous firms.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether a local business tax cut raises rents requires advanced spatial-equilibrium and tax-incidence intuition (capitalization, firm entry, worker sorting) and is sensitive to housing supply elasticities and mobility. The sign is plausible but not unambiguous (alternative channels and local confounders exist), though the described panel and apportionment variation give reasonably good empirical traction.

triplet_key: 10726|access to global capital markets|firm incentives for better governance
context: This paper develops a theoretical model and tests it empirically using firm-level corporate governance and transparency ratings — the CLSA survey (conducted in 2001; 376 non-financial firms across 25 emerging markets) and S&P ratings (data compiled by April 2003; 711 non-financial firms across 39 countries). The unit of observation is individual firms (using ~2000 firm characteristics like sales growth, assets, ownership, cash holdings), combined with country-level measures (GNP per capita, stock market capitalization/GDP, legal investor-protection indices). The setting contrasts developed vs. developing countries and examines financial globalization (ADR listings) effects on governance.

difficulty.justification: Selection of better-governed firms into global markets and reverse causality make the causal sign ambiguous without strong identification. Effects also vary with country institutions and enforcement, and establishing causality requires addressing multiple channels (selection, monitoring, disclosure incentives) and potential omitted variables.

context: Using administrative data on roughly 28,000 Boston public school students (sixth graders) across 51 traditional, pilot, and charter schools during 2006–07 through 2013–14, the paper assesses school value-added. Boston’s centralized deferred-acceptance match and charter admissions use random tie-break lotteries and public lotteries, creating quasi-experimental assignment. Outcomes are standardized MCAS test scores (grade 6, with multi-grade analyses to grade 8); analyses exploit lottery-induced random offers, OLS value-added models, and hierarchical estimation to study selection bias and causal school effects.

difficulty.justification: Randomized charter lotteries provide strong quasi-experimental variation so the causal sign is relatively straightforward to identify for this sample, but understanding compliance, attrition, spillovers, and external validity requires intermediate econometric/policy knowledge and moderates confidence.

context: Analyzes elementary school admissions in Cambridge, MA (Controlled Choice Plan) over 2004–2009, using individual kindergarten applicants (≈400/year, 13 schools) and submitted rank-order lists. The institutional setting is an Immediate Acceptance (Boston) mechanism with proximity and sibling priorities and free/reduced-lunch program quotas. The paper documents strategic reporting around proximity-priority boundaries, estimates the underlying distribution of student preferences from manipulated reports, and simulates counterfactual assignments (including Deferred Acceptance) to evaluate welfare and assignment outcomes.

difficulty.justification: Requires specialized mechanism-design and preference-estimation knowledge; the sign depends strongly on local priorities, strategic misreporting, and counterfactual assumptions. Estimating latent preferences and simulating DA vs IA is methodologically complex and alternative outcomes are plausibly supported without strong identifying assumptions.

context: This estimate is based on program applicants in Michigan over the June 2008–May 2014 sample period, using winter months (September–March). The authors estimate household-level panel regressions (with household fixed effects) of monthly natural gas consumption on heating degree days and interactions with a WAP indicator to compare slopes for weatherized versus unweatherized homes.

difficulty.justification: Physical theory predicts a negative effect, and the panel HDD interaction is a plausible identification strategy, but selection, time-varying confounders, behavioral rebound, and sample-specific institutional details mean the sign is moderately context-dependent and not trivial to pin down definitively.

triplet_key: 31332|equal pay act and title vii|women’s wages at lower percentiles
treatment: Equal Pay Act and Title VII
context: Analyzes U.S. labor markets from the 1950s through the 1970s with emphasis on the 1960s, leveraging the passage and implementation of the 1963 Equal Pay Act and 1964 Civil Rights Act. Uses individual-level census and CPS ASEC data (ages 25–64) and aggregated industry-occupation-state-group cells across 21 state groups to compare states with and without pre-existing equal-pay laws and jobs with larger 1960 gender wage gaps. Institutional context includes FLSA coverage, WHD versus EEOC enforcement, and staggered state law variation.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a positive effect of the Equal Pay Act/Title VII on women’s lower‑percentile wages requires specialized knowledge of mid‑20th century labor law, enforcement regimes, and labor market institutions; effects plausibly vary by state, sector, FLSA coverage and enforcement intensity. Confounding from changing female labor force participation, occupational sorting, pre‑trends, and GE or compositional effects makes the sign plausible but nontrivial to pin down even with staggered state variation and rich census/CPS data.

context: This paper uses administrative registers for the population of Denmark (≈4 million individuals, 1995–2009, ages 18–60) to study how retirement saving responds to policy changes. It exploits variation in employer-defined-contribution pension rates from job switches, a 1998 Mandatory Savings Plan that imposed a 1% income-tied contribution with an income cutoff, and a 1999 top‑bracket tax deduction reduction for capital pensions. The institutional setting includes tax‑favored capital and annuity pension accounts, collective‑bargained employer contributions, and third‑party reported flows into retirement and taxable accounts.

difficulty.justification: Intermediate policy and institutional knowledge is needed; rich administrative data and variation from job switches and an income-cutoff MSP aid identification. However, plausible offsetting (crowding-out, account substitution), coincident tax reform, and some selection/anticipation issues mean the sign is not trivial and requires careful design.

triplet_key: 23810|increase in prenatal medicaid eligibility|second generation average birthweight
context: This paper studies U.S. cohorts born 1975–1988 (first generation) and their children born 1990–2017 (second generation), exploiting 1980s state-level expansions in prenatal Medicaid eligibility. Using restricted Vital Statistics natality files linked to mothers’ state and year of birth and simulated eligibility from CPS and state Medicaid rules collected back to 1975, the authors compare individuals across U.S. states and cohorts (by state/year of birth) to estimate how in utero access to Medicaid affected infant health across generations.

difficulty.justification: Establishing a positive intergenerational effect of prenatal Medicaid requires specialized knowledge of Medicaid rules and life-course health mechanisms; the sign depends on state/cohort context, selection and migration, and competing channels (composition, fertility responses), and empirical threats (measurement error, omitted policies) make identification moderately-to-highly challenging.

triplet_key: 18993|tax on incumbents' continued operation|skilled labor reallocated to r&d
context: This paper combines a structural quality‑ladder model with firm‑level estimation using US microdata (1987–1997). It studies continuously‑innovative US manufacturing firms and product lines, using Census LBD and CMF, NSF RAD R&D surveys and NBER patent data, focusing on skilled‑labor allocation, firm entry/exit, R&D intensities and selection between high/low innovative types. The setting considers industrial policies (R&D subsidies, taxes/subsidies on incumbent operations) and evaluates their counterfactual impacts on reallocation, innovation and welfare.

difficulty.justification: Answering whether taxing incumbents increases skilled labor in R&D requires specialized knowledge of quality‑ladder growth models and firm dynamics, and the sign depends on institutions (labor mobility, incumbents' R&D behavior) and GE feedbacks. Theoretical channels point both ways (incumbent contraction freeing skilled labor vs. reduced incumbent R&D demand), so identification relies on a rich structural model and strong empirical support to rule out competing explanations.

context: This paper studies first-time U.S. consumer bankruptcy filers who filed between 1992 and 2005 (analysis draws on court dockets 1992–2009 but requires 5+ years post‑filing), linking individual Chapter 13 filings across federal bankruptcy offices to Social Security Administration tax records and county-level DataQuick foreclosure data. The setting exploits blind rotation judge assignment within bankruptcy offices (pre‑2005 reform) that produces cross‑judge variation in confirmation rates; outcomes analyzed at the individual level include earnings, employment, mortality, and home foreclosure over the subsequent five years.

difficulty.justification: Intermediate knowledge of bankruptcy law and labor outcomes is needed; the judge‑assignment design gives credible quasi‑random variation but identification hinges on exclusion (judge affects earnings only via confirmation) and heterogeneous effects across contexts, so sign is plausible but not trivial to pin down.

context: This paper studies U.S. over-the-counter (OTC) equity markets from January 2001 through October 2010 using a panel of ≈10,583 domestic firms (firm-months ≈955,716). It exploits variation across OTC trading venues (NASDAQ Bulletin Board, OTC Pink tiers, Grey Market), firms’ SEC filing status, inclusion in securities manuals, and state “blue sky” merit-review regimes — including several Pink Sheets regulatory changes — to estimate effects on market quality (liquidity and crash risk). Identification leverages within-firm venue and reporting-status transitions, regulatory reforms, and cross-state institutional differences.

difficulty.justification: Evaluating whether stricter OTC regulation raises liquidity requires specialized market-microstructure and regulatory knowledge, and the sign depends on institutional details and enforcement; multiple opposing channels (transparency improves liquidity vs. compliance costs and delistings reduce it) and selection issues make the sign nontrivial, though within-firm transitions and cross-state reforms provide reasonably strong identifying variation.

triplet_key: 25108|2016 presidential election outcome|allocation to safe assets
context: Using anonymized account-level data from a large U.S. financial institution, the paper studies how the unexpected 2016 U.S. presidential election affected portfolio allocations. It examines retirement investors (ages 25–64) across U.S. zip codes and counties over Oct 2015–Oct 2017, linking household portfolios to zip-level political affiliation (campaign donations and vote shares). The setting observes security-level holdings, employer and county identifiers, and retirement-account behavior, treating the 2016 election as a public shock to beliefs while controlling for local and employer economic exposure.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate political-economy and portfolio-choice knowledge; the sign varies by investor type, local exposure, and expectations, and is confounded by contemporaneous market moves, selection and heterogeneous treatment effects. Rich account-level data and controls help, but multiple channels and remaining confounders make clean identification challenging.

context: This paper studies the causal impact of expanding Medicaid via Oregon’s 2008 Medicaid lottery. From Jan–Sep 2008 the state randomly selected low-income adults (ages 19–64) on a waiting list for the opportunity to apply for OHP Standard; outcomes are measured through Sept 2009 (~16 months). The units are uninsured low-income adults/households (below ~100% FPL) who signed up for the lottery. The institutional setting includes Medicaid eligibility rules (citizens/legal immigrants, six months uninsured, income/assets limits) and uses administrative and survey data.

difficulty.justification: A randomized lottery greatly eases identification so moderate domain knowledge suffices, but perceived quality is subjective and sensitive to local access, expectations, and short follow-up, creating some plausible alternative mechanisms and moderate contextual dependence.

triplet_key: 20907|bundling with large urban region|benchmark silver plan premium
context: This paper analyzes the first year of Affordable Care Act (ACA) individual-market exchanges (2013–2014 open-enrollment) in 33 U.S. states using the federal healthcare.gov platform, covering 2,388 counties aggregated into 398 rating regions. The authors use county- and region-level observations to examine how state-determined coverage-region definitions affected insurer entry and benchmark (second-lowest silver) premiums, focusing on small/rural counties bundled with nearby urban areas and exploiting cross- and within-state variation in rating-region boundaries while controlling for observable cost and demand covariates.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether bundling small/rural counties with large urban areas lowers benchmark premiums requires intermediate policy and market knowledge and is highly context-dependent (local risk mixes, costs, and insurer responses). Multiple equilibrium channels (risk pooling, selection, entry/exit) make the sign plausible but confounded, and while within-state variation helps identification, remaining unobservables and market feedbacks leave moderate uncertainty.

context: Using two survey waves in New Zealand (first wave June–Oct 2021, follow-up Nov 2021–Feb 2022), the authors survey ~4,000 firms (about 2,000 reinterviewed) employing ≥6 workers. They randomized information treatments that provided professional forecasters' mean GDP forecasts and/or the spread between optimistic and pessimistic forecasts to exogenously shift firms' macroeconomic first- and second-moment beliefs during the pandemic period. Outcomes include firms' planned and realized prices, employment, investment, sales, advertising, and decisions to adopt technologies or open facilities.

difficulty.justification: Wage responses to higher macro uncertainty can run in multiple directions (cuts/hires freezes, precautionary retention premia, or positive composition effects) and depend heavily on institutional features, contract rigidity, and short-run selection; the randomized belief shocks help identification but the short follow‑up and contractual/composition channels make the sign ambiguous without detailed heterogeneity and longer-term data.

context: This paper studies whether forecasters underestimated fiscal multipliers by relating two-year real GDP growth forecast errors to planned changes in the structural fiscal balance across countries. The baseline is April 2010 IMF WEO forecasts for 26 European advanced economies, with extensions to all advanced and emerging economies and panel vintages covering 2009–2012 (and 1997–2008 as precrisis). Unit of observation is the national economy over two-year intervals. The setting is the post‑2008 financial crisis, featuring large multiyear consolidation plans, liquidity‑trap/zero‑lower‑bound conditions, and elevated sovereign and banking stress.

difficulty.justification: Identifying that planned fiscal consolidation causes positive unemployment forecast errors requires intermediate macro/labor knowledge but is highly context-dependent (ZLB, sovereign/banking stress) and subject to strong confounding/endogeneity and multiple feedbacks (monetary offset, spillovers, selection), so empirical evidence is informative but far from definitive.

triplet_key: 8521|fall in value of political connections|market value of politically connected firms
treatment: Fall in value of political connections
outcome: Market value of politically connected firms
context: Analyzes how the 1997–98 Asian financial crisis and Malaysia’s September 1998 capital controls affected market valuations of 424 Malaysian listed firms. Using Gomez and Jomo’s coding of political ties (Mahathir- and Anwar-connected firms), the paper examines July 1997–August 1998 (crisis) and the September 1998 policy/political shock (capital controls and Anwar’s dismissal), exploiting variation in political connections, Bumiputera status, and prior foreign listings to infer changes in expected subsidies and access to international finance.

difficulty.justification: Identifying this sign requires specialized institutional knowledge of Malaysian politics and crisis-era capital controls and is highly context-dependent; multiple confounding channels (subsidies, access to foreign finance, investor sentiment) and plausible opposing mechanisms make causal inference challenging even with an event-study design.

context: This paper studies U.S. equity returns and German government “twin” bonds amid rising environmental concerns. For stocks it analyzes firm- and portfolio-level monthly data (MSCI environmental scores merged to CRSP/I/B/E/S) from November 2012–December 2020, exploiting variation across firms and industries in greenness and shocks to climate concerns (Ardia et al. media index) and earnings. It also examines German 10-year twin bonds (Sept 2020–Nov 2021) to illustrate yield/price responses to growing climate tastes. Units are individual stocks, value-weighted portfolios, and sovereign bonds.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate asset-pricing/ESG knowledge; the sign depends on institutional setting and timing (investor composition, policy and sectoral shocks); confounding with correlated news, firm characteristics and flow effects makes the opposite sign plausible; multiple channels (preference shifts, flows, risk premia, composition) complicate identification, and while the cross-sectional/monthly variation helps, endogeneity and measurement limit certainty.

triplet_key: 26009|including forward cip return in sdf|ability to price cross-section of assets
treatment: Including forward CIP return in SDF
outcome: Ability to price cross-section of assets
context: This paper analyzes daily FX, interest‑rate swap, and forward data for seven major currencies (AUD, CAD, CHF, EUR, GBP, JPY, USD) from January 2003–August 2018, with emphasis on the post‑GFC period (2010–2018). Units are currency‑pair cross‑currency bases, forward‑CIP trading strategies, and portfolios, alongside other near‑arbitrages and intermediary‑wealth proxies. The institutional setting is global FX and interest‑rate derivatives markets under post‑GFC regulatory leverage constraints on financial intermediaries (quarter‑end effects, OIS vs IBOR conventions), used to test whether shocks to CIP violations are priced.

difficulty.justification: Establishing that adding forward CIP returns to an SDF improves cross‑section pricing requires specialized asset‑pricing and FX market knowledge and is sensitive to post‑GFC institutional details; it is plausibly confounded by arbitrage/friction, measurement, and intermediary balance‑sheet channels and demands addressing GE feedbacks, so daily panel evidence helps but identification remains nontrivial.

context: This paper analyzes a village-level randomized rollout (2004–05) of Mexico’s Programa de Apoyo Alimentario (PAL) across ~194 poor rural villages in eight southern states, comparing monthly in-kind food boxes (≈206 pesos) versus cash transfers (≈150 pesos) versus control. The study uses store-level price surveys (2003 baseline, 2005 follow-up), household consumption and production data, and exploits high program eligibility (~89%), government-procured trucked-in supplies and variation in village remoteness and Diconsa store presence.

difficulty.justification: Randomized village rollout plus price and production data gives fairly strong identification, but the sign is context-dependent (market integration, remoteness, presence of Diconsa) and multiple channels and local general-equilibrium price effects mean opposite or offsetting effects are plausible without careful analysis.

context: The paper analyzes U.S. mortgage origination and servicing data—primarily HMDA, Ginnie Mae/FHA loan data, and servicing records—covering roughly 2010–2016 (with some series through 2017). It compares online “FinTech” nonbank originators to traditional banks and nonbanks across loans, borrowers, lenders, counties, MSAs and census tracts in the post-2008, securitized mortgage environment, focusing on application-to-origination processing times, FHA default outcomes, lenders’ supply responses to aggregate refinancing demand shocks, and local refinancing behavior.

difficulty.justification: Identifying whether FinTech originations reduce FHA mortgage defaults requires intermediate-to-advanced market and mortgage knowledge because multiple offsetting channels (better screening/servicing vs easier access to riskier borrowers) and strong selection/composition issues are present; rich HMDA and servicing data help but do not eliminate endogeneity and context-specific institutional effects in the post‑2008 securitized environment.

context: Analyzes summer 1999 (June–Sept) hourly wholesale electricity markets in three restructured U.S. regions—California, New England, and PJM—using firm-hour observations of generation, retail obligations, and prices. Context: early post-restructuring regime with retail rate freezes and varied vertical arrangements (divestiture vs long-term contracts vs retained generation), ISOs operating spot markets, and price caps; authors reconstruct firm cost functions and simulate Cournot and competitive equilibria to estimate impact of vertical commitments on wholesale prices.

difficulty.justification: Identifying whether long-term vertical commitments reduce wholesale prices in 1999 electricity markets requires specialized IO and electricity-market knowledge and careful counterfactual modeling; the sign is sensitive to institutional details (price caps, retail freezes, peak demand, capacity constraints) and strategic withholding, and empirical inference depends heavily on reconstructed costs and equilibrium assumptions so opposite signs are plausible without strong model support.

context: This paper studies local economic and political effects of NAFTA’s tariff reductions around its 1993–1994 passage, using U.S. county and commuting-zone annual data from the mid-1980s through the early 2000s and individual survey/panel data (ANES repeated cross-sections and 1992–1994 panel, PSID). It exploits cross-sectional variation in 1990 industry employment shares, Mexico’s revealed comparative advantage, and 1990 tariff protection to measure county-level NAFTA exposure and estimates impacts on employment, migration, transfer-program take-up, mortality, and voting/party identification.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate trade and labor-market knowledge; the negative sign is plausible for import-exposed counties but depends strongly on local industry mix, offsetting export/spillover effects, migration and concurrent shocks (technology, other policies), making identification moderately difficult despite the paper's thoughtful exposure measure.

context: Focuses on Western European and other developed-country sovereigns during 2007–2010; for this triplet the unit is the country: pre-bailout financial sector distress is measured as the average bank CDS on September 22, 2008, and the outcome is post-bailout sovereign CDS measured on March 31, 2010. The analysis uses country-level cross-sectional regressions of log sovereign CDS on pre-bailout average bank CDS and pre-bailout debt-to-GDP within the institutional setting of the Fall 2008 public bank bailouts (beginning with Ireland). Data include CDS spreads, equity returns, and government debt ratios.

difficulty.justification: Temporal ordering helps, but identifying a causal + effect from pre-bailout bank distress on later sovereign CDS in cross-country OLS is made difficult by common global shocks, omitted confounders, selection into bailouts, heterogeneous institutional responses and small sample size, so specialized identification and institutional knowledge are needed.

context: This paper studies the causal effects of private equity (PE) ownership on U.S. for-profit nursing homes and Medicare short-stay patients using facility-level data (2000–2017) and patient-level Medicare/MDS claims (patient first stays 2005–2016). It matches 128 PE buyout deals (2004–2015) to ~1,674 facilities and 4.2 million Medicare patients, exploiting institutional features—Medicare/Medicaid per-diem payments, CMS Five Star ratings, and high government subsidy—to examine outcomes (90-day mortality, spending, staffing, quality) in the U.S. long-term care sector.

difficulty.justification: Moderately high difficulty: PE could plausibly raise billed amounts via higher treatment intensity, upcoding, or longer/targeted stays but could also lower billing through cost-cutting or patient selection; selection into buyouts, coding changes, and case-mix shifts create confounding. The paper's matched buyout design and rich Medicare/MDS data help identification but do not fully eliminate concerns about unobserved facility dynamics and incentive-driven measurement changes.

context: This paper uses a two-year (2010–2011) randomized field experiment in Jiangxi province, China, with ~3,500 rice-farming households across 134 villages to study how subsidy policies affect demand for a newly introduced area-index weather insurance. The product (actuarially fair premium ≈12 RMB/mu) faced randomized village-level first-year subsidies (free vs 70% subsidized) and household-level varied second-year prices (40–90% subsidies). The setting included large first-year payouts (~59% of insured) and rich social-network data; results are validated with administrative data and multi-year simulations (2010–2014).

difficulty.justification: The randomized design and administrative validation provide strong causal leverage, so domain knowledge needed is moderate and evidence is fairly sufficient. However, the sign can hinge on context (payout frequency, social learning), multiple mediating channels and spillovers, and measurement of ‘‘attention’’—making inference nontrivial and moderately difficult to generalize.

triplet_key: 25346|short-sale constraint (constrained past winner)|negative abnormal stock returns (years 0–5)
context: Using U.S. equity data (CRSP/Compustat/IBES and Markit lending fees) from May 1980–June 2020 (with lending-fee data from Aug 2004), the paper studies firm-level stocks listed on NYSE/AMEX/NASDAQ. Units are individual firms/stocks grouped into monthly value-weighted portfolios sorted by past 12-month returns (skip last month), institutional ownership (IOR), and short-interest (SIR) to identify short-sale constrained “winners” and “losers.” The analysis exploits securities-lending markets, analyst forecast dispersion, and earnings-announcement timing to infer how disagreement and investor beliefs evolve after large price/info shocks.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a negative causal effect of short-sale constraints on subsequent abnormal returns requires substantial market-structure and securities‑lending knowledge and is sensitive to institutional/time variation; multiple plausible confounders (risk, momentum, sentiment, selection into lending markets) and dynamic feedbacks make the sign ambiguous without strong identification, and available lending-fee data/ proxies (esp. pre-2004) leave nontrivial evidence gaps.

context: This empirical study examines how Celera’s short-term intellectual property on human genes affected subsequent scientific research and product development. It uses gene-level observations (≈27,882 genes; ≈1,682 initially held by Celera) linked to publications, genotype–phenotype links, and diagnostic-test availability. Identification exploits timing variation in sequencing and public re-sequencing around 1999–2003 (outcomes observed through 2009) in the Human Genome Project vs. Celera institutional setting, where Celera used contract-based IP that lapsed once public resequencing occurred.

difficulty.justification: Identification requires specialized knowledge of genomic IP and diagnostic development; the result hinges on Celera’s unique institutional timing, is vulnerable to selection/confounding on which genes were sequenced when, and needs careful causal design (the study’s timing variation helps but does not eliminate all threats).

triplet_key: 25151|expropriation intensity under the land reform|islamist vigilante incidents and casualties
treatment: Expropriation intensity under the land reform
context: This paper studies Indonesia's 1960 Basic Agrarian Law (BAL) during the early 1960s, exploiting district-level population-density cutoffs that made 5–9 ha holdings expropriable. Rural landowners transferred land into waqf (inalienable Islamic endowments). Using district- and village-level data (mosques, pesantren, waqf land, electoral returns, surveys) across 1960–2014, the authors analyze long-run effects on Islamic institutions, Islamist voting, local sharia regulations, and agricultural productivity.

difficulty.justification: Establishing that expropriation intensity causally increases Islamist vigilante violence requires specialized historical and institutional knowledge (waqf, local Islam) and is highly context-dependent; multiple plausible confounders and long-run mediating/feedback channels make the sign ambiguous without careful identification, and evidence on rare violent incidents over decades is challenging though not impossible to assemble.

context: This paper studies U.S. commercial banks during the Great Depression (1929–1933), using weekly and quarterly call-report, suspension, and reporting-bank data across Federal Reserve districts and major money centers (New York, Chicago). Units are banks (country, reserve-city, central reserve-city) and district aggregates. The institutional setting is the Federal Reserve System and the correspondent interbank deposit pyramid; analyses exploit episodes of banking panics, spatial clusters of bank suspensions, and policy variation in discount rates to estimate how interbank deposit withdrawals forced asset rebalancing and reduced bank lending.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a negative effect of interbank networks on aggregate lending in the 1929–33 U.S. context requires specialized banking and historical institutional knowledge, is highly context-dependent (panic episodes, correspondent relations, Fed policy), and is susceptible to strong confounders and reverse causality; the causal chain involves complex network propagation, liquidity hoarding and asset-rebalancing with general-equilibrium feedbacks, and while the paper’s event variation helps, remaining identification challenges make the question hard to settle.

context: This paper studies welfare effects of personalized pricing using two large randomized pricing field experiments run with ZipRecruiter in 2015 (Aug–Sept and Oct–Nov). Units are prospective new employers (primarily small “starter” firms) encountering a paywall for a monthly subscription on the ZipRecruiter platform in the U.S. (with some Canadian observations). The authors combine randomized price assignment with rich self-reported registration features (133 dummies), estimate heterogeneous logit demand, and evaluate firm profits and consumer surplus in the context of data-privacy policy debates (GDPR/CCPA).

difficulty.justification: Although randomized price variation and rich covariates give strong internal identification, recovering the net effect of more granular segmentation on total consumer surplus requires substantial microeconomic knowledge, structural demand modeling and counterfactual pricing assumptions; effects are highly context-dependent and alternative signs are plausibly supported without strong model assumptions.

triplet_key: 18535|food stamp program exposure in early childhood|obesity
treatment: Food Stamp Program exposure in early childhood
context: This paper studies long-run effects of childhood exposure to the U.S. Food Stamp Program, exploiting county-by-county rollout from 1961–1975.
