context: This paper studies Sweden’s 2007–2009 employer payroll‑tax reform that halved employer payroll taxes for young birth‑cohorts (eligibility up to age 26) using population administrative employer–employee records and firm accounting data spanning the 1990s–2013. Exploiting cohort eligibility rules, timing of the two reform steps, and cross‑firm variation in pre‑reform shares of young workers, it examines causal effects on individual net/gross wages, employment and transition rates, and firm outcomes (employment, sales, capital, profits); take‑up was automatic via payroll software.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate knowledge of tax incidence and wage-setting institutions; the sign depends on institutional context and composition effects (firms may hire more low‑paid young workers, lowering averages) so opposite effects are plausible, but the reform's sharp eligibility rules and rich administrative data give reasonably strong identification.

triplet_key: 15180|restrictions on bank activities|bank returns following lehman
context: This paper analyzes cross-country variation in buy-and-hold stock returns for 98 large deposit-taking banks (assets >$50bn) across about 20 countries during the 2007–2008 global credit crisis (main window July 2007–Dec 2008 and the Lehman month Sep 12–Oct 10, 2008). It links pre-crisis (2006) bank-level governance (RiskMetrics/CGQ), ownership, balance-sheet and profitability metrics (Tier 1, deposits, loans, liquidity), and country-level regulation and institutions (Caprio/Barth/Levine indices, Kaufmann governance) to differential bank performance.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate knowledge of bank regulation and crisis dynamics; the sign is highly context-dependent and confounded by correlated bank and country characteristics. Multiple channels (reduced risk-taking vs. limited diversification, regulatory forbearance, cross-border exposure) can push returns in opposite directions, so observational cross-country evidence needs strong identification to be convincing.

context: Model-based counterfactual for the US system of metropolitan areas (1999 county-based definitions, ≈275 cities). Using their calibrated urban-growth model, the authors simulate relaxing local planning regulations in the three most productive metropolitan areas; this policy counterfactual reallocates population away from low-productivity locations and yields estimated aggregate real gains of about 8% for the US economy.

difficulty.justification: Determining that relaxing local planning in a few top metros increases aggregate real output requires specialized urban/GE theory and careful identification because results hinge on calibration of supply elasticities, migration frictions, agglomeration/congestion externalities and local institutions; alternative parameterizations or omitted channels (amenity loss, commuting costs, political responses) can plausibly change magnitude or even sign, so model-based evidence is informative but not definitive.

context: Analyzes interwar Britain (primarily 1918–1938) at the national (UK) level, using quarterly aggregates constructed from annual Budget Financial Statements and Hansard to collect nearly 300 discrete tax legislations and classify 140 as exogenous. Units are macro quarterly outcomes (real GDP, unemployment, prices, Bank Rate) and tax changes measured as percent of GDP. The institutional setting features high post‑WWI debt, Gold Standard debates, budgetary orthodoxy, Chancellor Budget motives, and episodic rearmament that shape tax policy timing.

difficulty.justification: Interwar Britain requires substantial specialized knowledge (gold‑standard constraints, budget orthodoxy, rearmament timing) and the sign is highly context‑dependent; tax changes are plausibly endogenous and work through multiple fiscal–monetary and expectation channels, though the authors' careful exogeneity coding gives moderate empirical traction.

triplet_key: 19007|individual assessment of tax credits|employment of mothers
context: This triplet is based on a structural counterfactual simulation in the paper’s dynamic lifecycle model for UK women (BHPS cohorts, 1991–2008). The reform simulated changes assessment of tax credits from family-level (joint) to individual (single-filing). The result concerns individual women/households—particularly mothers of young children—and finds individual assessment reduces mothers’ employment (relative to joint assessment) once eligibility later ceases. The simulation is conditional on observed family-formation processes and the UK tax-credit, childcare and benefit rules (eligibility hours, tapering, etc.).

difficulty.justification: Assessing this sign requires specialized lifecycle and household bargaining knowledge and is tightly tied to UK tax-credit rules, cohort behavior and timing; multiple competing channels (eligibility, hours rules, selection into motherhood/partnership, dynamic responses) make opposite outcomes plausible and identification relies on strong structural assumptions.

context: This paper reports a large-scale randomized controlled trial in Tanzania (2013–2014) across a nationally representative sample of 350 public primary schools in 10 districts, covering over 120,000 students in grades 1–3. It evaluates causal effects of two interventions — unconditional capitation grants (TZS 10,000 per student) and teacher performance pay tied to externally administered tests — implemented by Twaweza against a background of limited school resources, large class sizes, and prevalent teacher absence.

difficulty.justification: Understanding incentive effects on learning requires intermediate policy/education knowledge; effects are highly context-dependent and there are many offsetting channels (effort, teaching-to-test, cheating, compositional changes), so the sign is ambiguous despite a large RCT which substantially improves evidence sufficiency.

context: The paper studies U.S. NYSE-listed railroads (with industrial firms as a comparison group) over 1905–1929, exploiting the implementation of Section 10 of the Clayton Antitrust Act (passed 1914, enforced Jan 1, 1921). In this early-20th-century U.S. setting, railroads relied on bond finance and securities underwriters often held board seats; the law exogenously disrupted underwriter–board relationships and thus delegated monitoring, allowing the authors to examine effects on firm value, investment, leverage, and borrowing costs.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate knowledge of corporate finance and early-20th-century institutional arrangements; the sign depends heavily on historical institutional details and there are multiple opposing channels (monitoring loss vs. reduced conflicts-of-interest) and potential concurrent reforms, though the law provides plausible exogenous variation.

triplet_key: 19796|adjacency to shale gas well pads|piped-water-dependent property values
treatment: Adjacency to shale gas well pads
context: Using property transaction data from January 1995–April 2012, this paper studies how shale gas development (Marcellus formation) in Pennsylvania — with bordering New York counties under a fracking moratorium — affected housing prices. The unit of observation is individual property transactions (repeat-sale and hedonic samples). Analyses contrast homes on private groundwater versus public piped-water service areas to isolate perceived groundwater contamination risk, adjacency disamenities, and broader vicinity (boom-town) effects, leveraging variation in well permits, drilled well pads, production, and visibility.

difficulty.justification: Understanding this sign requires intermediate housing-market and environmental-risk knowledge; the sign varies with local institutions (water infrastructure, visibility, regulations) and is plausibly reversed in other settings. Multiple confounding channels (boom effects, adjacency disamenities, selection into piped-water areas) make causal attribution nontrivial, though the paper's repeat‑sale/hedonic design and cross-border variation provide reasonably informative but not definitive evidence.

context: Using de-identified JPMorgan Chase monthly bank-account data from January 2014–June 2016 across 20 U.S. states, the paper studies roughly 182,000 households who received unemployment insurance (UI) by direct deposit. It exploits variation in UI generosity, timing, and duration (most states six months; e.g., Florida four months, New Jersey unusually generous and no waiting period) to trace high-frequency household income, nondurable spending categories, and job‑finding hazards around UI receipt and predictable benefit exhaustion.

difficulty.justification: Policy variation and high-frequency bank data give a strong identifying strategy, but the sign is sensitive to context (state rules, insurance/healthcare access) and confounded by reemployment, selection/attrition, anticipation, and health shocks—multiple channels and heterogeneity make precise causal attribution moderately difficult.

context: This paper uses affiliate-level BEA data on U.S. multinational firms from 1982–1997 (with detailed benchmark years 1982, 1989, 1994) across many host countries to study how country-level capital-account restrictions (IMF and a firm-focused Shatz measure) and subsequent liberalizations affect firm outcomes. Units are foreign affiliates of U.S. parents operating under institutional regimes that constrain profit repatriation, capital flows, and local borrowing; the paper links these policies to interest costs, transfer-pricing, dividend remittances, and affiliate size.

difficulty.justification: Multiple firm-level channels (transfer pricing, retained earnings, local borrowing costs, dividend remittances) and policy endogeneity make the net sign context-dependent and confoundable; affiliate-level panel data helps identification but does not fully eliminate alternative mechanisms or selection, so establishing the negative effect is moderately difficult.

context: Analyzes U.S. quarterly macrodata on real per‑capita GDP, government purchases (consumption+investment), and net taxes mainly over 1960:Q1–1997:Q4 (with extensions back to 1947), using institutional details of the U.S. tax/transfer system and tax‑collection timing. Identifies effects of discretionary and automatic fiscal shocks (including the 1975:Q2 tax rebate and the 1950–52 Korean War buildup). Units are national quarterly aggregates; setting is U.S. fiscal policy and macroeconomic responses.

difficulty.justification: Intermediate policy knowledge is needed (fiscal timing, taxes, rebates) and the Keynesian prediction is for a positive sign, but monetary responses, financing, openness, and endogenous policy create multiple channels and confounding. Using institutional/narrative identification and event examples improves identification, so inference is plausible but still moderately challenging.

context: This paper studies the U.S. housing bust during the Great Recession (roughly 2006–2013), using national time series and CBSA-level data (99 of the 100 largest metropolitan areas) from CoreLogic. Units include owner-occupied homes, REO sales, and metropolitan-area aggregates of prices, sales, LTV distributions and foreclosures. The institutional setting features large foreclosure waves, lender capital constraints, mortgage pre-approval rules and foreclosure-related credit flags; the authors combine cross-sectional regressions with a calibrated dynamic search model and counterfactual policy simulations.

difficulty.justification: Identifying whether delaying foreclosure completions worsens downturns requires substantial theoretical knowledge about mortgage markets, lender balance sheets and search/price dynamics, and the effect is highly sensitive to institutional details (foreclosure law, lender constraints, credit screening). Multiple confounding channels (selection, liquidity, zombie-home effects) and GE feedbacks make causal inference hard, though the paper's cross-sectional variation plus a calibrated dynamic model meaningfully improves evidence.

context: This triplet refers to a modelled counterfactual in the UK: using the dynamic life-cycle model estimated on BHPS data (1991–2008) together with a tax-benefit microsimulation, the authors simulate assessing tax credits at the individual (single-filing) rather than family level. The counterfactual focuses on women—especially mothers of young children—within the UK tax-credit, childcare, housing-benefit and income-tax institutional environment (eligibility rules/hours thresholds) and evaluates impacts on savings, experience, wages, employment and overall welfare.

difficulty.justification: Correctly signing this effect requires detailed knowledge of UK tax‑credit, childcare and housing‑benefit rules and household labor‑supply/bargaining behavior; multiple channels (hours, experience, wages, savings, benefit interactions) operate and can push welfare in opposite directions depending on parameters and population. The result is highly institution‑ and population‑dependent and reasonably ambiguous without strong empirical identification or robust sensitivity analysis.

context: This paper analyzes hourly generation and demand across U.S. Power Control Areas (PCAs) and individual generating units from 1999–2012, using a nationwide panel of PCA-date-hour and unit-hour observations. It exploits the staggered creation and expansion of centralized wholesale electricity markets (market-based dispatch) under FERC reforms, comparing PCAs that switched to market dispatch to those that did not. The study measures allocative efficiency changes—out-of-merit operating costs and gains from interarea trade—holding capacities, fuel and emissions prices, and demand fixed.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a positive effect of market-based dispatch on gains from trade requires intermediate policy and electricity-market knowledge and is fairly context-dependent (market design, transmission capacity, and period matters). Staggered adoption and rich panel data help, but endogenous adoption, coincident transmission or institutional changes, heterogeneous market rules, strategic generator responses, and spatial spillovers make alternative signs or attenuated effects plausibly without careful identification.

context: Analyzes the United States, 1940–2018, linking decadal Census/ACS microdata on occupations and industry write-ins to a constructed database of new job micro‑titles and to the full text of U.S. utility patents. Units of observation are occupations and occupation×industry cells (decadal panels); key institutional features are the Census Alphabetical Index (CAI) and the Dictionary of Occupational Titles (DOT) used to map patents (via word‑embeddings) into augmentation vs. automation exposure. The paper leverages exogenous demand shocks (China import exposure, demographic shifts) and patent‑based instruments (breakthrough patents lagged two decades) to identify causal effects.

difficulty.justification: Requires specialized empirical and measurement knowledge (patent-to-occupation mapping, word embeddings, historical Census conventions) and faces strong context- and period-specific variation; multiple plausible confounders and GE/selection channels make the sign ambiguous without careful identification, and the proposed instruments help but do not fully eliminate concerns.

context: This paper examines U.S. publicly traded firms across three-digit SIC industries during 1984–2006, using Compustat-CRSP, SDC M&A records, FRED industrial production, and Census data. It analyzes firm-year R&D responses to downstream demand shocks, industry M&A activity, and a firm's probability of being an acquisition target, emphasizing differences between small and large firms, target bargaining power, and product-market competition within the U.S. corporate acquisition market.

difficulty.justification: A positive effect is plausible but not obvious: competing forces (competition spurs innovation vs squeezes profits), endogeneity of competition and target status, firm-size and bargaining heterogeneity, and measurement/selection issues mean credible identification requires specialized IO knowledge and careful quasi-experimental strategies.

triplet_key: 31553|zones of choice program|uc and csu course requirements
context: This paper studies Los Angeles Unified School District’s Zones of Choice (ZOC) reform—implemented starting around 2012–2013—using administrative data from roughly 2008–2019. It evaluates effects on high-school students (chiefly 11th graders and graduating cohorts) and market-level school outcomes across ZOC versus non-ZOC neighborhoods in Los Angeles. ZOC combined catchment areas, expanded within-district choice with centralized rank-ordered (immediate-acceptance) assignment and lotteries for oversubscription, thereby changing competitive incentives among attendance-zone schools.

difficulty.justification: Understanding ZOC effects requires intermediate knowledge of school-choice assignment and district responses; the sign depends on LA-specific implementation and population. Multiple confounding pathways (selection, sorting, peer and teacher responses) and nonrandom rollout make the sign plausible but not obvious, and administrative panel data help yet do not fully eliminate identification challenges.

context: This paper analyzes the randomized Perry Preschool Project that enrolled 123 disadvantaged African-American children born in the 1960s in Ypsilanti, Michigan. The program provided two years of high-quality preschool plus weekly teacher home visits starting at age three; controls received no treatment. The authors follow the original participants through age 54 using surveys and administrative criminal/education records and link to outcomes for their biological children into young adulthood to estimate intergenerational effects on cognition, health, education, crime, employment, and family stability.

difficulty.justification: Random assignment of children gives credible identification of parental spillovers, but the small sample, potential attrition/measurement issues, multiple mediating channels (stress, labor supply, monitoring) and limited external validity mean the negative sign is plausible but not trivial to establish decisively.

context: This paper analyzes California school districts using bond referenda from 1987–2006 (with housing transactions through 1988–2005 and multi-year fiscal and test-score panels) to estimate causal effects. The unit is the school district; the institutional setting is California’s voter-approved general obligation school bonds (initially requiring a 2/3 supermajority, later sometimes 55%), whose revenues are legally restricted to capital projects. The design exploits close elections and repeated proposals to identify impacts on district finances, housing prices, and student achievement.

difficulty.justification: A close-election, repeated-proposals design in California provides credible quasi-experimental leverage, but identifying the sign is complicated by timing lags of capital investments, legal restrictions (capital-only), housing-price driven student sorting, spillovers, and changes in voting rules — any of which can attenuate or reverse observed score changes without careful adjustment.

context: This study analyzes the long-term effects of the HighScope Perry Preschool randomized trial conducted in the mid-1960s in Ypsilanti, Michigan. The sample comprises 123 disadvantaged, low-IQ African American children enrolled at ages 3–4 and randomized to a two-year preschool program with daily classroom sessions and home visits or to a no-program control; outcomes were followed longitudinally with assessments through ages 19, 27, and 40. The institutional setting is U.S. public schooling post-intervention; analyses exploit experimental variation and longitudinal measurements of cognition and personality.

difficulty.justification: Randomized assignment and rich longitudinal follow-up give strong internal identification of a positive effect, so domain knowledge and confounding concerns are limited. However, the small, specific 1960s disadvantaged sample and setting mean the sign’s applicability to other populations/times is moderately context-dependent.

context: This paper studies 3rd–7th grade students in the Brookline (MA) public schools using ITBS scores from 1994–2000 to measure how Boston’s METCO desegregation program — which buses predominantly Black students from Boston into suburban districts — altered class composition and peer achievement. The unit of observation is individual students and grade/school/year classroom cells; the setting features voluntary METCO placements, contractual class-size caps, and administrative assignment rules used to probe peer and class-size effects.

difficulty.justification: Determining a negative effect requires intermediate-to-specialized knowledge of peer‑effects and selection in school settings because voluntary METCO placement, teacher/class assignment rules, and class‑size caps create multiple channels (selection, composition, class‑size spillovers) that can plausibly push the sign either way; administrative assignment rules improve identification but the setting and mechanisms remain context‑dependent and moderately ambiguous.

context: Using a randomized Section 8 housing voucher wait-list lottery in Chicago (applications July 1997) and administrative data through 2005, the paper studies low-income applicant households (working-age, able-bodied adults) living in private-market housing. CHAC randomly assigned waiting-list positions and offered roughly 18,110 vouchers by May 2003. The institutional setting is means-tested housing vouchers (Fair Market Rent rules) interacting with TANF, Medicaid and Food Stamps; outcomes include employment, quarterly earnings and public-assistance receipt.

difficulty.justification: Randomized wait-list assignment gives strong identification for compliers (so evidence is relatively sufficient), but the sign depends on multiple interacting channels (income/substitution effects, neighborhood/job-access, benefit interactions) and on Chicago-specific institutions, making the true effect moderately ambiguous and context-dependent.

triplet_key: 29466|politically motivated public security contract|surveillance-oriented commercial software development
context: Study of facial-recognition AI in China (2013–2020) focusing on facial‑recognition AI firms: the treatment is a “politically motivated” public‑security procurement contract, defined as a public‑security contract issued after an above‑median level of local political unrest in the prior quarter. The outcome is firm‑level commercial software production with surveillance components (monitoring/tracking). Identification compares firms’ pre/post first‑contract trajectories using event‑study / triple‑differences around receipt of the first government contract.

difficulty.justification: Assessing whether politically motivated public‑security contracts induce firms to produce surveillance‑oriented commercial software requires detailed knowledge of Chinese procurement incentives and firm strategy, is highly sensitive to local institutions and timing, and faces strong selection and alternative‑mechanism problems; event‑study / triple‑diff designs help but do not fully eliminate plausible confounders or complex feedbacks.

context: This paper studies bank CoCo (contingent convertible) issuance from January 2009 to December 2015, using 731 issues ($521bn) and focusing empirically on banks in advanced economies (top 500 bank universe, bank-months and issuance events). The institutional setting is the post‑crisis Basel III framework (AT1/T2 classification, mechanical and discretionary triggers) and evolving resolution regimes (TLAC, BRRD). The authors analyze determinants of issuance and announcement effects on issuers’ CDS spreads and equity prices.

difficulty.justification: Requires specialized knowledge of CoCo design and resolution regimes; effects depend strongly on whether conversion is voluntary or distress-driven and are highly confounded by selection, signaling, and concurrent shocks, so isolating a negative causal effect on CDS spreads is difficult despite a large event dataset.

triplet_key: 31332|equal pay act and title vii|women’s weekly wages
treatment: Equal Pay Act and Title VII
context: Analyzes U.S. labor markets from the 1950s through the 1970s with emphasis on the 1960s, leveraging the passage and implementation of the 1963 Equal Pay Act and 1964 Civil Rights Act. Uses individual-level census and CPS ASEC data (ages 25–64) and aggregated industry-occupation-state-group cells across 21 state groups to compare states with and without pre-existing equal-pay laws and jobs with larger 1960 gender wage gaps. Institutional context includes FLSA coverage, WHD versus EEOC enforcement, and staggered state law variation.

difficulty.justification: Requires solid labor‐law and labor-market knowledge (enforcement, FLSA coverage) and attention to timing/heterogeneity; concurrent social and economic trends, selection into covered jobs, and enforcement variation make the sign plausible but not automatic, so careful identification is needed.

context: A nationwide correspondence field experiment (conducted in repeated waves around 2019–2021, paused Mar–Aug 2020 for COVID) sent over 83,000 fictitious applications to entry-level job postings across U.S. counties on the web portals of 108 large employers. Applicants were randomized on racially distinctive names and other legally protected signals (sex, age, LGBTQ indicators); the primary outcome is employer contact within 30 days. The study situates results in the U.S. Title VII regulatory context, comparing federal contractors and industry/firms.

difficulty.justification: Randomized correspondence design with large nationwide sample gives strong internal validity that a distinctively Black name reduces callback rates, so causal identification is relatively easy; however results can vary by employer type, platform, and other contextual factors and names may proxy for multiple attributes (class/SES) which moderates interpretation.

triplet_key: 28029|changes in regulatory capital requirements|consumer credit risk premium
context: Analyzes U.S. credit-card asset-backed securities and issuer master trusts over 2000–2020 (monthly observations) for the ten largest card issuers, using tranche prices, portfolio yields, and charge-offs to identify the market risk premium on unsecured consumer credit. Exploits the post-2008 regulatory environment — notably the Jan 1, 2010 FAS 166/167 reconsolidation, Dodd-Frank risk-retention and Basel III capital rules — comparing pre/post-crisis dynamics and cross-sectional issuer variation in balance-sheet capital intensity to infer causal effects.

difficulty.justification: Determining the sign requires advanced knowledge of bank regulation, securitization accounting, and ABS pricing; multiple countervailing channels (funding/capital costs vs risk-taking/portfolio composition), reconsolidation/accounting changes, and macro/coincident policy shifts make the sign highly ambiguous and causally complex, and while rich issuer-level ABS data and pre/post variation help identification, endogeneity and concurrent shocks leave important inferential gaps.

context: Using U.S. NYSE trading and analyst data primarily from 1993–2002 (main analyses focus on Feb 1994–Jul 2001), the paper studies how investors (individual trades classified as small vs. large/institutional) respond to sell‑side analyst recommendations for NYSE-listed firms. Analyst affiliation is measured via recent underwriting relationships (IPOs/SEOs, co‑underwriting, bond underwriting). The institutional setting emphasizes sell‑side conflicts of interest, short‑selling constraints, and market microstructure (TAQ trade initiation), linking recommendation issuance to subsequent trade imbalances and portfolio returns.

difficulty.justification: Positive bias from underwriting affiliations is intuitive but rests on institutional incentives that vary by era and market setting; identification is confounded by selection (better firms attract underwriters), omitted variables, and timing, so credible causal inference requires nontrivial econometric strategies though suitable data often exist for this period.

context: Using commercial claims from a large insurer (covering tens of millions) over 2011–2015 across U.S. hospital emergency departments, the paper analyzes ED episodes and hospitals to study out-of-network physician billing. It exploits the entry timing of large ED outsourcing firms (EmCare, TeamHealth) into specific hospitals (2011–2015) and a 2014 New York arbitration law to estimate causal effects. Institutional context: hospitals outsource ED staffing, physicians and hospitals contract separately with insurers, producing surprise balance bills and a bargaining outside option for physicians.

difficulty.justification: Understanding the sign requires specialized knowledge of provider–insurer contracting, surprise-billing mechanics, and arbitration rules. The effect depends heavily on local market structure, insurer responses, and state law, multiple plausible countervailing mechanisms exist, and credible identification is feasible with the described policy variation but still faces selection and unobserved-trend concerns.

triplet_key: 18565|higher employer pension contribution rate|accrued wealth at retirement
context: Using Danish administrative registers (population-wide, 1995–2009), the paper exploits variation in employer-defined-contribution pension rates from job switches. For the long-run analysis underlying this triplet, it restricts to individuals whose first firm switch occurred at ages 46–54 and who reach age 60 within the sample; accrued wealth is defined as the cumulative sum of retirement and non-retirement account savings from the switch date up to age 60.

difficulty.justification: Mechanically higher employer DC contributions directly raise pension-account balances, but behavioral offsets (crowding out of private saving, wage compensation) and selection on who switches jobs complicate identification. Rich Danish administrative registers and using job‑switch variation make identifying a positive sign plausible but not trivial.

context: Using Austrian social security administrative records for involuntary job losses from 1981–2001, the paper analyzes about 92,969 individuals aged 20–50 to study exit hazards around unemployment insurance (UI) benefit exhaustion. Austria’s UI rules provide 20 or 30 weeks of benefits depending on months worked in the prior five years (36-month threshold), alongside severance pay rules and means-tested post-exhaustion assistance. The analysis contrasts registered-unemployment duration and time-to-next-job (including recalls) to assess whether spikes at benefit expiration reflect true re-employment or exits from the register.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a positive effect requires intermediate policy and econometric knowledge; the sign depends importantly on Austrian institutions (severance, means-tested aid, recall rules) and measurement (register exits vs true re‑employment). Multiple plausible confounders and dynamic/selection issues raise complexity, though rich administrative data and comparing registered-unemployment versus time-to-next-job provide reasonably good empirical leverage.

context: This paper studies long-term causal effects of Boston Public Schools' universal K1 preschool on educational and behavioral outcomes for first-time preschool applicants (four-year-olds) who applied between 1997 and 2003 in Boston, MA. Using randomized school-assignment tie-break lotteries within BPS, linked to Massachusetts DESE administrative records and National Student Clearinghouse data with ~20 years of follow-up, the study evaluates impacts on college enrollment, SAT-taking, MCAS scores, high-school graduation, and disciplinary outcomes for a largely low-income, nonwhite urban cohort.

difficulty.justification: The randomized school-assignment lotteries provide strong internal identification which reduces confounding, but long-run effects on college graduation require reasoning about mediation, attrition, heterogeneous LATEs, spillovers and external validity; these make a definitive null somewhat harder to establish despite good administrative follow-up.

context: This triplet concerns the Gavin→UP takeover in Boston: Gavin middle‑school students were grandfathered into UP Academy in Fall 2011. The analysis focuses on student‑level outcomes for middle‑school cohorts (grades 6–8) using MCAS ELA scores from Spring 2012–2014 and administrative enrollment and demographic records; grandfathering guaranteed legacy students seats in UP and is the source of exogenous variation exploited for the estimated effects on ELA.

difficulty.justification: Grandfathering in a school takeover creates plausibly exogenous variation, so intermediate domain knowledge suffices, but identifying a clean positive effect requires accounting for selection/attrition, peer and teacher changes, and possible spillovers. Administrative MCAS and enrollment data are helpful but not definitive without careful design checks, so the task is moderately difficult.

triplet_key: 19796|adjacency to shale gas well pads|groundwater-dependent property values
treatment: Adjacency to shale gas well pads
context: Using property transaction data from January 1995–April 2012, this paper studies how shale gas development (Marcellus formation) in Pennsylvania — with bordering New York counties under a fracking moratorium — affected housing prices. The unit of observation is individual property transactions (repeat-sale and hedonic samples). Analyses contrast homes on private groundwater versus public piped-water service areas to isolate perceived groundwater contamination risk, adjacency disamenities, and broader vicinity (boom-town) effects, leveraging variation in well permits, drilled well pads, production, and visibility.

difficulty.justification: Moderate domain knowledge (housing markets and resource development) suffices, but the sign depends on local institutions, hydrogeology, and water-service types. Multiple channels (perceived/actual contamination, disamenity, boom-town demand, selection) make the sign plausibly reversed absent careful identification; the transaction data and contrasts (private vs piped water, permit/production timing, visibility) help identification but do not fully eliminate endogeneity and perception measurement concerns.

context: This paper analyzes U.S. microdata—pooled NHIS (1992–2001) and BRFSS (1998–2002) surveys, state hospital discharge records (California and Florida, 1995–1999/2000), and national death files—to study individuals and hospital admissions around the Medicare eligibility cutoff. Exploiting the institutional rule that most people become eligible for Medicare at age 65 (Part A/B availability subject to work-history rules), the authors estimate how the near-universal jump in coverage at 65 affects access, utilization, procedures, and self-reported and mortality outcomes for people just below and above 65.

difficulty.justification: A sharp institutional cutoff at age 65 and rich linked datasets make identification plausible (RD design), so moderate difficulty; however, institutional details (Medicare parts, prior coverage, provider responses) and coincident age-related health changes mean context matters and some alternative explanations must be addressed.

context: This triplet analyzes U.S. mortgage loan applications (accepted and rejected) over 2009–2015 using HMDA as the primary loan‑level source. For the accept/reject analysis the sample is limited to conventional, conforming applications (used as a proxy for GSE‑eligible loans) and pools applied‑for maturities (15, 20, and 30 years) because HMDA lacks maturity and GSE flags. Units are individual mortgage applications across lenders (FinTech vs. face‑to‑face); the paper models rejection with linear probability specifications controlling for HMDA observables and census‑tract proxies for GSE underwriting variables.

difficulty.justification: Determining a causal effect of borrower race on mortgage rejection requires specialized identification because HMDA (2009–2015) omits key underwriting variables (credit score, precise LTV/DTI) and applicants are nonrandomly selected; lender heterogeneity and multiple mediating channels (creditworthiness, neighborhood, discrimination, selection) make the sign plausible but easily confounded, so the data described are unlikely to be sufficient for a credible causal claim without stronger quasi‑experimental or audit evidence.

context: This paper studies earnings, wages, hours, employment and job-to-job mobility for U.S. male household heads using the Panel Study of Income Dynamics (PSID) waves 1975–1997 (calendar years 1975–1996, with usable wage observations 1978–1996). The unit is individual-year observations (men age 18–62, non‑self‑employed), with annual survey measures of employment at the survey date, hourly wages, annual hours, tenure and unemployment. The setting is the U.S. labor market across cohorts and careers; the authors model dynamics (tenure, experience, job‑specific shocks) under assumptions about measurement error and unobserved heterogeneity.

difficulty.justification: Establishing a negative effect of unemployment shocks on hourly wages requires intermediate-to-advanced labor-economics knowledge (search/matching, human capital, tenure effects) and is context-dependent; selection, composition, duration and measurement issues make the sign moderately ambiguous and identification highly complex despite useful panel data (PSID) — evidence is informative but not definitively sufficient without strong dynamic/selection controls.

context: Using 2007–2010 U.S. Medicare data, the paper studies individual Medicare beneficiaries’ choices and insurers’ pricing across 34 Part D geographic markets (plan-level and 20% sample claimant data). It analyzes how CMS’s bid-based premium subsidy formula, risk-adjustment payments, LIS (low-income subsidy) random assignment rules, and the Medicare Advantage (MA–PD) outside option shape insurer incentives, consumer sorting by health-risk type, equilibrium prices, and welfare. The authors estimate a structural demand and supply model and run counterfactual subsidy simulations.

difficulty.justification: Identifying whether the observed subsidy mechanism reduces versus increases insurers’ incentives to ‘raise the subsidy’ requires intimate knowledge of Medicare Part D rules and strategic pricing, and must resolve selection, risk‑adjustment, and general equilibrium responses; rich data and structural estimation help but substantial alternative mechanisms remain plausible.

context: This paper studies causal effects of federal undergraduate Stafford loan limit increases (legislation that raised annual loan maxima in 2007–2009) on dependent first‑year student borrowers’ enrollment, degree completion, earnings, and credit outcomes. The authors combine Texas administrative higher‑education records linked to Texas UI earnings for cohorts entering 2001–2008 (focusing on pre‑Great Recession entrants) with a national 5% CCP/Equifax credit‑report panel (cohorts 2004–2008), comparing students who borrowed at the statutory first‑year federal limit (constrained) to those who borrowed less and exploiting staggered cohort exposure to higher limits.

difficulty.justification: Substitution between student loans and credit cards is intuitive but depends on institutional detail and borrower heterogeneity; alternative mechanisms (increased liquidity raising card use, selection into borrowing, lender responses) are plausible, and the authors' constrained-vs-not plus staggered-policy design gives useful identification but still leaves mechanism and external‑validity questions.

triplet_key: 17649|cuts in legislated standard hours|weekend market work hours
context: This paper studies how permanent legislated reductions in statutory work hours affected time use in Japan and Korea. Using nationally representative time-diary surveys (Japan: 1976–2006, focus on legal changes around 1988–1997; Korea: 1999, 2004, 2009 with phase-in in 2004), the unit is individuals (age 10/15+) reporting two-day diaries. The institutional shocks were statutory cuts in the standard workweek that raised employers’ overtime costs and reduced market hours, allowing a natural-experiment analysis of reallocations to household production, tertiary activities, and leisure.

difficulty.justification: Understanding the sign requires intermediate policy and labor-market knowledge; institutional details (exemptions, enforcement, cultural scheduling norms) strongly condition whether hours are shifted off weekdays or onto weekends. Multiple plausible offsetting channels (employer scheduling, hiring, unpaid overtime) and measurement limits of two-day diaries mean empirical identification is feasible but nontrivial.

context: This paper develops and estimates a structural equilibrium search and wage-posting model of formal and informal labor in Brazil, using Pesquisa Mensal de Emprego (PME) rotating-panel data from six large metropolitan regions (2002–2007). Units are low-educated individuals (age 23–65) and heterogeneous firms deciding sector and posted wages. The institutional setting includes minimum wage, payroll and corporate taxes, employer social security contributions, severance pay, unemployment insurance (funded by labor taxes), and monitoring/fines for informality; counterfactuals simulate policy changes to informality costs and labor regulations.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether abolishing informality improves workers' welfare requires specialized labor-market theory and identification (search, wage posting, taxes, transfers) and is sensitive to institutional details (tax/subsidy incidence, enforcement, heterogeneity). Multiple opposing channels (employment vs. benefits, firm entry/exit, general-equilibrium wage effects) and reliance on structural assumptions make the sign plausible but far from unambiguous without strong model and data support.

triplet_key: 8825|worker profiling and reemployment services (wprs) program|subsequent earnings
treatment: Worker Profiling and Reemployment Services (WPRS) program
context: This paper evaluates the Worker Profiling and Reemployment Services (WPRS) system in Kentucky using administrative UI records for individual claimants filing between October 1994 and June 1996. Claimants received profiling scores (1–20) and, because of local capacity limits, those in marginal score groups were randomly assigned to mandatory reemployment services (a tie‑breaking experiment). The institutional setting is the Kentucky Unemployment Insurance system; outcomes include weeks and dollars of UI benefits, quarterly earnings, employment hazards, and service receipt.

difficulty.justification: Randomized tie‑breaking among marginal profile groups and rich administrative UI data make identification of the sign relatively straightforward; however, external validity and institutional/context specifics (time, Kentucky UI operations, capacity limits) moderately affect how stable the sign is across settings.

context: This paper analyzes U.S. mortgage loan applications and originated 30‑year fixed‑rate GSE‑conforming mortgages from 2009–2015 using linked loan‑level HMDA, ATTOM, McDash, Equifax and GSE data. Units are individual mortgage applications and originated loans (including Latinx and African‑American borrowers), across lenders (FinTech vs. traditional), counties and months. It exploits the Fannie Mae/Freddie Mac LLPA/GSE pricing grid and U.S. fair‑lending legal framework to isolate lender strategic markups and accept/reject decisions while holding observed credit‑risk (credit score, LTV) fixed.

difficulty.justification: The setting requires specialized knowledge of mortgage pricing, LLPA grids and fair‑lending institutions to credibly separate strategic markups from risk pricing; linked loan‑level data and the GSE grid help identification but selection, unobserved risk attributes and lender fee/packaging strategies leave plausible alternative explanations, and the sign may vary by lender, place and time.

context: This paper analyzes cross-country variation in buy-and-hold stock returns for 98 large deposit-taking banks (assets >$50bn) across about 20 countries during the 2007–2008 global credit crisis (main window July 2007–Dec 2008 and the Lehman month Sep 12–Oct 10, 2008). It links pre-crisis (2006) bank-level governance (RiskMetrics/CGQ), ownership, balance-sheet and profitability metrics (Tier 1, deposits, loans, liquidity), and country-level regulation and institutions (Caprio/Barth/Levine indices, Kaufmann governance) to differential bank performance.

difficulty.justification: Inferring that stronger capital supervision causally raised bank returns in the crisis requires specialized knowledge of banking regulation and crisis dynamics, and is highly context-dependent (bailout expectations, market structure). Cross-country confounding, reverse causality and multiple mediating channels mean the sign is plausible but not unambiguous and credible identification typically requires stronger quasi-experimental variation than standard controls provide.

context: Using a randomized Section 8 housing voucher wait-list lottery in Chicago (applications July 1997) and administrative data through 2005, the paper studies low-income applicant households (working-age, able-bodied adults) living in private-market housing. CHAC randomly assigned waiting-list positions and offered roughly 18,110 vouchers by May 2003. The institutional setting is means-tested housing vouchers (Fair Market Rent rules) interacting with TANF, Medicaid and Food Stamps; outcomes include employment, quarterly earnings and public-assistance receipt.

difficulty.justification: Randomized voucher assignment provides strong identification (low evidence gap), but multiple countervailing channels (income effect, mobility/neighborhood effects, benefit-cliffs) and important local institutional interactions (TANF/Medicaid/Food Stamps rules, labor market) make the sign context-dependent and ambiguous, requiring intermediate policy knowledge and careful causal reasoning.

context: This paper reports a two-stage randomized field experiment conducted in 198 rural villages in southern Mali during 2010–2012 (with a long-term follow-up in 2017), offering group agricultural loans to women’s associations in 88 villages and cash grants (40,000 FCFA) randomly to individuals across villages. Units are female farmers/households in a rain‑fed, capital‑poor agricultural setting with limited formal finance; the microcredit program (Soro Yiriwaso) used group liability and balloon repayments timed to planting/harvest. The study assesses selection into borrowing and heterogeneous returns of grants and loans on agricultural investment, output and profits.

difficulty.justification: An RCT reduces identification concerns, but heterogeneous effects across borrowers/non-borrowers, timing of grants, group dynamics, liquidity vs. risk tradeoffs, input markets and local seasonality make the sign context-dependent and plausibly mixed; moderate empirical evidence exists but precise attribution of mixed effects requires careful subgroup and spillover analysis.

context: The paper analyzes Japan’s sharp reduction in patient cost sharing at age 70, exploiting administrative Patient Survey and mortality data pooled largely from 1984–2008 (and the 2007 Comprehensive Survey of Living Conditions) to compare individuals and medical events just below and above age 70. Units include outpatient visits, inpatient admissions, deaths, and individual-level survey reports. Institutional context: national Elderly Health Insurance causes a 60–80% drop in coinsurance and different monthly stop-loss rules at the 70-year threshold; eligibility begins the first day of the month after one’s 70th birthday.

difficulty.justification: Large, sharp coinsurance drop at age 70 gives a credible RD-style source of identification, but institutional details (stop-loss rules and eligibility timing), possible age-related health/retirement discontinuities, and offsetting mechanisms (substitution to outpatient care, provider supply responses) make the sign nontrivial to pin down without careful empirical work.

context: This paper evaluates Colombia’s PACES secondary-school voucher program (implemented 1991–97), focusing on the Bogotá cohort that applied in 1994 for 1995 entry to 6th grade. The unit is individual students who applied for means‑targeted vouchers covering about half of private secondary tuition; vouchers were renewable annually conditional on grade promotion and often allocated by lottery, enabling randomized comparisons. For the outcome 'years of schooling completed,' effects are measured roughly three years after the lottery (a three‑year follow‑up).

difficulty.justification: Randomized allocation by lottery makes identifying the sign (vouchers increasing years of schooling) straightforward within the applicant/complier population, so little specialized theory is needed; main caveats are attrition, compliance, and limited external validity to non-applicants or other settings.

context: This paper uses U.S. quarterly macro data (1955q1–2014q2, with pre-Volcker and Great Moderation subperiods) to estimate a monetary DSGE model augmented with fiscal details—government consumption and transfers, maturity structure of nominal government debt, and steady-state distortionary taxes—to quantify government spending multipliers under two monetary–fiscal regimes (active monetary/passive fiscal and active fiscal/passive monetary). The unit is the aggregate U.S. economy; observables include output, consumption, investment, hours, wages, government consumption, market-value debt, inflation and the federal funds rate.

difficulty.justification: The effect of a government spending rise on consumption depends critically on monetary–fiscal regime, financing (taxes vs debt/maturity), and expectations (Ricardian versus non‑Ricardian behavior), so specialized DSGE theory/identification is needed and the sign is highly ambiguous without strong model assumptions and rich identifying variation.

context: This estimate is based on U.S. national annual macro data (roughly 1912–2006, with primary samples starting 1917/1939/1950 and ending 2006) using per‑capita aggregates. Identification for defense‑spending shocks relies on war‑driven exogenous variation (WWI, WWII, Korean War), Ramey’s defense‑news series, and IV / time‑series regressions. The paper reports that defense multipliers <1 imply crowding out of other GDP components, including non‑defense government purchases, but notes non‑defense multipliers cannot be reliably estimated directly due to lack of valid instruments.

difficulty.justification: Requires substantial macro and public‑finance knowledge and specialized identification (war shocks, news IVs). The sign is highly context‑dependent, plausible alternatives (no change or positive reallocation) exist, channels and GE feedbacks are complex, and direct empirical identification for non‑defense purchases is weak.

context: A month-long randomized field experiment (14 one-month rounds in 201?–2016) with 378 male seasonal manufacturing workers in semi-rural Odisha, India (near Bhubaneswar). Workers were organized into 3-person production units making local goods, received a two-week training baseline, then units were randomized to wage structures (equal vs. unequal flat daily wages) with post-treatment observation for 20 days. Outcomes include daily attendance, individual output, endline cooperative games, and surveys; pay secrecy and task observability were experimentally varied.

difficulty.justification: A randomized field experiment with small, fixed teams and orthogonal variation in pay secrecy/observability gives strong internal identification so determining the sign is relatively easy; however cultural, task, and monitoring context and multiple behavioral channels (fairness vs. incentives) limit generalizability.

triplet_key: 31646|regulation using self-identified race|share of loans to black borrowers
outcome: Share of loans to Black borrowers
context: This triplet comes from a simulated counterfactual in which regulators switch from BISG-based evaluations to regulation using self-identified race — operationalized in the paper by using image-based race (from LinkedIn photos) as a proxy for actual race. The exercise uses U.S. small-business lending data (Lendio applications, 2017–2019, and PPP first-draw loans, Apr 2020–Feb 2021) at the applicant/borrower and lender level and finds higher shares of loans to actual Black borrowers but a reallocation toward more affluent, better-educated areas.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate policy and market knowledge; the sign depends heavily on institutional details and lender responses, is confounded by measurement error and selection/composition effects, and the simulation proxy (image-based race) leaves substantial uncertainty about real-world effects.

context: Using individual-level monthly credit-bureau data from the U.S. (2012–2017), this study compares unsecured personal loans originated by online fintech firms and traditional lenders. The unit is the loan/borrower (monthly panel spanning 3 months pre- to 15 months post-origination) across U.S. zip codes and states. Fintechs are defined as non-deposit, online-only, recently founded lenders; the institutional background includes rising fintech market share and regulation primarily by state regulators/CFPB. Analyses exploit local time variation and lender switching to infer causal effects on defaults, balances, and pricing.

difficulty.justification: Rich credit-bureau panel and a lender-switching/local time-variation design give reasonably good leverage, but fintech lending involves heterogeneous business models, pricing and borrower selection that vary by geography and time; alternative channels (better algorithms vs. adverse selection/looser underwriting) make the sign nontrivial and require careful identification to rule out remaining time-varying confounders.

context: This paper studies the causal impact of expanding Medicaid via Oregon’s 2008 Medicaid lottery. From Jan–Sep 2008 the state randomly selected low-income adults (ages 19–64) on a waiting list for the opportunity to apply for OHP Standard; outcomes are measured through Sept 2009 (~16 months). The units are uninsured low-income adults/households (below ~100% FPL) who signed up for the lottery. The institutional setting includes Medicaid eligibility rules (citizens/legal immigrants, six months uninsured, income/assets limits) and uses administrative and survey data.

difficulty.justification: The Oregon lottery RCT gives strong internal identification that Medicaid increases self-reported access, so domain knowledge and evidence needs are moderate; remaining issues are imperfect take-up, self-reporting bias, and limited external generalizability which modestly raise difficulty.

triplet_key: 24181|rent control expansion|number of owners in treated buildings
outcome: Number of owners in treated buildings
context: This paper exploits San Francisco’s 1994 ballot initiative that extended rent control to small (≤4‑unit) multifamily buildings built before 1980. Using address‑level panel data on nearly all adults (Infutor), DataQuick parcel records, permits and condo conversions, the authors track renters and landlords from the early 1990s through 2016 and analyze impacts mainly during 1995–2012. The unit of analysis includes individual tenants, parcels/landlords and neighborhoods (zipcodes); institutional details include within‑tenancy rent caps, eviction rules and condo/TIC conversion channels.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether rent‑control expansion increases owner counts depends on institutional details (condo/TIC conversion rules, eviction caps) and multiple opposing mechanisms (landlords selling to owner‑occupiers, conversions, or value declines reducing sales). Rich address‑level panel + conversion/permit data make identification feasible but substantial confounding and mechanism heterogeneity remain, so inference is moderately difficult.

context: This paper studies U.S. private-equity transactions and their syndicated loan financing from 1983–2009 (loan-level analyses mainly 1988–2008; CLO-flow analyses 2001–2008), comparing bank‑affiliated versus stand‑alone sponsors and parent‑financed deals. Units are private‑equity deals and associated loans, linking loan terms, syndicate retention, and ex‑post outcomes (downgrades, exits). Identification leverages credit‑cycle variation—predefined peak years (1985–89, 1998–2000, 2005–07) and CLO fund‑flow shocks—within the institutional setting of the U.S. syndicated loan market and post‑crisis regulation debates (e.g., the Volcker Rule).

difficulty.justification: Establishing a negative causal effect of bank affiliation on ex‑post loan outcomes hinges on specialized knowledge of PE-bank incentives, syndicated lending and CLO markets; selection, monitoring vs. conflict channels, and cyclical/compositional interactions create strong alternative explanations, and although credit‑cycle and CLO‑flow variation aid identification, substantial confounding risks remain.

context: This triplet is estimated using North Carolina administrative K–12 records for students born near January 1 in recentered birth years 1993–1998. The analysis focuses on students ever eligible for free and reduced-price lunch (FRL) as a proxy for likely EITC eligibility, restricted to births within 28 days of January 1 (excluding an eight-day donut). Leveraging the January 1 dependent-status cutoff, the paper uses an RD design to estimate the effect of infancy cash-transfer eligibility on test scores, suspensions, and high-school graduation.

difficulty.justification: An RD around a January 1 eligibility cutoff provides a credible local identification strategy, so moderate domain knowledge suffices, but long-run impacts from infancy to high-school graduation operate through multiple mediating channels and are sensitive to institutional details (EITC take-up, seasonality, sorting, FRL proxy). Remaining uncertainties about actual transfer receipt, cohort heterogeneity, and limited bandwidth make the sign plausible but not straightforward to pin down.

context: This paper analyzes payday loan outcomes using CFPB administrative loan-level data covering July 2011–June 2012 (six months before and after Jan 1, 2012) to evaluate a Texas statewide behaviorally‑motivated disclosure mandate and concurrent Austin and Dallas municipal loan‑size and amortization ordinances. The setting compares loans, stores, and borrowers in Texas to lenders in other states and across Texas cities, exploiting policy timing and enforcement start‑dates (Austin May 1, Dallas June 17, 2012) to study effects on loan volume, renewals, pricing, and compliance.

difficulty.justification: Understanding disclosure effects in payday markets requires specialized knowledge of consumer finance and regulatory response; the sign is sensitive to local institutional details and concurrent municipal ordinances. Identification is complicated by potential confounding, substitution and enforcement timing, though loan‑level CFPB data and staggered policy start dates give a reasonably strong empirical lever if exploited carefully.

context: This paper evaluates a large-scale cluster randomized trial embedded in Colombia’s nationwide Hogares Infantiles Mejorados (HIM) rollout. Randomization occurred in late 2012 with baseline data collected March–May 2013 and an 18-month endline in Oct–Nov 2014 across 120 HIs in eight major Colombian cities. Units are preschools (HIs) and enrolled children (baseline ages 18–36 months; main analysis focuses on children ≥48 months at endline). HIM increased per-child funding—primarily to hire teaching assistants—while a second arm (HIM+FE) added low-cost video-conferenced teacher training.

difficulty.justification: Although the cluster RCT design strongly reduces confounding, interpreting a null effect is still challenging because power, fidelity/compliance, measurement error, heterogeneous effects, spillovers, and context-specific implementation (how funds were used, age/exposure) can all mask true impacts; these require intermediate-specialized knowledge and careful analysis to rule out alternative explanations.

triplet_key: 10277|trade openness|effect of current account reversal on gdp growth
outcome: Effect of current account reversal on GDP growth
context: Using an annual panel of 157 countries from roughly 1970–2001 (2,228 country-year observations), this paper studies how abrupt stops in capital inflows (“sudden stops”) and large current-account reversals affect national real GDP growth. Units are countries observed annually across world regions. Key institutional features include measures of financial openness (Quinn index), trade openness (exports+imports/GDP), reserve levels, external debt, short-term debt shares, and de-facto exchange-rate regimes; the analysis evaluates how capital-account restrictions and exchange-rate flexibility mediate crisis incidence and growth impacts.

difficulty.justification: Determining that trade openness positively moderates the growth effect of current-account reversals requires advanced understanding of trade–finance interactions and is highly context-dependent; openness is endogenous, mechanically correlated with many institutional and macro factors, and multiple opposing channels and selection effects make credible identification very difficult with standard panel data.

context: This paper uses country-level panel data (samples of 28–95 countries) from the 1980s–1990s to study official equity-market liberalizations across emerging and developed economies. The unit of analysis is countries and the authors compare 3–10 year growth windows (primary k=5) before and after regulatory liberalization dates, controlling for macro, demographic and financial-development covariates (trade openness, inflation, private credit, stock turnover) and institutional features (insider-trading laws, legal origin). Liberalization dates come from official regulatory changes, ADRs/country funds and IFC inclusion.

difficulty.justification: Understanding effects requires intermediate finance/macro knowledge; the sign depends strongly on country institutions and timing, and reverse causality/omitted variables and macro feedbacks make the positive effect plausible but not unambiguous despite the panel controls and event-window design.

context: This paper studies how race-prediction algorithms (BISG) shape fair-lending measurement and lender incentives in U.S. small-business credit markets using Lendio loan application data (2017–2019) and Paycheck Protection Program first-draw loans (Apr 2020–Feb 2021). Units are individual applicants/borrowers and lenders. The authors construct an image-based race measure from LinkedIn photos, benchmark it to self-reported PPP race, estimate how BISG errors bias approval gaps and lender-level loan shares, and simulate counterfactual regulatory shifts from BISG to self-identified race under U.S. fair-lending rules.

difficulty.justification: Identifying whether BISG-based regulation increases lending to disadvantaged areas requires intermediate knowledge of fair‑lending rules and measurement error effects, is sensitive to institutional/enforcement context, and is confounded by strategic lender responses and selection; available benchmarked data help but do not eliminate substantial identification challenges.

context: This paper evaluates the US Home Affordable Modification Program (HAMP) introduced in March 2009, using loan-level monthly panel data (OCC Mortgage Metrics and a Treasury application sample) from July 2008–June 2012 covering ≈23–34 million mortgages serviced by large banks. Units are individual first-lien mortgages and servicers/zip regions. HAMP's institutional rules (owner-occupancy, $729,750 balance cap, NPV test, trial-to-permanent modifications) and servicer incentives ($1,000 upfront plus pay-for-success) provide quasi-experimental variation to estimate effects on modifications, foreclosures, delinquencies, house prices, and spending.

difficulty.justification: Large loan-level data and HAMP's institutional rules provide credible quasi-experimental variation, but identifying a positive effect on durable spending still requires handling selection into modification, dynamic responses, local price spillovers and concurrent policies—making sign inference moderately hard though feasible with careful design.

context: This paper uses a randomized field experiment in Pennsylvania (late 2015–2016) targeting about 30,000 Medicaid-enrolled elderly (age 60+) who were not enrolled in SNAP to estimate causal effects of mailed information and phone-based application assistance on SNAP calls, applications, enrollment, and benefit amounts. Treatments (Information Only; Information Plus Assistance) were mailed Jan–Mar 2016 and outcomes tracked in administrative SNAP and Medicaid data over 9–23 months. The institutional setting involves SNAP’s progressive, means‑tested benefit formula and a non‑automatic application/verification process.

difficulty.justification: Randomized outreach with administrative outcomes gives fairly clean identification of the average effect, so evidence sufficiency and overall difficulty are moderate-to-low; however the sign is context-dependent and plausibly ambiguous ex ante because assistance can both improve application quality (lower rejections) and change applicant composition (raise rejections), requiring intermediate institutional knowledge to predict.

context: The paper's firm-level analysis examines Sweden's 2007–2009 employer payroll-tax reform (halving employer payroll taxes for workers up to age 26). Using firm accounting panel data for firms active 2003–2013, it proxies a firm's tax-windfall exposure by its pre-reform share of young workers and compares firms with large versus moderate shares. Outcomes analyzed include employment, sales, capital, value added and profits; take-up was automatic via government payroll software.

difficulty.justification: The age-targeted reform provides plausibly exogenous variation and automatic take-up reduces measurement error, making a positive effect on value added credible; however differential pre-trends by firms with many young workers, industry composition, wage pass-through versus employment effects, and the contemporaneous 2008 shock mean the sign is plausible but not trivial to identify.

context: This paper studies Sweden’s 2007–2009 employer payroll‑tax reform that halved employer payroll taxes for young birth‑cohorts (eligibility up to age 26) using population administrative employer–employee records and firm accounting data spanning the 1990s–2013. Exploiting cohort eligibility rules, timing of the two reform steps, and cross‑firm variation in pre‑reform shares of young workers, it examines causal effects on individual net/gross wages, employment and transition rates, and firm outcomes (employment, sales, capital, profits); take‑up was automatic via payroll software.

difficulty.justification: Cohort eligibility, timing, and rich administrative data make identification feasible without highly specialized theory, but age-specific trends, firm selection, substitution across age groups and possible general-equilibrium or spillover effects leave nontrivial alternative explanations, so the sign is moderately but not trivially established.

triplet_key: 18346|industry merger and acquisition activity|firm r&d responsiveness
context: This paper examines U.S. publicly traded firms across three-digit SIC industries during 1984–2006, using Compustat-CRSP, SDC M&A records, FRED industrial production, and Census data. It analyzes firm-year R&D responses to downstream demand shocks, industry M&A activity, and a firm's probability of being an acquisition target, emphasizing differences between small and large firms, target bargaining power, and product-market competition within the U.S. corporate acquisition market.

difficulty.justification: Establishing a positive effect of industry M&A on firm R&D responsiveness requires specialized IO/corporate-finance knowledge because effects vary by firm size, bargaining power, competition and demand shocks and involve multiple mediating channels (selection, incentives, integration effects). Strong confounding and reverse causality risks mean observational Compustat/SDC data provide suggestive but not definitive evidence without credible identification strategies.

context: This triplet uses U.S. MSA‑level AIDS incidence data for 1982–2001, with gender‑specific rates analyzed for age groups 20–29 and 30–34. Regressions relate beer taxes and other alcohol‑policy measures to logged AIDS rates across MSAs, with independent variables (and some specifications) lagged eight years to reflect likely timing of transmission.

difficulty.justification: Linking beer taxes to AIDS rates requires specialized knowledge of alcohol-behavior links and infectious-disease dynamics; the sign is highly context-dependent and subject to strong confounding (policy endogeneity, contemporaneous public-health interventions, migration, reporting changes) and complex dynamic/network channels, so credible identification is difficult though not impossible with careful strategies.

context: This paper studies domestic outsourcing in Germany using the universe of social-security administrative records (IEB) mainly from the mid-1970s through 2009 (with some analyses starting 1979 or 1999 for specific codes). It analyzes individual workers, jobs and establishments (West Germany focus in some tests) in food, cleaning, security and logistics occupations during a period of deregulation, rising temp-agency use and declining collective-bargaining coverage, exploiting observed worker flows, on-site spin-offs, and establishment histories to identify wage and rent changes.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a negative wage effect from temp-agency employment requires intermediate knowledge of German labor institutions and deregulation, and is fairly context-dependent (bargaining coverage, occupations, time). Rich administrative data and clever comparisons (flows, spin-offs) help, but selection, unobserved heterogeneity, and general-equilibrium/firm-side rent-shifting make causal attribution moderately difficult.

context: This empirical paper studies U.S. state-chartered commercial banks from 1996–2011, exploiting the Riegle Act–mandated alternating supervision (12- or 18‑month rotations) between state regulators and federal agencies (Fed for state‑member banks; FDIC for non‑members). Using National Information Center on-site exam data (CAMELS) merged with Call Reports across U.S. states, the authors analyze how regulator identity affects supervisory ratings and bank outcomes (capital ratios, nonperforming loans, ROA) for individual banks under predetermined rotation schedules.

difficulty.justification: Understanding this requires specialized knowledge of bank regulation and the Riegle rotation design; the mandated rotations provide quasi‑experimental leverage but multiple channels (ratings, capital adjustments, provisioning, risk‑taking) and heterogeneous institutional settings make the negative effect plausible but not trivial to pin down with complete confidence.

triplet_key: 24632|reallocation of workers to low-wage cities|aggregate welfare
treatment: Reallocation of workers to low-wage cities
context: Quantitative analysis of U.S. metropolitan areas (MSAs), using cross-sectional MSA-level data around 2007 on wages, employment, expenditures and trade flows, with workers divided into high-skill (college) and low-skill (non-college). The paper combines a general-equilibrium spatial model with calibration to U.S. data and draws spillover elasticities from existing estimates (e.g., Ciccone & Hall 1996; Diamond 2016) to evaluate optimal spatial transfer policies, worker reallocation, and welfare impacts across U.S. cities.

difficulty.justification: Assessing this requires specialized spatial general-equilibrium knowledge and parameter choices; the welfare sign hinges critically on agglomeration spillovers, housing/amenity differences, trade and selection effects, so opposite signs are quite plausible and cross-sectional calibration plus borrowed elasticities offers limited causal identification.

context: This triplet refers to U.S. households and studies a constructed sequence of stimulus checks chosen to replicate a persistent fiscal-purchases shock identified by Caldara & Kamps (2017), normalized to 1% of steady-state quarterly consumption (≈$150 per household). The implied transfer path is recovered by inverting a micro-estimated MPC spending profile (from Parker et al./Broda & Parker), assuming no anticipation; the resulting checks (time-0 ≈ $300) induce a persistent increase in household consumption. The matched fiscal expansion is persistently deficit-financed with limited monetary offset.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a positive effect of repeated small stimulus checks on household consumption draws on intermediate micro and macro knowledge and is sensitive to institutional details (anticipation, financing, monetary response). Multiple plausible offsets (Ricardian responses, intertemporal substitution, monetary tightening) and GE feedbacks make the sign plausible but nontrivial to pin down without careful identification.

triplet_key: 15499|interstate banking deregulations|time to reach max size
context: This empirical study uses US Census Bureau Longitudinal Business Database establishment-level employment data (annual, 1976 onward, focusing on entrants roughly 1977–1996) to examine how state-level intrastate and interstate branch-banking deregulations (1970s–mid-1990s), enacted at different times across US states, affected entry sizes and early growth of new private‑sector establishments (startups). The paper exploits staggered reform timing across states, conditions on four-year survival for some analyses, and controls for state and year effects to study financing-constraint effects on initial and near‑term firm size.

difficulty.justification: Understanding the likely sign needs intermediate knowledge of how bank deregulation affects firm finance and entry dynamics. Identification is complicated by heterogeneous state/industry contexts, selection and composition effects, and concurrent policies—staggered timing and controls help but do not eliminate plausible alternative stories.

triplet_key: 29627|exposure by anti-corruption audits|worker employment and labor income
context: This paper studies how randomized municipal audits by Brazil’s Office of the Comptroller General (CGU) during 2003–2014 affected firms and workers. The CGU ran televised lotteries selecting municipalities for audits of federally funded municipal procurement (covering contracts in the two prior years); auditors publicly listed firms implicated in irregularities. The unit of analysis is firm- and worker-level administrative data (matched RAIS, procurement, BNDES loans, and PIA investment) for firms—mostly outside audited municipalities—exposed by these audits across Brazilian municipalities.

difficulty.justification: Randomized audits provide a credible source of exogenous variation, so intermediate domain knowledge suffices, but the net effect on employment and wages depends on multiple competing channels (loss of procurement rents, reputational/credit effects, reallocation and local GE spillovers) that are context- and institution-dependent. Rich matched administrative data improves inference but cannot fully eliminate ambiguity about mediating mechanisms and heterogeneous/spillover effects.

triplet_key: 7532|current marginal capital gains tax rate|capital gains realizations
treatment: Current marginal capital gains tax rate
context: This paper analyzes US individual tax returns from the IRS Sales of Capital Assets (SOCA) panel covering 1986–1993 (following a 1985 baseline). Using state-level and federal capital gains tax variation—including the 1986 Tax Reform Act and subsequent state tax changes—it studies capital gains realizations by individual taxpayers (oversampling high-wealth and “sophisticated” investors) across US states, exploiting anticipated and transitory rate movements to separate timing responses from long-run behavioral effects.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate tax-economics knowledge (realization/timing behavior) and is context-sensitive (anticipation, asset types, taxpayer sophistication). Anticipation, price-driven realizations, and selection make the sign plausibly confounded, and although the paper's federal/state variation and anticipation/transitory decomposition improve identification, nontrivial challenges remain.

context: The study analyzes monthly and annual country‑level data (primarily 1980–2005, with monthly series from 1973–2005) for 50 countries (developed and emerging). The unit is the country‑year industry‑weighted earnings‑yield differential (SEG) across 38 industries. The context covers liberalization of capital accounts and equity markets, trade openness, institutional quality and market development, and global risk episodes (e.g., the 1997–98 crises). The authors link de jure openness, political/institutional indicators and global risk factors to observed valuation segmentation using panel and time‑series econometric models.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate policy and market knowledge; the sign is context-sensitive (country type, crises, institutional strength) and is confounded by endogeneity, omitted variables and heterogeneous channels, so identification is moderately hard and demands careful econometric strategies.

context: Using California hospital discharge records (January 1992–December 2002) linked to death records, the paper studies individual unplanned emergency-room hospital admissions for “non-deferrable” diagnoses around the Medicare eligibility threshold. The unit is the patient admission for people just under and just over age 65 in California hospitals. It exploits the institutional rule that most U.S. workers become eligible for Medicare at 65 (sharp changes in primary coverage, supplemental coverage, and managed-care incidence) to estimate effects on treatment intensity, transfers, readmissions, and mortality.

difficulty.justification: Although a regression-discontinuity around age-65 with emergency admissions gives strong quasi-experimental leverage, correctly attributing a rise in hospital list charges to Medicare eligibility requires specialized knowledge of hospital pricing, payer mix, and coding behavior; results are sensitive to institutional context and plausible alternative mechanisms (cost-shifting, billing practices, selection, measurement of 'charges' versus payments).

context: Using U.S. metropolitan statistical area (MSA) variation over roughly 2005–2011, the paper links firm/job turnover (“creative destruction”) measured from Census BDS and LEHD to subjective wellbeing from Gallup (2008–2011) and BRFSS (2005–2010). Units of observation are MSAs and working-age individuals.
