Size is highly confounded with financing constraints, selection into being a target, bargaining power and competition, so multiple mediating channels and careful identification are needed and observational data alone are unlikely to be decisive without strong quasi-experimental strategies.

triplet_key: 15598|compulsory licensing|patenting by u.s. inventors relative to other non-german inventors
outcome: Patenting by U.S. inventors relative to other non-German inventors
context: The paper studies U.S. chemical invention across 8,422 USPTO subclasses from 1875–1939, exploiting the 1917 Trading with the Enemy Act (TWEA) and subsequent confiscation/licensing of German enemy patents (licenses mainly issued 1919–1921) as an exogenous shock. Using yearly patent counts by domestic inventors (subclass-year units) and firm-level (DuPont) patents, it assesses how compulsory licensing of enemy-owned patents affected domestic innovation in the U.S. chemical industry.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a positive effect requires specialized knowledge of IP law, technology diffusion, and historical institutional detail; multiple channels (knowledge diffusion, competitor exit, market-share shifts, incentive effects) and wartime confounders make the sign context-dependent and moderately ambiguous, though the exogenous TWEA shock and rich subclass-level data make credible inference plausible with careful empirical design.

context: This empirical study assesses the causal impacts of the Canada–U.S. Free Trade Agreement (implemented Jan 1, 1989) on 213 Canadian 4‑digit manufacturing industries (and a 1,026 industry-by-plant-size balanced panel) using annual data spanning 1980–1996. It exploits cross-industry variation in FTA‑mandated preferential tariff concessions (τ_US − τ_ROW) and compares pre‑FTA (1980–86/1980–88) and post‑FTA (1988/1989–1994/1996) growth rates while controlling for U.S. industry trends, business‑cycle timing, and industry fixed effects to identify effects on employment, productivity, wages, trade, and plant dynamics.

difficulty.justification: Interpreting tariff-concession effects on productivity requires intermediate trade and industrial-organization knowledge; the sign can vary by sector and is confounded by endogenous treatment, concurrent shocks, and multiple channels (selection, reallocation, technology adoption), though the panel pre/post design with controls makes a credible—yet nontrivial—identification possible.

triplet_key: 29010|decrease fees by 10% and reduce denial probability by 20%|average spending per visit
treatment: Decrease fees by 10% and reduce denial probability by 20%
context: This paper analyzes U.S. physician–insurer billing interactions using remittance data covering roughly 90 million visits (2013–2015) combined with physician-level Medicaid/Medicare participation data (2009–2015). Units include visit line-items, patient visits, individual physicians, and multi-state practice groups across U.S. states. The institutional setting centers on state-administered Medicaid (including MCOs) versus Medicare and commercial insurers, focusing on claim denials/resubmissions, state-by-state administrative variation, and how these administrative frictions affect physicians’ willingness to accept Medicaid patients.

difficulty.justification: Answering whether lower fees and fewer denials reduce billed spending per visit requires specialized knowledge of billing incentives, coding, and provider selection, and is highly context-dependent across payers and states; multiple opposing channels (price effects, upcoding/cost‑shifting, selection, measurement via billed amounts) make the sign ambiguous and identification challenging even with large administrative data.

triplet_key: 14665|3 percentage point decline in aggregate consumption growth|ratio of consumption top 20 to bottom 80
treatment: 3 percentage point decline in aggregate consumption growth
outcome: Ratio of consumption top 20 to bottom 80
context: This empirical paper studies US households using the Consumer Expenditure Survey (1982–2004) and IRS-based Piketty–Saez taxable income data (primarily 1982–2006, with historical comparisons back to 1929–1982). Units are households and tax units; the paper examines how consumption and non‑capital income growth across consumption and income percentiles (especially top deciles and top 1/0.1/0.01%) covary with aggregate real per‑capita consumption and income, decomposing wage versus non‑wage channels to explain differential exposure to aggregate booms and busts.

difficulty.justification: Determining that an aggregate consumption growth decline reduces the top20/bottom80 consumption ratio requires intermediate-to-advanced knowledge of heterogeneous MPCs, income composition (wages vs capital), and wealth effects; the sign is context-dependent and multiple opposing channels (luxury spending cuts vs larger declines for low‑income households, selection and composition) and measurement issues for top tails make the causal sign nontrivial to identify with confidence.

triplet_key: 25876|automatic enrollment into the thrift savings plan|d2 total debt (includes auto)
treatment: Automatic enrollment into the Thrift Savings Plan
context: This paper studies the causal effect of automatic enrollment into the Thrift Savings Plan (TSP) on employee debt by exploiting the U.S. federal policy that began automatically enrolling civilian U.S. Army hires at a 3% default on August 1, 2010. It compares pre- (Aug 1, 2009–Jul 31, 2010) and post-enrollment (Aug 1, 2010–Jul 31, 2011) hire cohorts of Army civilian employees using Department of Defense payroll/personnel data linked to national credit-bureau reports, analyzing outcomes through 2014–2015 while controlling for calendar time and demographics.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a null effect requires intermediate knowledge of retirement-savings vs. borrowing behavior and is sensitive to context (liquidity, income, match structure). Multiple plausible channels (crowding out, borrowing to smooth, heterogenous responses) and potential cohort/time confounders make the sign ambiguous despite good administrative and credit-bureau data.

context: This triplet comes from a randomized online survey and incentivized donation experiment conducted in the United States in Nov–Dec 2017 with 2,666 adult respondents matched to US demographics. Respondents were randomly assigned (50%) to a morality-assessment module about paid-donor systems; afterwards they were offered an incentivized choice to authorize a $1 donation on their behalf to a pro-compensation organization (the American Transplant Foundation) or, with lower probability, to an anti-compensation organization. The outcome is the binary decision to donate to the pro-compensation foundation.

difficulty.justification: Randomized incentivized design gives good internal identification, but moral‑assessment primes can operate through multiple opposing psychological channels (norm activation, moral licensing, identity), and effects are context- and framing-sensitive, so sign is not obvious without the experiment.

triplet_key: 10358|financial development|likelihood of converging to frontier growth rate
outcome: Likelihood of converging to frontier growth rate
context: The paper develops a Schumpeterian growth model and tests its implications with cross‑country data for 71 countries over 1960–1995. The unit of observation is the country (average growth rates, productivity, and financial‑intermediation measures such as private credit/GDP), using the United States as the world technology frontier. Empirical identification exploits cross‑country variation in financial development and legal‑origin instruments (French/English/German/Scandinavian) and initial relative GDP/TFP to study how creditor protection affects convergence.

difficulty.justification: Establishing that financial development raises the probability of convergence relies on Schumpeterian theory and nontrivial identification (e.g., legal‑origin IV) and is sensitive to institutional/context heterogeneity, reverse causality and omitted‑variable concerns; multiple competing channels (allocation vs. misallocation, selection, GE feedbacks) make the sign plausible but empirically challenging to pin down.

context: This triplet is identified from within-country before-and-after comparisons of lending-rate skewness around financial liberalization events. The author uses Kaminsky and Schmukler (2008) dates for 16 countries (1973–2005) and monthly lending-rate series, computing skewness of lending-rate changes before versus after each country’s main liberalization (and, separately, episodes of financial restriction). The unit of observation is the country-month, and the reported pattern is that skewness declines after liberalization in most cases (exceptions noted).

difficulty.justification: Determining that liberalization causally reduces lending-rate skewness requires intermediate-to-advanced knowledge of financial-market behavior and is sensitive to institutional features, timing, and concurrent reforms. There are multiple plausible opposing mechanisms and confounders (macro shocks, selection of episodes, measurement issues), so identification needs careful empirical strategies beyond simple before–after comparisons.

context: This paper studies Los Angeles Unified School District’s Zones of Choice (ZOC) reform—implemented starting around 2012–2013—using administrative data from roughly 2008–2019. It evaluates effects on high-school students (chiefly 11th graders and graduating cohorts) and market-level school outcomes across ZOC versus non-ZOC neighborhoods in Los Angeles. ZOC combined catchment areas, expanded within-district choice with centralized rank-ordered (immediate-acceptance) assignment and lotteries for oversubscription, thereby changing competitive incentives among attendance-zone schools.

difficulty.justification: Identifying whether ZOC increases four‑year college enrollment requires substantial knowledge of school‑choice assignment mechanics, peer/quality effects, and district competition; LA's institutional details importantly shape the sign. Multiple confounders and mediating channels (sorting, capacity, peer effects) make causal inference challenging, although administrative data and lotteries provide levers for credible identification.

triplet_key: 30539|private appeals to regulator or firm|so2 emission concentration
treatment: Private appeals to regulator or firm
context: This paper reports a nationwide randomized field experiment in China (May–Dec 2020) on 24,620 major polluting plants monitored by the national Continuous Emissions Monitoring System (CEMS) across ~333 prefectures. The unit of observation is CEMS-monitored firms and prefectures; treatments randomly assigned firms to private appeals (hotline/website/DM/phone) or public Weibo appeals (with randomized visibility), while treatment intensity was cross-randomized at the prefecture level (95% vs 70%). Outcomes include daily violation indicators, firm SO2/COD concentrations, and ambient SO2 levels.

difficulty.justification: The nationwide randomized design with firm-level CEMS data gives strong internal validity and measurement, lowering required domain expertise and raising evidence sufficiency; however heterogeneous institutional contexts, potential spillovers/manipulation, and the special challenges of interpreting a null (power, offsetting channels) keep the problem moderately difficult.

context: This paper analyzes perceptions and criminal behavior using two U.S. longitudinal cohorts: the NLSY97 (youth aged 12–16 in 1997, followed 1997–2000) and the National Youth Survey (respondents surveyed in 1983 and 1986). Units are individual young males (with sibling linkages), linked to county-level arrest rates and neighborhood measures. The institutional setting is the U.S. criminal justice system—local arrest outcomes, policing/neighborhood disorder—and the study examines how personal/sibling crimes and arrests update beliefs about arrest probabilities and how those beliefs causally affect subsequent offending.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether arrest reduces later offending requires specialized criminological theory (deterrence vs labeling), is highly sensitive to institutional and demographic context, and is subject to strong confounding/selection and multiple opposing channels; longitudinal cohorts and sibling links help but do not eliminate identification challenges without exogenous variation.

triplet_key: 21007|predictable local default risk|gse mortgage contract rates
context: This paper analyzes U.S. mortgage markets using loan-level data from Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac (1999–2012, with focal analyses for 2001–2006 and 2007–2009/Great Recession) and private “prime jumbo” loans across metropolitan statistical areas. The units are individual mortgage loans and MSAs. The institutional setting contrasts government-sponsored enterprise (GSE) securitization—with an implicit federal backstop and a national conforming-loan limit—and privately securitized jumbo mortgages; the authors exploit regional lagged default, prepayment, and the conforming-loan threshold to study spatial redistribution via mortgage pricing.

difficulty.justification: Answering whether predictable local default risk raises or lowers GSE mortgage rates requires substantial institutional knowledge of GSE pricing, securitization and the conforming loan channel; effects vary by time period, market segment, and regulatory/backstop features, and are confounded by borrower selection, prepayment/default dynamics and spatial redistribution, so rich loan‑level exploits help but do not fully eliminate competing explanations.

context: This paper studies U.S. publicly listed firms (Compustat and Audit Analytics) over 1999–2015, using annual firm-level panels of earnings restatements (revenue-recognition and irregularity cases) and measures of intangible investment (SG&A or R&D). It exploits regulatory variation around Sarbanes–Oxley (pre/post) and firm-level restatement episodes to estimate a calibrated dynamic general-equilibrium model in which managers choose real (intangibles) investment versus earnings misreporting, intermediaries extract information rents, and disclosure regulation affects cost of capital, firm value, and aggregate growth.

difficulty.justification: Answering whether eliminating earnings misreporting increases real investment distortion requires specialized agency/disclosure and macro-finance theory to trace offsetting channels (cost of capital, managerial incentives, information rents), the net sign depends strongly on institutional and firm-level context so the opposite sign is readily plausible, and although SOX/restatement variation helps identification, selection, measurement, and dynamic GE feedbacks make conclusive evidence difficult without strong modeling assumptions.

triplet_key: 30539|higher regional treatment intensity (95% vs 70%)|ambient so2 concentration
context: This paper reports a nationwide randomized field experiment in China (May–Dec 2020) on 24,620 major polluting plants monitored by the national Continuous Emissions Monitoring System (CEMS) across ~333 prefectures. The unit of observation is CEMS-monitored firms and prefectures; treatments randomly assigned firms to private appeals (hotline/website/DM/phone) or public Weibo appeals (with randomized visibility), while treatment intensity was cross-randomized at the prefecture level (95% vs 70%). Outcomes include daily violation indicators, firm SO2/COD concentrations, and ambient SO2 levels.

difficulty.justification: Randomized assignment across thousands of firms and prefectures gives strong identification of firm responses, so intermediate domain knowledge suffices; however translating firm-level emission changes into ambient SO2 involves meteorology, spatial spillovers and local source composition, creating moderate context dependence and causal complexity despite generally sufficient experimental evidence.

context: This paper studies long-term causal effects of Boston Public Schools' universal K1 preschool on educational and behavioral outcomes for first-time preschool applicants (four-year-olds) who applied between 1997 and 2003 in Boston, MA. Using randomized school-assignment tie-break lotteries within BPS, linked to Massachusetts DESE administrative records and National Student Clearinghouse data with ~20 years of follow-up, the study evaluates impacts on college enrollment, SAT-taking, MCAS scores, high-school graduation, and disciplinary outcomes for a largely low-income, nonwhite urban cohort.

difficulty.justification: Randomized tie-break lotteries and long (≈20-year) administrative follow-up give strong internal identification that preschool increases on-time college enrollment for this Boston cohort, so identifying the sign is relatively straightforward; however, effects depend on program quality, population, and local institutions, and long-run mediating channels and external validity raise moderate caveats.

triplet_key: 23377|fall in m2 growth|entrusted lending intermediated by nonstate banks
outcome: Entrusted lending intermediated by nonstate banks
context: This paper studies China during 2009–2015 (with the monetary rule estimated over 2000Q1–2016Q2), analyzing bank‑level responses to contractionary People’s Bank of China policy that targeted M2. Using trustee‑level entrusted‑loan announcements and quarterly balance‑sheet panels for listed and other trustees, it examines how exogenous M2 shocks affected shadow‑banking (entrusted loans) and risky on‑balance‑sheet investments (ARI/ARIX). The setting stresses institutional features: M2‑targeting, the loan‑to‑deposit (LDR) and safe‑loan regulations, and the state vs nonstate bank divide.

difficulty.justification: Identifying this sign requires detailed institutional knowledge of China’s M2‑targeting, LDR/safe‑loan constraints and state vs nonstate bank behavior; the mechanism rests on regulatory arbitrage and deposit flows, so context is crucial. Multiple alternative channels and confounders make the opposite sign plausible without careful identification, though trustee‑level data and an estimated monetary‑rule shock allow a reasonably credible test.

context: Using a randomized Section 8 housing voucher wait-list lottery in Chicago (applications July 1997) and administrative data through 2005, the paper studies low-income applicant households (working-age, able-bodied adults) living in private-market housing. CHAC randomly assigned waiting-list positions and offered roughly 18,110 vouchers by May 2003. The institutional setting is means-tested housing vouchers (Fair Market Rent rules) interacting with TANF, Medicaid and Food Stamps; outcomes include employment, quarterly earnings and public-assistance receipt.

difficulty.justification: Randomized wait-list offers provide strong exogenous variation, so identification is feasible, but policy interactions (means-testing, TANF rules), take-up behavior, and multiple mediating channels (income effects, eligibility changes, administrative interactions and neighborhood/job effects) make the sign context-dependent and leave plausible alternative outcomes without detailed institutional accounting.

triplet_key: 21760|eliminating state and local tax (salt) deduction|aggregate real gdp
treatment: Eliminating State and Local Tax (SALT) deduction
context: The authors simulate elimination of the State and Local Tax (SALT) deduction (as altered by the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act) using their spatial general-equilibrium model calibrated to U.S. state data (structural parameters estimated from 1980–2010 and equilibrium calibration to 2007). The analysis evaluates aggregate U.S. outcomes — notably worker welfare and aggregate real GDP — under counterfactuals that either hold each state’s government spending fixed or allow spending to respond endogenously.

difficulty.justification: Correctly signing this effect requires specialized knowledge of spatial general-equilibrium tax incidence and fiscal federalism, results depend heavily on assumptions about migration, housing prices and state spending responses, opposite-signed channels (efficiency gains vs. reduced local public goods and migration) are plausible, and the model calibration/estimation choices limit how definitive the sign is.

context: This paper analyzes hourly generation and demand across U.S. Power Control Areas (PCAs) and individual generating units from 1999–2012, using a nationwide panel of PCA-date-hour and unit-hour observations. It exploits the staggered creation and expansion of centralized wholesale electricity markets (market-based dispatch) under FERC reforms, comparing PCAs that switched to market dispatch to those that did not. The study measures allocative efficiency changes—out-of-merit operating costs and gains from interarea trade—holding capacities, fuel and emissions prices, and demand fixed.

difficulty.justification: Theory predicts market-based dispatch tends to reduce production costs via merit-order and better interarea trade, but outcomes hinge on market design, congestion, and market power so the sign can plausibly flip; the staggered PCA panel and controls help identification but selection, anticipation, and heterogeneous institutional features leave nontrivial identification challenges.

context: This paper analyzes the randomized Perry Preschool Project that enrolled 123 disadvantaged African-American children born in the 1960s in Ypsilanti, Michigan. The program provided two years of high-quality preschool plus weekly teacher home visits starting at age three; controls received no treatment. The authors follow the original participants through age 54 using surveys and administrative criminal/education records and link to outcomes for their biological children into young adulthood to estimate intergenerational effects on cognition, health, education, crime, employment, and family stability.

difficulty.justification: Randomized assignment of parents provides strong exogenous variation, so basic causal sign is plausible, but intergenerational identification faces complex mediating channels (fertility selection, parental behavior, composition), small sample/attrition, and a historically specific disadvantaged cohort that limit generalization and make alternative explanations plausible without careful analysis.

context: This paper develops and estimates a structural equilibrium search and wage-posting model of formal and informal labor in Brazil, using Pesquisa Mensal de Emprego (PME) rotating-panel data from six large metropolitan regions (2002–2007). Units are low-educated individuals (age 23–65) and heterogeneous firms deciding sector and posted wages. The institutional setting includes minimum wage, payroll and corporate taxes, employer social security contributions, severance pay, unemployment insurance (funded by labor taxes), and monitoring/fines for informality; counterfactuals simulate policy changes to informality costs and labor regulations.

difficulty.justification: Multiple countervailing channels (increased supply to formal jobs, changed outside options, firm cost pass-through, enforcement and tax incidence, selection/composition and general-equilibrium effects) mean the sign is highly context-specific and theoretically ambiguous; a structural model and rich institutional data help but strong identifying assumptions remain necessary to pin the sign.

triplet_key: 21562|casino payments (per-capita tribal cash transfers)|arguments with parent (frequency)
context: This empirical study uses the Great Smoky Mountains Study (annual waves from 1993) of 1,420 children in 11 counties in western North Carolina (oversampling Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians) to estimate effects of exogenous per‑capita casino transfers (~$4,000/year, disbursed mid‑1990s to enrolled adult tribal members) on adolescent personality, emotional, and behavioral outcomes. It compares younger American Indian cohorts exposed to payments before age 16 with older cohorts and non‑Indian families, exploiting panel data on children, parents, household characteristics, and census‑tract location.

difficulty.justification: Quasi‑experimental, cohort‑based variation in tribal cash transfers helps identification, so common policy/household‑level knowledge suffices, but effects depend heavily on tribal/institutional context, multiple competing channels (stress reduction, parental behavior change, composition/migration), and modest sample/measurement limits leave plausible alternative explanations.

context: The paper studies U.S. chemical invention across 8,422 USPTO subclasses from 1875–1939, exploiting the 1917 Trading with the Enemy Act (TWEA) and subsequent confiscation/licensing of German enemy patents (licenses mainly issued 1919–1921) as an exogenous shock. Using yearly patent counts by domestic inventors (subclass-year units) and firm-level (DuPont) patents, it assesses how compulsory licensing of enemy-owned patents affected domestic innovation in the U.S. chemical industry.

difficulty.justification: The TWEA/licensing episode provides a plausibly exogenous shock so intermediate domain knowledge suffices to see a positive channel, but multiple competing mechanisms (knowledge diffusion vs. reduced incentives, wartime demand, subclass heterogeneity) and institutional specifics make the sign context-dependent and nontrivial to pin down empirically.

context: This paper analyzes U.S. commercial banks in the 1980s (roughly 1980–1989/1981–1990) using a panel of 147 publicly traded banks across 36 states and DC. It exploits staggered state-level interstate banking deregulation (changes in state laws permitting out-of-state entry) to study how increased corporate-control market competitiveness affected CEO pay levels, pay structure (equity/options), and turnover. Units of observation are banks and bank CEOs; identification leverages cross-state and over-time variation in regulatory regimes with bank- and CEO-fixed effects and year controls.

difficulty.justification: Understanding this triplet requires intermediate institutional knowledge of banking deregulation and CEO contracting; the sign can vary with state-specific rules, bank size and charter, and is confounded by concurrent changes in risk-taking, market structure, and selection, so staggered-panel identification helps but does not fully eliminate plausible alternative explanations.

triplet_key: 23976|payroll tax cut for young workers|employer labor costs for young workers (gross wages)
treatment: Payroll tax cut for young workers
outcome: Employer labor costs for young workers (gross wages)
context: This paper studies Sweden’s 2007–2009 employer payroll‑tax reform that halved employer payroll taxes for young birth‑cohorts (eligibility up to age 26) using population administrative employer–employee records and firm accounting data spanning the 1990s–2013. Exploiting cohort eligibility rules, timing of the two reform steps, and cross‑firm variation in pre‑reform shares of young workers, it examines causal effects on individual net/gross wages, employment and transition rates, and firm outcomes (employment, sales, capital, profits); take‑up was automatic via payroll software.

difficulty.justification: Standard tax‑incidence theory gives ambiguous predictions because pass‑through depends on labor supply/demand elasticities, bargaining institutions, and composition effects, so the sign is not obvious a priori. The Swedish reform and rich quasi‑experimental data make empirical identification feasible, but theoretical ambiguity and multiple channels keep the task fairly difficult.

context: Analyzes U.S. quarterly macrodata on real per‑capita GDP, government purchases (consumption+investment), and net taxes mainly over 1960:Q1–1997:Q4 (with extensions back to 1947), using institutional details of the U.S. tax/transfer system and tax‑collection timing. Identifies effects of discretionary and automatic fiscal shocks (including the 1975:Q2 tax rebate and the 1950–52 Korean War buildup). Units are national quarterly aggregates; setting is U.S. fiscal policy and macroeconomic responses.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a negative effect of net tax increases on quarterly U.S. output requires specialized institutional and econometric knowledge to separate discretionary vs automatic shocks and timing effects; results are context- and policy-dependent and subject to substantial endogeneity/feedback from monetary policy and business-cycle responses, though narrative/institutional identification can make a credible inference.

context: Analyzes U.S. single women aged 19–44 over 1984–1996 using CPS March and ORG data. The paper exploits federal and state policy changes — large EITC expansions, federal/state income tax changes, AFDC/Food Stamp benefit and implicit-tax changes, Medicaid expansions, state welfare waivers, state EITCs, and shifts in child-care and training funding — across states and over time to estimate effects on employment and hours of single mothers relative to single childless women within the U.S. tax-transfer institutional setting.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a positive effect of welfare benefit reductions on single mothers' employment requires intermediate labor‑economics knowledge about taxes, transfers, and work incentives and careful empirical design; the sign is context‑sensitive and confounded by many concurrent policy changes (EITC, Medicaid, child care, waivers) and compositional/selection issues, so multiple mediating channels and plausible opposite explanations make credible identification moderately difficult.

context: This triplet refers to the effect of per-capita HOLC loans (1933–1936) on county-level median nonfarm owned-home values in U.S. counties with populations under 50,000 (1930), using county-level nonfarm housing data spanning 1920–1940. The setting is New Deal-era HOLC mortgage purchases and refinancings; the paper emphasizes smaller counties where local financial markets were less developed and reports a positive, significant relationship (elasticity ≈ 0.153).

difficulty.justification: Credible identification of a positive HOLC→home-value effect requires specialized historical and housing-finance knowledge because HOLC targeting, local credit market development, and program rules matter; the setting is moderately context-specific. Selection into loans, reverse causality, spatial spillovers, and composition/quality changes make the sign ambiguous without careful empirical strategies, and while the 1920–1940 panel is helpful, remaining confounders and measurement limits leave identification nontrivial.

context: This paper studies bank CoCo (contingent convertible) issuance from January 2009 to December 2015, using 731 issues ($521bn) and focusing empirically on banks in advanced economies (top 500 bank universe, bank-months and issuance events). The institutional setting is the post‑crisis Basel III framework (AT1/T2 classification, mechanical and discretionary triggers) and evolving resolution regimes (TLAC, BRRD). The authors analyze determinants of issuance and announcement effects on issuers’ CDS spreads and equity prices.

difficulty.justification: Understanding CoCo→CDS requires specialized knowledge of bank capital instruments and post‑crisis regulation; the sign depends on trigger design, issuance motive (strengthening capital vs. distress financing), market conditions and concurrent regulatory changes, so adverse-selection or signaling in the opposite direction is plausible and endogeneity is a key challenge despite a large sample.

context: The study analyzes U.S. NYSE/AMEX-listed firms drawn from the June 2004 Russell 3000, using stock-level and portfolio returns mainly from January 1980 through June 2007 (with post‑period checks to 2016). It exploits the SEC’s Regulation SHO pilot (May 2005–July 2007), which quasi-randomly exempted every third volume-ranked stock from short-sale price tests (lifting the uptick/bid tests), to compare pilot versus non-pilot stocks and identify how easing short-sale constraints causally affected 11 cross-sectional asset-pricing anomalies (with focus on short-leg dynamics).

difficulty.justification: Understanding and interpreting this triplet requires intermediate market-structure and short‑selling knowledge; the Regulation SHO pilot provides a credible quasi-experiment (so evidence is relatively strong), but multiple mediating channels, spillovers and market-wide dynamics make sign identification moderately complex and leave room for plausible alternative mechanisms.

context: This paper evaluates Pathways to Education, a comprehensive youth support program launched in Regent Park, Toronto in 2001 for Grade 9 cohorts and expanded to Rexdale and Lawrence Heights in 2007. Using administrative Toronto District School Board and housing postal-code data for students entering Grade 9 between 2000–2007 who resided in public housing projects, the authors compare treated (eligibility by residence) and comparison housing-project cohorts to estimate impacts on Grade 9 outcomes, five-year high-school graduation, and postsecondary enrollment. Pathways provided mentoring, mandatory tutoring, student-parent support workers, and financial incentives.

difficulty.justification: Eligibility-by-residence for a comprehensive support program has a clearly plausible positive mechanism (tutoring, mentoring, incentives), so intermediate domain knowledge suffices, but identification is complicated by nonrandom mobility, cohort and neighborhood trends, spillovers and uptake heterogeneity. With rich administrative data and phased expansion one can get credible estimates, but addressing selection, composition and general equilibrium/peer effects requires careful design.

triplet_key: 9909|patent systems|distribution of innovative activity across industries
outcome: Distribution of innovative activity across industries
context: This paper uses exhibit-level data from the Crystal Palace Exhibition (London, 1851) and the Centennial Exhibition (Philadelphia, 1876) to study how nineteenth-century domestic patent regimes affected innovation across countries. Units are individual exhibits (innovations) aggregated by country and industry for mostly Northern European states and the U.S. The institutional setting exploits variation in patent systems (existence and statutory patent length), selection/award information from uniform world-fair juries, and country controls (population, GDP, education, agricultural employment) to compare levels and industrial composition of innovation.

difficulty.justification: Evaluating how 19th-century patent regimes shifted the industrial composition of innovation requires specialized historical and institutional knowledge and is highly context-dependent across countries, industries, and fairs. Identification is challenged by selection into exhibitions, measurement and aggregation issues, and multiple confounding channels (demand, technology diffusion, policy bundles), so rigorous strategies are needed to credibly pin down even the sign.

context: Using commercial claims from a large insurer (covering tens of millions) over 2011–2015 across U.S. hospital emergency departments, the paper analyzes ED episodes and hospitals to study out-of-network physician billing. It exploits the entry timing of large ED outsourcing firms (EmCare, TeamHealth) into specific hospitals (2011–2015) and a 2014 New York arbitration law to estimate causal effects. Institutional context: hospitals outsource ED staffing, physicians and hospitals contract separately with insurers, producing surprise balance bills and a bargaining outside option for physicians.

difficulty.justification: Identifying that EmCare entry increases potential balance billing requires intermediate market and contracting knowledge and is highly sensitive to institutional features (network contracting, state laws). There are plausible confounders and multiple bargaining/selection channels, and although the entry timing and NY arbitration variation provide credible quasi-experiments, nonrandom entry and heterogeneous responses leave some identification uncertainty.

context: This paper uses the US National Longitudinal Survey of Youth Child–Mother data (children of the 1979 NLSY cohort, surveyed in 1986–1990) to estimate effects of participation in the federal Head Start preschool program on children’s cognitive scores, grade repetition, immunizations, and height, and on longer‑run outcomes for women. Units are children (and mothers with sister comparisons); the sample is national with oversamples of African‑Americans and Hispanics. Identification relies on within‑family comparisons to address time‑invariant family confounders.

difficulty.justification: Estimating Head Start effects on test scores requires substantial knowledge of program heterogeneity, selection into preschool, and child development; sibling fixed effects help but do not eliminate time‑varying confounding, spillovers, measurement and quality variation, so alternative signs and mixed results remain plausible without stronger identification.

context: This empirical study analyzes individuals who appealed initially denied Disability Insurance (DI) claims in Norway during 1994–2005 (with linked administrative records spanning 1967–2010 and appeals 1989–2011). The unit of analysis is DI appellants and their households (including spouses), using register data on earnings, taxes, transfers, assets and constructed consumption. The institutional setting is Norway’s national DI program and a written appeal process in Oslo with randomly assigned Administrative Law Judges, enabling quasi-experimental variation; analyses include multi-year dynamics and a structural household labor-supply model for welfare assessment.

difficulty.justification: Randomized assignment of judges gives strong quasi-experimental identification, so confounding is limited, but translating DI receipt into a monetary willingness-to-pay involves dynamic household labor-supply, consumption-smoothing and structural assumptions—requiring substantial domain expertise and moderately complex causal reasoning.

context: This paper studies causal effects of increases in bank size from postwar West German banking reforms (1947–1957) that broke up and later allowed reconsolidation of three national commercial banks, exploiting the 1952 zonal and 1957 national reconsolidations. Using newly digitized firm-level microdata on stock and non-stock firms (primarily 1951–1960), bank-level aggregates, and municipality outcomes, the author compares firms with ‘treated’ relationship banks to others to estimate how exogenous, reform-driven increases in bank size affected lending, employment, and productivity across West Germany.

difficulty.justification: Assessing whether larger banks reduce opaque-borrower growth requires specialized banking and institutional knowledge; effects are highly context-dependent and plausibly confounded by borrower sorting, local demand shocks, and bank behavior, though the postwar reform provides credible quasi-experimental leverage if identification assumptions hold.

context: This paper reports a cluster randomized field experiment implemented in 2011–2013 across 254 government-run peripheral health clinics in four Sierra Leone districts (Bombali, Tonkolili, Bo, Kenema), with surveys of ~5,080 households, clinics, and communities. Two accountability interventions (community monitoring and non‑financial awards) were randomized at the clinic level. The authors later link clinic treatment status to 2014–15 Ebola case (VHF) data aggregated to section-weeks to estimate longer-run effects on reporting and Ebola mortality.

difficulty.justification: A cluster-randomized trial gives credible identification, so specialized theory is not required, but the effect on under‑5 mortality depends on local health system capacity and can operate through multiple channels (utilization, quality, reporting) with potential spillovers and measurement/rare-event noise, making inference moderately challenging.

context: This paper studies non-U.S. firms’ foreign listing choices, valuations, and capital-raising from 1990–2005, comparing New York venues (NYSE, AMEX, NASDAQ, OTC/Rule 144a) to London markets (LSE Main Market, AIM). Using firm-level panel data on listings, Tobin’s q, ownership, and country legal/institutional variables, it evaluates whether regulatory changes—notably Sarbanes–Oxley (2002) and differing U.K. listing regimes—altered firms’ propensity to list, listing premia, and post-listing financing.

difficulty.justification: Identifying the valuation effect of a London listing requires specialized cross-listing and market-structure knowledge; the sign is highly context-dependent and confounded by strong selection, reverse causality, and multiple mediating channels, though regulatory shocks (e.g. SOX) and panel data offer useful but not definitive identification leverage.

context: This paper studies Greece’s macroeconomic boom (1998–2007) and prolonged depression (2007–2017), using national accounts, firm surveys, bank balance sheets, and financial‑market indicators for households, firms, banks, and the government in a small open‑economy euro‑area setting. It evaluates how external demand shocks, fiscal policy (spending/transfers and tax prepayments), sovereign and idiosyncratic risk, utilization and financial frictions, and international bailouts shaped output, consumption, wages, investment, and sectoral allocation.

difficulty.justification: Assessing whether external bailouts raised Greek output requires specialized knowledge of sovereign-financial linkages, conditionality and euro-area institutional constraints. The sign is highly context-dependent and entangled with simultaneous fiscal, banking and demand shocks, making credible identification difficult even with rich data.

context: This paper quantifies pro‑competitive and Ricardian gains from international trade by calibrating an oligopolistic heterogeneous‑firm model to Taiwanese manufacturing product‑level data (7‑digit) for 2000 and 2002–2004, matched to HS‑6 import data. Units are establishments and products; authors estimate cross‑sectional and panel relationships between sectoral sales shares, labor/capital shares and markups to recover elasticities and productivity distributions, then simulate changes in trade costs/tariffs. Complementary evidence from US manufacturing is used to corroborate pro‑competitive effects.

difficulty.justification: Pinning down welfare effects of eliminating markups in an oligopolistic heterogeneous‑firm/trade setting requires specialized structural knowledge (demand elasticities, passthrough, firm selection) and is sensitive to industry/institutional context; rich product‑level data help but strong identifying assumptions and general equilibrium/dynamic channels (entry, variety, quality/innovation, income distribution) make the sign nontrivial and alternative outcomes plausible without deeper analysis.

triplet_key: 18845|strategic trading by informed agents|speed of information revelation
context: This paper studies incorporation of private information into asset prices using 18th-century trans-Atlantic securities (British East India Company, Bank of England, 3% Annuities) traded in London and Amsterdam during 1771–1777 and 1783–1787. It exploits the twice-weekly official packet-boat mail service across the North Sea—whose weather-driven delays created exogenous market segmentation—to analyze security-level price returns and strategic insider trading. Institutional details include futures/spot trading conventions, intermediaries (e.g., Hope & Co.), and hand-collected boat arrival and wind records used to measure information flow.

difficulty.justification: Weather-driven exogenous market segmentation helps identification but the sign hinges on strategic concealment vs. aggressive trading, cross-market arbitrage, intermediaries and historical institutional details; multiple plausible channels and potential selection/measurement issues mean specialized theory and careful identification are needed.

context: This paper examines large U.S. industrial firms during the Great Depression (primarily 1928–1933, with bond maturities 1930–1934). Using a hand‑collected Moody’s‑Manual panel of ~1,010 firms, it links firm employment changes to pre‑existing corporate bonds maturing during the crisis and to local national‑bank suspensions across U.S. counties. The institutional setting features frozen public bond markets, a unit‑banking system, widespread national bank failures (1929–1933), limited equity issuance, and potential substitution between public and private debt.

difficulty.justification: Requires specialized knowledge of historical banking, corporate finance, and Great Depression institutions; key institutional features (unit banking, frozen bond markets, bond maturities) crucially affect mechanisms. Multiple confounders and channels—local demand shocks, contagion, substitution between public and private debt, maturity‑driven defaults—make the sign highly ambiguous and identification hard despite a rich hand‑collected panel.

context: This paper studies the causal effect of reductions in collectively-bargained standard weekly hours in (West) Germany, primarily 1984–1989 with additional analysis for 1990–1994, exploiting monthly industry‑level panel data on 201 manufacturing industries and individual-level German Socio‑Economic Panel data on full‑time workers. The institutional setting is industry-level union bargaining (notably IG Metall) that implemented stepwise cuts in standard hours, overtime premia, and greater hours flexibility; outcomes examined include employment, hours per worker, worker‑hours, and wage bills.

difficulty.justification: Evaluating whether cuts in collectively‑bargained standard hours raise straight‑time hourly pay requires substantial labor‑economics and institutional knowledge because effects operate through bargaining, overtime premia, hours flexibility and composition. The sign is context‑sensitive and confoundable by contemporaneous bargaining/wage shocks and demand changes; the industry panel and SOEP data help identification but multiple mediating channels and potential selection/measurement issues keep this moderately difficult to pin down.

context: This empirical study exploits the 1698 Dai Viet–Khmer boundary in southern Vietnam to estimate long-run effects of historical state institutions. It compares nearby villages/hamlets and households using historical maps (1878–1926), South Vietnamese-era hamlet and public-opinion surveys (1969–1973), income data (1970–72), and modern household consumption surveys (VHLSS 2002–2012). The setting contrasts centralized, village-based Dai Viet administration with patron-client Khmer governance, across episodes including French colonialism, the Vietnam War, and postwar communist rule.

difficulty.justification: Requires substantial institutional knowledge and advanced identification (historical RD) rather than mere common sense. The sign is context-dependent across colonial/war/postwar episodes, multiple mediating channels and plausible confounders (migration, colonial investment, geography, ethnic composition) make inference challenging though feasible with the rich, multi-period data used.

context: The paper analyzes annual country‑level banking‑sector balance‑sheet data for 17 advanced economies over 1870–2015 (country‑year units), examining how bank capital, deposits and non‑deposit (non‑core) debt relate to systemic banking crises and post‑crisis macroeconomic recoveries. Crises are narratively dated and business‑cycle peaks are used for local projections. The setting emphasizes long‑run institutional change in funding and regulation (post‑WWII shifts, rise of wholesale funding). Analyses exploit within‑country temporal variation with extensive macro controls and market‑based robustness checks.

difficulty.justification: The sign is economically plausible (higher loan-to-deposit implies greater funding/liquidity risk) but depends strongly on institutional regime, macro cycle and funding composition; reverse causality and omitted cyclical or regulatory shifts make identification nontrivial even with within-country controls and robustness checks.

context: This paper studies market reactions to China’s 2001–2002 policy episodes (the July 24, 2001 announcement to sell remaining state-owned A shares and the June 23, 2002 cancellation) using firm-level stock returns. The analysis uses 107 publicly traded B‑share firms listed on Shanghai and Shenzhen exchanges as the units, exploiting variation in government ownership, legal‑person shares, and senior managers’ prior government posts. The setting is China’s partial privatization with segmented A/B markets, non‑tradable state shares, SEZ heterogeneity, and strong local government discretion.

difficulty.justification: China-specific institutional details (A/B market segmentation, non‑tradable state shares, SEZ heterogeneity, local government discretion) and competing channels (bailout/connection benefits vs. agency/expropriation concerns) make the sign highly context-dependent and confoundable; event-based variation helps identification but substantial selection and mechanism complexity keep this hard to pin down.

triplet_key: 24632|higher labor income taxes in high-wage cities|worker reallocation to small low-wage cities
treatment: Higher labor income taxes in high-wage cities
outcome: Worker reallocation to small low-wage cities
context: Quantitative analysis of U.S. metropolitan areas (MSAs), using cross-sectional MSA-level data around 2007 on wages, employment, expenditures and trade flows, with workers divided into high-skill (college) and low-skill (non-college). The paper combines a general-equilibrium spatial model with calibration to U.S. data and draws spillover elasticities from existing estimates (e.g., Ciccone & Hall 1996; Diamond 2016) to evaluate optimal spatial transfer policies, worker reallocation, and welfare impacts across U.S. cities.

difficulty.justification: Determining that higher labor taxes in high‑wage MSAs causally push workers to small low‑wage cities requires specialized spatial general‑equilibrium theory and careful identification because migration responses depend on amenities, housing, moving costs, and fiscal externalities. Cross‑sectional calibration with borrowed spillover elasticities helps but is sensitive to parameter choices and tax endogeneity, so alternative signs (e.g., taxes financing desirable public goods that attract workers) remain plausible without strong empirical leverage.

context: This paper studies loan-level lending by 17 Mexican banks in the mid-1990s, focusing on the top-300 loans (snapshot Dec 1995/March 1996) and a random sample of ~1,500 loans followed through December 1999. It compares loans to related borrowers (bank owners, officers, family, or firms the bank owns) versus unrelated ones across the 1994 devaluation and ensuing banking crisis, in a setting of privatized banks, weak supervision and blanket deposit insurance (FOBAPROA). Units are individual loans/borrowers and bank-level episodes of distress.

difficulty.justification: Identifying that related lending causally reduces recovery rates requires intermediate specialized knowledge of banking governance and crisis dynamics; the sign is highly context-dependent (privatized banks, blanket insurance, weak supervision) and confounded by borrower selection, bank distress, and regulatory interventions. Multiple mediating channels and remaining unobservables mean evidence from loan-level panels helps but may not fully rule out alternative explanations without strong identification.

triplet_key: 20653|establishment of community health centers|medicare spending per-enrollee
context: Focusing on county-year outcomes, this triplet examines whether the 1965–1974 rollout of Community Health Centers affected county-level Medicare spending per enrollee. The analysis uses county-level Medicare administrative spending (Medicare per-enrollee expenditures available from 1966 onward, with total per-capita medical spending available 1959–1988) and exploits cross-county, staggered timing of CHC establishment under the Office of Economic Opportunity during the War-on-Poverty period.

difficulty.justification: Determining a null effect requires specialized health‑policy and causal‑inference knowledge because CHCs could plausibly raise or lower Medicare spending via multiple substitution, access, and billing channels; staggered rollout and administrative data help identification but endogenous placement, spillovers, concurrent policy changes, and complex GE/selection pathways make the sign hard to pin down.

context: This paper analyzes the randomized Perry Preschool Project that enrolled 123 disadvantaged African-American children born in the 1960s in Ypsilanti, Michigan. The program provided two years of high-quality preschool plus weekly teacher home visits starting at age three; controls received no treatment. The authors follow the original participants through age 54 using surveys and administrative criminal/education records and link to outcomes for their biological children into young adulthood to estimate intergenerational effects on cognition, health, education, crime, employment, and family stability.

difficulty.justification: The original RCT provides a credible source of exogenous variation in parents' early experiences, but translating that into a positive effect on the next generation's home environment requires dealing with selection into parenthood/differential fertility, attrition and small sample size, and multiple mediating channels (income, education, behavior), so the sign is plausible but not straightforward to identify.

triplet_key: 29800|ssi removal at age 18|annual likelihood of incarceration
context: This paper estimates effects of losing Supplemental Security Income (SSI) at age 18 due to the 1996 federal welfare reform (PRWORA) medical‑review rule that applied adult eligibility criteria to children with 18th birthdays after August 22, 1996. Using U.S. Social Security administrative records (SSI receipt, earnings, family measures) linked to Criminal Justice Administrative Records System (CJARS) court and corrections data, the authors follow SSI‑recipient children (1976–98 birth cohorts) in jurisdictions with CJARS coverage from ages 18–38 (1997–2017) to study criminal charges, incarceration, employment, and Medicaid channels.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a positive effect is aided by a clear policy cutoff and rich administrative linkages, but the sign depends on local institutional context (Medicaid, labor market, CJARS coverage) and multiple mediating channels (income, health care access, employment, family dynamics), leaving plausible alternative mechanisms and measurement concerns that complicate causal attribution.

context: This paper analyzes two large-scale representative U.S. surveys conducted Feb–May 2019 (n≈2,780 for income tax; n≈2,360 for estate tax) of individuals aged 18–69. It studies knowledge, beliefs, and normative views about U.S. federal income and estate taxation, elicits perceived behavioral and distributional mechanisms, and implements randomized short instructional video treatments to test how pedagogical explanations (Redistribution, Efficiency, Economist) causally shift support for progressive tax policies.

difficulty.justification: Random assignment of short instructional videos gives credible within‑sample causal identification, so detecting a positive effect is relatively straightforward; however framing effects depend on message wording, prior beliefs, political identity and external validity (estate tax salience), making broader inference and heterogeneous responses moderately uncertain.

context: This paper studies U.S. county-level farmland markets and local banking from roughly 1900–1960, focusing on the WWI-era commodity boom (1917–1920) and the subsequent 1920s collapse. Units are counties (with banks, farms, and bank failures as sub-units). It uses Census and Department of Agriculture land-price and farm data plus county bank counts/deposits and exploits state banking regulations (unit vs branch banking), deposit insurance, Federal Reserve district and state borders, and pre-boom bank presence to isolate exogenous variation in local credit availability and its effects on prices, indebtedness, bank failures, and long-run outcomes.

difficulty.justification: Requires substantial historical banking and land-market knowledge and is very sensitive to institutional and temporal context; opposite mechanisms (credit amplifying vs smoothing price responses) and multiple feedbacks/selection channels make sign identification highly ambiguous, though the paper's rich institutional instruments can provide moderately sufficient evidence.

triplet_key: 14759|restricting the choice set to three lowest cost options|welfare
treatment: Restricting the choice set to three lowest cost options
context: This paper studies Medicare Part D plan choices by U.S. Medicare beneficiaries aged 65+ around the 2006 rollout, using 2005–2006 Wolters Kluwer longitudinal prescription claims matched to CMS plan menus. It focuses on individual elders’ choices among dozens of stand-alone PDPs (typical county choice set ≈40 plans) under the newly privatized Part D benefit (premiums, deductibles, copays, and “donut hole” rules), excluding dual eligibles and employer-sponsored plans, to evaluate whether seniors optimize given premiums, expected out-of-pocket costs, and risk.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate knowledge of insurance choice and behavioral frictions; the sign depends on heterogeneity, coverage details, and tradeoffs between choice overload (helpful to restrict) versus excluding plans that better match some beneficiaries, and credible counterfactual welfare calculation typically needs a structural model and strong identifying assumptions despite rich claims/menus data.

context: Using DOE SBIR Phase 1 application and ranking data (1995–2013) linked to U.S. Census firm records and IRS W-2s (earnings 2005–2013), the paper studies small, U.S.-based high‑tech firms that applied to DOE Fossil Energy and EERE competitions. The institutional setting is DOE SBIR grants (≈$150k Phase 1), awarded by ranked cutoffs with no ex-post spending restrictions. Units of analysis are firms and their employees; the design exploits ranking cutoffs and panel timing to estimate causal effects of one‑time cash grants on wages, employment, and revenue.

difficulty.justification: The RD-like ranking cutoff and linked admin data give reasonably strong identification, but the sign of effects on the wage-tenure profile is driven by multiple competing channels (productivity-driven pay increases, hiring of different skill mixes, turnover/tenure dynamics and sorting) that vary by firm, industry, and timing; distinguishing composition versus within-worker wage effects and potential spillovers remains challenging.

context: This analysis exploits Canada’s 2001 reform that extended job‑protected parental leave for births on/after Dec 31, 2000, using individual‑level Canadian microdata (primarily the 2006 long‑form Census) to measure self‑employment about five years after the cutoff among parents (first‑time singleton mothers versus fathers) across provinces. For this triplet the paper reports heterogeneous effects concentrated on individuals who had been wage‑employed in industries with a high wage penalty for leaving and then returning from entrepreneurship.

difficulty.justification: The policy effect is economically plausible and interpretable with intermediate labor‑market and entrepreneurship knowledge and benefits from a clear birthdate cutoff design, but the sign depends on institutional context (leave generosity, childcare, industry re-entry norms) and heterogeneous mechanisms (wage‑penalty avoidance, human‑capital depreciation, selection) leave room for alternative explanations that require careful measurement to rule out.

context: This empirical study uses the Russian Longitudinal Monitoring Survey (household panel) for 1998 and 2000–2004 to evaluate Russia’s January 2001 personal income tax reform that replaced a progressive schedule with a 13% flat tax. The unit of analysis is households (and heads of household) across Russian regions; outcomes include reported income, consumption, and the consumption–income gap as a proxy for evasion. The paper exploits pre/post timing, tax-bracket thresholds, and regional administration/enforcement variation to identify voluntary compliance, productivity, and welfare effects.

difficulty.justification: Identifying whether Russia’s switch to a 13% flat tax reduced evasion needs intermediate tax-compliance knowledge and is sensitive to institutional features (enforcement, informality), multiple channels (reporting incentives, labor supply, formalization), and measurement error in the consumption–income gap. The paper's panel and exploitation of thresholds and regional enforcement strengthen identification but residual confounding and imperfect proxying keep the sign nontrivial to pin down.

context: This triplet reflects a theoretical mechanism derived within the paper's micro-founded endogenous-growth model of the energy sector: firms choose between clean and dirty technologies and direct R&D accordingly. The model (which the authors also estimate with U.S. energy-sector microdata from 1975–2004) implies that higher carbon taxes raise the relative profitability of clean intermediates and therefore increase firms' incentives and aggregate clean research effort.

difficulty.justification: Theory straightforwardly predicts a positive effect in endogenous-growth frameworks, but the sign depends on institutional details (pass-through, complementary policies, firm selection) and can be offset by profit compression; identifying the net effect requires handling complex general-equilibrium, selection, and empirical endogeneity issues.

context: This analysis focuses on U.S. OTC firms quoted on the Pink Sheets (NBB) using the PS tier history from October 2007 through October 2010. The unit is firm-month observations for Pink Sheets and Grey Market firms; regressions use firm fixed effects and a within-firm difference-in-differences exploiting four Pink Sheets regulatory changes (relative to the Bulletin Board) to test how higher Pink Sheets information tiers relate to future crash risk.

difficulty.justification: Understanding OTC tiers and crash risk needs intermediate market/regulatory knowledge; the sign depends on institutional details (time, Pink Sheets rules) and there are plausible opposing mechanisms (transparency can both reduce or reveal downside risk). The provided within‑firm diff‑in‑diff is helpful, but selection, anticipatory behavior and multiple mediating channels leave nontrivial identification challenges.

triplet_key: 8145|establishment of the sugar institute|output of atlantic refiners
context: This paper studies collusion in the U.S. cane-sugar refining industry surrounding the formation and operation of the Sugar Institute (formed December 1927; active through 1936), combining executive meeting notes (Louis V. Placé’s weekly memos, Jan 1929–mid‑1930) with industry price, margin and quantity series. Units of analysis are refiners (firm-level behavior and meetings) and industry aggregates (Atlantic refiners), in the institutional setting of detailed Institute rules, rising beet and Cuban imports, the Great Depression, and an eventual 1936 Supreme Court antitrust ruling.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a negative effect of the Sugar Institute on Atlantic refiners’ output requires substantial historical and antitrust knowledge (industry rules, cartel practice) and is sensitive to setting (Great Depression, rising imports, legal changes). Multiple confounders and channels (price/margin incentives from collusion, demand collapse, import competition, firm-level allocation) make sign inference nontrivial though firm minutes and series can provide plausible evidence with careful identification.

context: This triplet examines California school districts, using HMDA-derived data on new homebuyers (mapped to districts) from 1992–2006. The unit is the district-year characteristics of in-migrating homebuyers (average income and racial composition measured by the shares white and Asian). Identification exploits close local school bond referenda (2/3 or 55% thresholds) via a regression-discontinuity design extended to panel/dynamic treatment effects.

difficulty.justification: Understanding requires intermediate knowledge of housing and school-quality signaling; the sign is context-dependent and could reverse because of offsetting tax versus amenity/gentrification channels, and identification must contend with selection, dynamic and general-equilibrium effects even though the RD provides a credible local LATE.

triplet_key: 26591|city productivity and population|strictness of planning regulations
context: The paper analyzes systems of US metropolitan areas (county-based 1999 definitions, ≈275 cities) using city-, block-group-, household-, trip-, and longitudinal worker-level data spanning roughly mid-20th century to 2010 (with microdata windows such as CPS 1986–2016, ACS 2008–2012, NHTS 2008–09). It studies how human-capital spillovers, commuting and housing costs, and local planning regulations (zoning/restrictiveness) determine city sizes and affect aggregate income and growth in the US institutional setting.

difficulty.justification: Identifying that more productive/larger cities cause stricter planning requires intermediate urban‑economics and political‑economy knowledge and is sensitive to institutional setting (state law, geography, era). Reverse causality, omitted factors, and strong general‑equilibrium/selection feedbacks make the sign plausible but nontrivial to pin down even with rich US microdata.

context: This paper studies French firms using administrative firm panel data (FICUS) matched to patents (PATSTAT) over 1994–2007, exploiting size-contingent labor regulations that sharply increase at 50 employees. Units are firms (manufacturing exporters for dynamic tests). Identification uses discontinuities near the 50-worker threshold and firm-level export demand shocks constructed from product–destination changes (shift-share). The institutional setting is France’s labor law (works councils, firing/consultation requirements) and the authors combine reduced-form regressions with a structural Schumpeterian growth model and calibrated counterfactuals.

difficulty.justification: Credible institutional RD at the 50-employee threshold and rich administrative-patent data give a strong starting point, but identifying a negative aggregate innovation effect requires substantial domain knowledge about firm-level responses, and is sensitive to institutional context, selection/manipulation around the cut-off, spillovers and general-equilibrium reallocation—issues that make the causal reasoning complex even if empirical evidence is fairly informative.

context: This triplet refers to a structural counterfactual simulation for four crisis‑hit euro‑area periphery countries (Greece, Spain, Ireland, Portugal). Within the paper’s country‑year 2000–2012 framework and open‑economy heterogeneous‑household model, the authors simulate a 10% “fiscal devaluation” implemented in 2009 and report outcomes (exports, recession dynamics, and public debt) through 2012. The unit of analysis is the national (country‑level) counterfactual within the model; the reported effect is a lower public‑debt level by 2012.

difficulty.justification: Evaluating whether a fiscal devaluation reduces public debt requires advanced open‑economy macro and fiscal-policy knowledge and is highly sensitive to country institutions, timing, and calibration; multiple opposing channels (demand contraction, competitiveness gains, revenue composition, exchange‑rate pass‑through and GE feedbacks) make the sign ambiguous without a well‑specified structural model and strong empirical anchors.

context: This paper studies four large unemployment insurance (UI) benefit reforms in Austria (1976, 1985, 1989, 2001) using administrative social-security and UI registry data covering workers and firms from 1972 onward. The unit is individual employees (age 25–54) and their employers; reforms differentially changed UI benefit levels across earnings-based reference wages, in a setting with broad UI eligibility, high take-up (quitters eligible), no experience rating, and linked unemployment assistance. The authors compare treated and nearby control earnings percentiles to estimate wage responses to exogenous UI benefit shifts.

difficulty.justification: Multiple plausible channels (reservation-wage/search effects, firm bargaining/compensation responses, sorting/spillovers) make the sign ambiguous and context-sensitive, but the setting’s institutional details and rich administrative data with local percentile comparisons provide reasonably strong identification, so the task is moderately difficult to hard.

context: This paper empirically evaluates districting plans used to elect U.S. state lower‑house legislators during the 1990s redistricting cycle (elections 1992, 1994, 1996, 1998, 2000) across 28 single‑member‑district states. The unit of observation is state legislative districts aggregated to state seat‑vote curves. Data include district‑level election returns, Census district demographics, and survey estimates of Independents. The analysis tests a micro‑founded model’s optimal seat‑vote curve, implementability conditions, and welfare gains from alternative districting (including proportional representation).

difficulty.justification: Assessing whether implementing an optimal seat‑vote curve raises aggregate welfare requires advanced political‑economy theory and careful identification to map electoral rules into policy and utility outcomes. The sign depends strongly on institutional details, strategic responses, and counterfactual districting assumptions, and available data typically leave welfare measurement and confounding underdetermined.

triplet_key: 28029|issuer capital intensity × balance-sheet cost|consumer credit risk premium
context: Analyzes U.S. credit-card asset-backed securities and issuer master trusts over 2000–2020 (monthly observations) for the ten largest card issuers, using tranche prices, portfolio yields, and charge-offs to identify the market risk premium on unsecured consumer credit. Exploits the post-2008 regulatory environment — notably the Jan 1, 2010 FAS 166/167 reconsolidation, Dodd-Frank risk-retention and Basel III capital rules — comparing pre/post-crisis dynamics and cross-sectional issuer variation in balance-sheet capital intensity to infer causal effects.

difficulty.justification: Identification requires specialized knowledge of securitization accounting and post‑2008 regulatory mechanics; the sign is highly context‑dependent and plausibly confounded by issuer selection, macro and market conditions, and alternative channels, and it involves complex mediating and feedback mechanisms, although the institutional shocks and rich tranche/charge‑off data provide useful but not definitive leverage.

context: This triplet refers to the demand/beliefs experiment on Early Childhood Development (ECD) conducted at 14 CNM national and regional meetings in 2017–2018. The randomized partisan-support message (truthfully stating recent leftist governments’ support for ECD) was randomly assigned to participants—primarily mayors, vice-mayors, municipal secretaries and local legislators—among 900 officials from 657 municipalities, and was presented before eliciting priors, willingness-to-pay (WTP), and subsequent belief-updating.

difficulty.justification: Interpreting how a partisan-support message shifts priors, WTP and belief-updating requires intermediate political-economy knowledge (motivated reasoning, credibility signaling) and is sensitive to officials' partisan alignment and local context. Randomized assignment gives good internal identification, but heterogeneous mechanisms, demand effects, and external validity leave moderate ambiguity about the sign and generalizability.

context: This empirical study evaluates long-run effects of city-council dismissals (CCDs) for alleged Mafia infiltration in Italy, analyzing 245 CCD events from 1991–2016 across municipalities in ten regions. It uses administrative employer–employee social security data (1983–2017), real-estate, public-procurement, politician, and municipal finance records to measure impacts on firms, employment, wages, and real-estate. Institutional context: the central government investigates alleged Mafia ties and appoints commissioners to run dismissed municipalities for about two years before new elections, creating plausibly exogenous variation for causal analysis.

difficulty.justification: Plausibly exogenous dismissals and rich administrative panels make identification feasible, but the institutional specificity (Mafia context), multiple opposing channels (disruption vs. anti-corruption benefits), and risk of selection/anticipation effects mean the positive sign is nontrivial to establish convincingly.

triplet_key: 20103|mothers' pension cash transfers|probability of survival past age 70
context: This paper studies long-run effects of early-20th-century cash transfers by analyzing male children born 1900–1925 whose mothers applied (before 1930) to state Mothers’ Pension programs (1911–1935) in 11 U.S. states. Using administrative application records (accepted vs rejected), linked to Social Security death records, WWII enlistment files and the 1940 census, it evaluates impacts on longevity, schooling, nutrition and adult income for boys raised in female-headed poor households receiving modest monthly pensions.

difficulty.justification: Understanding this triplet requires intermediate knowledge of historical welfare programs and life-course mechanisms; program rules and local implementation matter. The sign is plausibly positive but vulnerable to selection, migration and measurement confounders and involves multiple long-run mediating channels, though the accepted-vs-rejected administrative design provides moderately strong (but not definitive) evidence.

context: This triplet is evaluated within a calibrated general equilibrium tax model applied to Canada, capturing the Canadian GST introduction in 1990. The analysis focuses on household-level supply choices and informal-sector producers (e.g., small-scale construction and untaxed services), with tax-induced substitution into household production and underground activity. The model treats informal-sector supply as predominantly from lower-income households selling tax-free output to higher-income buyers.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether VAT base broadening raises informal activity requires specialized knowledge of tax incidence, household production and underground markets; the sign depends strongly on enforcement, demand elasticities, income composition and market structure, making the opposite sign plausible. Calibrated GE models can illuminate channels and feedbacks but empirical measurement of informality is difficult, so inference remains moderately uncertain.

context: Using the paper's 1960–1995 five‑year country‑period panel, this triplet examines how a constructed federalism (political decentralization) index affects urban primacy. The federalism index is available only for a subset of about 40 countries (those with population >10 million in 1990) with values 1960–1995; results (Table 5, column 2) come from first‑differenced GMM panel regressions, with an OLS cross‑country check. Specifications control for income, national scale, openness, and transport infrastructure where applicable; estimated federalism effects are modestly negative.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether political decentralization reduces urban primacy requires substantial political‑economy and urban‑economics knowledge and is highly context‑dependent (institutions, history, geography). Strong endogeneity and measurement concerns, multiple mediating channels, and a small selected sample with a constructed index make the sign highly confoundable and hard to identify convincingly.

context: This lecture synthesizes empirical studies from the late 20th and early 21st centuries using administrative, lottery, and census data across settings: Israeli elementary schools (Maimonides Rule), U.S. urban school systems (Boston, New York, Chicago; KIPP Lynn lotteries 2005–08; Chicago exam schools 2009–12), and national samples (e.g., 1980–1990 Censuses). Units include students, school applicants, and families/mothers. Identification exploits institutional features—lotteries, admission cutoffs, centralized assignment, class-size rules, and fertility shocks—to estimate causal effects on achievement, school choice, and labor supply.

difficulty.justification: Randomized lotteries and quasi-experiments give credible identification of positive impacts in many settings, but effects vary by institutional context, selection/attrition and multiple mediating channels (peer, teacher quality, class size), so sign is plausible but not universally guaranteed.

context: The paper analyzes the causal effects of an unexpected removal of employers’ standardized ‘preferred gender’ field from all job ads on XMRC.com (a Xiamen, China job board) on March 1, 2019. Using internal ad-, application-, resume- and call-back records for roughly one year surrounding the ban, the authors study ad- and application-level outcomes (jobs, workers, firms), exploiting within-ad pre/post changes, high-frequency (daily/weekly) time variation, and a 2018–2019 lunar-calendar comparison. Only the encoded gender label was removed; embedded text preferences remained.

difficulty.justification: Identifying this sign requires intermediate labor-market knowledge and depends importantly on institutional and cultural context (online ad design, norms in China, embedded text remaining). Rich within-ad, high-frequency administrative data make identification reasonably credible, but plausible alternative channels (applicant selection, employers changing ad text, unobserved concurrent shifts) keep the problem moderately challenging.

context: This paper evaluates Chile’s P-900 program (1990–1992), which targeted roughly 900 low-performing fourth-grade schools across 13 regions using 1988 mean test-score cutoffs. Using school-level fourth-grade test scores from 1984, 1988, 1990, and 1992, the authors analyze program impacts on school-level gain scores, exploiting the cutoff-based assignment, regional cutoff variation, and school observables (enrollment, SES) to address selection and mean-reversion.

difficulty.justification: The cutoff-based assignment with multiple pre-periods and regional variation makes identification plausible but not trivial: concerns remain about mean reversion, manipulation or sorting around cutoffs, spillovers and composition changes at the school level, and contemporaneous regional reforms that could bias estimated gains.
