context: Using de-identified linked Census short forms, federal tax records, and the ACS covering roughly the U.S. population from 1989–2015, the paper studies intergenerational income mobility for the 1978–1983 birth cohorts in the United States. Units are children linked to parents (parent income measured 1994–2000; children’s incomes 2014–15), analyzed across geographies (Census tracts, blocks, commuting zones). The design exploits variation in parental characteristics, childhood neighborhood exposure, and timing of moves to assess drivers of racial gaps in adult earnings and related outcomes.

difficulty.justification: Plausible negative effect via reduced exposure to crime and better schools, but strong selection into moving, heterogeneity by race and policing/institutional context, and measurement/identification challenges (even with timing variation and rich linked administrative data) make sign identification nontrivial.

context: This paper estimates effects of Boston Public Schools’ K1 preschool for first-time four-year-old applicants (1997–2003) using randomized school-assignment tie-break lotteries, linked to Massachusetts DESE administrative records and the National Student Clearinghouse with roughly 20 years of follow-up. The SAT Math top-quartile outcome is measured using Massachusetts public-school records and is restricted to applicants observed with a 10th-grade MCAS score in the state data (non-takers are coded as zeros for top-quartile indicators).

difficulty.justification: Randomized lottery assignment provides strong identification (low confounding) so the sign is relatively straightforward, but long-run follow-up, attrition/selection into observed MCAS/SAT records and the coding of non-takers as zeros create measurable threats and limit external generalizability to other preschool contexts.

context: This paper examines 88 private equity buyouts of U.S. for-profit, Title IV–eligible colleges from 1987–2016, covering 994 school UnitIDs and their parent SystemIDs. Using administrative IPEDS and College Scorecard/NSLDS data, it studies how buyouts and policy changes in the federal student-aid system—notably the 2007 Stafford loan limit increases and Title IV revenue rules—affect tuition, enrollment, per-student borrowing, graduation, loan repayment, earnings, and regulatory enforcement.

difficulty.justification: Determining that PE buyouts causally reduce graduation rates requires specialized knowledge of for‑profit higher‑education incentives and federal Title IV rules, is highly sensitive to institutional and temporal context, is subject to strong selection/confounding and plausible opposing mechanisms (operational improvement vs. scraping rents), and involves multiple mediating and composition channels that administrative data only partly resolve.

triplet_key: 23473|three-bidder irs rule (reduce tax shield when <3 bidders)|bidder markups
treatment: Three-bidder IRS rule (reduce tax shield when <3 bidders)
context: Analyzes 14,631 competitive municipal bond auctions in the United States between 2008 and 2015 (issuers: states, cities, counties, school districts), linking auction outcomes (winning interest-rate bids and participation by underwriters) to variation in the effective personal income tax advantage for municipal-bond holders (driven by state and federal tax changes, including SALT interactions). Institutional setting: first-price sealed-bid underwriter auctions, limited supply responsiveness, and later policy shocks (TCJA17, IRS three-bidder rule) and federal/state tax reforms used for counterfactuals.

difficulty.justification: Identifying this sign requires specialized auction- and tax-policy knowledge because tax advantages, bidder participation, and underwriter pricing interact in non-obvious ways; institutional details (auction format, entry costs, state tax regimes) matter a lot. Reasonable identification is possible given large sample and policy shocks, but concurrent reforms, selection into low-bidder auctions, and multiple strategic channels make opposite explanations plausible without careful design.

context: This paper reports a randomized field experiment conducted in 2007 across 20 rural prenatal clinics in four districts of western Kenya (Busia, Bungoma, Butere, Mumias), randomizing clinic-level prices of long-lasting insecticide-treated bed nets (ITNs) offered to pregnant women. The study records baseline hemoglobin at prenatal visits, ITN take-up, and follow-up in-home usage weeks later, set against an environment of active PSI social-marketing and recent mass free-net campaigns, to evaluate how cost-sharing versus free distribution affects demand, selection, and effective coverage.

difficulty.justification: Clinic-level randomization of price helps identification, but hemoglobin is affected by multiple delayed individual and community channels (take-up, use, infection reduction, herd effects) and by concurrent free-net campaigns and other health inputs; power, spillovers, selection on usage, and timing make establishing a true null especially difficult.

context: This paper investigates how international trade openness affects urban sulfur dioxide (SO2) concentrations using panel data from the Global Environment Monitoring System (GEMS) covering 1971–1996. The unit of observation is site‑year SO2 median concentrations for 293 monitoring sites in 109 cities across 44 countries. The analysis links site‑level pollution to country‑level GDP per km2, capital–labor ratios, lagged GDP per capita, and trade openness (exports+imports/GDP), controlling for site fixed effects, weather, and time trends to decompose scale, composition and technique effects.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether trade openness raises, lowers, or has no effect on local SO2 requires specialized knowledge of scale/composition/technique channels, heterogeneous local institutions and meteorology, and strong identification to address endogeneity and measurement mismatch between country-level trade and site-level pollution; given these multiple plausible opposing mechanisms and data limitations, the sign is hard to pin down.

triplet_key: 8629|higher entry subsidy rate|export revenues per peso spent
context: Using annual plant-level panel data on 62 Colombian chemical plants operating continuously during 1982–1991, this paper estimates a dynamic structural model of firms’ export participation and export revenue decisions. The institutional setting is Colombia’s trade and exchange-rate environment (real peso depreciation and export subsidy regimes) in the 1980s–early 1990s. Units are manufacturing plants; key features include firm heterogeneity in marginal costs and foreign demand, stochastic AR processes for exchange rates and profit shocks, and stochastic sunk start-up and fixed costs of exporting. The estimated model is used to simulate devaluations and subsidy policies.

difficulty.justification: Correctly signing this effect requires specialized knowledge of dynamic trade models and identification strategies; the sign depends on institutional details (exchange-rate dynamics, subsidy design) and is confounded by selection into exporting and heterogeneous firm responses, while the available panel and simulation-based counterfactuals leave substantive but not definitive empirical gaps.

context: Using Danish matched employer–employee administrative data over 2003–2008, the paper studies firms and individual employees around a legal threshold (firms with ≥35 employees) after Denmark’s 2006 Act No. 562 requiring firms above the cutoff to produce gender-disaggregated wage statistics (effective 2007). The analysis focuses on firms with 20–50 employees (treated: 35–50; controls: 20–34) to estimate how mandated pay-transparency affected wages, hiring, promotions, productivity, and profits in the private sector.

difficulty.justification: A regression‑discontinuity using rich Danish matched admin data gives fairly credible local identification of effects on departures, so moderate domain and evidence requirements; nevertheless sorting around the firm‑size cutoff, heterogeneous effects across worker groups and limited power/local external validity make a zero‑effect conclusion nontrivial.

context: The paper studies firm-level stock-market liquidity across 26 European countries from 2001–2011 (about 112,260 firm-quarter observations, ~4,846 firms), exploiting staggered national transposition dates of two EU directives—the Market Abuse Directive (MAD) and the Transparency Directive (TPD)—that tightened insider-trading and disclosure rules. It analyzes regulated versus unregulated market listings, constructs a liquidity factor from bid-ask spreads, price impact, zero returns, and trading costs, and examines heterogeneity by prior regulatory quality, supervisory powers, penalties, and regulator staffing while controlling for other FSAP directives.

difficulty.justification: Understanding the sign requires intermediate market-microstructure and regulatory knowledge; the effect plausibly varies by enforcement, market structure, and timing, and alternative mechanisms (compliance costs, altered trading incentives) could produce the opposite sign. The paper's staggered-transposition panel helps identification, but simultaneous reforms, enforcement heterogeneity, and composition/selection channels make clean sign identification fairly challenging.

context: This paper studies causal effects of local state capacity on public goods provision and prosperity across Colombian municipalities (~1,019 observations), using municipal-level state presence measured circa 1995 and outcomes spanning the 1990s–2000s (e.g., enrollment 1992–2002, utilities 2002, poverty 1993 & 2005). Identification exploits 18th-century colonial records and the colonial “royal road” network as historical instruments. The setting emphasizes Colombia’s decentralized institutional structure (locally vs nationally assigned agencies) and spatial adjacency, allowing for network spillovers and strategic complementarities in state-building.

difficulty.justification: Establishing a positive causal effect of local state capacity on life quality requires specialized institutional knowledge and identification strategies (long-run persistence, valid instruments), while being complicated by spatial spillovers, selection, and multiple mediating channels; the colonial-instrument approach is promising but leaves important threats to exclusion and heterogeneous/context-specific effects.

context: This paper uses firm- and plant-level panels (up to half a million firms; IT establishment sample ≈160,000) across 12 European countries over 1996–2007 to estimate how rising Chinese import competition — especially after China’s 2001 WTO accession and the phased abolition of textile/apparel quotas (MFA reforms in 2002 and 2005) — affected firm innovation (patents, R&D), IT adoption, TFP, employment and survival. Identification exploits differential industry exposure to quota removal and pre-existing Chinese export shares, with firm and industry fixed effects and trend controls.

difficulty.justification: Quota removal is a plausible source of exogenous variation, but multiple opposing channels (escape‑competition innovation vs. resource/exit effects), strong heterogeneity across firms/industries, and selection/composition issues make the sign context‑dependent and moderately ambiguous despite a large panel design.

triplet_key: 16007|paying brokers with both cash and lender commission|higher total closing charges
treatment: Paying brokers with both cash and lender commission
context: This paper analyzes 1,525 FHA‑insured, brokered 30‑year fixed‑rate purchase mortgages in the United States during a six‑week window in 2001, using HUD‑1 settlement disclosures (including yield‑spread premiums) and lenders’ rate‑sheet information. The unit of observation is the loan/borrower transaction. Institutional setting: mortgage brokers who receive cash closing charges and lender-paid yield‑spread premiums (YSP), RESPA/HUD‑1 disclosure rules, and brokers who do not bear default risk. The authors combine distributional quantile estimation with a structural auction-style shopping model to assess borrower confusion and overpayment.

difficulty.justification: Identifying the sign requires specialized knowledge of mortgage broker incentives, RESPA/YSP mechanics and borrower shopping, and is sensitive to institutional details and selection; multiple mediating channels and equilibrium/selection concerns make causal identification complex, though the rich HUD‑1 transaction data and structural modeling provide reasonable (but not definitive) leverage.

context: This paper evaluates the causal impact of attending Boston charter and pilot public schools using student-level administrative and MCAS test-score data from roughly 2001–2008. The unit of analysis is individual Boston Public School students (elementary, middle, and high school cohorts). The study exploits randomized admissions lotteries at over-subscribed charter and some pilot schools as a quasi-experiment, compares lottery-based IV estimates to observational regressions with baseline scores and demographics, and situates findings in Massachusetts’ charter/pilot institutional setting (union rules, charter caps).

difficulty.justification: Lottery-based quasi-experiment provides strong identification and limits confounding, so the sign is relatively straightforward to establish; however, institutional specifics (Boston, charter/pilot rules, time period), LATE interpretation, spillovers, and attrition limit external validity and require intermediate domain knowledge to interpret.

context: For this triplet, the authors analyze 231 publicly traded U.S. bank holding companies (assets > $1bn), measuring holdings of AAA/AA/A securitization tranches as of December 31, 2006 (constructed from FR Y-9C regulatory filings) and relating those holdings to buy-and-hold excess stock returns over the crisis window July 1, 2007–December 31, 2008 using cross-sectional regressions with firm-level controls.

difficulty.justification: Interpreting this triplet requires intermediate financial and banking knowledge; the negative effect is strongly context-dependent (crisis window) and multiple channels (mark-to-market losses, funding strains, signaling) and omitted bank characteristics make the sign reasonably confoundable. Measuring holdings pre-crisis helps identification, but cross-sectional regressions with controls may not fully eliminate alternative explanations, so inference is moderately difficult.

triplet_key: 25573|tightening of intermediary constraints|high-yield bond excess returns
context: Using U.S. exchange-traded options and asset-return data from January 1991 to December 2012, the paper studies how constraints on financial intermediaries affect asset prices. Unit of observation is daily and monthly trading in deep out-of-the-money S&P 500 (SPX) put contracts, classified by the Options Clearing Corporation into public investors versus broker‑dealers/market‑makers. The authors infer intermediary tightness from net public buy-to-open DOTM put volumes and price–quantity dynamics (variance premium, jump risk), exploiting crisis episodes and regulatory/funding shocks to link supply stress to risk premia.

difficulty.justification: Establishing that tighter intermediary constraints raise high‑yield bond excess returns requires specialized asset‑pricing and intermediary‑frictions knowledge, and the sign is sensitive to institutional/timing details (crisis vs normal). There are plausible alternative channels (risk aversion, liquidity, default fundamentals) and cross‑market/general‑equilibrium transmission, so while the paper's event/flow-based strategy helps, identification remains challenging.

triplet_key: 8922|nonnegativity constraint on portfolio weights|risk of estimated optimal portfolios
context: This paper examines how imposing portfolio weight constraints (no‑shorts, upper bounds) affects out‑of‑sample risk and estimation error for mean–variance portfolios. Empirical analyses use U.S. equity data (random samples of ~500 large domestic stocks drawn from NYSE/AMEX/NASDAQ, monthly and daily returns across 1968–1999 with 60‑month estimation windows) and Fama–French 25 portfolios, supplemented by Monte Carlo simulations calibrated to U.S. stock return factor structures; it compares sample, factor, and shrinkage covariance estimators and microstructure corrections.

difficulty.justification: In high-dimensional, small-sample portfolio problems nonnegativity constraints usually act as regularization that reduces estimation error and out-of-sample risk, but the direction can reverse if sample size is large or the true optimum requires short positions. Determining the sign therefore needs intermediate portfolio/estimation knowledge and attention to institutional/sample specifics and estimator choice.

triplet_key: 21562|casino payments (per-capita tribal cash transfers)|behavioral disorder symptoms
context: This empirical study uses the Great Smoky Mountains Study (annual waves from 1993) of 1,420 children in 11 counties in western North Carolina (oversampling Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians) to estimate effects of exogenous per‑capita casino transfers (~$4,000/year, disbursed mid‑1990s to enrolled adult tribal members) on adolescent personality, emotional, and behavioral outcomes. It compares younger American Indian cohorts exposed to payments before age 16 with older cohorts and non‑Indian families, exploiting panel data on children, parents, household characteristics, and census‑tract location.

difficulty.justification: Interpreting cash transfers’ effects on child behavioral disorders requires intermediate knowledge of family/child development economics and local tribal institutions; the sign depends on contextual features (transfer size, cultural response, labor/leisure changes), multiple mediating channels and plausible opposing mechanisms (income reduces stress vs. increases substance exposure), and while the described quasi‑experimental cohort design gives reasonably strong identification, non‑trivial threats (migration, differential trends, measurement and spillovers) keep the problem moderately difficult.

triplet_key: 27057|high school personal finance requirements|high-interest student loan borrowing
context: Evidence on this triplet comes from U.S. quasi-experimental studies of state high‑school personal finance graduation requirements (standalone courses or integrated standards). Using difference‑in‑differences that compare cohorts who graduated just before versus just after mandate adoption across states, these studies analyze individual students’ longer‑run financial behaviors — including shifts from high‑interest to lower‑interest student‑loan borrowing — measured in administrative and survey data.

difficulty.justification: Quasi-experimental mandate studies provide credible leverage, but results hinge on curriculum content/enforcement, concurrent state policies, and heterogeneous student responses; multiple mediating channels and plausible alternative mechanisms mean the negative sign is reasonably but not straightforwardly identified.

context: This paper studies U.S. county-level effects of Clean Air Act regulation on air pollution and housing values across 1970–1990, focusing on 1970–80 and 1980–90 decades. It uses county TSP monitor data, attainment/non‑attainment designations (mid‑decade: 1974–75 and 1984–85), and county housing values (Census/CCDB and PUMS). The institutional setting is the 1970 and 1977 Clean Air Act Amendments and EPA enforcement, with important background shocks such as the 1981–82 recession and regional industrial declines (e.g., Rust Belt).

difficulty.justification: Identifying a positive effect of Clean Air Act regulation on county housing values requires specialized institutional knowledge (monitoring, attainment rules) and is highly context-dependent (decade, region, economic shocks). There are strong confounders (industrial decline, recessions, monitor placement, endogenous designation) and multiple channels (air quality, migration/sorting, local demand/GE effects), so decent data and careful identification are needed but not decisive without robustness.

triplet_key: 10445|increase in medicaid hospital payments (dsh)|competition among hospitals / access to high quality care
outcome: Competition among hospitals / access to high quality care
context: Examines births in California around 1989–1995 (focus on 1990 vs 1995) using individual birth/death records aggregated to county-year-race-education cells. The study exploits California’s 1991 Disproportionate Share Hospital (DSH) policy that raised Medicaid hospital reimbursements and induced a rapid redistribution of Medicaid deliveries across hospitals. Units are expectant mothers (Medicaid vs privately insured) and counties/hospitals; outcomes include neonatal mortality and low/very low birth weight. Identification leverages within-county changes in hospital segregation and hospital-market incentives under DSH.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate health‑policy and hospital‑market knowledge; the sign depends on California’s institutional details and payer/hospital structure. Multiple plausible alternative channels (selection, referral, closures, contemporaneous policies) make the sign not obvious, and while the within‑county/segregation variation is informative it may not fully rule out confounding.

context: This paper uses MDRC’s randomized Jobs First waiver experiment in Connecticut (random assignment April 1996–February 1997; follow‑up through December 2000) to estimate causal effects on quarterly earnings, cash welfare and Food Stamps for 4,803 welfare cases (women) in New Haven and Manchester. Jobs First implemented a 21‑month time limit, an expanded earnings disregard (earnings below the federal poverty line disregarded), work requirements, and sanctions; administrative UI and welfare records are the primary data.

difficulty.justification: Randomized assignment and administrative records give strong identification for effects, but assessing impacts on the full income distribution is moderately challenging because of heterogeneous treatment effects, offsetting channels (earnings up, cash transfers down, in‑kind benefits changing), measurement/valuation of noncash benefits, and some context‑specific program features.

context: This paper studies causal effects of increases in bank size from postwar West German banking reforms (1947–1957) that broke up and later allowed reconsolidation of three national commercial banks, exploiting the 1952 zonal and 1957 national reconsolidations. Using newly digitized firm-level microdata on stock and non-stock firms (primarily 1951–1960), bank-level aggregates, and municipality outcomes, the author compares firms with ‘treated’ relationship banks to others to estimate how exogenous, reform-driven increases in bank size affected lending, employment, and productivity across West Germany.

difficulty.justification: The sign depends on detailed historical and institutional features (universal vs relationship banking, regional reconsolidation rules) and is subject to strong, countervailing mechanisms (credit expansion vs weakened monitoring/crowding‑out) and spillovers. Even with rich microdata and a quasi-experiment, credible identification requires specialized strategies to handle selection, spillovers, composition, and general‑equilibrium feedbacks.

context: This empirical study analyzes US nonfinancial firms (firm-year observations) from 1985–2008 (46,219 obs), testing whether firm default probabilities (S&P issuer ratings and Moody’s KMV EDF) reflect static tradeoff predictions. Using Compustat/CRSP financials, simulated marginal tax rates, and Moody’s EDF, the authors examine how firm size, asset tangibility, R&D, profitability, and market shocks relate to default risk, account for selection into ratings (visibility instruments: NYSE listing, S&P indexes, industry-rated share), and exploit panel variation and regime-switching to assess dynamic responses to shocks.

difficulty.justification: Understanding the sign needs intermediate corporate-tax and finance knowledge because taxes affect default both via tax shields and via after‑tax cash flow; the sign depends on firm profitability, loss carryforwards, and leverage so context matters. Identification is made difficult by endogeneity/confounding (profitability and size drive both MTR and default) and requires careful instruments/panel/regime methods, so substantial but not intractable effort is needed.

triplet_key: 19248|central bank commitment to keep rates near the zlb|fiscal multiplier (targeted-inflation regime)
treatment: Central bank commitment to keep rates near the ZLB
context: This paper estimates a small-scale New Keynesian DSGE with an explicit zero lower bound and Markov sunspot shocks for the United States and Japan using quarterly country-level macro data (output growth, GDP deflator inflation, and short-term nominal rates). Estimation uses pre-ZLB samples (U.S. 1984:Q1–2007:Q4; Japan 1981:Q1–1994:Q4) and post-sample filtering through 2013 to detect regime switches around ZLB episodes (Japan’s prolonged 1990s–2000s deflation; U.S. Great Recession starting 2008). The paper conducts structural counterfactual fiscal policy experiments and simulations.

difficulty.justification: Establishing that a commitment to keep rates near the ZLB raises the fiscal multiplier relies on specialized New Keynesian theory about expectation and monetary–fiscal interactions and is highly sensitive to institutional credibility and regime details; empirical identification is hard because the counterfactual relies on limited pre‑ZLB data, regime‑switch inference, and strong model structure, leaving opposite or attenuated signs plausible without very strong evidence.

context: This paper evaluates the causal impact of improving implementation of India’s National Rural Employment Guarantee Scheme (NREGS) by randomizing the 2010–2012 staggered rollout of biometric “Smartcard” payment reforms across sub-districts (mandals) in rural Andhra Pradesh. It uses an RCT at mandal-scale with household surveys of ~5,000 NREGS-registered jobcard holders and independent government censuses (SECC, Economic and Livestock Censuses) to measure effects on wages, employment, incomes, prices, and poverty in imperfectly competitive local labor markets.

difficulty.justification: An RCT of biometric payment rollout substantially improves identification of whether better NREGS implementation lowers income poverty for the sampled mandals, so evidence sufficiency is good; however, multiple mediating channels (wage effects, labor supply, price and GE spillovers), and strong dependence on local market/institutional conditions mean the sign can vary across settings, making correct inference moderately challenging.

triplet_key: 22711|removal of chapter 13 bankruptcy flag|formal sector employment
context: This empirical study analyzes U.S. individual bankruptcy filers (aged ~23–47 at filing) who filed between 1995–2004, exploiting that credit bureaus voluntarily remove Chapter 13 bankruptcy flags after seven years but Chapter 7 flags after ten. Using Equifax CCP credit-bureau panels (1999–2015) and matched Social Security administrative earnings records (available through 2014), the authors estimate how flag removal (roughly 2002–2011 removals) affects credit scores, credit-card and mortgage borrowing, and formal labor-market outcomes within the FCRA credit-reporting institutional setting.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether removal of a Chapter 13 flag raises formal-sector employment requires specialized knowledge of credit-reporting rules, employer screening practices, and bankruptcy heterogeneity; multiple mediating channels (credit scores, borrowing, employer use of reports) and heterogeneous institutional contexts make the sign ambiguous, though the 7-vs-10 year removal timing offers plausible quasi-experimental leverage.

context: This paper reports a countrywide field experiment in Mexico beginning May 2007 with 29 months of follow-up, randomizing permanent interest-rate cuts (20 percentage-point vs. 10pp reductions from roughly 100% APR) across 80 geographic regions covering Compartamos Banco branches. Units include region-month aggregates and individual borrowers/groups using the Crédito Mujer group microloan product (female borrowers). The institutional setting is Mexico’s largest for‑profit microlender with group liability loans, weekly repayments, and credit‑bureau reporting; analyses combine administrative loan data, credit bureau snapshots, and competitor rate surveys.

difficulty.justification: Understanding the sign requires specialized microfinance pricing and borrower-behavior knowledge; while the randomized design gives strong identification, multiple offsetting channels (margin vs. volume vs. default vs. selection and competition) and profit accounting/dynamic effects make the net sign nontrivial.

context: Using a 2018–2019 World Bank "Doing Business" procurement module, the paper analyzes procurement laws, actual procurement practices, and outcomes for a hypothetical $2.5 million road-resurfacing contract across 187 countries. Units are countries (responses from lawyers, contractors, and procuring entities). It links cross-country variation in legal rules versus practice to outcomes (perceived corruption, procedural efficiency, cost/time/quality) and examines heterogeneity by public-sector capacity (human capital, government efficiency).

difficulty.justification: Identifying whether procurement laws reduce corruption requires specialized knowledge of legal design and enforcement and is highly context-dependent (capacity, political economy, enforcement). Cross-country, perception-based data face severe endogeneity, measurement error, and omitted-variable/confounding risks, so the sign is ambiguous without strong identification.

context: This paper studies Berlin’s internal city structure across 1936, 1986 and 2006 using block-level data on land prices, workplace and residence employment and commuting. It exploits the exogenous shock of the Berlin Wall (1961) severing interactions between East and West Berlin and the 1989–1990 fall and reunification as a natural experiment. Units are thousands of statistical blocks within Berlin; outcomes include land values, employment distributions and commuting flows. The institutional setting involves travel-cost barriers, transport-network closures, and West Berlin’s market economy during division.

difficulty.justification: Identifying that the Berlin division reduced residence employment requires specialized urban/labor-economics and historical-institutional knowledge; the sign depends on Berlin‑specific transport, political and migration contexts, faces plausible alternative mechanisms (relocation, selection, policy responses), and involves GE and network feedbacks, although the Wall offers a strong quasi‑experiment that helps identification.

context: Using de-identified JPMorgan Chase monthly bank-account data from January 2014–June 2016 across 20 U.S. states, the paper studies roughly 182,000 households who received unemployment insurance (UI) by direct deposit. It exploits variation in UI generosity, timing, and duration (most states six months; e.g., Florida four months, New Jersey unusually generous and no waiting period) to trace high-frequency household income, nondurable spending categories, and job‑finding hazards around UI receipt and predictable benefit exhaustion.

difficulty.justification: Predictable UI exhaustion creates a plausibly sharp income drop so high-frequency bank data can credibly detect a spending decline, but identification requires intermediate policy/labor-market knowledge and careful handling of selection (only those still unemployed exhaust), anticipation, substitution (savings/credit/other transfers), and heterogeneous responses across states and households.

context: This triplet focuses on the impact of NAACP litigation—particularly lawsuits beginning in the mid-1930s to equalize teacher salaries—on Black public schooling access and quality in Georgia and other Southern states. The analysis uses school-district–level measures (pupil–teacher ratios, teacher salaries, term length, teacher certification, enrollments) within the 1911–1960 window but emphasizes gains after 1930/mid-1930s when litigation and the threat of litigation prompted preventative actions by Southern school authorities.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a positive effect of NAACP litigation on schooling requires specialized historical/legal and education-finance knowledge and varies strongly by local institutions and timing; selection, contemporaneous policies and spillovers make alternative explanations plausible, so careful identification (staggered DiD, threat-vs-filing timing, placebo checks) is necessary even with rich district-level data.

context: Analyzes U.S. single women aged 19–44 over 1984–1996 using CPS March and ORG data. The paper exploits federal and state policy changes — large EITC expansions, federal/state income tax changes, AFDC/Food Stamp benefit and implicit-tax changes, Medicaid expansions, state welfare waivers, state EITCs, and shifts in child-care and training funding — across states and over time to estimate effects on employment and hours of single mothers relative to single childless women within the U.S. tax-transfer institutional setting.

difficulty.justification: Moderately high difficulty: identifying a positive effect of training on employment requires understanding program targeting, timing, childcare constraints, and disentangling many simultaneous policy changes (EITC, welfare waivers, tax reforms) and selection into training. The stated diff‑in‑diff approach (single mothers vs. childless women) strengthens identification but important confounders and heterogeneity remain, so the sign is plausible but not straightforward.

context: This paper uses the US National Longitudinal Survey of Youth Child–Mother data (children of the 1979 NLSY cohort, surveyed in 1986–1990) to estimate effects of participation in the federal Head Start preschool program on children’s cognitive scores, grade repetition, immunizations, and height, and on longer‑run outcomes for women. Units are children (and mothers with sister comparisons); the sample is national with oversamples of African‑Americans and Hispanics. Identification relies on within‑family comparisons to address time‑invariant family confounders.

difficulty.justification: Determining Head Start's effect on grade repetition requires intermediate policy and child-development knowledge and is sensitive to program quality, cohort/institutional context, and heterogeneous effects. Within‑family identification helps but time‑varying selection, sibling spillovers, measurement and compensating classroom/parental responses make the sign plausibly mixed without strong additional assumptions or richer quasi‑experimental variation.

context: This paper analyzes long-run causal effects of the Gautreaux Assisted Housing Program (program operated 1976–1998; main sample 1982–1994) in the Chicago metropolitan area, linking historical placement records to administrative data through 2019/2020. Units are Black public-housing/voucher recipient households and their children. The program—created by a class-action settlement and a 1981 consent decree—placed families into “General” (<30% Black) or “Revitalizing” tracts based largely on registration order and unit availability. Authors use linked LEHD, MAF-ARF, Decennial Census and Numident data to estimate placement effects.

difficulty.justification: Interpreting long-run effects of Gautreaux placements requires specialized knowledge of housing program identification and labor-market mechanisms; context (Chicago, period, program rules) matters for sign, multiple mediating channels and potential selection/mobility issues complicate inference, though the quasi-experimental placement and rich administrative linkage give reasonably strong empirical leverage.

triplet_key: 18674|commander’s emergency response program (cerp) spending|insurgent attacks per capita
context: Using panel data from Feb 2004–Dec 2008/Feb 2009 on Iraq’s reconstruction, this paper studies effects of U.S. development spending across districts (N≈103) observed in half‑year intervals. It links IRMS project‑level spending (CERP, USAID CAP/CSP, large vs. small projects), troop strength (maneuver battalions), and Provincial Reconstruction Team presence to SIGACT insurgent‑incident rates, exploiting variation in timing, project size, local security, and implementation modality to evaluate when aid reduced violence during the Iraq war and U.S. counterinsurgency operations.

difficulty.justification: Identifying whether CERP spending causally reduced insurgent attacks requires specialized counterinsurgency and econometric knowledge; effects vary strongly by local/institutional context and are highly confoundable (targeting, reverse causality, unobserved shocks). Multiple mediating channels and dynamic feedbacks make causal reasoning hard, and although the panel/project-level variation helps, remaining identification challenges are substantial.

triplet_key: 21920|shocks to intermediary equity capital ratio|cross-sectional expected returns
treatment: Shocks to intermediary equity capital ratio
context: This empirical study (quarterly data, 1970–2012) tests whether shocks to the aggregate equity capital ratio of financial intermediaries—constructed from New York Fed primary-dealer holding companies matched to CRSP/Compustat and Datastream—explain cross-sectional expected returns. Units include primary-dealer holding companies and portfolios across asset classes (U.S. equities, government and corporate bonds, foreign sovereign bonds, options, CDS, commodities, and FX). The setting leverages intermediary asset-pricing theory and the institutional role of NY Fed primary dealers as major counterparties in OTC and derivative markets.

difficulty.justification: Requires specialized intermediary asset-pricing and market-microstructure knowledge; the sign depends on market/liquidity conditions and whether capital shocks signal greater risk-bearing or binding constraints. Identification is hampered by many plausible confounders and reverse causality, multiple mediating GE and liquidity channels, and limited ability of quarterly primary-dealer data to isolate exogenous shocks.

context: The paper develops a static model of incumbents and outside entrepreneurs in economies with regulatory entry barriers and imperfect contract enforcement, focusing on developing and transition settings where licenses, firm control, and managerial talent matter. It tests model predictions using cross-country × industry data: country-level measures of contract enforcement and industry-level proxies for the importance of the market for control (e.g. M&A activity), with outcomes measured at the industry–country (firm/industry) level such as entry rates and average productivity.

difficulty.justification: Establishing that deregulation raises average management quality rests on specialized IO/development theory about entry, market-for-control and contract enforcement and is highly sensitive to institutional and industry context; alternative channels (selection, regulatory capture, low-quality entrants) make the opposite sign plausible, and cross-country×industry proxies offer limited identification, so convincing causal inference is difficult.

triplet_key: 16977|introduction of risk adjustment (hcc model)|differential payments to ma plans
context: This paper studies how introducing Medicare Advantage risk adjustment altered selection into private plans and government payments in the U.S. Medicare program. It uses individual-level MCBS claims and survey data for roughly 55,000 beneficiaries (1994–2006, with county-level checks through 2008) to analyze transitions between traditional fee-for-service and Medicare Advantage around the phased HCC risk-adjustment reform (2004–2007), concurrent benchmark and budget-neutrality changes, and outcomes including risk scores, Medicare expenditures, and enrollee satisfaction.

difficulty.justification: Understanding HCC risk adjustment requires intermediate policy knowledge; the sign depends on institutional details (benchmark rules, budget neutrality) and is confounded by selection, coding/upcoding, and simultaneous policy changes. Microdata and phased rollout provide useful identification, but multiple channels and concurrent reforms make a clean sign nontrivial to establish.

context: This paper studies the causal impact of the U.S. Training Within Industry (TWI) program—a government-funded in-plant management training initiative delivered largely 1940–1945—on firm performance and spillovers. It uses a newly assembled panel of 11,575 U.S. war-contractor applicant firms (1935–1955 financials), primarily large manufacturers (covering ~40% of 1939 manufacturing employment), exploiting voluntary application windows, decentralized subdistrict implementation, and variation in instructor assignment. Eligibility required an active war contract; outcomes are firm-level sales, TFP, profitability, managerial practices, and supply-chain links.

difficulty.justification: Intermediate managerial/training knowledge needed: J‑R plausibly raises on‑the‑job training but identification must address voluntary application/selection, substitution between training types, and firm heterogeneity. The paper's panel and quasi‑experimental variation (application windows, decentralized rollout, instructor assignment) materially help ID, yet the wartime institutional setting and remaining unobservables leave moderate uncertainty.

triplet_key: 23473|three-bidder irs rule (reduce tax shield when <3 bidders)|municipal borrowing costs
treatment: Three-bidder IRS rule (reduce tax shield when <3 bidders)
context: Simulates the 2017 IRS "three-bidder" rule in U.S. municipal bond auctions by modeling it as a reduction in the tax shield for auctions with fewer than three bidders. Using the paper's structural auction model estimated on 2008–2015 competitive-auction data and counterfactual simulations (performed for 2015 auctions), it evaluates impacts on issuer borrowing costs and bidder markups. The rule binds in about 7% of auctions; for r=0.5 the model predicts a 1.25% average increase in borrowing costs, with substantial heterogeneity.

difficulty.justification: Determining this sign requires specialized auction and municipal-finance knowledge and is sensitive to institutional details (bidder composition, tax prevalence) and competing channels; structural-model assumptions and limited applicability (rule binds only ~7% of auctions) leave plausible alternative signs without stronger identification.

context: State-level analysis using BRFSS: the triplet estimates the effect of increases in insurance coverage (driven by the Massachusetts individual mandate enacted April 2006) on "days health not good" for insured nonelderly adults (ages 18–64). It uses state-year BRFSS averages comparing pre-reform (2004–2005) to post-reform (2008–2010) and exploits cross-state variation (Massachusetts vs. other states) induced by the reform. The institutional setting includes the Massachusetts mandate and pre-existing community rating rules; units are state-year aggregates of insured individuals' health measures.

difficulty.justification: Compositional changes in the insured pool (selection/adverse selection), concurrent MA policy changes and state-level trends, and self-report/measurement issues make the sign ambiguous despite plausible health benefits of insurance; credible identification requires intermediate-to-advanced policy knowledge and strong quasi-experimental controls to rule out alternative channels.

context: This paper analyzes perceptions and criminal behavior using two U.S. longitudinal cohorts: the NLSY97 (youth aged 12–16 in 1997, followed 1997–2000) and the National Youth Survey (respondents surveyed in 1983 and 1986). Units are individual young males (with sibling linkages), linked to county-level arrest rates and neighborhood measures. The institutional setting is the U.S. criminal justice system—local arrest outcomes, policing/neighborhood disorder—and the study examines how personal/sibling crimes and arrests update beliefs about arrest probabilities and how those beliefs causally affect subsequent offending.

difficulty.justification: Economic deterrence theory predicts a negative effect, but belief formation is endogenous and correlated with past offending, policing intensity, and local crime dynamics; longitudinal and sibling-linked data improve identification but substantial confounding, heterogeneous responses, and dynamic feedbacks make the sign nontrivial to establish.

context: This study examines how automation and offshoring altered the US wage structure from 1980 to 2016. Using industry-level data (BEA, 49 industries, 1987–2016), robot and software adoption measures, and Census/ACS microdata for 500 demographic groups (by gender, age, education, race, nativity) across national regions and commuting zones, the authors link industry labor-share declines and routine-task exposure to group-level changes in wages and employment. The setting is the United States across manufacturing and service sectors, controlling for trade, markups, unionization, and regional specialization.

difficulty.justification: Evaluating whether task displacement lowers real wages requires intermediate labor-market and trade knowledge but not exotic theory; however effects vary across sectors, worker types, and institutions so context matters. Substantial endogeneity and heterogeneous/compositional and general-equilibrium channels make the sign plausible but nontrivial to identify convincingly even with rich controls and micro–macro linkage.

triplet_key: 27699|efficiency treatment|perceived behavioral responses to estate tax for wealthy
outcome: Perceived behavioral responses to estate tax for wealthy
context: This paper analyzes two large-scale representative U.S. surveys conducted Feb–May 2019 (n≈2,780 for income tax; n≈2,360 for estate tax) of individuals aged 18–69. It studies knowledge, beliefs, and normative views about U.S. federal income and estate taxation, elicits perceived behavioral and distributional mechanisms, and implements randomized short instructional video treatments to test how pedagogical explanations (Redistribution, Efficiency, Economist) causally shift support for progressive tax policies.

difficulty.justification: A randomized instructional-video treatment measuring beliefs gives strong internal identification, so pinning the sign is relatively straightforward; however framing effects depend on audience and message details and alternative interpretations of the efficiency message make the sign somewhat context- and interpretation-dependent.

context: Analyzes hospital–MCO bargaining and merger effects in Northern Virginia (HPD8 plus Fauquier County) using 2003–2006 administrative claims and Virginia inpatient discharge data. Units are hospitals, hospital systems, managed-care organizations, and insured enrollees; the authors estimate a structural Nash‑bargaining model (with multinomial logit demand and GMM) to recover prices, marginal costs, and simulate counterfactuals — notably the proposed 2006 Inova–Prince William Hospital acquisition and coinsurance‑rate changes — in a setting of negotiated prices, low patient coinsurance (~2–3%), and self‑insured employer contracts.

difficulty.justification: Correctly signing a hospital–MCO merger effect requires specialized knowledge of bargaining models and empirical identification; the sign depends strongly on local insurer leverage, substitution patterns, and contract types. Rich administrative data and a structural Nash‑bargaining approach help identification, but strong modeling assumptions and multiple mediating channels leave nontrivial ambiguity.

context: This paper studies U.S. household-level responses to the 2008 Economic Stimulus Act payments (mid-2008), using Consumer Expenditure Survey interviews June 2008–March 2009. Units are U.S. households; payments ($300–$1,200, incl. $300 per child) were disbursed by the IRS by mail or electronic transfer, with randomized timing determined by recipients’ SSN digits. The authors exploit this exogenous timing and CE panel expenditure data to estimate the causal effect of receiving ESPs on short-run nondurable and durable spending.

difficulty.justification: Randomized timing of payments provides strong exogenous variation so the sign is fairly easy to identify, though interpretation requires intermediate knowledge (MPC, liquidity constraints) and attention to measurement, timing, and sample issues in CE data.

context: This paper analyzes French matched employer–employee administrative data (DADS) merged with the French Labor Force Survey for 1997–2007, with robustness checks using the CPS in the United States. Units include individual male workers (age 30–52), establishments/firms and 328 commuting-zone local labor markets. The setting emphasizes housing constraints, search frictions, and place-based policies (e.g., French Zones Franches Urbaines). The author combines panel flow decompositions with structural equilibrium estimation to identify employers’ role in spatial unemployment and to simulate policy counterfactuals.

difficulty.justification: Establishing that a corporate profit subsidy increases local welfare requires specialized structural general-equilibrium identification to account for housing constraints, search frictions, firm and worker relocation, and fiscal externalities. Context-specific features (place-based policies, local labor market structure) and plausible opposing mechanisms (congestion, housing-price redistribution, deadweight losses) make the sign highly sensitive and hard to pin down without strong data and modeling assumptions.

triplet_key: 7249|equalization of family income distribution|white-minority college entry gap
context: This paper analyzes schooling dynamics for U.S. male youth using the 1979–1989 waves of the National Longitudinal Survey of Youth, following males aged 13–16 in January 1978 through ages 15–24. It studies racial/ethnic differences (Black, Hispanic, White) in high‑school completion and college entry amid rising returns to college and changing tuition, leveraging geographic variation in college proximity and local labor markets, detailed family background and AFQT ability measures, and policy-relevant tuition/Pell‑grant considerations.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether equalizing family income would shrink the white–minority college entry gap requires specialized knowledge of intergenerational transmission, labor/education market responses, and racial differences in returns and constraints; non‑income drivers (school quality, discrimination, preferences), selection and mediation channels make the opposite sign plausible and a clean causal estimate requires strong, often unavailable exogeneity assumptions despite rich NLSY controls.

context: This study (Fang, Keane, Silverman, 2006) analyzes selection into Medigap supplemental insurance among U.S. Medicare beneficiaries (age 65+) using Medicare Current Beneficiary Survey data (circa 1999–2001/2000–2001) and Health and Retirement Study waves (2000–2002). The unit of analysis is individual Medicare beneficiaries across U.S. states where Medigap pricing and standardized plans and a six-month open-enrollment rule limit risk-based pricing. The paper leverages detailed medical expenditure records, rich health measures, and HRS measures (income, cognition, risk aversion, expectations) to study why healthier or lower-risk seniors purchase Medigap.

difficulty.justification: Identifying whether Medigap raises total medical spending requires intermediate insurance/Medicare policy knowledge and close attention to institutional details (standardized plans, open enrollment). The sign is contested because selection and moral hazard run in opposite directions; the paper's rich data and controls help, but residual confounding and multiple channels make credible identification moderately difficult.

triplet_key: 11883|ex-post private control of self-dealing|number of domestic firms per million inhabitants
outcome: Number of domestic firms per million inhabitants
context: The paper constructs cross-country measures of legal protection against corporate self-dealing based on Lex Mundi law-firm responses about laws in force in May 2003 for 72 countries (covering most global market capitalization). It codes a hypothetical related-party transaction to build ex-ante/ex-post private enforcement and public-sanction indices and links these country-level legal indices to stock-market outcomes (market capitalization, IPOs, listed firms, block premiums, ownership concentration) using financial data from the late 1990s–early 2000s.

difficulty.justification: Identifying that stronger ex-post private control of self-dealing raises firms per million requires substantial corporate‑law and institutional knowledge; the sign is highly context-dependent and subject to many omitted variables and reverse causation concerns. Cross‑country measurement and enforcement heterogeneity create complex mediating and general‑equilibrium channels, and the available observational indicators provide only moderately sufficient evidence without strong identification strategies.

triplet_key: 29748|county racial bias|bank ppp borrowing probability for black-owned restaurants
outcome: Bank PPP borrowing probability for Black-owned restaurants
context: This paper studies uptake of CARES Act emergency lending (Paycheck Protection Program and EIDL) during 2020–early 2021 for a near-population of licensed seating restaurants in Florida. The unit of analysis is individual restaurants linked to firm corporate records and owners’ self-reported race from voter registration; administrative PPP and EIDL loan records, UCC filings, Yelp characteristics, and county Project Implicit (2008–2019) bias measures are used to examine how location, firm traits, prior bank relationships, and local racial bias affected access to bank versus nonbank PPP lending.

difficulty.justification: High but not maximal difficulty: linking county-level implicit bias to bank PPP access for Black-owned restaurants requires intermediate policy/market knowledge and strong identification because bias correlates with many structural factors (bank presence, outreach, applicant selection). Project Implicit county averages are noisy and may not map cleanly to banking discrimination, so separating discrimination from differential application, network effects, and local institutional features is challenging.

context: The paper studies state-level angel investor tax credit programs across 31 U.S. states from 1988–2018, exploiting staggered introductions and terminations. It analyzes state-year outcomes (firm entry, jobs) and deal-, firm-, and investor-level data (Crunchbase, Venture databases, Form D, AngelList) as well as beneficiary vs failed-applicant firms and a survey of angels. The institutional setting is state personal income tax credits for accredited investors (certification required), with variation in credit rates, eligibility restrictions, and program budgets, intended to spur high-tech entrepreneurial entry and job creation.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate policy/market knowledge; the sign plausibly varies with program design, state ecosystem, and timing and is confounded by endogenous adoption, selection, crowding-out and composition effects; the rich staggered-adoption and microdata improve identification but do not eliminate these challenges.

context: This paper uses a randomized field experiment run Jan–Mar 2019 in 41 storefronts of a large payday lender in Indiana, surveying 1,205 payday-borrower customers and matching responses to administrative loan records (including the state-wide Veritec database) to study repeat borrowing, present-bias, and demand for commitment. It also analyzes the lender’s nationwide 2017 data and elicits expert beliefs. Indiana’s regulatory environment (interest and loan-size caps, reporting rules) and common two-week loan maturities frame the institutional setting for structural estimation and welfare simulations of policy changes.

difficulty.justification: Assessing net welfare from a payday-loan ban requires specialized knowledge of lending markets, present-bias/commitment benefits, and structural policy evaluation; institutional details (availability of substitutes, regulation, lender responses) critically determine the sign and opposite outcomes are plausibly supported without careful structural and equilibrium analysis.

triplet_key: 26149|increase in foreign employment share|domestic firm employment
context: This paper studies the effects of foreign-owned multinationals on U.S. workers and local firms using linked employer–employee tax records (W-2 and corporate tax filings including Form 5472) for 1999–2017, with main direct-effect estimates focused on 2010–2015. The setting is U.S. commuting zones across industries; units are individual workers (moves between firms) and firms (value added, employment, wage bill). It exploits country-of-origin spatial clustering and worker movers to identify causal direct and indirect wage and productivity spillovers of foreign multinationals.

difficulty.justification: Multiple opposing channels (demand/productivity spillovers versus labor poaching/competition) and location/industry heterogeneity make the sign context-dependent and confoundable, requiring intermediate-to-advanced knowledge to reason through. Still, the rich linked W-2 and corporate tax data plus mover- and spatial-clustering identification substantially improve empirical leverage, reducing but not eliminating difficulty.

context: This paper studies aircraft-level data worldwide (489,916 aircraft-year observations, 5,987 operators) from 1978–2003 across 129 countries to estimate how country-level creditor protection (Djankov et al. creditor rights index) affects airlines’ use of vintage capital. Units are aircraft-years and airline operators in commercial and military sectors; outcomes are aircraft age, technological age, and fleet size (owned vs leased). The empirical setting exploits panel variation in creditor-rights reforms, country/operator/year fixed effects, and cross-sample comparisons (military vs commercial, leased vs owned, financially constrained vs not) to identify financing effects on investment.

difficulty.justification: Understanding the sign requires intermediate finance/industrial-organizational knowledge about aircraft financing and leasing markets; the effect depends strongly on institutional and market context and is confounded by contemporaneous reforms, selection into ownership versus leasing, and macro trends. Although panel fixed effects and reform variation strengthen identification, multiple mediating channels and potential correlated policy changes make establishing the negative sign fairly challenging.

triplet_key: 4934|interbank cooperation|unwarranted bank failures during the panic
outcome: Unwarranted bank failures during the panic
context: This paper examines bank failures during the Great Depression, focusing on the June 1932 Chicago banking panic. Using individual Chicago bank-level observations (stock prices, balance sheets, income statements, liability composition, and failure dates) from 1931–1932, the authors compare banks that failed during the panic, banks that failed outside the panic window, and survivors. Institutional context includes absence of deposit insurance, clearing-house and correspondent arrangements, Reconstruction Finance Corporation interventions, municipal fiscal stress and major corporate failures (Insull), and episodes of fraud (Bain chain).

difficulty.justification: Establishing that interbank cooperation causally reduced unwarranted failures in the 1932 panic requires specialized historical banking knowledge, is highly context-dependent, and faces strong confounding/selection concerns and multiple feedback channels; rich bank-level data help but do not eliminate identification challenges.

triplet_key: 19226|exposure to import competition from china|cumulative earnings
treatment: Exposure to import competition from China
context: This empirical study evaluates how rising import competition from China during 1991–2007 affected U.S. workers. Using a 1% longitudinal Social Security sample of individual earnings and employer SIC codes, the authors analyze manufacturing workers’ earnings, employment, job-to-job flows, and SSDI receipt across the United States as China’s exports (and U.S. import penetration) surged—notably after WTO accession in 2001. The setting exploits industry-level variation in exposure to Chinese import growth and instruments supply-driven Chinese export gains using import growth in other high-income countries.

difficulty.justification: Administrative earnings data and a supply-driven instrument give fairly strong empirical leverage, but identifying the negative cumulative-earnings effect requires intermediate trade/labor-market knowledge and careful handling of confounders, selection, and general-equilibrium reallocation channels.

context: This paper uses firm- and plant-level panels (up to half a million firms; IT establishment sample ≈160,000) across 12 European countries over 1996–2007 to estimate how rising Chinese import competition — especially after China’s 2001 WTO accession and the phased abolition of textile/apparel quotas (MFA reforms in 2002 and 2005) — affected firm innovation (patents, R&D), IT adoption, TFP, employment and survival. Identification exploits differential industry exposure to quota removal and pre-existing Chinese export shares, with firm and industry fixed effects and trend controls.

difficulty.justification: Mechanically plausible but not obvious: understanding whether import competition raises IT intensity requires intermediate trade/firm-investment knowledge and depends strongly on industry and firm heterogeneity (reallocation vs. adaptation). Panel fixed effects and differential exposure help identification, but selection, timing, spillovers and alternative channels make the sign moderately ambiguous and causally complex.

triplet_key: 17149|collective government bailout guarantee|financial sector equity risk premium
context: This paper analyzes US equity option and stock data for S&P500 firms and SPDR sector ETFs from January 2003 to June 2009, with emphasis on the financial‑crisis period (August 2007–June 2009) and key policy dates (e.g., TARP). Units are individual bank stocks, sector ETFs (financials versus non‑financials), and their option contracts traded on the CBOE. The institutional setting is the US financial sector facing government bailout programs and sector‑wide guarantees that can truncate aggregate tail losses.

difficulty.justification: Understanding this requires substantial finance/economics knowledge (option-implied tail risk, systemic guarantees, and policy timing). The sign depends strongly on institutional credibility and concurrent policies and is confounded by macro shocks, moral-hazard feedbacks and selection, though event-study option/ETF data can still yield reasonably informative evidence.

context: This paper develops and estimates a structural equilibrium search and wage-posting model of formal and informal labor in Brazil, using Pesquisa Mensal de Emprego (PME) rotating-panel data from six large metropolitan regions (2002–2007). Units are low-educated individuals (age 23–65) and heterogeneous firms deciding sector and posted wages. The institutional setting includes minimum wage, payroll and corporate taxes, employer social security contributions, severance pay, unemployment insurance (funded by labor taxes), and monitoring/fines for informality; counterfactuals simulate policy changes to informality costs and labor regulations.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether higher informality costs raise or lower formal-sector wages hinges on firm entry/sector-choice, worker reallocation, and general-equilibrium demand/supply responses under Brazil's specific labor institutions, so specialized theory and structural identification are needed and the sign is plausibly reversed under alternative elasticities or behavioral responses.

context: This empirical paper studies U.S. state-chartered commercial banks from 1996–2011, exploiting the Riegle Act–mandated alternating supervision (12- or 18‑month rotations) between state regulators and federal agencies (Fed for state‑member banks; FDIC for non‑members). Using National Information Center on-site exam data (CAMELS) merged with Call Reports across U.S. states, the authors analyze how regulator identity affects supervisory ratings and bank outcomes (capital ratios, nonperforming loans, ROA) for individual banks under predetermined rotation schedules.

difficulty.justification: The Riegle rotation provides strong quasi‑experimental variation so identification is feasible, but interpretation of a positive effect (more NPLs under federal supervision) requires specialized knowledge of supervisory classification incentives, examiner behavior, bank responses, and crisis‑period heterogeneity; reclassification versus real deterioration and remaining measurement/selection issues keep this moderately difficult.

context: This paper studies long-term causal effects of Boston Public Schools' universal K1 preschool on educational and behavioral outcomes for first-time preschool applicants (four-year-olds) who applied between 1997 and 2003 in Boston, MA. Using randomized school-assignment tie-break lotteries within BPS, linked to Massachusetts DESE administrative records and National Student Clearinghouse data with ~20 years of follow-up, the study evaluates impacts on college enrollment, SAT-taking, MCAS scores, high-school graduation, and disciplinary outcomes for a largely low-income, nonwhite urban cohort.

difficulty.justification: Randomized tie-break lotteries and long administrative follow-up give strong internal identification and substantial empirical power, so the sign is fairly identifiable; however, interpretation of disciplinary outcomes can be affected by multiple mediating channels, reporting/measurement changes, and setting-specific factors, so moderate contextual nuance is required.

context: Using firm-level data on non-U.S. firms from 1990–2005, this triplet concerns firms' post-listing capital raising — measured as security issuances at home, in the U.S., the U.K., and other markets. The comparison is between firms that cross-list on U.S. exchanges (NYSE, AMEX, NASDAQ, OTC/Rule 144a) versus London markets (LSE Main Market, AIM), focusing on firm-level outcomes after the listing and whether a U.S. exchange cross-listing leads to increased capital-raising.

difficulty.justification: Identification requires specialized market and listing knowledge (regulation, investor base) and is highly context-dependent (time, venue, firm type). Strong selection on unobservables, multiple mediating channels (liquidity, bonding, investor recognition), and regulatory changes in 1990–2005 make clean causal inference challenging though feasible with careful methods.

context: This paper evaluates the causal impact of the GoBifo community-driven development (CDD) program in Sierra Leone using a village-level randomized rollout. Baseline surveys were collected in 2005, the program ran 2006–2009 (block grants of roughly $4,667 per village with participation requirements for women and youth), and follow-up and structured community activities were fielded in 2009 across 236 villages and about 2,832 households in Bombali and Bonthe districts. Units of analysis are villages and households in a post-conflict decentralization setting where VDCs, bank accounts, and participatory rules were introduced to reshape local institutions.

difficulty.justification: The village-level randomized rollout gives strong internal identification, so empirical sign can be estimated, but multiple opposing channels (crowding‑in vs crowding‑out), implementation heterogeneity, spillovers and the post‑conflict institutional setting make the expected sign context‑dependent and nontrivial to reason from theory alone.

context: For this triplet the analysis uses the same annual country‑year panel of 134 non‑OECD countries from 1972–2006 and the paper's IV 2SLS strategy (lagged U.S. wheat production interacted with each country's propensity to receive U.S. food aid, with country and region‑year fixed effects). The outcome here is the incidence of large‑scale armed conflicts—defined as conflicts with at least 1,000 combat deaths—measured using the Correlates of War (COW) dataset rather than the baseline UCDP/PRIO series.

difficulty.justification: Identifying whether U.S. food aid increases large-scale conflict requires specialized knowledge of aid targeting, conflict dynamics, and IV identification (and how alternative conflict measures like COW vs UCDP affect measurement). Multiple mediating channels, strong endogeneity concerns and plausible exclusion violations mean the opposite sign is also credible without very strong assumptions or additional evidence.

triplet_key: 23288|fintech lender status for high-fico borrowers|interest rate premium
treatment: Fintech lender status for high-FICO borrowers
context: This triplet refers to individual conforming mortgage loans (Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac data) in the U.S., analyzing borrower-level interest rates for fintech versus non‑fintech originators. The regression compares fintech and non‑fintech rates with zip‑code × quarter fixed effects and borrower controls, focusing on the premium paid by highest‑credit borrowers (top decile of FICO). The premium is especially pronounced in the later sample years (notably 2014–2015).

difficulty.justification: Determining whether fintech status causally raises rates for top‑decile FICO borrowers requires specialized knowledge of mortgage origination channels, investor pricing and product mix; results are highly context- and time-dependent and easily confounded by selection into fintech, unobserved loan features, and channel/institutional differences, so the opposite sign is plausibly as likely without stronger identification.

context: The paper examines how monetary policy shocks change aggregate TFP and resource allocation by reallocating inputs across heterogeneous firms. Empirical analyses use identified monetary shocks (Romer and Romer; high-frequency shocks) in U.S. time-series and panel contexts, and firm-level data (Compustat for U.S. public firms; Belgian firm-level pass-through estimates) across industries and years. Units are the aggregate economy and firms. The setting emphasizes nominal rigidities (Calvo/menu costs), heterogeneous markups and pass-throughs, and monetary and discount-factor shocks as exogenous demand disturbances.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a positive short-run output response to an expansionary monetary shock is standard economic reasoning and aided here by credible instruments (Romer & Romer, high-frequency), but heterogeneous firm-level reallocation, potential supply/news confounds, and GE feedbacks make the sign moderately context-dependent and require nontrivial causal accounting.

context: This empirical study analyzes individuals who appealed initially denied Disability Insurance (DI) claims in Norway during 1994–2005 (with linked administrative records spanning 1967–2010 and appeals 1989–2011). The unit of analysis is DI appellants and their households (including spouses), using register data on earnings, taxes, transfers, assets and constructed consumption. The institutional setting is Norway’s national DI program and a written appeal process in Oslo with randomly assigned Administrative Law Judges, enabling quasi-experimental variation; analyses include multi-year dynamics and a structural household labor-supply model for welfare assessment.

difficulty.justification: Random assignment of judges and rich Norwegian administrative panels give strong identification and data for estimating the sign, but correctly interpreting effects requires intermediate understanding of DI incentives, labor‑supply responses and multi‑year dynamics (benefit replacement vs. earnings loss), so moderate contextual and causal complexity remains.

context: This triplet refers to MTO participants (adult respondents and those who were ages 13–17 at random assignment) who used unrestricted Section 8 vouchers. The MTO randomized lottery occurred 1994–1998 across five U.S. cities (Baltimore, Boston, Chicago, Los Angeles, New York). Analysis links HUD participant records to annual Experian credit reports (2001–2017) to measure the delinquency index (overdue debts, tax liens, judgments, collections). Reported effects compare Section 8 voucher users to control families, focusing on outcomes for adults and older children.

difficulty.justification: Although MTO provides a randomized assignment that helps identify effects, the contrast is between voucher users (take‑up) and controls so one must deal with noncompliance/selection (IV/LATE) and credit‑report measurement issues. Multiple mediating channels (moving costs, rent burden, employment, landlord behavior) and heterogeneous local institutional conditions make the sign plausible but not unambiguous without careful identification and robustness checks.

context: This paper evaluates the causal impact of improving implementation of India’s National Rural Employment Guarantee Scheme (NREGS) by randomizing the 2010–2012 staggered rollout of biometric “Smartcard” payment reforms across sub-districts (mandals) in rural Andhra Pradesh. It uses an RCT at mandal-scale with household surveys of ~5,000 NREGS-registered jobcard holders and independent government censuses (SECC, Economic and Livestock Censuses) to measure effects on wages, employment, incomes, prices, and poverty in imperfectly competitive local labor markets.

difficulty.justification: Multiple competing channels make the sign ambiguous: improved NREGS pay reliability could reduce borrowing by smoothing consumption or increase borrowing by relaxing liquidity constraints or enabling investment/credit access, and local labor-market/general-equilibrium effects matter. The RCT gives good identification of local causal effects, but context-specific credit market structure and household responses leave nontrivial uncertainty.

context: Using the February 2000 Swedish policy experiment that opened state liquor stores (Systembolaget) on Saturdays in six treated counties (control and buffer counties excluded), the paper analyzes individual-level pawn and mainstream credit behavior and performance. The triplet focuses on low-income young adults ages 20–25 (the cohort that saw the largest effects), using pawnbroker records, credit bureau snapshots, tax/employment registers, crime convictions, and household cash journals in 1999–2001. Legal off‑site purchase age is 20; the reported effect is an increase in loan delinquencies for the 20–25 cohort.

difficulty.justification: The setting is a well-defined Swedish policy experiment with rich administrative and credit data, so identification is plausible but requires intermediate domain knowledge (alcohol economics, credit behavior). The sign is context-dependent on institutional details (state monopoly, legal age, local credit markets) and involves multiple mediating channels, though alternative explanations are fairly limited given the data and research design.

context: This paper examines U.S. firm-level effects of workplace union elections (NLRB records, 1977–2010) on publicly traded firms’ senior unsecured bond prices and bankruptcy outcomes. Using establishment-level secret-ballot elections (50% certification cutoff) and Chapter 11 case data (UCLA–LoPucki, 1980–2010; Moody’s LGD 1990–2009), the unit of analysis is firms and their bond claims; institutional context includes NLRA certification rules, state right-to-work laws, pension priority, and U.S. Chapter 11 procedures.

difficulty.justification: Assessing whether unionization causally depresses bond values for financially distressed firms requires substantial specialized knowledge of labor law, bankruptcy priority, and fixed‑income pricing; the sign depends heavily on institutional details (NLRA rules, right‑to‑work, pension priority, Chapter 11 practice). Multiple confounding pathways (selection into elections, simultaneity with distress), complex renegotiation and creditor holdout dynamics, and heterogeneous legal/institutional environments make identification challenging though an election cutoff RDD and rich administrative data can materially help.

triplet_key: 11006|decrease in bank competition (via large bank mergers)|property prices
treatment: Decrease in bank competition (via large bank mergers)
context: Using U.S. microdata from the 1990s (loan and merger data 1992–1999; crime scores 1990, 1995, 2000), the paper studies commercial real estate transactions across 11 states and DC and analyzes neighborhood (15-mile radius), census-tract, county, and state units. It exploits mergers of large, non‑failing banks (≥ $1bn) and state branching deregulation as plausibly exogenous shocks to local bank competition to trace effects on loan terms, construction, property prices, demographic change, and subsequent property crime (CAP Index and FBI UCR measures).

difficulty.justification: Mechanically plausible and studied in standard banking/urban literature so intermediate domain knowledge suffices, but the sign depends strongly on local supply elasticities, institutional details, and whether large merged banks expand or contract credit. Multiple mediating channels (loan terms, construction, selection, crime) and plausible opposing mechanisms mean good identification is challenging though the 1990s microdata and exogenous shocks provide reasonably informative evidence.

context: Examines Massachusetts health reform (individual mandate enacted April 2006) using hospital discharge (Case Mix) and BRFSS data from 2004–2005 (pre) and 2008–2009 (post), focusing on nonelderly insured individuals (ages 18–64). Units are county-year aggregates of hospital costs per insured resident and state-year health measures. The institutional setting includes the Massachusetts mandate and pre-existing community rating rules. Analyses exploit county- and state-level coverage changes induced by the reform to test for adverse selection into insurance.

difficulty.justification: Determining a negative effect requires understanding adverse selection versus moral hazard and provider/coding responses in insurance markets; the MA-specific rules, time period, and county-aggregate data leave substantial scope for confounding and context-specific mechanisms, making identification moderately to highly challenging.

context: This paper studies how declining trade costs since the early 1970s through 2007 affected GDP volatility across a multi-country sample (24 core countries plus a rest-of-world aggregate).
