Units include households, barcode-level transactions, individual stores and store profits, workers, and municipalities. The setting follows Mexico’s 1993 retail FDI liberalization and a major expansion of global chains (notably Walmart), using administrative and survey microdata to assess impacts on prices, household welfare (cost-of-living), employment and retail profits.

difficulty.justification: The negative effect is economically plausible (competition, buying power, lower markups) but identification requires intermediate retail/market-structure knowledge and careful handling of endogenous entry, product-assortment/quality changes, spillovers and dynamic pricing. Rich barcode- and store-level microdata plus staggered timing help, but measurement and selection issues and GE feedbacks keep this a moderately challenging inference.

triplet_key: 23101|increase in corporate taxes on largest firms|retail cost of living inflation (bottom 20%)
treatment: Increase in corporate taxes on largest firms
context: Using U.S. AC Nielsen home and retail scanner microdata from 2006–2014, this paper studies how heterogeneous firms’ sizes and qualities are embodied in household consumption baskets. The analysis links on average ~59,000 households and ~27,000 retail stores per half-year across 1,000+ disaggregated product modules (brands within modules) and compares consumption by income quintile. It estimates demand and supply parameters, then calibrates a structural GE model to quantify policy counterfactuals—e.g., progressive income-tax reform, closing corporate-tax loopholes, and trade integration—affecting prices and real-income inequality.

difficulty.justification: Multiple countervailing channels (tax pass-through, markups, entry/exit, income redistribution and general-equilibrium demand shifts) make the sign highly assumption- and context-dependent; rich scanner data and a structural GE model help identification but substantial modelling and identification choices remain, so inference is difficult.

context: This triplet is estimated using the July 1997 Chicago Section-8 wait-list lottery (random assignment) and administrative data through 2005 for low-income applicant households (working-age, able-bodied adults) living in private-market housing. Neighborhood outcomes are measured on a random 10% subsample for which passive address histories were collected and linked to 2000 census block-group data, PHDCN community-survey measures (collective efficacy, social capital) and Chicago Police beat crime rates; comparisons use treatment (voucher-offer) versus control cohorts and event-time analyses.

difficulty.justification: Randomized offer gives strong internal validity, so basic causal link is identifiable, but compliance/take-up, heterogeneous effects, limited (10%) subsample measurement, power to detect null effects, and Chicago-specific institutional context make confidently asserting no effect difficult.

context: This paper develops and applies tools to measure ex-ante expected-utility welfare of insurance subsidies and mandates using reduced-form willingness-to-pay and cost curves from the Massachusetts subsidized health exchange (Commonwealth Care). It focuses on low-income adults at about 150% of the federal poverty level during the 2011 plan year (open enrollment fall 2010), exploiting subsidy-schedule discontinuities to identify demand and cost functions. The institutional setting includes subsidies, mandates/penalties, moral hazard, adverse selection, and uncompensated-care externalities affecting insurer costs.

difficulty.justification: Determining the sign requires specialized knowledge of adverse selection, moral hazard, subsidy-mandate interactions and reduced-form identification strategies; institutional details (penalties, subsidies, cost externalities) strongly shape the sign and the opposite outcome is plausibly supported without strong identifying assumptions, though the subsidy-discontinuity design provides useful empirical leverage.

context: This paper studies the 2013 French dividend payroll-tax reform that tripled the tax rate on dividends to owner-managers of SARL firms (from 15.5% to 46%). It uses administrative tax and accounting panel data covering the universe of private firms over 2008–2017. The unit of analysis is closely-held firms/owner-managers (SARL treated, SAS controls), exploiting legal-status variation and staggered fiscal-year treatment timing to estimate effects on payouts, retained earnings, capital and labor accumulation, revenues, and exit.

difficulty.justification: The administrative universe panel and legal-status staggered design provide credible identification, but firm heterogeneity, legal-form switching (SARL→SAS), anticipatory responses and multiple mediating margins (payouts, retained earnings, investment, conversion) create nontrivial ambiguity in the sign, so care is required to rule out selection and compositional channels.

context: This estimate exploits the original U.S. Medicaid rollout (state implementations 1966–1970) comparing birth cohorts born roughly 1936–1975, aggregated by birth state, birth year, and race. Adult mortality is measured as 20-year cumulative mortality (1980–1999), conditional on surviving to 1980. Identification predicts migration‑adjusted cumulative childhood Medicaid eligibility from cross‑state differences in pre‑Medicaid AFDC rates (categorical eligibility) and implements event‑study (leads/lags) and IV specifications; effects are concentrated for early childhood exposure (ages 0–10), particularly for whites.

difficulty.justification: Establishing this negative long‑run effect requires specialist knowledge of the 1960s Medicaid rollout and identification (IV/event‑study) and careful handling of timing, migration, and racial heterogeneity. The design described is strong and plausibly identifies effects, but residual confounding from state trends, selection/migration, and mechanism complexity leave nontrivial uncertainty.

context: This paper studies the causal effect of US vehicle exhaust standards under the Clean Air Act (Tier 0, I, II) and of registration fees on vehicle emissions and fleet turnover using new-vehicle certification records (model years 1957–2020) and used-vehicle smog-check and remote-sensing tests (mainly Colorado 1997–2014, with multi-state and international checks). Units include vehicle types, model-year cohorts, and individual used-vehicle tests. Identification leverages variation across federal vs. California standards, across vehicle classes and model years, and across state/local registration fee schedules.

difficulty.justification: Theory predicts stricter exhaust standards lower emissions (negative sign), so no exotic theory is needed, but real-world measurement, compliance, fleet turnover, and heterogeneity across states/vehicle classes introduce moderate contextual and identification challenges; the paper's rich multi-source variation materially helps identification.

triplet_key: 23515|fresh start bankruptcy reform|fraction in inaction region
context: Using U.S. firm-level Compustat data (1980–2014) on publicly listed firms and bankruptcy events, the paper studies how U.S. bankruptcy law (Chapter 7 liquidation vs Chapter 11 reorganization) affects corporate capital structure, investment, entry/exit, and aggregate allocative efficiency. It estimates a dynamic general-equilibrium structural model (endogenous entry and exit) calibrated by simulated method of moments and evaluates a “fresh start” policy counterfactual proposed by the American Bankruptcy Institute.

difficulty.justification: Assessing whether a fresh-start bankruptcy reform reduces the fraction of firms in an inaction region requires specialized knowledge of bankruptcy institutions and dynamic firm behavior, and the sign depends on institutional detail and firm heterogeneity. Multiple opposing channels (debt overhang relief vs selection and risk-taking), dynamic general-equilibrium feedbacks, and calibration/data limits make the identification and robust inference quite challenging.

context: This paper studies how monetary policy redistributes across heterogeneous households and thereby affects aggregate consumption, combining theory with micro evidence from household surveys: Italy’s SHIW (2010, self-reported MPCs), the U.S. PSID (1999–2013, semi-structural panel estimates of MPCs) and the U.S. Consumer Expenditure Survey (2001–2002, exploiting tax-rebate timing). Units are households with detailed balance sheets (nominal positions, maturing assets/liabilities); the analysis targets transitory monetary shocks (real rate falls, inflation, output effects) under a closed-economy fiscal rule with lump-sum rebates.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether unexpected inflation raises aggregate consumption depends on detailed institutional and distributional facts (nominal positions, MPC heterogeneity, fiscal rebate rules) and involves several competing channels (redistribution, real-balance effects, intertemporal substitution, GE feedbacks), so the opposite sign is readily plausible and measurement/identification is challenging despite useful micro data.

triplet_key: 13967|increase in unemployment benefits|unemployment exit hazard rate
context: This paper studies U.S. job losers using SIPP panels (1985–2000) and Mathematica surveys (Pennsylvania 1991; 25-state 1998), focusing on prime‑age unemployed individuals (mostly males) who claimed UI. It exploits within‑state over‑time and cross‑state variation in state UI rules (e.g. maximum weekly benefits, ~26‑week benefit duration) and cross‑firm variation in lump‑sum severance payments to estimate effects on individual unemployment durations and to separate moral‑hazard from liquidity channels.

difficulty.justification: Understanding this requires intermediate UI policy and labor-market knowledge; the sign is context-dependent because moral-hazard (lengthen spells) and liquidity/matching (possibly shorten spells) channels both plausibly operate, and credible identification needs to address duration dependence, unobserved heterogeneity, and policy endogeneity—SIPP/state variation helps but does not eliminate all concerns.

triplet_key: 17776|increased state transfers|black teenagers' school enrollment and educational quality
outcome: Black teenagers' school enrollment and educational quality
context: The paper analyzes U.S. southern counties (1957–1982), exploiting the 1965 Voting Rights Act’s removal of literacy tests in seven "treatment" states versus comparison states (Arkansas, Florida, Tennessee, Texas). Using county-level real per-capita state transfers (largely for education) and election turnout, it documents that counties which experienced increases in state transfers after the VRA also saw increases in the share of black teenagers enrolled in school and in measures of black children’s educational quality.

difficulty.justification: Identifying this sign requires specialized historical/institutional knowledge about Southern politics and public finance and careful tracing of political channels from the VRA to state transfers and schooling; there are multiple plausible confounders and mediating mechanisms (selection, migration, differential allocation by race, concurrent reforms) so credible identification is moderately to highly challenging.

context: This study models U.S. labor and health‑insurance markets using worker and establishment data (SIPP 1996, MEPS 1997–99, RWJ‑EHI 1997) to estimate an equilibrium search model of heterogeneous firms and risk‑averse workers facing medical shocks. Units are non‑elderly workers and private employers; key institutional features include employer‑sponsored insurance, HIPAA rules, and 2010 Affordable Care Act provisions (individual mandate, employer mandate, exchanges, subsidies, with major changes phased in by 2014). The paper uses structural estimation to evaluate the ACA and counterfactual reforms.

difficulty.justification: Requires advanced health‑and‑labor economics knowledge and careful modeling of insurance incentives; the sign depends on institutional details (ACA, HIPAA, employer‑sponsored coverage) and is confounded by selection, moral hazard, provider supply, and labor‑market sorting, so structural estimation helps but relies on strong assumptions.

context: The analysis uses U.S. manufacturing workers in three SIPP longitudinal panels (1993–1995, 1996–1998, 2001–2003), with individuals assigned to manufacturing industries. For this triplet, trade exposure is measured both by industry import penetration and by offshoring, where offshoring is proxied by the share of imported intermediate inputs in industry inputs. The paper estimates within-industry changes using panel regressions with industry and year fixed effects (explanatory variables measured at panel start and with one-year lags) to relate industry-level offshoring to persistent idiosyncratic labor-income risk.

difficulty.justification: Inferring that offshoring reduces persistent idiosyncratic income risk requires intermediate trade and labor-market knowledge; the sign depends strongly on institutional and industry context; opposite effects (risk ↑) are also highly plausible; identification must confront multiple channels (input‑output propagation, selection, composition, sorting) and nonrandom industry changes, and the described panel FE design helps but does not eliminate important endogeneity and measurement concerns.

context: This paper examines aggregate short-selling behavior in U.S. equity markets, focusing on monthly NASDAQ data during the dot‑com era (1995–2002)—including NASDAQ short‑interest ratios and CBOE put‑call ratios—and annual NYSE short‑sales data from 1960–2002. Units are market‑level measures (aggregate short interest, put/call volume, and short‑sales ratios). The institutional setting is U.S. equity markets, options trading, and the open‑end structure of professional funds, with attention to how performance‑related redemptions constrain arbitrageurs.

difficulty.justification: Assessing whether aggregate short-selling stabilizes the market requires specialized market‑microstructure and arbitrage knowledge; effects vary sharply by regime, are confounded by reverse causation and fund‑flow constraints, and demand strong identification (exogenous shocks or instruments) which aggregate series typically lack.

triplet_key: 18865|estate tax information treatment|support for increasing estate tax
context: This paper uses randomized online survey experiments conducted in the United States between January 2011 and August 2012 (with one-month follow-ups and additional rounds) to study how customized, interactive information about U.S. income inequality, historical top tax rates and economic growth correlations, and estate-tax incidence affects individual preferences for redistribution. The unit of observation is U.S. adult respondents recruited primarily via Amazon Mechanical Turk (≈10,000 participants across rounds, with some supplemental panel recruitment). The institutional background is contemporary U.S. tax and transfer policy amid rising top income/wealth shares.

difficulty.justification: Randomized survey experiments give strong internal identification of an information effect, so moderate domain knowledge suffices; however heterogeneity by ideology/wealth, potential backlash, sample nonrepresentativeness (MTurk) and temporal/institutional dependence make generalizing the positive sign context-dependent and moderately ambiguous.

context: This paper uses a randomized field experiment run Jan–Mar 2019 in 41 storefronts of a large payday lender in Indiana, surveying 1,205 payday-borrower customers and matching responses to administrative loan records (including the state-wide Veritec database) to study repeat borrowing, present-bias, and demand for commitment. It also analyzes the lender’s nationwide 2017 data and elicits expert beliefs. Indiana’s regulatory environment (interest and loan-size caps, reporting rules) and common two-week loan maturities frame the institutional setting for structural estimation and welfare simulations of policy changes.

difficulty.justification: Identifying whether a $500 loan-size cap reduces borrower surplus requires intermediate policy/market knowledge but is highly sensitive to institutional features (availability of substitutes, term lengths, interest caps) and borrower heterogeneity (liquidity needs vs present-biased overborrowing). Although the randomized field data give strong internal evidence, counterfactual welfare calculations rely on structural assumptions and heterogeneous channels (commitment value, selection, repeated borrowing), leaving substantial ambiguity without careful modeling.

triplet_key: 18493|policy encouraging investment at one stage|investment at other stages
treatment: Policy encouraging investment at one stage
context: Using U.S. data (Children of the NLSY79, NLSY79 cohort and CPS benchmarks, samples through ~2010 and 2006), the paper studies children and households to assess how parental resources and credit constraints shape early (ages 0–11) versus late (ages 12–23) human capital investments and later earnings. The institutional setting emphasizes U.S. schooling (public K–12, subsidized postsecondary education and student loans), young parents’ labor income profiles, and imperfect credit markets. The authors combine reduced-form regressions with a calibrated dynastic lifecycle model to evaluate policy interventions (subsidies, loans, transfers).

difficulty.justification: Determining whether investment at one stage raises investment at other stages requires specialized dynastic human-capital theory and institutional knowledge (credit markets, schooling) and is highly context-sensitive; multiple channels (complements vs crowding-out, selection, intertemporal budget constraints) make the sign ambiguous without strong identification, and the available longitudinal data plus calibrated model help but rely on substantive structural assumptions.

context: This paper studies U.S. publicly traded bank holding companies (171 banks, 1987–2015, with emphasis on the pre‑crisis window 1987–2006) using FRY‑9C and CRSP/Compustat data to assess how market valuation (Tobin’s q and market-to-book) varies with bank asset size—particularly above the Dodd‑Frank enhanced‑supervision threshold ($50 billion in 2010 dollars). The analysis contrasts pre‑ and post‑Global Financial Crisis regulatory regimes (Basel revisions, Dodd‑Frank) and uses cross‑sectional and within‑bank variation to probe TBTF‑related valuation effects.

difficulty.justification: Estimating the sign requires substantial banking- and regulation-specific knowledge because size brings offsetting forces (diversification and scale economies vs complexity, risk-shifting, and TBTF subsidy) that vary with regime (pre/post‑GFC and Dodd‑Frank). Endogeneity (selection, growth dynamics), measurement issues, and many plausible opposite mechanisms mean strong identification is needed and the evidence in typical BHC panels is unlikely to pin a uniform sign without tight design.

context: This paper analyzes a monthly panel of individual U.S. credit‑card accounts (accounts open as of June 1995) from several large issuers, followed from 1995:Q3 to 1997:Q2. The unit is the credit‑card account (with linked credit‑bureau variables); outcomes are serious delinquency and bankruptcy. The study situates these accounts amid the 1994–97 rise in personal default during a strong economy, considering changes in credit supply (limits, new accounts) and potential declines in default costs (stigma, legal/information costs) to disentangle risk‑composition versus willingness to default.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate knowledge of bankruptcy, lender behavior and borrower incentives; sign depends on institutional and macro context and is confounded by concurrent credit‑supply changes, selection, and information effects, so disentangling willingness to default from risk composition is fairly challenging.

context: This paper evaluates the causal impact of the GoBifo community-driven development (CDD) program in Sierra Leone using a village-level randomized rollout. Baseline surveys were collected in 2005, the program ran 2006–2009 (block grants of roughly $4,667 per village with participation requirements for women and youth), and follow-up and structured community activities were fielded in 2009 across 236 villages and about 2,832 households in Bombali and Bonthe districts. Units of analysis are villages and households in a post-conflict decentralization setting where VDCs, bank accounts, and participatory rules were introduced to reshape local institutions.

difficulty.justification: The village-level randomized rollout provides strong internal identification so inferring a positive effect is plausible, but the sign depends on post-conflict institutional details, implementation fidelity, spillovers, and heterogeneity in community governance. Measurement of ‘quality of public goods’ and potential elite capture or crowding-out make the effect context-sensitive rather than universally clear.

triplet_key: 20810|summer youth employment program participation|non-profit sector earnings
context: This paper analyzes causal effects of New York City’s Summer Youth Employment Program (SYEP) lotteries from 2005–2008 on applicants (youth aged ~14–21) in NYC. It merges provider-level randomized lottery records to IRS tax data, New York State incarceration records, and NYC death records to estimate impacts on earnings, employment, college enrollment, incarceration, and mortality. SYEP provided paid, ~7-week, minimum-wage summer jobs across public and private providers; the analysis excludes non-random “special slots” and exploits within-provider random allocation.

difficulty.justification: Within-provider random lotteries and linked IRS records make identification of a positive effect on non-profit earnings relatively straightforward; however, effects may be context- and cohort-specific and there remain modest concerns about measurement, compliance, and external validity.

triplet_key: 24081|state banking deregulation|labor marginal revenue product gap
context: This triplet concerns 1977–1993 U.S. state banking deregulation (intrastate and interstate branching reforms) and its effect on the labor marginal revenue product gap. Using plant- and firm-level data (LBD, Census of Manufacturers, QFR) for single‑plant manufacturing firms across states and industries, the authors focus on young firms (roughly age 1–5) and test whether deregulation caused a convergence (reduction) in the labor marginal revenue product gap between high‑ and low‑productivity young firms.

difficulty.justification: Requires specialized knowledge of finance–firm dynamics and how banking deregulation differentially affects young firms; the sign depends strongly on institutional and industry context and there are multiple plausible opposing channels (credit easing vs. incumbents capturing benefits), though rich plant‑ and firm‑level datasets make credible inference possible with careful identification.

context: This effect refers specifically to MTO participants who were under age 13 at random assignment (moved in early childhood) in the U.S. MTO experiment. Using linked annual Experian credit reports (observed 2001–2017), the paper measures total credit card limits (sum across all open cards) in adulthood and estimates a treatment-on-the-treated effect of using the Experimental voucher (required move to <10% poverty tracts with counseling) on adult credit limits.

difficulty.justification: Random assignment in MTO gives a strong identification anchor, so intermediate domain knowledge suffices, but estimating a treatment‑on‑the‑treated long-run effect requires instrumental‑variable assumptions, dealing with compliance/attrition, and tracing multiple lifecycle mediating channels; results are also institution- and cohort-specific so opposite or null outcomes are reasonably plausible without careful analysis.

triplet_key: 26698|usage fees and operations and maintenance failures|farmers' adoption decisions
treatment: Usage fees and operations and maintenance failures
context: This paper evaluates government-built hillside irrigation schemes in Karongi and Nyanza districts of Rwanda (construction 2009–2014) using plot- and household-level panel surveys (baseline 2015 with follow-ups through 2018). The unit of analysis is cultivator households and their plots near a gravity-fed canal ‘command area’ on terraced hillsides. Key institutional details: gravity-fed valves, terracing, formal land titles, seasonal rainy/dry cycles, and schemes initially operated with no routine water fees. The design exploits the command-area boundary to identify causal effects.

difficulty.justification: Determining the sign requires specialized knowledge of water-infrastructure incentives because fees can both deter adoption (higher costs) and enable better O&M (greater reliability), while O&M failures reduce incentives but may interact with access to alternatives. The command-area boundary and panel help identification, but strong context heterogeneity, measurement noise on failures/fees, and plausible opposite mechanisms make definitive inference moderately difficult.

triplet_key: 26815|ban on underwriter rebates|quality of issuance price
context: This paper studies corporate debt issuance in China’s interbank market during 2015–2019, using 18,229 commercial paper and medium-term note issues by 2,558 nonfinancial firms. The institutional setting is auction-based single-price issuance dominated by banks (notably the Big Four) that act as underwriters and investors; central state-owned enterprises are high-value issuers. The authors exploit a natural experiment—the NAFMII ban on underwriter rebates effective Oct 1, 2017—and variation in underwriter competition and self-purchases to study effects on issuance pricing.

difficulty.justification: Assessing whether banning underwriter rebates improves issuance price quality requires specialized knowledge of underwriting incentives and Chinese interbank auction institutions, is sensitive to institutional details (competition, state-owned issuers, self-purchases), admits plausible opposite mechanisms (reduced rebate distortions vs. reduced competition or compensation shifting), and hinges on multiple strategic channels; the natural experiment helps but important confounders and mechanism identification remain nontrivial.

triplet_key: 11832|universal $5/day childcare program|child behavioral and health outcomes
context: Using the Canadian NLSCY (pre: 1994-95, 1996-97; post: 2000-01, 2002-03), this analysis focuses on children aged 0–4 in married/cohabiting (two-parent) families across provinces to evaluate Quebec’s phased 1997–2000 universal $5/day childcare reform. The reform rapidly expanded subsidized center and licensed family care and altered subsidy/tax treatment; the outcomes for this triplet are parent-reported child behavioral and health measures (e.g., hyperactivity, inattention, aggressiveness, motor/social skills, general health, illness). Estimates are reduced-form DiD comparisons of Quebec versus other provinces.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether Quebec’s low-fee expansion caused worse child behavioral/health outcomes requires specialized knowledge of early-childhood program quality, parental labor responses, and measurement issues; the sign is highly context-dependent and confoundable (selection, concurrent policies, reporting bias), involves multiple mediating and general-equilibrium channels, and the NLSCY DiD design and parent-reported outcomes leave important identification and measurement gaps.

context: This study analyzes the effect of state-level wrongful discharge laws (public-policy, implied-contract, and especially the good-faith exception) on firm innovation and entrepreneurship in the U.S. using firm-year data from 1971–1999. Exploiting staggered, precedent-driven adoption of these common-law exceptions across states, the authors link USPTO patents (counts and citations) matched to Compustat firms, patents per employee/R&D, and Business Dynamics Statistics on new establishments and job creation to identify changes in innovative effort and start-up formation.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether state-level good‑faith exceptions increase innovation requires specialized institutional knowledge of employment law and firm‑level R&D incentives, and the sign is highly context dependent (industry, enforcement, time). Multiple competing channels (worker mobility, firm-specific human capital, selection, general equilibrium effects) and potential confounders/endogenous adoption make identification challenging despite the plausibly useful staggered adoption design.

context: This triplet concerns Latin American case studies of trade liberalization and their sector-level effects: it focuses on manufacturing‑sector labor productivity responses to reduced tariffs and non‑tariff barriers. The paper reports that Chilean manufacturing labor productivity rose an average 13.4% annually between 1978 and 1981, while Mexico saw more modest post‑reform gains (an index rising from 92.7 to 105.1 and a reported 2.3% annual increase excluding maquiladoras). The institutional setting is trade reforms that increased foreign competition.

difficulty.justification: Establishing that trade liberalization raises manufacturing labor productivity requires specialized trade and firm‑level theory (selection, reallocation, scale and productivity growth) and is sensitive to institutional details (stage of development, presence of maquiladoras, complementary reforms). Confounding (concurrent macro/reform shocks), measurement issues and multiple mediating channels make the sign plausible but nontrivial to identify cleanly from case studies.

context: This paper studies the relationship between stability of the largest firms/business groups and long-run national economic growth across 44 countries. It constructs employment-weighted top‑10 firm stability indices using Dun & Bradstreet lists for circa 1975 and 1996, then relates these indexes to country-level per‑capita GDP, capital accumulation, and TFP growth over 1990–2000. Analyses control for 1990 initial income, physical and human capital and institutional features (state-controlled firms, legal origin, financial development, government size, and openness). Units are countries.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether turnover among the largest firms raises TFP requires specialized knowledge of firm dynamics, reallocation and growth channels and is highly context-dependent (institutions, development stage, reform episodes). Reverse causality, severe omitted-variable and measurement issues, and GE/selection effects make the sign ambiguous without strong identification (IV/experiments or micro-to-macro reallocation evidence).

triplet_key: 29905|20% decrease in social security benefits|human capital investment
context: This paper uses U.S. microdata (SIPP panels 1984–2008 and CPS panels 1979–2018) to estimate a structural life-cycle model of consumption, human-capital investment, labor supply and Social Security claiming. Focusing on male high-school graduates (individual-level units, estimation from ages ~22 up to 80), it models U.S. Social Security rules (claiming from age 62, normal retirement 65, using 2004 rules) and simulates counterfactual benefit and tax changes. Estimation employs indirect inference to match life-cycle employment and measured-wage profiles; extensions include health shocks and part-time work.

difficulty.justification: Identification requires specialized life‑cycle human‑capital theory and dynamic identification strategies; income vs. substitution effects, credit constraints, claiming and labor‑supply responses create multiple mediating channels and plausible opposing signs, and while rich microdata plus a structural model help, results remain sensitive to institutional detail and modeling assumptions.

triplet_key: 21890|monetary tightening (fall in m2 growth)|risky entrusted lending via banks
context: This paper analyzes transaction-level entrusted-loan data in China from 2007–2013 (with emphasis on the 2010–2013 monetary-tightening episode), using ~775 lender–borrower–trustee loan observations to study how trustees—especially commercial banks and, within them, small versus large banks—channeled risky entrusted (shadow) loans. The institutional setting includes the People’s Bank of China’s M2-focused monetary policy, loan-to-deposit (LDR) and “safe-loan” regulations, trustee types (banks vs nonbank trustees), and asymmetries between state-owned large banks and smaller commercial banks.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a positive effect of M2 tightening on risky entrusted lending requires specialized knowledge of Chinese monetary transmission and shadow‑banking regulatory arbitrage; the sign is highly context‑dependent and easily confounded by concurrent regulations, bank heterogeneity, and macro endogeneity, while multiple mediating channels and feedbacks make clean identification difficult despite transaction‑level data.

triplet_key: 5625|relaxing universal factor price equalization|hov model fit
context: This empirical study (Davis et al., 1996) tests the Heckscher-Ohlin-Vanek framework using cross-country data (roughly 35 countries) and detailed regional Japanese data (10 aggregated regions from prefectures), drawing on Japanese input-output coefficients, factor endowments, and household/absorption data in the late 20th century. Units of observation are countries and subnational Japanese regions; the analyses compare measured and predicted factor contents of production, consumption, and net trade under alternative assumptions about technologies, homothetic preferences, and factor-price equalization (world vs. Japan-only).

difficulty.justification: Assessing whether allowing departures from universal factor-price-equalization improves HOV fit requires substantial trade-theory and empirical-identification knowledge; the sign is sensitive to sample, technology and preference heterogeneity, trade costs, and measurement error, and the available cross-country/regional data are informative but not definitive.

triplet_key: 21534|state personal income tax increase|number of pass-through establishments
context: This paper uses U.S. establishment-level Census data (Longitudinal Business Database, 1977–2011, with ASM/CMF manufacturing subsample) for multi-state firms (≥100 employees) across all 50 states plus DC to estimate how state corporate and personal income tax changes — including apportionment weights, throwback/throwout rules, and federal-induced shocks (ERTA81, TRA86) — affect establishment counts, employment, and capital. Identification exploits differences between C‑corporations (subject to corporate tax) and pass‑through entities (taxed at personal rates), firm‑state panels, and state tax policy variation.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate-to-advanced knowledge of tax incidence, firm organizational choice, and apportionment rules; the firm-state, C-corp vs pass-through contrast and rich LBD panel provide good identification, but responses vary by state institutions and mobility and plausible confounders (policy endogeneity, composition/selection, apportionment interactions) mean the sign is not trivial to pin down.

triplet_key: 24133|directed entry of a new public nursing home|social welfare
treatment: Directed entry of a new public nursing home
context: This paper studies Pennsylvania’s nursing home industry using resident-level MDS admissions and facility surveys and cost reports, primarily for 2000–2002 (with robustness to earlier years). Units are nursing homes and newly admitted residents; outcomes are skilled nurse staffing per resident and private/Medicaid pricing. The institutional setting is Pennsylvania’s cost-based, peer-group Medicaid reimbursement rule (rates set from 3–5 year lagged peer costs across counties), high Medicaid coverage among residents, and variation in local market structure (county/MSA competition, no CON law). The analysis evaluates causal effects of Medicaid rates and entry on staffing and welfare.

difficulty.justification: Determining that directed public nursing‑home entry reduces social welfare rests on specialized knowledge of Medicaid cost‑based reimbursement, market spillovers, selection and quality measurement; the sign is highly institution‑dependent and subject to many plausible opposing channels (access, competition, cost inefficiencies, reimbursement feedbacks), making clear causal identification difficult even with rich administrative data.

context: Using U.S. administrative data linking W‑2G lottery forms to W‑2 wage records and 1040 tax returns for 1999–2016, the paper studies working‑age individuals and households (age 21–64) who win state lotteries. Wins serve as idiosyncratic, plausibly exogenous shocks to household wealth/unearned income. The setting covers winners across multiple states and cohorts, with tax‑calculator–imputed taxes and marginal rates used to measure labor earnings, employment, capital income, and consumption responses to windfalls.

difficulty.justification: Lottery wins are a plausibly exogenous shock and basic income‑effect theory predicts a negative effect on labor earnings, so only modest domain expertise is required; however heterogeneity in win size, tax/benefit interactions, household responses and measurement/reporting channels introduce moderate context dependence and ambiguity so identifying the sign cleanly is of moderate difficulty.

context: The paper studies Chinese facial-recognition AI firms over roughly 2013–2019, linking firm-level software releases to government procurement contracts issued by public-security agencies across Chinese prefectures. The unit of analysis is firms (and their first public-security contract), using procurement records, prefecture surveillance-camera stocks per capita to measure ‘‘data-richness,’’ and firms’ software registration data. The institutional setting is China’s expanding public-security surveillance and AI procurement (e.g., Skynet/Peaceful City), where contracts can grant firms access to large government datasets potentially shareable with commercial products.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a positive effect requires intermediate knowledge of procurement, AI development, and Chinese surveillance institutions; the sign depends on institutional details (data-sharing rules, firm selection) and is subject to strong confounding (selection on firm ability, reverse causality) and measurement issues (proxy for data access). Multiple mediating channels and incomplete direct measures of data access make credible identification moderately difficult.

context: This paper studies welfare effects of personalized pricing using two large randomized pricing field experiments run with ZipRecruiter in 2015 (Aug–Sept and Oct–Nov). Units are prospective new employers (primarily small “starter” firms) encountering a paywall for a monthly subscription on the ZipRecruiter platform in the U.S. (with some Canadian observations). The authors combine randomized price assignment with rich self-reported registration features (133 dummies), estimate heterogeneous logit demand, and evaluate firm profits and consumer surplus in the context of data-privacy policy debates (GDPR/CCPA).

difficulty.justification: Identifying a positive welfare effect of personalized pricing under inequality‑averse social preferences requires specialized knowledge of heterogeneous demand and welfare aggregation, is sensitive to market and institutional context and countervailing channels (surplus extraction, selection, long‑run effects), and although randomized pricing plus rich covariates provide substantial evidence for short‑run surplus distributions, important ambiguities and externalities remain.

context: This paper examines whether monitored debt—particularly internationally‑syndicated term loans (and especially subsequent syndicated term loans)—reduces managerial overinvestment in publicly listed non‑financial firms across 18 emerging‑market countries. It combines ownership structure measures (control vs. cash‑flow rights, measured circa 1995–96), firm accounting data (primarily 1990–1997), and a debt‑issuance record (1980–1997). Effects are strongest for firms with relatively high capital expenditures, reflecting intensive monitoring’s role in mitigating agency problems in pyramid‑ownership settings.

difficulty.justification: Theory straightforwardly predicts monitored debt disciplines managers, but in emerging‑market pyramids the sign is sensitive to institutional detail and ownership structure. Strong endogeneity/selection, reverse causality and omitted governance/enforcement variation mean credible identification requires careful strategies and rich controls.

triplet_key: 28029|capital intensity × issuer credit spread|consumer credit risk premium
context: Analyzes U.S. credit-card asset-backed securities and issuer master trusts over 2000–2020 (monthly observations) for the ten largest card issuers, using tranche prices, portfolio yields, and charge-offs to identify the market risk premium on unsecured consumer credit. Exploits the post-2008 regulatory environment — notably the Jan 1, 2010 FAS 166/167 reconsolidation, Dodd-Frank risk-retention and Basel III capital rules — comparing pre/post-crisis dynamics and cross-sectional issuer variation in balance-sheet capital intensity to infer causal effects.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a positive interaction effect here requires specialized knowledge of ABS/tranche pricing, accounting/regulatory regime shifts, and issuer balance-sheet behavior; the sign is highly context-dependent and subject to many plausible confounders (endogenous capital choice, macro shocks, selection) and complex channels (pass-through, funding costs, reconsolidation effects), and while the 2000–2020 panel and regulatory breaks help, they do not eliminate substantial identification challenges.

triplet_key: 20172|cash or credit incentive for migration|re-migration rate in subsequent years
treatment: Cash or credit incentive for migration
context: This paper evaluates randomized incentives to seasonal out-migration during the 2008 ‘monga’ lean season in Rangpur (Kurigram and Lalmonirhat districts), northwestern Bangladesh. Between August 2008 and follow-ups through 2009 and 2011, 1,900 poor households in 100 villages (selected by low landholding and prior meal loss) were randomly assigned to cash (Tk600+bonus), credit, information, or control arms via local NGOs. Outcomes include household migration, consumption, earnings, savings, and re-migration in a setting of seasonal famine and constrained local labor markets.

difficulty.justification: Randomized assignment gives good internal identification of long-run ITT, but the sign for re-migration hinges on multiple competing channels (learning/networks, remittances, consumption smoothing) and local labor-market/institutional context, so the direction is moderately ambiguous and context-dependent.

context: This paper studies the causal effect of U.S. NIH public R&D on private-sector patenting in the biopharmaceutical industry, linking NIH grants (1980–2005) to patents through 2012. The unit of analysis is a disease–science–year (DST) pairing and private-sector patents. Identification exploits NIH institutional features—chartered study sections, within-study “science” ranks, and Institute-level “rank-of-ranks” payline funding rules—across NIH Institutes and study sections in the U.S.

difficulty.justification: Assigning a positive causal effect of NIH funding on private-sector patents requires intermediate domain knowledge of NIH and patenting, faces moderate context sensitivity and confounding (selection, reverse causality, citation measurement), and involves multiple mediating channels and feedbacks; the described institutional quasi-experiments provide credible identification but do not eliminate all complexities.

context: Using Danish matched employer–employee administrative data over 2003–2008, the paper studies firms and individual employees around a legal threshold (firms with ≥35 employees) after Denmark’s 2006 Act No. 562 requiring firms above the cutoff to produce gender-disaggregated wage statistics (effective 2007). The analysis focuses on firms with 20–50 employees (treated: 35–50; controls: 20–34) to estimate how mandated pay-transparency affected wages, hiring, promotions, productivity, and profits in the private sector.

difficulty.justification: An RD around a clear legal cutoff with rich matched administrative data gives relatively strong identification, but multiple mediating channels (composition, promotions, bargaining, spillovers) and potential threshold manipulation/anticipation mean the negative effect on male wages is plausible but not trivial to pin down.

triplet_key: 20640|charter attendance|test score gains for disadvantaged students
outcome: Test score gains for disadvantaged students
context: This paper analyzes Boston (Massachusetts) charter middle schools using administrative data on Boston Public School students who were in fourth grade between 2006 and 2009 (followed to eighth grade). The unit of analysis is individual students; the institutional setting is a decentralized charter application system with school-specific randomized admission lotteries, distance to charter schools, and a state charter-cap constraint. The study links charter application/lottery records, student addresses, and MCAS test scores to estimate achievement effects and selection.

difficulty.justification: School-specific randomized lotteries give strong internal identification so intermediate domain knowledge and evidence suffice, but noncompliance, application selection, attrition, heterogeneous effects, and limited external validity mean moderate context dependence and causal complexity.

context: This triplet estimates the impact of using the MTO Experimental voucher (a voucher requiring a move to census tracts with <10% poverty plus relocation counseling) on adult credit scores for individuals who were under age 13 at random assignment (1994–1998). The analysis links HUD MTO records from five U.S. cities (Baltimore, Boston, Chicago, Los Angeles, New York) to annual Experian credit reports (2001–2017); the unit is MTO participants who age into credit markets and are observed as adults.

difficulty.justification: Random assignment in MTO gives strong causal leverage (and can be used as an instrument for voucher use), but the effect works through long-run, multi-step channels (education, labor market, financial access), and is subject to take-up, attrition/measurement in credit files, and heterogeneity across cities and time, making sign identification moderately challenging.

context: Using state-level annual panel data for U.S. states from 1981–2001, the paper examines how the percentage of the state population living in dry (no-beer) counties affects reported gonorrhea incidence among teenagers and young adults. The evidence for this triplet focuses on male gonorrhea rates for ages 15–19 and 20–24. Models include state and year fixed effects, lag structures, and dynamic panel estimators (including first-difference two-stage least squares/FD2SLS) as well as OLS, with standard errors clustered by state.

difficulty.justification: Determining a null effect requires intermediate domain knowledge about alcohol access, sexual behavior, and STI reporting; the sign is context-dependent (institutional enforcement, urban/rural composition, cross-border purchases) and highly confoundable (culture, income, healthcare access, reporting). Panel fixed effects and dynamic estimators help but spatial spillovers, measurement error, and endogenous selection make identification of a true zero effect moderately to highly difficult.

context: This study uses Colombian Annual Manufacturing Survey panel data (1982–2012) on individual manufacturing establishments to jointly estimate production and CES demand systems, recovering plant-level technical efficiency (TFPQ), demand/quality shifters, markups, quality-adjusted input prices and residual wedges. The units are plants/establishments in 22 three-digit manufacturing sectors and 112 product groups; data include product-level quantities and prices for outputs and inputs. The framework employs structural estimation and counterfactual simulations to decompose size and growth heterogeneity and quantify welfare effects.

difficulty.justification: Determining the sign requires specialized IO and structural welfare knowledge because many countervailing channels (price/markup changes, quality adjustments, reallocation of output and labor, variety effects and general-equilibrium feedbacks) and strong model assumptions drive the result; the sign is highly context- and model-dependent and empirical identification is therefore very difficult.

triplet_key: 19423|deselection of low-va and retention of high-va teachers|academic achievement
treatment: Deselection of low-VA and retention of high-VA teachers
context: This paper uses administrative data from a large U.S. urban school district (student test scores and teacher assignments spanning ~1989–2009) linked to federal tax records on parents (1996–2011) for over one million children in grades 3–8. Units of analysis include student-year-subject observations, classrooms, teachers, and school-grade cells. The institutional setting involves evolving statewide testing regimes and frequent teacher turnover. The authors leverage cohort-to-cohort changes in school-grade teaching staff, rich lagged-score controls, and teacher fixed effects to assess causal bias in teacher value-added estimates.

difficulty.justification: Requires standard education-economics knowledge; the positive effect is plausible but highly context-dependent and confounded by teacher/student sorting, measurement error, spillovers, and dynamic staffing responses — the rich longitudinal data and cohort/fixed-effect strategies improve identification but do not eliminate these complexities.

triplet_key: 20762|differential access to lenders|market-wide racial differences in loan pricing
outcome: Market-wide racial differences in loan pricing
context: This paper analyzes market-level racial and ethnic differences in the incidence of high-cost (HMDA rate-spread) home-purchase and refinance mortgages originated between 2004–2008 in seven large U.S. metropolitan areas (Chicago, Cleveland, Denver, Los Angeles, Miami–Palm Beach, selected Maryland counties, and San Francisco). The unit of analysis is mortgage-level borrower observations matched to HMDA, county transaction records and archival credit reports (Vantage scores). The setting covers the pre-crisis/subprime mortgage boom after HMDA’s 2004 expansion and evaluates how borrower, loan, lender, and neighborhood factors relate to high-cost lending.

difficulty.justification: Interpreting this requires intermediate knowledge of mortgage markets, HMDA, and lender behavior; the effect will vary by local market structure and institutional details. Numerous confounders (borrower credit, product mix, steering, lender selection) and selection/market equilibrium channels make the sign nontrivial, though matched HMDA/credit-score data provide reasonable but not definitive identification leverage.

triplet_key: 10121|jobs first program|probability of having any earnings
context: This paper uses MDRC’s randomized Jobs First waiver experiment in Connecticut (random assignment April 1996–February 1997; follow‑up through December 2000) to estimate causal effects on quarterly earnings, cash welfare and Food Stamps for 4,803 welfare cases (women) in New Haven and Manchester. Jobs First implemented a 21‑month time limit, an expanded earnings disregard (earnings below the federal poverty line disregarded), work requirements, and sanctions; administrative UI and welfare records are the primary data.

difficulty.justification: Randomized assignment in the MDRC Jobs First experiment and rich administrative earnings data provide strong internal validity, so identifying the sign (+) is straightforward. Minor uncertainty arises from the program being a bundle of policies and from possible heterogeneity across local labor markets and subgroups.

context: This paper evaluates the causal impact of the GoBifo community-driven development (CDD) program in Sierra Leone using a village-level randomized rollout. Baseline surveys were collected in 2005, the program ran 2006–2009 (block grants of roughly $4,667 per village with participation requirements for women and youth), and follow-up and structured community activities were fielded in 2009 across 236 villages and about 2,832 households in Bombali and Bonthe districts. Units of analysis are villages and households in a post-conflict decentralization setting where VDCs, bank accounts, and participatory rules were introduced to reshape local institutions.

difficulty.justification: The village-level randomized rollout gives strong internal identification, so empirical inference is feasible, but the sign is context-sensitive in a post-conflict setting and multiple competing mechanisms (empowerment/participation vs elite capture, conflict or dependency) make the direction ambiguous; measurement of collective-action capacity also adds noise.

context: This paper studies how Federal Reserve stress tests (CCAR/DFAST) conducted 2012–2016 affected small-business lending by 32 large U.S. bank holding companies and their subsidiary banks. Using subsidiary-bank–county-year CRA loan originations (2012–2015) and micro loan records from the Survey of Terms of Business Lending (Q1 2013–Q4 2016), it links each BHC’s modeled decline in capital under the severely adverse stress scenario to changes in small-business loan quantities and prices across U.S. counties and states, exploiting local employment risk and branch presence.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a negative effect of CCAR exposure on non‑local small‑business lending requires specialized knowledge of stress‑test mechanics, BHC/subsidiary structure and CRA reporting, and is sensitive to institutional and temporal context; multiple plausible confounders (portfolio composition, concurrent shocks, reallocation across markets) and limited sample/measurement challenges make credible identification moderately difficult.

context: Using U.S. county-level data from 1997–2003, the paper studies how the 2000 Benefits Improvement and Protection Act (BIPA), implemented March 2001, raised Medicare Advantage (MA) county capitation payments via urban/rural payment floors. The analysis uses counties (and county-year averages of premiums, benefits, enrollment, and Traditional Medicare costs) and plan-level data to estimate how insurers adjusted premiums and benefits for Medicare beneficiaries in the pre-2004 demographic risk-adjustment regime.

difficulty.justification: Requires substantial Medicare-policy and insurer-pricing knowledge; the sign can vary with market structure, plan entry/exit, and selection. Multiple mediating channels (premiums vs. benefits vs. enrollment composition) and plausible alternative explanations mean credible identification is challenging though the policy cut and rich plan/county data can support reasonable inference.

context: Using confidential loan-level data for 2007–2013 (with focus on the 2009 stimulus), this paper studies China’s banking and investment response to the 2008–09 crisis. The unit of observation is firm-quarter loans (new long-term loans) from 17 large commercial banks to over 160,000 firms across sectors, distinguishing SOEs and non-SOEs. The institutional setting features the People’s Bank of China’s large M2 expansion and the State Council’s 2008–09 four-trillion RMB fiscal package that prioritized infrastructure and involved government guarantees for infrastructure projects.

difficulty.justification: Accurately assessing a negative effect of infrastructure investment on non‑SOE manufacturing credit requires substantial knowledge of Chinese policy lending, state guarantees, and bank behavior; the sign is highly context‑dependent and confounded by contemporaneous liquidity expansion, demand effects, and endogenous placement of projects. Rich loan‑level panel data improve identification prospects, but multiple plausible opposite channels and complex general equilibrium/selection issues make causal inference challenging.

context: This paper studies eurozone bank-level interventions during the Global Financial Crisis and subsequent sovereign-debt shock, using hand-collected State Aid Register data on interventions from 2007–2009 and balance-sheet and loan-level outcomes through 2012. The unit of analysis is ultimate-parent banks (N=830) and bank–firm loan relationships, across eurozone countries differing in fiscal capacity and access to ECB facilities (including LTRO). The institutional setting includes national government recapitalizations, guarantees, liquidity measures, and EU Commission approvals, with fiscal constraints proxied by revenue/GDP, debt/GDP and maturing debt.

difficulty.justification: The sign hinges on detailed institutional mechanisms (types of support, fiscal capacity, ECB access) and requires specialized identification to separate selection into forbearance from its causal effect; heterogeneous interventions and strong confounders make the causal sign hard to pin down without careful design.

context: This paper studies the causal effects of foreign supermarket entry in Mexico from January 2002 to March 2014, exploiting variation in the timing of first foreign-store openings across primarily urban municipalities. Units include households, barcode-level transactions, individual stores and store profits, workers, and municipalities. The setting follows Mexico’s 1993 retail FDI liberalization and a major expansion of global chains (notably Walmart), using administrative and survey microdata to assess impacts on prices, household welfare (cost-of-living), employment and retail profits.

difficulty.justification: Determining the sign requires intermediate domain knowledge of retail markets and labor reallocation; effects vary by local context (urbanization, market penetration) and there are plausible opposing channels (displacement and wage pressure vs. job creation and higher-paid formal jobs). Rich microdata and timing variation help identification, but endogenous entry, general-equilibrium feedbacks, and worker composition effects make causal attribution moderately difficult.

context: Examines births in California around 1989–1995 (focus on 1990 vs 1995) using individual birth/death records aggregated to county-year-race-education cells. The study exploits California’s 1991 Disproportionate Share Hospital (DSH) policy that raised Medicaid hospital reimbursements and induced a rapid redistribution of Medicaid deliveries across hospitals. Units are expectant mothers (Medicaid vs privately insured) and counties/hospitals; outcomes include neonatal mortality and low/very low birth weight. Identification leverages within-county changes in hospital segregation and hospital-market incentives under DSH.

difficulty.justification: Identifying whether higher DSH payments reduce hospital segregation requires specialized knowledge of hospital reimbursement incentives and patient/hospital sorting and is tightly tied to California’s 1991 policy details and market structure. Multiple plausible, opposing mechanisms (capacity expansion vs. cream‑skimming, selection, concurrent policies) and complex feedbacks make the sign hard to pin down without careful within‑county/hospital identification.

context: This paper studies the effects of the 1970 and 1977 U.S. Clean Air Act Amendments on manufacturing using plant-level Census of Manufactures data from 1967–1987. It exploits county-level, pollutant-specific attainment/nonattainment designations for four criteria pollutants across 3,070 counties and 1.75 million plant observations; polluting plants in nonattainment counties faced stricter plant-specific regulation. The unit of analysis is individual manufacturing plants; identification leverages cross-sectional and temporal variation in county nonattainment status and industry emitting status to estimate regulation impacts on employment, capital, and shipments.

difficulty.justification: The sign is theoretically ambiguous because stricter O3 nonattainment rules can reduce shipments via compliance costs or plant exit but also raise measured shipments via sorting, productivity improvements, or selection of surviving plants; the rich plant‑level panel helps identification but heterogeneity, enforcement variation, and spillovers require careful strategies to pin down the sign.

triplet_key: 26717|receiving a government r&d grant|incumbent employee earnings
context: Using DOE SBIR Phase 1 application and ranking data (1995–2013) linked to U.S. Census firm records and IRS W-2s (earnings 2005–2013), the paper studies small, U.S.-based high‑tech firms that applied to DOE Fossil Energy and EERE competitions. The institutional setting is DOE SBIR grants (≈$150k Phase 1), awarded by ranked cutoffs with no ex-post spending restrictions. Units of analysis are firms and their employees; the design exploits ranking cutoffs and panel timing to estimate causal effects of one‑time cash grants on wages, employment, and revenue.

difficulty.justification: The RD-like cutoff design and linked administrative data make causal identification plausible, but wage effects operate through multiple channels (productivity, rent-sharing, hiring/selection), depend on firm/sector specifics, and residual concerns (manipulation, compliance, worker sorting, measurement) leave some ambiguity.

context: This paper studies U.S. doctoral university endowments using annual NACUBO/IPEDS panel data from roughly 1986–2009, exploiting variation in endowment returns (and shocks normalized by endowment-to-cost ratios) across university‑years — including the 2001–02 tech‑bubble collapse and 2008–09 financial crisis. The unit is the doctoral university/endowment‑year; institutional context includes multi‑year smoothing payout policies, state UMIFA/UPMIFA rules, public vs. private status, and presidents’ tenure benchmarks; identification leverages within‑university and state-by-year-by-public/private comparisons.

difficulty.justification: Basic knowledge of endowment payout rules and smoothing is sufficient to form expectations but specialized institutional detail matters; multiple countervailing channels (smoothing formulas, legal rules, public vs private behavior, managerial choices) make the sign context-dependent and somewhat ambiguous, though the described panel and crisis variation provide reasonably strong empirical leverage.

context: Using the universe of U.S. business and worker tax records for 2001–2015, the paper studies matched employer–employee relationships across commuting-zone × industry markets in the United States. Units are individual workers (W-2/1099) and firms (EINs). The authors exploit variation in firm- and market-level value added, worker mobility, procurement-auction shocks, and shift-share industry shocks to estimate a structural equilibrium model with worker heterogeneity over non-wage amenities, firm productivity, and progressive labor-income taxation (amenities treated as fixed during estimation).

difficulty.justification: Assessing whether removing labor and tax wedges raises total output requires substantial structural general-equilibrium and public‑finance knowledge; multiple offsetting channels (incidence, revenue-financed public goods, selection and mobility, firm entry) make the sign context-dependent and model-dependent. Rich administrative data helps identification but the result hinges on strong modeling assumptions and counterfactual revenue/reallocation effects.

triplet_key: 18804|change in surgeon quality|future surgeon patient volume
context: This triplet is tested in the Pennsylvania CABG setting using PHC4 discharge data on individual cardiac surgeons. The treatment is measured as the change in surgeon risk-adjusted mortality (RAMR) (e.g., the change between 1994–95 and 2000, or 1994–95 to 1995 in a robustness check). The outcome is future surgeon patient volume (measured in 2003). The specification regresses 2003 surgeon volume on prior changes in RAMR, controlling for prior volumes and baseline RAMR to test for long-run pecuniary returns to quality in a fee-for-service, regulated-price environment.

difficulty.justification: Identifying whether prior changes in risk-adjusted mortality causally affect later surgeon volume requires intermediate domain knowledge but is highly context-dependent (referral networks, hospital constraints, information/reporting) and faces strong confounders (reverse causality, regression-to-the-mean, measurement error, unobserved shocks). The PHC4 controls help somewhat, but omitted time-varying factors and complex feedbacks make determining the sign very difficult without stronger identification strategies.

triplet_key: 9643|poor firm financial condition|probability of receiving additional bank credit
outcome: Probability of receiving additional bank credit
context: This paper studies Japanese bank lending and its real‑economy effects during 1993–1999, using linked firm–lender annual data for first‑ and second‑section firms on the Tokyo Stock Exchange and their individual banks and nonbank lenders. The analysis exploits institutional features — main‑bank and keiretsu ties, Basel risk‑based capital requirements, government forbearance, and a prolonged banking crisis and low interest‑rate environment — with units of observation being firm–bank (loan) relationships and firm outcomes (stock returns, ROA, sales, liquidity).

difficulty.justification: Determining a positive effect of poor firm health on additional lending hinges on specialized knowledge of Japan's main‑bank/keiretsu ties, forbearance and regulatory constraints in 1993–1999, and is highly context‑specific; substantial confounding, reverse causality and interacting bank incentives/regulatory channels make the causal sign hard to pin down without careful identification.

context: This triplet is based on a structural counterfactual simulation calibrated to Brazil’s municipal model estimated for mayors audited in the 1.5 years prior to the 2012 mayoral elections. The simulation raises ego rents (value of holding office) — e.g., increasing mayoral wages by the average rents extracted — while holding the pool of potential candidates constant. The predicted outcome is first-term corruption measured as log acts of corruption, which falls about 3% (4.02 → 3.99).

difficulty.justification: Requires specialized political-economy knowledge and careful structural assumptions about selection and incentives; the sign is context-sensitive and alternative mechanisms (more attractive rents drawing more opportunistic entrants or increasing ex post extraction incentives) are plausibly competitive, and the simulation result hinges on model calibration and the maintained assumption of a fixed candidate pool.

triplet_key: 28779|lower marginal out-of-pocket cost (from one to zero)|total healthcare spending
treatment: Lower marginal out-of-pocket cost (from one to zero)
context: Analyzes employer-sponsored health insurance for public school employees in Oregon using administrative data from 2008–2013 (≈44,562 households) across 187 school districts. The Oregon Educators Benefit Board set a state master list of plans (Kaiser, Moda, Providence) while districts independently chose menus and employer premium contributions, creating plausibly exogenous variation in available coverage levels and prices. The unit of analysis is the household (employee plus dependents); the paper combines a theoretical model of optimal menu design with structural estimation of demand, utilization (moral hazard), and welfare under regulated, community-rated pricing.

difficulty.justification: Basic economic theory (moral hazard) predicts an increase in spending, and the Oregon district-level menu variation provides fairly strong quasi-experimental leverage, but supply responses, preventive-care offsets, and institutional specifics mean multiple channels and some remaining ambiguity requiring structural work to pin down magnitudes and the net sign.

context: Using Rhode Island administrative DCYF records (2000–2015) linked to public school records (2003–2016), this triplet estimates the causal effect of removal from home on grade repetition for children investigated before age six, focusing specifically on young girls. Grade retention is measured during grades 3–8. Identification exploits quasi-random assignment of cases to Child Protective Investigators and investigator-level removal tendencies as an IV to estimate LATEs.

difficulty.justification: Judging this effect requires substantive child-welfare and education policy knowledge and is highly context-specific to RI institutions and the sample of young girls. The IV strategy helps with selection but the exclusion restriction is plausibly violated (investigators differ in many practices and downstream services), multiple mediating channels exist, and opposite signs are substantively plausible, so credible identification is moderately to highly difficult.

context: The paper studies state-level angel investor tax credit programs across 31 U.S. states from 1988–2018, exploiting staggered introductions and terminations. It analyzes state-year outcomes (firm entry, jobs) and deal-, firm-, and investor-level data (Crunchbase, Venture databases, Form D, AngelList) as well as beneficiary vs failed-applicant firms and a survey of angels. The institutional setting is state personal income tax credits for accredited investors (certification required), with variation in credit rates, eligibility restrictions, and program budgets, intended to spur high-tech entrepreneurial entry and job creation.

difficulty.justification: Identification requires intermediate knowledge of startup finance and tax policy, and the sign varies with program design and local ecosystem; multiple plausible channels (additionality vs crowd-out, spillovers, selection into adoption) make the sign ambiguous, though the paper's rich microdata and donor/failed-applicant comparisons make credible inference feasible with careful methods.

context: This paper studies A+B scoring auctions versus standard A-only contracts for highway construction projects awarded by the California Department of Transportation between 2003 and 2008. The unit of observation is individual procurement contracts (over 1,300 projects) and bidders (contracting firms) across multiple Caltrans districts, exploiting variation in assignment rules, user-cost weights, and liquidated damages. The institutional setting includes engineer’s days estimates, lane-closure constraints, traffic-derived daily social costs, and district-level policy differences (notably district 4), allowing comparison of bids, committed completion days, and actual completion times.

difficulty.justification: Understanding the sign requires intermediate auction/procurement and transportation policy knowledge; institutional details (traffic volumes, enforcement, district rules) strongly affect whether time-incentivizing A+B improves net commuter welfare. Multiple opposing channels (higher bids, selection, quality tradeoffs versus faster completion and lower user costs) create nontrivial identification challenges, though the rich cross-district variation in the setting makes credible inference feasible with careful design.

triplet_key: 16965|changes in household credit limits|aggregate employment change
context: This paper analyzes how shocks to household credit limits affected consumption and employment across U.S. states and in the aggregate around the Great Recession. It uses annual state-level panels for 51 U.S. states (1999–2015) and quarterly U.S. aggregates (1984–2015) to estimate a structural macro model with household liquidity constraints, loan-to-value limits, sticky prices/wages, and the zero lower bound on the federal funds rate. Units are households/regions; key institutional context includes mortgage LTV limits, house-price dynamics, and constrained monetary policy (2008–2015).

difficulty.justification: Determining the sign requires advanced macro‑finance theory and identification because effects depend heavily on housing cycles, mortgage LTV mechanics and the monetary policy regime (ZLB), while state shocks, reverse causation and rich GE feedbacks create plausible alternative signs and require careful empirical strategies even with panel and structural-model evidence.

context: This study models U.S. labor and health‑insurance markets using worker and establishment data (SIPP 1996, MEPS 1997–99, RWJ‑EHI 1997) to estimate an equilibrium search model of heterogeneous firms and risk‑averse workers facing medical shocks. Units are non‑elderly workers and private employers; key institutional features include employer‑sponsored insurance, HIPAA rules, and 2010 Affordable Care Act provisions (individual mandate, employer mandate, exchanges, subsidies, with major changes phased in by 2014). The paper uses structural estimation to evaluate the ACA and counterfactual reforms.

difficulty.justification: Multiple interacting channels (wage compensation, worker–firm matching, selection/adverse selection into individual markets, and firm entry/exit) and strong dependence on institutional details (mandates, subsidies, HIPAA, market segmentation) mean the sign is highly context‑dependent and could plausibly reverse; credible identification requires sophisticated structural GE methods and strong assumptions, so evidence is unlikely to pin the sign down without them.

context: This analysis uses a novel, near-global dataset (AIS signals matched to vessel registries) on large fishing vessels to measure fleet size as the count or characteristics of vessels targeting particular stocks. The estimation matches individual vessels to the resources they target and exploits cross-country variation from EEZ implementation combined with species-specific mobility to define property-right security. Units of observation for this triplet are fleets/vessels targeting stock-country combinations, and the effect of increasing property-right security (EEZ-based, mobility-adjusted) on fleet size is estimated within the paper's regression framework.

difficulty.justification: The EEZ × species-mobility design gives credible variation and permits reasonable inference, but sign depends on institutional details (enforcement, subsidies, market access) and competing mechanisms (incentives to invest vs. exclusion/exit, displacement across jurisdictions, reporting biases) that make the opposite sign plausible without careful identification.

context: This paper studies the effects of the 1970 and 1977 U.S. Clean Air Act Amendments on manufacturing using plant-level Census of Manufactures data from 1967–1987. It exploits county-level, pollutant-specific attainment/nonattainment designations for four criteria pollutants across 3,070 counties and 1.75 million plant observations; polluting plants in nonattainment counties faced stricter plant-specific regulation. The unit of analysis is individual manufacturing plants; identification leverages cross-sectional and temporal variation in county nonattainment status and industry emitting status to estimate regulation impacts on employment, capital, and shipments.

difficulty.justification: The sign is ambiguous: nonattainment can drive down capital through closures or disinvestment but can also raise measured capital via capital‑intensive abatement/retrofits and selective exit; determining the net effect requires intermediate policy/industry knowledge, accounting for heterogeneous timing, selection, and reallocation even with rich plant‑level panel data.

triplet_key: 21498|openness to international trade|exposure to domestic shocks
context: This paper studies how declining trade costs since the early 1970s through 2007 affected GDP volatility across a multi-country sample (24 core countries plus a rest-of-world aggregate). The authors combine sector-level data (24 sectors), bilateral trade flows, and country-year observations with a calibrated multi-sector stochastic trade model that features country- and sector-specific productivity shocks and ex-ante labor allocation. Using the model and data, they back out trade costs and shocks and run counterfactuals to quantify trade’s causal impact on aggregate volatility.

difficulty.justification: Establishing that greater openness reduces exposure to domestic shocks requires specialized trade-theory and identification strategies, is highly context-dependent (sectoral structure, terms-of-trade, input linkages), and faces plausible opposing mechanisms (specialization, shock propagation) while demanding complex GE reasoning and strong model assumptions for credible evidence.

triplet_key: 19467|higher bank capital ratio|loan supply during downturn
context: This paper analyzes bank–firm lending in Italy around the 2008 financial crisis (2007–2010), using Bank of Italy credit-registry data on about 72,000 firms and 179 banks at the bank–firm loan level. It treats the Lehman Brothers collapse (Sept 2008) as an exogenous aggregate shock, comparing pre-crisis (2007:q2) and post-crisis (2010:q1) lending, interest rates, and defaults while classifying relationship vs. transaction banks by geographic proximity and controlling with firm and bank fixed effects.

difficulty.justification: Understanding the sign requires intermediate banking and crisis-era policy knowledge; results depend on institutional features (market access, regulation) and bank heterogeneity. Although the Lehman shock and rich bank–firm fixed-effects lend credible identification, capital is endogenous to bank health and multiple channels and spillovers make opposite explanations plausible without careful design.

context: This empirical study exploits the 1698 Dai Viet–Khmer boundary in southern Vietnam to estimate long-run effects of historical state institutions. It compares nearby villages/hamlets and households using historical maps (1878–1926), South Vietnamese-era hamlet and public-opinion surveys (1969–1973), income data (1970–72), and modern household consumption surveys (VHLSS 2002–2012). The setting contrasts centralized, village-based Dai Viet administration with patron-client Khmer governance, across episodes including French colonialism, the Vietnam War, and postwar communist rule.

difficulty.justification: A historical-boundary comparison benefits from plausible identification but requires specialized institutional and historical knowledge; long-run persistence across colonialism, war, migration and policy changes creates multiple mediating channels and plausible confounders, and available archival and survey data reduce but do not eliminate identification challenges.

triplet_key: 20627|performance pay for tax collectors|tax base (assessed value)
context: This paper reports a two‑year (FY2011–12 to FY2012–13) randomized field experiment in Punjab, Pakistan, allocating 482 urban property-tax circles (three-person tax teams, ≈550 staff) to three performance-pay schemes or control.
