Using circle-level administrative quarterly revenue records and a survey of ~16,000 properties (≈12,000 random, ≈4,000 re-assessed), the study evaluates impacts on revenue, assessments, taxpayer satisfaction, and bribe incidence in a context of formulaic GARV property taxation, weak enforcement, and pervasive collusion between inspectors and taxpayers.

difficulty.justification: Randomized assignment and rich admin + survey data make estimating an average effect feasible, but institutional features (GARV formula, weak enforcement, widespread collusion) and multiple channels (reassessment, stricter enforcement, inspector gaming) make the sign context-dependent and require intermediate specialist knowledge to interpret.

context: Analyzes hospital–MCO bargaining and merger effects in Northern Virginia (HPD8 plus Fauquier County) using 2003–2006 administrative claims and Virginia inpatient discharge data. Units are hospitals, hospital systems, managed-care organizations, and insured enrollees; the authors estimate a structural Nash‑bargaining model (with multinomial logit demand and GMM) to recover prices, marginal costs, and simulate counterfactuals — notably the proposed 2006 Inova–Prince William Hospital acquisition and coinsurance‑rate changes — in a setting of negotiated prices, low patient coinsurance (~2–3%), and self‑insured employer contracts.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether a separate‑bargaining remedy prevents post‑merger price increases requires specialized knowledge of hospital–MCO Nash bargaining and institutional contracting details; the sign is highly context‑dependent, with many plausible opposing channels and complex general equilibrium and selection effects, and structural estimation helps but hinges on strong functional‑form and identification assumptions.

context: This paper studies the causal effect of U.S. NIH public R&D on private-sector patenting in the biopharmaceutical industry, linking NIH grants (1980–2005) to patents through 2012. The unit of analysis is a disease–science–year (DST) pairing and private-sector patents. Identification exploits NIH institutional features—chartered study sections, within-study “science” ranks, and Institute-level “rank-of-ranks” payline funding rules—across NIH Institutes and study sections in the U.S.

difficulty.justification: NIH→private patenting is a plausible positive link but requires intermediate domain knowledge about biopharma R&D and institutional NIH rules; exploiting NIH allocation quirks can credibly identify the sign, yet selection, signaling, complementarity, crowding-out and heterogeneous/long-run channels create moderate ambiguity and causal complexity, so evidence is reasonably sufficient but not trivial to interpret.

context: This paper studies welfare effects of personalized pricing using two large randomized pricing field experiments run with ZipRecruiter in 2015 (Aug–Sept and Oct–Nov). Units are prospective new employers (primarily small “starter” firms) encountering a paywall for a monthly subscription on the ZipRecruiter platform in the U.S. (with some Canadian observations). The authors combine randomized price assignment with rich self-reported registration features (133 dummies), estimate heterogeneous logit demand, and evaluate firm profits and consumer surplus in the context of data-privacy policy debates (GDPR/CCPA).

difficulty.justification: Randomized price assignment and rich covariates make identification of average treatment effects feasible (so moderate domain knowledge suffices), but the sign of personalized pricing on total consumer surplus depends heavily on market structure, heterogeneity, and counterfactual demand interpolation. Estimating aggregate welfare requires nontrivial demand estimation, accounting for selection and distributional/GE effects and has limited external validity, so the problem is moderately hard but tractable with the described data.

context: This paper uses annual country‑year data for 134 non‑OECD countries from 1972–2006 to estimate how U.S. wheat food aid (PL‑480/USDA shipments) affects civil conflict incidence, onset and duration. Identification exploits exogenous year‑to‑year variation in U.S. wheat production (weather‑driven) interacting with each country's historical propensity to receive U.S. food aid, controlling for country fixed effects and region‑year shocks. Outcomes are civil and interstate armed conflict (UCDP/PRIO); analyses include panel regressions, IV 2SLS, and hazard models for conflict duration.

difficulty.justification: Understanding whether U.S. food aid lengthens conflict requires substantial specialized knowledge of aid–conflict mechanisms and how targeting/leakage operate; the sign is highly context-dependent and multiple plausible opposing channels exist (relief reducing grievances vs. aid financing or attracting fighters), creating serious identification challenges. The paper’s weather‑production × propensity IV and panel controls strengthen inference, but selection, spillovers, and exclusion restriction concerns mean evidence is suggestive rather than definitive.

context: This estimate measures the effect of SSI removal at age 18 (driven by the PRWORA age-18 medical‑review rule applied to those with 18th birthdays after August 22, 1996) specifically on prostitution charges for women who received SSI as children. The analysis links SSA administrative records (SSI receipt, earnings, family measures) to CJARS court and corrections data for SSI‑recipient cohorts born 1976–1998 residing in counties with CJARS coverage, following outcomes from ages 18–38 (1997–2017). The reported effect is estimated using the paper's RD/IV framework around the 1996 cutoff.

difficulty.justification: Evaluating this triplet requires intermediate policy knowledge of SSI/PRWORA and local criminal‑justice measurement; the sign depends strongly on institutional, policing, and labor‑market context and admits plausible alternative mechanisms (measurement changes, family support, informal work) so identification is moderately complex despite the RD/IV design and rich administrative linkage.

context: This triplet examines the effect of New Jersey's April 1, 1992 minimum-wage increase (from $4.25 to $5.05) on fringe benefits—measured specifically as the incidence of free and reduced-price meal programs—using survey data on 410 franchised fast-food restaurants (Burger King, KFC, Wendy's, Roy Rogers) in New Jersey and eastern Pennsylvania, interviewed Feb–Mar 1992 (pre) and Nov–Dec 1992 (post). The unit of analysis is individual establishments, with comparisons of within- and between-state changes.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate labor-economics knowledge and attention to franchise-level policies and border spillovers; the NJ–PA pre/post comparison helps identification but chain policies, selection, measurement of a single fringe benefit, and other confounders leave substantial uncertainty.

context: The paper examines Mexico’s 1997 privatization of the social security pension system, using administrative data on all individual account-holders, contributions, opening balances, fund-manager fees, and registered sales agents from 1997–2007. Units are individual workers/accounts (linked to municipalities) and 17 private fund managers (Afores). The institutional setting features nationally set flow and balance fees, tightly regulated portfolios under CONSAR, extensive sales-agent recruitment at launch, difficult switching, and centralized processing—enabling analysis of how local sales forces affected demand, price sensitivity, and equilibrium fees.

difficulty.justification: Requires substantial institutional knowledge of Mexico’s Afore regulation and fee-setting; the sign is plausible via demand composition and equilibrium responses but is confounded by agent placement, centralized/national fee rules, and multiple supply- and demand-side channels, so credible identification is moderately-to-highly challenging.

context: This paper uses matched administrative employer–employee and tax records for Norway over 1995–2010 to estimate how uninsurable wage risk affects household financial portfolios. Units of observation are individual workers/households linked to firm-level accounting (value added), employment separations and bankruptcy records. The institutional setting includes Norway’s wealth-tax reporting of assets, generous unemployment/disability insurance, high union coverage, and private-sector wage-setting with partial firm-level pass-through. The sample focuses on private‑sector primary earners (roughly ages 25–60) with continuous employment.

difficulty.justification: Worker wages responding to firm performance involves bargaining, rent‑sharing, selection and composition effects that require intermediate-to-specialized labor‑economics knowledge; institutional features (unions, insurance, contracts) make the sign context-dependent. Rich matched administrative data improves identification but substantial endogeneity and dynamic sorting/composition concerns mean the positive sign is plausible but nontrivial to establish causally.

context: The paper uses 1990 country–sector data (49 sectors) for the 25 largest countries by 1990 GDP plus five regional aggregates and compares 1990 applied MFN tariffs to 2000 applied tariffs as outcomes of the Uruguay Round (1986–1994). Units are country–sector bilateral trade flows, value added, and tariffs. The institutional setting is GATT/WTO tariff bargaining (MFN rule, tariff bindings, principal‑supplier protocol); the authors structurally estimate an Eaton–Kortum multi‑sector trade model with sectoral dispersion and simulate counterfactual tariff‑bargaining protocols.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether abandoning MFN lowers world welfare requires specialized trade-theory and tariff-bargaining knowledge and depends sensitively on institutional details (bindings, retaliation, principal-supplier rules) and the cross‑sectoral structure of comparative advantage. Multiple plausible opposing channels (discriminatory efficiency gains vs. retaliation and breakdown of multilateral liberalization) and heavy reliance on structural model assumptions make the sign hard to pin down without strong identification.

triplet_key: 10990|equity market liberalization|ability to exploit global growth opportunities
outcome: Ability to exploit global growth opportunities
context: This paper analyzes a panel of 50 countries (annual December observations broadly covering 1980–2002) using country-level units to test whether industry-weighted global price-earnings (P/E) ratios—combined with country industry weights (market cap or value-added)—predict five-year-ahead real GDP and investment growth. Data sources include Datastream, IFC, UNIDO and IMF. The authors exploit variation across countries and over time in financial openness (capital account, equity market, and banking liberalizations), include country fixed effects, and interact openness measures to assess whether openness enables exploitation of exogenous global growth opportunities.

difficulty.justification: Assessing whether equity market liberalization causally improves a country's ability to exploit global growth requires intermediate policy/market knowledge and is sensitive to country-specific institutions and timing; reverse causality, omitted reforms, and heterogeneous channels (capital allocation, selection, volatility, GE feedbacks) make the sign plausibly ambiguous and demand stronger identification than fixed effects and interactions alone.

triplet_key: 28073|expected china shock|probability of voting in favor of ntr
outcome: Probability of voting in favor of NTR
context: This paper studies how U.S. House members (N=435) anticipated and weighed the “China Shock” when voting on China’s Normal Trade Relations across annual roll-call votes from 1990–2001. Using commuting-zone and congressional-district Bartik-style measures of future import exposure from China (constructed from 1988–2006 trade and employment data) and legislator ideology (DW-Nominate), the authors model legislators’ expectations and votes in the institutional setting of NTR/PNTR renewals, applying moment-inequality estimators to infer information sets and electoral weights on constituency shocks.

difficulty.justification: Understanding legislators' responses to anticipated import exposure requires specialized knowledge of legislative politics and trade shocks; the sign varies with party, district, timing, and is confounded by ideology, party discipline, contributions, and strategic voting. Bartik-style instruments and moment-inequality estimators improve identification but measurement of expectations and separating constituency versus national incentives remain challenging.

context: This paper studies U.S. auto lending (2009–2020) using an anonymized 10% TransUnion consumer credit panel and county-by-year analyses split by VantageScore bins. Units are loans/borrowers, lenders, and county-year-credit-score groups; supplemental state-level data on lenders’ purchases of TransUnion’s CreditVision (2016–2019) is used. The institutional setting is the U.S. auto-loan market—largely uninsured, segmented by credit score, with direct and indirect lending—and focuses on how local market concentration (county HHI) relates to lenders’ screening, delinquency, loan quantities, and interest rates.

difficulty.justification: Determining that higher local HHI raises interest rates for high-credit-score auto borrowers requires specialized empirical identification because HHI is endogenous to demand, risk composition, and lender entry, and multiple channels (market power raising rates vs. scale/efficiency or better screening lowering them) can produce opposite signs; effects also vary with institutional details (direct vs. indirect lending, score segmentation).

triplet_key: 14595|growth slowdown in late 1970s–1980s|adoption of market-oriented policies
context: This paper studies the evolution of countries' choices between market-oriented and state-interventionist policies using a structural Bayesian learning model estimated on a panel of 133 countries over 1950–2001 (priors informed by 1900–1950 data). Units are countries; outcomes are Sachs–Warner-based binary market-orientation indicators and per-capita GDP growth. The institutional setting spans global regions and postwar policy regimes; key mechanisms are policymakers' Bayesian updating from own and geographically-weighted neighbors' growth experiences, used to infer policy adoption and simulate counterfactuals (e.g., Great Depression-sized shocks).

difficulty.justification: Determining that slow growth causally increases market‑oriented reform requires specialized knowledge of policy diffusion and Bayesian learning, and is highly context‑dependent with many plausible opposite mechanisms (e.g., turn to intervention). The identification problem is complex (dynamic learning, spatial spillovers, selection, measurement error) and, despite rich panel data and a structural model, substantial confounding and heterogeneity make the sign hard to pin down.

triplet_key: 17347|trade negotiations starting at zero tariffs|government welfare
treatment: Trade negotiations starting at zero tariffs
context: This paper uses 2007 industry-level data covering seven regions (Brazil, China, EU, India, Japan, US, Rest of World) and 33 agriculture/manufacturing sectors, estimating CES demand elasticities from UN trade data (1994–2008) and calibrating to GTAP8 and tariff sources (Market Access Map, TRAINS). Units are bilateral industry-region trade flows, prices, and tariffs. These observables are embedded in a multi-country, multi-industry general-equilibrium model with iceberg trade costs, monopolistic competition, and political-economy weights to simulate optimal, Nash, and cooperative tariff and welfare counterfactuals.

difficulty.justification: Whether opening trade talks at zero tariffs raises government welfare hinges on political‑economy weights, tariff‑revenue versus consumer‑surplus tradeoffs, and bargaining/GE adjustments, so alternative signs are plausible. The calibrated multi‑country GE model provides structure, but results remain sensitive to elasticities, market power, and institutional details.

context: This empirical study examines U.S. security analysts’ recommendations and annual earnings forecasts using IBES data (primarily 1994–2008) and NYSE TAQ trading data (1993–2002). The units are analyst–stock–day observations and investor trades (small vs. large) for U.S.-listed firms (NYSE, Amex, NASDAQ). The institutional setting emphasizes incentives from underwriter affiliation, investment-banking pressure, reputational capital, and the post-2001 analyst conflicts-of-interest spotlight, exploiting earnings-announcement and recommendation/forecast update windows.

difficulty.justification: Understanding analyst optimism due to IB pressure requires intermediate institutional knowledge; the sign varies with regulatory era, firm/analyst selection and reputation incentives; plausible alternative mechanisms (better information, selection) make the sign ambiguous; event-window and affiliation data help identification but do not eliminate complex selection and feedback issues.

context: This paper studies how the mid‑1990s HIV treatment breakthrough (the spread of HAART) altered sexual risk behavior in the United States. It uses patient‑level data on HIV+ adults in care from HCSUS (interviews March 1996–Jan 1998) and high‑risk uninfected subsamples from the General Social Survey (pre‑HAART 1988–1994; post‑HAART 1998–2002). The empirical design exploits cross‑state variation in Medicaid eligibility rules (medically‑needy thresholds and SSI rules) and state controls to instrument for HAART receipt and estimate causal effects.

difficulty.justification: The sign is driven by opposing biological (reduced infectiousness from viral suppression) and behavioral (risk compensation) channels, varies by population and institutional context, and requires strong identification (exclusion restriction for Medicaid-IV) amid selection and equilibrium feedbacks—making correct sign identification difficult.

context: This lecture synthesizes empirical studies from the late 20th and early 21st centuries using administrative, lottery, and census data across settings: Israeli elementary schools (Maimonides Rule), U.S. urban school systems (Boston, New York, Chicago; KIPP Lynn lotteries 2005–08; Chicago exam schools 2009–12), and national samples (e.g., 1980–1990 Censuses). Units include students, school applicants, and families/mothers. Identification exploits institutional features—lotteries, admission cutoffs, centralized assignment, class-size rules, and fertility shocks—to estimate causal effects on achievement, school choice, and labor supply.

difficulty.justification: Randomized lotteries and regression discontinuity used in KIPP/charter research give fairly strong identification of a positive effect, so domain knowledge need not be specialized; however heterogeneity across places/cohorts, attrition/noncompliance, peer and selection channels, and external‑validity concerns raise moderate context dependence and causal complexity.

context: This triplet is evaluated via a counterfactual simulation within the paper’s supply-side pricing model of Mexico’s 1997 privatized pension system. Using administrative data on individual account-holders (1997–2007), 17 Afores, and municipality-level sales-force measures, the authors estimate demand parameters and then simulate Nash–Bertrand firm pricing after adding a low-fee government-backed entrant to compute equilibrium average fees. Units are individual workers/accounts (linked to municipalities) and fund managers; fees are nationally set flow and balance fees under CONSAR with limited switching.

difficulty.justification: Although the paper has rich administrative data and a structural Nash–Bertrand counterfactual, correctly signing the effect requires specialized knowledge (demand elasticities, switching frictions, cost structure, and regulator constraints) and strong assumptions about firm conduct and entrant credibility; multiple plausible channels (cream‑skimming, selection, dynamic responses, marketing/quality competition) could plausibly change the sign in this Mexico‑specific institutional setting.

triplet_key: 21975|fasb/sec expensing and disclosure reforms|probability of option number-rigidity
context: This paper analyzes U.S. publicly traded firms (CEOs of firms ever in the S&P 500) over 1992–2010, using grant-level ExecuComp data on at-the‑money option and stock awards across the tech-boom and mid‑2000s. It leverages institutional details—Black–Scholes grant‑date valuation, stock splits, and regulatory reforms in 2006 (FASB expensing and SEC disclosure of option value)—to study how firm/board practices, peer competition, and reporting rules affected the propensity to grant the same number of options and consequent compensation growth and dispersion.

difficulty.justification: Understanding the channels requires specialized knowledge of executive compensation, accounting valuation, and regulatory incentives, and the sign likely varies by firm/period and interacts with market and peer pressures. Rich grant‑level data and clear institutional shocks help identification, but concurrent trends, selection, and alternative mechanisms make a correct sign nontrivial to establish.

triplet_key: 20583|access to home equity|pre-entry income of marginal entrants
context: This result focuses on homeowners (age 25–50) in Denmark around the May 1992 mortgage reform, using the 25% administrative sample for 1988–1996. It compares treated individuals (those with substantial equity-to-value in 1991 who gained access to home equity) pre- and post-reform and finds that among marginal entrants—specifically treated individuals who founded firms in industries where they lacked prior work experience—the entrants had higher pre-entry income percentile (income in the year prior to entry).

difficulty.justification: Requires specialized knowledge of household finance, mortgage reform mechanics, and entrepreneurial selection; the sign depends on institutional details and is confounded by selection/composition and anticipation effects, so multiple channels must be disentangled and administrative data help but do not fully eliminate identification challenges.

context: This empirical study analyzes individuals who appealed initially denied Disability Insurance (DI) claims in Norway during 1994–2005 (with linked administrative records spanning 1967–2010 and appeals 1989–2011). The unit of analysis is DI appellants and their households (including spouses), using register data on earnings, taxes, transfers, assets and constructed consumption. The institutional setting is Norway’s national DI program and a written appeal process in Oslo with randomly assigned Administrative Law Judges, enabling quasi-experimental variation; analyses include multi-year dynamics and a structural household labor-supply model for welfare assessment.

difficulty.justification: Random assignment of judges and rich administrative panel data give strong quasi-experimental leverage so the negative substitution effect is fairly identifiable; nonetheless correctly interpreting magnitudes requires moderate institutional knowledge (eligibility rules, household benefit interactions) and accounting for multi-year household labor-supply responses.

context: This study estimates long-run effects of Head Start’s county-level rollout in the United States (1965–1980) on cohorts born 1950–1980, using linked 2000 Census and 2001–2018 ACS data merged to the SSA Numident. The unit is individuals (adults observed at ages 25–54), and identification exploits county Head Start launch dates combined with school-entry age eligibility (ages 3–5 vs age 6+), controlling for county fixed effects and state-by-birth-year trends. The setting features a rapid, idiosyncratic OEO grant roll-out and phased program implementation.

difficulty.justification: Requires specialized knowledge of early-childhood program implementation, take-up, and labor-market dynamics; results are context-sensitive (cohort, county, program quality) and vulnerable to migration, measurement error, pre-trends, and offsetting channels, so confidently declaring a true null is moderately hard even with the described quasi-experiment.

context: Using confidential loan-level data for 2007–2013 (with focus on the 2009 stimulus), this paper studies China’s banking and investment response to the 2008–09 crisis. The unit of observation is firm-quarter loans (new long-term loans) from 17 large commercial banks to over 160,000 firms across sectors, distinguishing SOEs and non-SOEs. The institutional setting features the People’s Bank of China’s large M2 expansion and the State Council’s 2008–09 four-trillion RMB fiscal package that prioritized infrastructure and involved government guarantees for infrastructure projects.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a negative effect here requires specialized knowledge of China’s monetary/fiscal transmission and bank–SOE relationships; concurrent fiscal stimulus, government guarantees, heterogeneous bank exposures, and strong endogeneity/selection concerns make the sign highly context-dependent and hard to pin down without sophisticated identification.

triplet_key: 19858|caaa implementation|fraction at bottom of earnings distribution
outcome: Fraction at bottom of earnings distribution
context: This empirical study exploits the 1970 Clean Air Act Amendments in the United States, using LEHD administrative data on ~5.7 million individuals born in 1969–1974 in 148 counties (24 states). It compares cohorts born before versus after nonattainment-driven reductions in county-level total suspended particulates (TSP) and measures labor-market outcomes around ages 29–31 (observed 1998–2007), combining county TSP monitors, natality records, county fixed effects, and state×year controls to isolate early-life pollution exposure effects.

difficulty.justification: Assessing whether CAAA-driven early-life pollution reductions reduce the fraction at the bottom of the earnings distribution requires intermediate policy/health knowledge, is sensitive to institutional and demographic context, and is confoundable by selection, concurrent economic changes, and migration; the causal chain involves multiple mediators (health, cognition, schooling) and some general-equilibrium/selection issues, and while the described natural experiment and administrative data make inference plausible, important identification challenges remain.

triplet_key: 16579|voucher program (school choice)|public school stayers' high school graduation rate
outcome: Public school stayers' high school graduation rate
context: The paper analyzes U.S. secondary-school students using the NELS:88 cohort to assess how a private-school voucher/school-choice program would affect public-school “stayers.” Authors measure student and school characteristics, estimate a private-school entry model and peer effects on high-school graduation, and simulate voucher-induced selection (cream-skimming) under alternative assumptions. The setting is national (U.S.) student cohorts and school peer groups, focusing on policy-driven student reallocation and resulting peer externalities on graduation outcomes.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate policy and peer-effects knowledge; sign depends heavily on which students are induced to leave and on institutional details, with plausible arguments for opposite effects. Identification is complex because of selection, peer externalities, and equilibrium composition and relies on strong simulation assumptions and imperfectly identified peer effects.

triplet_key: 28780|adding an early childhood education facilitator|executive function scores (awc)
treatment: Adding an early childhood education facilitator
context: This paper reports a large-scale randomized evaluation (Sep 2016–Apr/Jun 2018) in Tamil Nadu, India, studying 320 government anganwadi centers (AWCs) serving children aged roughly 3–6. The Government of Tamil Nadu hired half-time early childhood education (ECE) facilitators for randomly selected centers; outcomes include center time-use, child test scores (math, language, executive function), and anthropometrics. Data come from baseline (Sept–Nov 2016), unannounced classroom observations, AWC endline testing (Mar–Apr 2018), and household follow-up (May–Jun 2018). The intervention was implemented by the state within existing ICDS institutional routines.

difficulty.justification: Randomized assignment across 320 AWCs and implementation within existing government routines substantially reduces confounding and gives strong evidence for a positive effect, so specialized theory is not required. Effects may still vary with baseline program quality, fidelity, age mix, and local context, so the sign is moderately context-dependent.

context: This study analyzes US daily newspaper markets centered on 1924 (with historical panel data back to the late 19th century), covering ~1,910 city markets and 12,188 hinterland towns. Units include newspapers (entry, political affiliation, prices), households (town-level circulation/readership), and advertisers. The institutional setting is early twentieth-century US print media—radio nascent and newspapers the main daily news source—where many papers declared Republican or Democratic affiliations. The paper combines audited 1924 circulation by town, directories, readership surveys, and cost/revenue records to estimate a structural two-sided market model.

difficulty.justification: Requires specialized two‑sided market theory and identification strategies; the sign depends on institutional details (ad demand, entry responses, partisan niches) and multiple countervailing channels (cross‑side externalities, endogenous entry, quality changes). Rich historical panel and a structural model help but identification remains difficult due to potential confounders and equilibrium feedbacks.

context: This paper analyzes long-run causal effects of the Gautreaux Assisted Housing Program (program operated 1976–1998; main sample 1982–1994) in the Chicago metropolitan area, linking historical placement records to administrative data through 2019/2020. Units are Black public-housing/voucher recipient households and their children. The program—created by a class-action settlement and a 1981 consent decree—placed families into “General” (<30% Black) or “Revitalizing” tracts based largely on registration order and unit availability. Authors use linked LEHD, MAF-ARF, Decennial Census and Numident data to estimate placement effects.

difficulty.justification: Quasi-random placement in Gautreaux helps identification, so standard domain knowledge suffices, but effects on adult earnings operate through multiple long-run mediators, are highly context-specific to Chicago and the program era, and null findings are hard to interpret (offsetting channels, power and measurement concerns). These factors make confidently determining a true zero effect moderately difficult.

context: This paper studies how the ECB’s Corporate Sector Purchase Program (CSPP), announced March 10, 2016 and implemented mid‑2016, affected trade credit and real activity across euro‑area firm supply chains. Using firm‑year data (2013–2017) from ORBIS and FactSet Revere supplier‑customer links, the authors compare CSPP‑eligible (investment‑grade bond issuers) firms and their downstream customers across euro‑area core and periphery countries to assess how improved bond‑market access changes trade credit provision, investment, employment, and upstream market structure.

difficulty.justification: Determining a positive effect requires intermediate policy/market knowledge and is quite context-dependent (country, network position, timing); supplier selection, unobserved quality, and network spillovers make opposite or null effects plausible, and identification must untangle multiple channels (trade credit, pricing, investment) with limited quasi‑experimental leverage.

context: Using U.S. firm-level Compustat data (1980–2014) on publicly listed firms and bankruptcy events, the paper studies how U.S. bankruptcy law (Chapter 7 liquidation vs Chapter 11 reorganization) affects corporate capital structure, investment, entry/exit, and aggregate allocative efficiency. It estimates a dynamic general-equilibrium structural model (endogenous entry and exit) calibrated by simulated method of moments and evaluates a “fresh start” policy counterfactual proposed by the American Bankruptcy Institute.

difficulty.justification: Assessing whether a fresh-start bankruptcy reform raises aggregate consumption requires specialized corporate-finance and GE modelling knowledge; the sign depends strongly on institutional details and macro conditions, is confounded by opposing moral‑hazard versus reallocation/preservation channels, involves complex dynamic feedbacks (entry/exit, credit supply, investment), and rests on calibrated structural assumptions and a limited public‑firm sample, so empirical support is nontrivial.

triplet_key: 4605|herfindahl index of lease holdings|probability of exploratory drilling
context: This paper studies exploratory drilling on federal offshore wildcat tracts in the U.S. Gulf of Mexico (coasts of Texas and Louisiana) sold at auction between 1954 and 1990, with a detailed panel for 1954–1979. The unit of observation is individual five-year leases/tracts acquired in first-price sealed-bid OCS auctions with fixed royalties. The institutional setting includes public disclosure of sale bids, area-level correlation in geology, post-sale neighbor drilling outcomes, joint bids, and variation in local lease concentration, used to explain timing and incidence of exploratory drilling.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a positive effect of local lease concentration on exploratory drilling requires specialized knowledge of auction behavior, asset-specific geology, and firm strategy because concentration may reflect selection on prospectivity, joint-bidding arrangements, learning/externalities, and strategic free-riding; these multiple channels and potentially endogenous acquisition decisions make the sign plausible but far from unambiguous without strong institutional/identification leverage.

context: This paper studies eurozone bank-level interventions during the Global Financial Crisis and subsequent sovereign-debt shock, using hand-collected State Aid Register data on interventions from 2007–2009 and balance-sheet and loan-level outcomes through 2012. The unit of analysis is ultimate-parent banks (N=830) and bank–firm loan relationships, across eurozone countries differing in fiscal capacity and access to ECB facilities (including LTRO). The institutional setting includes national government recapitalizations, guarantees, liquidity measures, and EU Commission approvals, with fiscal constraints proxied by revenue/GDP, debt/GDP and maturing debt.

difficulty.justification: Requires specialized knowledge of banking regulation, state aid and central‑bank liquidity operations; the sign depends strongly on institutional context (fiscal capacity, ECB access) and on whether undercapitalization triggers public recapitalization. Selection, reverse causality and opposing mechanisms (losses lowering equity versus government injections raising equity) make the sign highly ambiguous without careful identification despite fairly rich data.

context: This paper evaluates Round I (1993) federal urban Empowerment Zones in six U.S. cities (Atlanta, Baltimore, Chicago, Detroit, New York City, Philadelphia/Camden) over the 1990s using tract-level microdata (1990 and 2000 Decennial Census) and the Longitudinal Business Database. Units are census tracts, firms, and workers (using Journey-to-Work to separate resident vs. commuter employment). The policy combined employment tax credits (from 1994) and $100M SSBG block grants. Identification compares treated tracts to rejected and later-round applicant tracts with DiD and reweighting/matching-based checks.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a positive effect requires intermediate knowledge of place‑based policy and labor/compliance channels; the sign is sensitive to local context, selection into designation, spatial spillovers and resident sorting, and while DiD with rejected‑applicant comparisons and LBD/JTW data provide useful leverage, residual confounding, compositional change, and limited pre‑trend information make definitive identification challenging.

context: This triplet is evaluated using confidential U.S. Census of Manufactures establishment‑level panel data, focusing on a strongly balanced sample of manufacturing establishments active from 1982–2012 within three‑digit NAICS industries. The paper constructs a “superstar” counterfactual (λ_STAR) by freezing establishments’ labor shares at their 1982 values and letting value‑added weights evolve to test whether initially low‑labor‑share establishments grew disproportionately and thereby drove the aggregate manufacturing labor‑share decline over 1982–2012.

difficulty.justification: Identifying whether superstar-driven reallocation caused the manufacturing labor‑share decline requires specialized knowledge of firm‑level production, measurement of value added and labor share, and reallocation mechanics; the sign is context‑dependent and confounded by automation, offshoring, pricing and entry/exit dynamics, and the balanced‑panel/ counterfactual construction helps but does not eliminate endogeneity and measurement concerns.

triplet_key: 7363|tax and welfare policy changes|total hours worked
context: Analyzes U.S. single women aged 19–44 over 1984–1996 using CPS March and ORG data. The paper exploits federal and state policy changes — large EITC expansions, federal/state income tax changes, AFDC/Food Stamp benefit and implicit-tax changes, Medicaid expansions, state welfare waivers, state EITCs, and shifts in child-care and training funding — across states and over time to estimate effects on employment and hours of single mothers relative to single childless women within the U.S. tax-transfer institutional setting.

difficulty.justification: Identification requires substantial program- and labor-supply knowledge because multiple simultaneous tax-transfer changes operate through income and substitution effects, extensive vs intensive margins, and interaction with childcare/Medicaid. State policy endogeneity, overlapping reforms, migration/compositional changes, and potential opposing income effects make the sign plausible but nontrivial to establish convincingly.

context: The paper evaluates Brazil’s 2004 ‘Banks for All’ (Banco para Todos) program using administrative employer–employee and bank-branch data from 2000–2014. It compares municipalities (minimum comparable areas) without federal government bank branches prior to 2004 (eligible) to control cities with pre-existing public-bank presence. The analysis links bank-branch entry, deposits and lending to firm entry, employment, wages, and wage inequality across Brazilian cities, exploiting ex-ante eligibility, branch expansion, and rich city-by-industry and worker panels.

difficulty.justification: Intermediate policy and labor-market knowledge is needed to interpret how bank access translates to wages; local institutions, sectoral composition and labor supply responses make the sign moderately context-dependent. Multiple mediating channels (credit to firms, firm entry, composition effects, GE feedbacks) and potential confounders mean inference is nontrivial, but the ex-ante eligibility rule and rich administrative panels give reasonably good identification.

context: The triplet concerns state-level malpractice liability reforms in the United States that directly reduce provider liability pressure, and their effect on mortality for elderly Medicare beneficiaries treated for serious heart disease in 1984, 1987, and 1990. Units are individual Medicare beneficiaries; mortality is assessed up to two and five years after reform adoption across states.

difficulty.justification: Determining a null effect requires substantial specialized knowledge of malpractice law, provider responses, and health-care markets; the sign is highly context-dependent (state rules, Medicare population, 1980s institutions) and subject to many plausible offsetting channels and confounders, so strong identification and rich data are needed to rule out positive or negative effects.

triplet_key: 8960|industry trade credit dependence|industry value added growth
context: This paper analyzes industry-level growth across 37 industries and 43–44 countries, focusing mainly on the 1980s (with robustness to the 1970s–1990s). The unit of observation is industry–country pairs, using real value-added growth as the outcome. Industry trade-credit propensity is measured from U.S. Compustat (accounts payable/total assets) while country financial development is proxied by private credit/GDP. The design exploits cross-industry heterogeneity and country institutional differences to test whether supplier trade credit substitutes for formal financial intermediation.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a positive effect requires intermediate applied-finance and trade-credit knowledge; the sign depends on institutional context (financial development, period, industry structure) and is confounded by selection, reverse causality, and measurement issues (US-based propensity applied across countries). The causal link operates through multiple channels (substitution for bank credit, supply‑chain dynamics) so empirical evidence can be suggestive but not definitive without strong identification.

context: This paper studies the effects of World War I liberty loan drives (1917–1919) across U.S. counties and states, using county-level annual panel data from 1910–1929 and individual Gallup surveys from the late 1930s. Units are counties (commercial bank assets, deposits, number/presence of investment banks) and individuals (stock/bond ownership). The campaigns were organized by Federal Reserve district committees and some states used a centralized “allotment” subscription system, which the authors exploit to isolate exogenous variation in bond take-up.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a negative effect requires specialized knowledge of early-20th-century banking, war finance, and the allotment institutional details because multiple offsetting channels (household substitution out of deposits, banks buying bonds, wartime fiscal flows) can push bank assets either way. The quasi-experimental allotment variation helps, but strong confounding, GE feedbacks, and measurement limits make sign identification moderately-to-highly challenging.

context: Analyzes U.S. publicly-traded firms, 1980–2012 (primary empirical analyses 1998–2012), using firm-pair observations to quantify how overlapping institutional ownership affects managers’ incentives to internalize inter-firm externalities. Combines 13F institutional holdings with proxy-voting (ISS) to model investor attention, and studies episodes such as index inclusions and the BlackRock–BGI merger (2009), with applications to industries like airlines and banking. The institutional background emphasizes rising index investing, asset-manager consolidation, and heterogeneous investor attention as mechanisms shaping governance and potential competitive effects.

difficulty.justification: Determining the sign requires specialized IO and corporate-governance knowledge because airline pricing depends on route-level competition, network structure, and strategic firm responses to common owners; overlapping ownership can plausibly raise, lower, or not affect fares and is confounded by selection, measurement (13F/ISS limits), and feedbacks, so credible identification is challenging despite useful event-based variation.

context: Using administrative records for Chicago public housing and Chicago Public Schools from the 1990s (student semesters 1991–92 through 2001–02), the paper studies how high-rise CHA building closures and demolitions in Chicago affected individual students and households. Units are individual students matched to building addresses; affected families were offered Section 8 vouchers or CHA transfers (forced relocations, limited counseling) amid CHA redevelopment and crisis-driven closures, altering housing, neighborhood poverty, and school assignments.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate knowledge of housing policy, school assignment, and student achievement mechanisms; effects vary strongly with local institutions and timing. Multiple competing channels (neighborhood, school quality, relocation stress), likely endogenous closures, and power/measurement issues make establishing a true null especially difficult despite rich administrative data.

triplet_key: 23526|medium-sized firm merged with large firm|market efficiency
treatment: Medium-sized firm merged with large firm
context: This paper studies firm bidding in Texas’s restructured wholesale electricity balancing (day-of) market in ERCOT during the early market years (sample spanning the first half of year 2: Aug 2002–Jan 2003). The unit of observation is hourly balancing auctions and firm-level bids (12 focal generating firms, covering ~66% capacity) with observed plant-level marginal costs. The institutional setting is a multi-unit, uniform-price balancing auction following the 2001 market restructuring; the study exploits a mid-sample two-month nuclear outage as a demand/cost shock and uses structural behavioral models to analyze efficiency and merger counterfactuals.

difficulty.justification: Determining this sign requires specialized knowledge of electricity market auction design and merger/market‑power theory; the ERCOT uniform‑price, multi‑unit setting and strategic bidding behavior are nontrivial. Institutional specifics (early market dynamics, the outage shock) and plausible alternative channels (efficiency gains vs withholding/market power) mean the sign is context‑sensitive, though plant cost data and the exogenous shock improve identification.

context: This estimate measures the effect of the CARD Act fee regulations on total fees for accounts with origination FICO < 660, using monthly account-level data (March 2008–December 2011) on consumer and small-business credit cards from eight large U.S. banks. Identification compares consumer cards (subject to CARD fee limits) to small-business cards (exempt) over the implementation phases (Feb 22, 2010 and Aug 22, 2010); the outcome is total fees expressed as an annualized percent of average daily balance.

difficulty.justification: The setting and DID design provide a credible strategy but require intermediate policy knowledge; institutional details (fee substitution, reclassification of accounts, interest-rate responses) and heterogeneous borrower behavior make the sign sensitive to context and plausible alternative mechanisms, so identification is moderately challenging.

triplet_key: 10548|news shock (innovation in stock prices orthogonal to tfp)|total factor productivity growth
treatment: News shock (innovation in stock prices orthogonal to TFP)
context: This paper uses U.S. macro time series (quarterly data 1948Q1–2000Q4, with annual checks 1948–89) for aggregate units — S&P500 per capita, measured TFP (baseline and utilization-adjusted/Basu–Fernald–Kimball alternatives), consumption, investment and hours — to examine whether news about future productivity (reflected in stock prices) drives post‑war U.S. business‑cycle fluctuations.

difficulty.justification: Stock‑price news shocks require specialized identification and theory because prices mix expectations and discount‑rate movements, while measured TFP is noisy and endogenous; alternative interpretations (financial, demand, measurement or discount‑rate shocks) are highly plausible, so aggregate quarterly data without strong instruments or structural restrictions make the sign hard to pin down.

context: The paper estimates stochastic transmission parameters from pooled daily confirmed-case data for 16 high-risk countries (January–February 2020) within an SIS framework and embeds these estimates in a partial-equilibrium asset-pricing model with firm-level mitigation and a Poisson vaccine-arrival (calibrated ≈1/year). For this triplet, the authors compute a model-based counterfactual comparing absence of firm/country mitigation to the optimal-mitigation equilibrium for aggregate stock-market valuations, finding markets would be about 15% lower and linking this to the late-February 2020 plunge and March rebound.

difficulty.justification: A negative effect is economically plausible (more infections → lower firm cash flows and valuations), but establishing the sign convincingly requires specialized epi–asset‑pricing knowledge and is highly model- and context-dependent; expectations about vaccine timing, behavioral responses, policy, sectoral heterogeneity, and many confounders make opposite or attenuated signs plausible without strong identification.

context: This triplet refers to a model-based counterfactual in the paper’s structural long-term-bond sovereign model, calibrated to annual 1970–2019 data for 37 emerging-market sovereigns. Debt relief is implemented in simulations by reducing the recovery factor on defaulted debt (mimicking HIPC-style relief). The reported effect (debt relief → lower debt levels) is an outcome from these simulated sovereign equilibria (country-year units).

difficulty.justification: Determining whether debt relief reduces sovereign debt in calibrated long-term-bond models requires specialized sovereign-debt theory and is sensitive to model assumptions (moral hazard, interest-rate response, selection). Opposite effects (increased borrowing after relief) are plausible without strong empirical or identification support, so sign identification is moderately-to-highly difficult.

context: This study analyzes Texas public high‑school cohorts (10th graders in 1998–2002; projected graduates 2000–2004), linking education records (TEA, THECB) and quarterly earnings (Texas Workforce Commission) for individual students. It evaluates causal effects of access to 2‑year community colleges on educational attainment and earnings, exploiting geographic variation in proximity to 2‑year and 4‑year college campuses across Texas commuting zones while controlling for neighborhood, school, cohort, and local labor‑market factors within the U.S. community‑college institutional setting.

difficulty.justification: Understanding this sign requires intermediate higher-education policy knowledge (transfer pathways, completion rates) and is quite context-dependent on Texas institutions and time. Multiple plausible channels (selection, transfer-credit loss, local labor-market pull, remediation) make the opposite sign plausible in some settings, and while linked administrative data and geographic variation provide reasonably strong identification, important threats (nonrandom access, credit-transfer frictions, heterogeneous treatment effects) leave nontrivial uncertainty.

context: This paper uses plant-level data from U.S. Census quinquennial Censuses (primarily 1972–1992) and 1992 PACE data to study how county ozone non-attainment designations under the Clean Air Act (post‑1977/1978) affected industrial organic chemicals (SIC 2865–9) and miscellaneous plastics (SIC 308) plants across U.S. counties. Units are manufacturing plants; the analysis exploits spatial variation in attainment status and temporal changes, with county and year fixed effects, to estimate impacts on investment timing, capital-labor usage, and average production costs.

difficulty.justification: Predicting the sign requires substantial regulatory and industry knowledge because non‑attainment can raise compliance costs, trigger costly investments, or induce exit depending on firm heterogeneity and local industry structure. Spatial spillovers, monitor placement and assignment, differential enforcement, selection/entry–exit responses, and possible time‑varying confounders mean fixed effects help but do not eliminate plausible opposite explanations without stronger identification.

context: The paper analyzes annual country‑level banking‑sector balance‑sheet data for 17 advanced economies over 1870–2015 (country‑year units), examining how bank capital, deposits and non‑deposit (non‑core) debt relate to systemic banking crises and post‑crisis macroeconomic recoveries. Crises are narratively dated and business‑cycle peaks are used for local projections. The setting emphasizes long‑run institutional change in funding and regulation (post‑WWII shifts, rise of wholesale funding). Analyses exploit within‑country temporal variation with extensive macro controls and market‑based robustness checks.

difficulty.justification: Basic intuition (credit booms precede crises) is familiar but establishing causality requires intermediate financial/macro knowledge; the sign is sensitive to institutional era, funding structure, and macro conditions, and is confoundable by common boom drivers, so identification relies on careful panel controls and robustness checks.

context: This paper studies how firm-level financial flexibility affected equity and credit-market reactions to the COVID-19 revenue shock in the United States, using 1,857 publicly listed non-financial U.S. firms (and a 512‑firm COVID‑exposed subset). It analyzes stock returns and five-year CDS changes during the collapse period (Feb 3–Mar 23, 2020), the ‘stimulus’ day (Mar 24), and recovery to May 29, combining Compustat 2019 fiscal accounting, daily market data, and industry fixed effects to assess causal effects of cash, short- and long-term debt, and payout policies amid Fed interventions and CARES Act debates.

difficulty.justification: The positive sign is economically plausible (higher leverage raises default risk and thus CDS spreads) but is sensitive to COVID-specific institutional factors (Fed interventions, CARES expectations), liquidity effects, and firm heterogeneity. Identification requires controlling for pre-shock firm health, simultaneous equity-credit feedbacks, and market-wide liquidity shocks, so moderate-to-high difficulty despite useful pre-period accounting controls.

context: Compares regulatory enforcement and stock market development in Poland and the Czech Republic during the 1990s post-communist transition. Units include national securities regulators, firms and stock markets; the analysis focuses on periods when judicial systems remained underdeveloped and different regulatory approaches—Poland’s active Securities and Exchange Commission enforcing securities law versus the Czech Republic’s hands-off stance—affected market listings, investor protection, and market functioning.

difficulty.justification: Understanding the link requires intermediate policy/market knowledge of investor protection and market microstructure; the sign is highly context-dependent in transitional economies and many confounders (privatization pace, ownership structure, foreign investors) and alternative mechanisms (speculative listings raising short-run liquidity) are plausible. Multiple mediating channels and potential endogeneity make identification nontrivial, though the Poland–Czech institutional contrast provides some empirical leverage rather than a clear-cut inference.

context: This paper studies the U.S. macroeconomy from 1960:Q1 through 2022:Q3, using aggregate quarterly series (GDP, consumption, investment, hours, inflation, real and nominal interest rates, transfers, government consumption, debt/GDP and breakeven inflation). It analyzes episodes including the Great Inflation (1960s–1980s), post‑1990s trends, the Great Recession, and the COVID‑19 recession and stimulus (CARES, ARPA), within the institutional setting of Federal Reserve monetary policy, fiscal transfers and debt maturity to assess funded versus partially unfunded fiscal shocks.

difficulty.justification: Assessing whether unfunded fiscal shocks raise inflation requires advanced macro/fiscal-theory knowledge and careful identification of fiscal vs. monetary drivers; results hinge on institutional context (Fed reaction, debt maturity, expectations) and are easily confounded by supply and monetary shocks. Empirical evidence is informative but noisy—distinguishing funded from unfunded impulses and accounting for general-equilibrium and expectation channels is challenging.

context: This empirical study analyzes 29,361 IPOs across 89 countries from January 1990 through December 2007, using firm-level IPO observations aggregated to country-year measures (domestic and global IPO counts and proceeds). It examines how home‑country institutions (legal origin, investor protection, disclosure, enforcement), economic and financial development, and global market conditions influenced firms’ decisions to pursue domestic versus global IPOs during a period of rapid financial globalization and liberalization.

difficulty.justification: Understanding the sign requires intermediate knowledge of investor‑protection indices and IPO motives; the effect is plausibly negative but can flip depending on country development, complementary institutions, firm selection and global cycles. Correlation of anti‑director rights with legal origin, enforcement and financial development creates substantial confounding and multiple mediating channels, so careful identification is needed.

context: Using an unbalanced panel of Slovenian manufacturing plants over 1994–2000, the paper estimates firm‑level markups and links them to export participation. The setting is post‑socialist Slovenia undergoing rapid trade reorientation and market restructuring, with large entry/exit and a quadrupling of exporters. The unit of observation is manufacturing establishments with plant‑level accounts (sales, input expenditures, exports and destinations); analyses exploit cross‑industry variation and within‑firm export entry/exit dynamics.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether export exit reduces markups is highly confounded: selection into/export exit, scale and demand shocks, composition/selection and measurement of markups can all reverse the sign, and transition‑specific institutions matter. The plant panel and within‑firm dynamics help, but substantial identification challenges remain so the sign is hard to pin down without strong assumptions or instruments.

context: Study of Brazil’s 2004 “Banks for All” program using municipal (AMC) panel data from 2000–2014. Treated units are municipalities that had no federal government bank branch prior to 2004, with controls having pre-existing public-bank presence. The triplet outcome is formal-sector firm dynamics (number of firms, firm entry and exit rates) measured in mandatory administrative employer–employee (RAIS) data; the reported effects therefore pertain to formal, registered firms (not informal-sector entry).

difficulty.justification: Reasonable economic theory predicts a positive effect via credit and transaction-cost channels but identification requires intermediate institutional knowledge (bank allocation rules) and is sensitive to local conditions, potential selection into treatment, formalization versus new-entry effects, and contemporaneous confounders, so credible inference is moderately difficult.

triplet_key: 19484|regulatory limits on credit card fees|borrowing costs
treatment: Regulatory limits on credit card fees
context: This paper evaluates causal effects of the 2009 U.S. Credit Card Accountability Responsibility and Disclosure (CARD) Act — signed May 2009 with key provisions effective Feb 22, 2010 and Aug 22, 2010 — using monthly account-level data from March 2008–December 2011 covering ~160 million consumer and small-business credit card accounts issued by eight large U.S. banks. Treatment is consumer cards (subject to CARD rules) versus small-business cards (exempt); outcomes include fees, interest charges, repayment behavior, and credit volume, with heterogeneity by origination FICO score.

difficulty.justification: Understanding net effect requires intermediate policy and market knowledge; multiple offsetting channels (fee cuts vs interest-rate/credit-supply responses), selection and composition changes, and institution-specific responses make the sign context-dependent and plausibly ambiguous despite strong account-level panel data and a consumer-vs.-small-business comparison.

context: Analyzes U.S. single women aged 19–44 over 1984–1996 using CPS March and ORG data. The paper exploits federal and state policy changes — large EITC expansions, federal/state income tax changes, AFDC/Food Stamp benefit and implicit-tax changes, Medicaid expansions, state welfare waivers, state EITCs, and shifts in child-care and training funding — across states and over time to estimate effects on employment and hours of single mothers relative to single childless women within the U.S. tax-transfer institutional setting.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a positive effect of welfare waivers on single mothers' employment requires intermediate policy knowledge and careful accounting for many contemporaneous state/federal policy changes, selection into waivers, and heterogeneous treatment effects; the sign is plausible but context- and specification-sensitive, so credible identification is challenging though feasible with strong research design.

context: This paper studies how the mid‑1990s HIV treatment breakthrough (the spread of HAART) altered sexual risk behavior in the United States. It uses patient‑level data on HIV+ adults in care from HCSUS (interviews March 1996–Jan 1998) and high‑risk uninfected subsamples from the General Social Survey (pre‑HAART 1988–1994; post‑HAART 1998–2002). The empirical design exploits cross‑state variation in Medicaid eligibility rules (medically‑needy thresholds and SSI rules) and state controls to instrument for HAART receipt and estimate causal effects.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether HAART changed safe‑sex frequency requires intermediate epidemiological and behavioral-economics knowledge; the sign is highly context-dependent (HIV+ vs uninfected, era, counseling) and nearly equally plausible in either direction (risk compensation vs enhanced care). Identification also involves strong selection, spillovers, and instrument-validity concerns, so empirical evidence must be carefully interpreted.

context: This paper uses bilateral annual data on international bank assets and liabilities from the BIS for 18 advanced economies over 1978–2006 (153+ country-pair observations). The unit of observation is country-pair–year. It studies how de-facto banking integration and a de-jure regulatory harmonization index (timing of transposition of 27 EU FSAP Directives) affect business-cycle synchronization among developed countries, controlling for pair fixed effects and common shocks; analyses focus on tranquil pre-2007 periods and exploit within-pair changes and staggered legislative adoption.

difficulty.justification: Establishing that harmonization reduces output synchronization requires specialized knowledge of banking regulation and international finance; the effect depends on institutional specifics and timing, faces substantial endogeneity/confounding risks, and operates through multiple transmission channels, while the staggered-adoption panel design helps but sample, measurement, and remaining identification threats leave the sign moderately hard to pin down.

context: This triplet concerns the U.S. banking system during the 2007–2008 financial crisis (weekly window ~Jan 2007–Jan 2009). The paper argues that increases in repo haircuts (collateral initial margins) reduced available short-term financing in the large U.S. repo market (authors illustrate with a $10 trillion benchmark), producing funding shortfalls that forced asset sales, depressed collateral values, and contributed to the U.S. banking system becoming effectively insolvent during the crisis.

difficulty.justification: Establishing that higher repo haircuts worsened solvency in 2007–08 requires specialized knowledge of repo mechanics and funding networks; the effect is highly context-dependent, subject to many confounders and feedbacks (asset fire sales, runs, policy interventions), and data/identification are limited so the sign is hard to pin down without strong structural or quasi-experimental evidence.

triplet_key: 15745|new payroll tax regime|labor supply (hours, overtime, bonuses, jobs)
context: This paper studies the long-run effects of a 1992 Greek pension/payroll-tax reform that, from Jan 1, 1993, raised the monthly earnings cap for social security contributions for new entrants. Using full-population administrative IKA data on individuals who first entered 1988–1997 and linked March 2004–2007 job records (workers and firms), the authors compare cohorts just before and after the cutoff to estimate how employer and employee payroll taxes affected gross/posted/net earnings, labor supply, turnover, and pay growth.

difficulty.justification: Determining a true null effect of a payroll‑tax change requires intermediate labor‑economics knowledge and is quite context dependent (institutional details of Greek social security and entry cohorts). Multiple offsetting incidence channels, selection/composition and measurement issues (hours, bonuses, informal adjustments) make opposite signs plausible without careful identification, and even rich administrative data leave nontrivial concerns about power and unobserved margins, so establishing 'no effect' is moderately difficult.

context: The paper evaluates Brazil’s 2004 ‘Banks for All’ (Banco para Todos) program using administrative employer–employee and bank-branch data from 2000–2014. It compares municipalities (minimum comparable areas) without federal government bank branches prior to 2004 (eligible) to control cities with pre-existing public-bank presence. The analysis links bank-branch entry, deposits and lending to firm entry, employment, wages, and wage inequality across Brazilian cities, exploiting ex-ante eligibility, branch expansion, and rich city-by-industry and worker panels.

difficulty.justification: Understanding the sign requires intermediate knowledge of finance–labor channels and is context dependent because effects hinge on local credit constraints, firm composition, and labor-market institutions. Although the paper's ex-ante eligibility design and rich panels give credible leverage, multiple competing mechanisms (firm entry, worker sorting, composition and GE feedbacks) and plausible opposing signs keep identification nontrivial.

triplet_key: 20455|u.s. tariff reductions|share of manufacturing workers in formal sector
outcome: Share of manufacturing workers in formal sector
context: This paper studies Vietnam around the 2001 U.S.-Vietnam Bilateral Trade Agreement (implemented Dec 10, 2001) using nationally representative VHLSS waves (2002 and 2004 covering 2001/02 and 2003/04) and enterprise data. The unit of observation is individual workers (ages 20–64) linked to 2-digit ISIC industries. The BTA induced large, plausibly exogenous, industry-level declines in U.S. tariffs (avg. −20.9 percentage points) that differentially raised export opportunities, enabling causal analysis of labor reallocation between informal household businesses and registered enterprises and implications for aggregate productivity.

difficulty.justification: The BTA provides plausibly exogenous industry-level tariff variation so intermediate trade/policy knowledge suffices, but sign depends on institutional context and is confoundable by concurrent shocks and selection; identifying the net effect requires tracing multiple channels (firm entry, worker reallocation, wage/GE feedbacks), and the available data are good but not definitive.

context: Using a 2018–2019 World Bank "Doing Business" procurement module, the paper analyzes procurement laws, actual procurement practices, and outcomes for a hypothetical $2.5 million road-resurfacing contract across 187 countries. Units are countries (responses from lawyers, contractors, and procuring entities). It links cross-country variation in legal rules versus practice to outcomes (perceived corruption, procedural efficiency, cost/time/quality) and examines heterogeneity by public-sector capacity (human capital, government efficiency).

difficulty.justification: Identifying the net effect of procurement regulation on outcomes across 187 countries requires specialized knowledge of procurement law, measurement of de jure vs de facto practices, and institutional interactions; cross-country heterogeneity, reverse causality, measurement error, and many plausible confounders make the sign highly context-dependent and empirically difficult to pin down.

context: Using US commuting-zone data (722 commuting zones), the paper estimates the impact of exposure to robots (constructed from industry robot adoption, 1993–2007) on changes in employment rates and average wages by 10-year age groups between 1990 and 2007. The analysis focuses on middle-aged workers (roughly ages 35–54) and finds negative effects on their employment and earnings, with no negative effects for workers 55+. Regressions include Census region fixed effects and controls for local demographics, industry shares, trade exposure, routinization and offshoring.

difficulty.justification: Moderately high difficulty: standard labour-economics theory suffices but the sign depends on local/time-specific industry composition and reallocation channels; endogenous robot adoption, simultaneous trade/technology shocks, and multiple mediating channels make opposite explanations plausible despite reasonable controls.

context: This paper analyzes U.S. income and wage concentration using annual IRS tax-return and estate-return tabulations and national accounts from roughly 1913–1998 (estates to 1997). The unit of observation is the tax unit (household) and decedents, ranked into top fractiles (P90–P99.99) with decomposition by income sources (wages, dividends, business income). The institutional background includes the U.S. federal income and estate tax system, corporate taxation, wartime wage controls, the Great Depression and World Wars, and major tax reforms (e.g., TRA86), used as historical shocks.

difficulty.justification: Assessing whether progressive capital and estate taxation reduces wealth concentration requires substantial institutional and historical tax knowledge, and is highly context-dependent (policy changes, wars, reporting rules). Identification is made difficult by tax avoidance/evasion, compositional/top-coding and measurement issues, and multiple general-equilibrium and selection channels, though long-run tabulations and policy shocks make careful empirical work feasible.

context: This paper analyzes two large-scale representative U.S. surveys conducted Feb–May 2019 (n≈2,780 for income tax; n≈2,360 for estate tax) of individuals aged 18–69. It studies knowledge, beliefs, and normative views about U.S. federal income and estate taxation, elicits perceived behavioral and distributional mechanisms, and implements randomized short instructional video treatments to test how pedagogical explanations (Redistribution, Efficiency, Economist) causally shift support for progressive tax policies.

difficulty.justification: Predicting whether an efficiency-focused instructional video raises support for the estate tax requires intermediate economic knowledge but is highly context-dependent: effects hinge on prior beliefs, partisan identity, and the exact framing. Although a randomized treatment gives credible causal identification within the study, motivated reasoning, heterogeneous responses, and generalizability make the sign nontrivial to pin down without empirical estimation.

triplet_key: 29253|higher retail market concentration|pass-through of wholesale price increases
context: Using store-level outlet prices from Statistics Canadas CDER-CPI across Canadian census metropolitan areas, the authors analyze monthly item-outlet data for 2009	62018. They estimate item(i)-outlet(j)-month(t) panel regressions that compare bread to control categories by interacting a linear time trend with a bread indicator and with local market-structure measures (HHI, top-3 chain share, single-discounter) constructed from Infogroup, to assess how retail concentration affects pass-through of wholesale price increases.

difficulty.justification: Understanding whether concentration raises pass-through requires intermediate to advanced retail/vertical-market knowledge; the sign is context-dependent (product, contracts, promotions) and alternative mechanisms (retailer buying power reducing pass-through vs market power increasing it) are plausibly competitive, while identification is complicated by endogenous location/entry, bargaining and dynamic pricing, so rich panel data helps but substantial confounding and measurement challenges remain.

context: This paper analyzes U.S. local labor markets using Census microdata (Longitudinal Business Database) spanning 1977–2013 (concentration trends) and firm-level panels through 2011/2014 for estimation and calibration. Markets are defined as commuting-zone × 3-digit NAICS; units are firms (firm×market×year employment and payroll) and aggregated workers. The core empirical strategy exploits state corporate tax rate changes as quasi-exogenous shocks to firm labor demand—focusing on tradeable sectors and C‑corporations—combined with firm×market and year fixed effects to estimate size-dependent employment and wage responses.

difficulty.justification: Reasonable applied-economics knowledge plus tax-incidence theory is needed because effects depend on firm type, tradability, and profit-shifting; multiple confounding channels (state policy endogeneity, reallocation, pass-through to wages, GE feedbacks) make sign identification nontrivial, though rich LBD data and quasi‑experimental tax shocks provide fairly good empirical leverage.

context: This paper evaluates the causal impact of the GoBifo community-driven development (CDD) program in Sierra Leone using a village-level randomized rollout. Baseline surveys were collected in 2005, the program ran 2006–2009 (block grants of roughly $4,667 per village with participation requirements for women and youth), and follow-up and structured community activities were fielded in 2009 across 236 villages and about 2,832 households in Bombali and Bonthe districts. Units of analysis are villages and households in a post-conflict decentralization setting where VDCs, bank accounts, and participatory rules were introduced to reshape local institutions.

difficulty.justification: A village-level randomized rollout substantially eases identification, so intermediate policy knowledge suffices; however, spillovers, implementation heterogeneity, measurement and external‑validity issues create moderate context dependence and causal complexity despite reasonably strong evidence.

context: Using a global panel of up to 130 countries over 1970–2012 (baseline regressions 1980–2012), the paper treats announcements of giant oil and gas field discoveries as plausibly exogenous news shocks to future output. The unit of observation is the country-year; the authors construct discovery NPV (percent of GDP), exploit the typical 4–6 year lag from discovery to production, and estimate dynamic responses of macro aggregates (current account, saving, investment, GDP, consumption, employment) while controlling for country and year fixed effects.

difficulty.justification: Identifying the sign requires advanced open‑economy/resource macro knowledge and is highly context dependent (institutions, exchange rate, capital mobility, timing). Multiple offsetting channels (expected windfall saving vs investment/imports, Dutch disease, fiscal responses) and measurement/identification challenges make the direction highly ambiguous without careful heterogeneity and channel analysis.

context: This paper studies how the COVID-19 pandemic (primarily 2020, comparing Dec 2019–Dec 2020 against pre-2014–2019 averages) reshaped within‑metropolitan housing values across the largest 30 U.S. MSAs. Using ZIP-code–month observations of quality‑adjusted house prices (ZHVI), rents (ZORI), listings, and cellphone-based migration, the authors compare urban core versus suburban ZIPs to assess pandemic-era migration, remote‑work capacity, local lockdowns, and housing supply/regulatory constraints as drivers of bid-rent flattening.

difficulty.justification: Interpreting whether regulation amplifies pandemic-era bid‑rent flattening needs intermediate urban‑economics knowledge and depends on where supply constraints bind (central vs suburban) and on heterogeneous demand shocks; it is plausibly confounded by occupation/remote‑work composition, lockdowns, and endogenous migration and therefore requires strong identification to be credible.

context: This triplet refers to US interstate and metropolitan-area migration rates of individual workers, measured from CPS migration between MSAs (1962–2016). The paper documents a decline (about 3% in 1980 to ~1.5% recently) and links the Compustat-based rise in firm-level markups (1950–2014, focus 1960–2014) via reduced job-flow responsiveness across local labor markets to lower migration rates.

difficulty.justification: Establishing that rising firm markups causally reduce individual interstate/MSA migration requires substantial IO and labor-economics knowledge and careful identification; the relationship is highly context-dependent and confounded by many alternative explanations (housing, demographics, labor supply, policy) and measurement mismatches, while causal pathways involve multiple mediating channels and economy-wide feedbacks, so available evidence is unlikely to pin the sign without strong assumptions or quasi-experimental variation.

triplet_key: 28780|adding an early childhood education facilitator|stunting rate
treatment: Adding an early childhood education facilitator
context: This paper reports a large-scale randomized evaluation (Sep 2016–Apr/Jun 2018) in Tamil Nadu, India, studying 320 government anganwadi centers (AWCs) serving children aged roughly 3–6. The Government of Tamil Nadu hired half-time early childhood education (ECE) facilitators for randomly selected centers; outcomes include center time-use, child test scores (math, language, executive function), and anthropometrics. Data come from baseline (Sept–Nov 2016), unannounced classroom observations, AWC endline testing (Mar–Apr 2018), and household follow-up (May–Jun 2018). The intervention was implemented by the state within existing ICDS institutional routines.

difficulty.justification: Linking an additional ECE facilitator to reduced stunting requires intermediate knowledge of nutrition, ICDS service delivery, and caregiver-behavior pathways; the sign depends heavily on local nutrition inputs, attendance and time horizon. A randomized rollout aids identification, but stunting is slow-moving and noisy, so effects are hard to detect and alternative null outcomes are plausible.

triplet_key: 17814|unemployment insurance extensions|probability of nonemployment after reemployment
