context: This paper uses administrative social-security records for Germany (1975–2004) to study unemployment insurance (UI) duration effects for workers who became unemployed 1987–1999. The unit of observation is individual employees (ages 40–43 meeting experience criteria). Policy variation arises from a statutory discontinuity at age 42 that increased maximum UI from 12 to 18 months for eligible claimants. Outcomes are days on UI benefits and days in nonemployment up to five years after the initial UI claim, with dynamic effects analyzed around the RD cutoff.

difficulty.justification: The age-based RD with administrative data gives strong internal identification but interpreting the sign hinges on competing channels (moral hazard vs better match quality) and dynamic selection, so substantive labor-market knowledge is needed. Results are also context-specific to mid-career German workers in 1987–1999, limiting generalization and raising moderate ambiguity about the sign without empirical estimation.

triplet_key: 28779|allocating all households to optimal single contract|social welfare
treatment: Allocating all households to optimal single contract
context: Analyzes employer-sponsored health insurance for public school employees in Oregon using administrative data from 2008–2013 (≈44,562 households) across 187 school districts. The Oregon Educators Benefit Board set a state master list of plans (Kaiser, Moda, Providence) while districts independently chose menus and employer premium contributions, creating plausibly exogenous variation in available coverage levels and prices. The unit of analysis is the household (employee plus dependents); the paper combines a theoretical model of optimal menu design with structural estimation of demand, utilization (moral hazard), and welfare under regulated, community-rated pricing.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether a single optimal contract raises welfare requires specialized knowledge of insurance design, adverse selection and moral hazard; the sign is highly dependent on institutional details (community rating, employer contributions) and multiple countervailing channels make the opposite sign quite plausible, so credible identification hinges on strong structural assumptions and rich data.

triplet_key: 25245|initial allowance of a high-value patent|earnings of inventors
treatment: Initial allowance of a high-value patent
context: This paper studies US firms and their workers using linked USPTO patent applications (published 2001–2011, analysis up to 2010 filings) and US Treasury business and W2/1099 tax records (1997–2014). The unit of analysis is patent-applying firms (EINs), typically small newly-innovative firms (median ~17 employees), and their employees. It exploits USPTO initial allowance versus rejection decisions (within art-unit/year) and predicted ex-ante patent value to identify how patent-induced productivity and rents affect firm outcomes, wages, hiring, and retention in the U.S. patent institutional setting.

difficulty.justification: Understanding the sign requires intermediate knowledge of patent institutions and labor-market rents; the within-art-unit/year allowance design plus rich tax data gives fairly strong identification but multiple mediating channels (productivity, bargaining, sorting, retention) and context (firm size, industry, patent regime) create moderate ambiguity and complexity.

context: This paper studies the 2013 French dividend payroll-tax reform that tripled the tax rate on dividends to owner-managers of SARL firms (from 15.5% to 46%). It uses administrative tax and accounting panel data covering the universe of private firms over 2008–2017. The unit of analysis is closely-held firms/owner-managers (SARL treated, SAS controls), exploiting legal-status variation and staggered fiscal-year treatment timing to estimate effects on payouts, retained earnings, capital and labor accumulation, revenues, and exit.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether higher dividend taxes raise firm revenues requires specialized knowledge of payout substitution, owner-manager incentives, tax avoidance and accounting rules (domain_knowledge=4) and is highly sensitive to legal form, labor/tax institutions and firm financial constraints (context_dependence=4). Opposite effects (reduced effort/investment or tax-motivated reporting) are plausibly competitive (ambiguity=4) and identification requires tracing multiple mediating and selection channels plus feedbacks (causal_complexity=5). The described administrative, legal-status variation and panel data substantially strengthen identification, so evidence sufficiency is fairly good (2), but overall difficulty remains elevated (4).

triplet_key: 29116|removal of explicit gender requests|gender mix in non-gendered job ads
outcome: Gender mix in non-gendered job ads
context: The paper analyzes the causal effects of an unexpected removal of employers’ standardized ‘preferred gender’ field from all job ads on XMRC.com (a Xiamen, China job board) on March 1, 2019. Using internal ad-, application-, resume- and call-back records for roughly one year surrounding the ban, the authors study ad- and application-level outcomes (jobs, workers, firms), exploiting within-ad pre/post changes, high-frequency (daily/weekly) time variation, and a 2018–2019 lunar-calendar comparison. Only the encoded gender label was removed; embedded text preferences remained.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate labor-market knowledge; the sign depends on local practices and remaining embedded text signals so context matters a lot. Rich administrative, within-ad pre/post and high-frequency data help identification, but multiple channels (employer wording, applicant selection, signaling, platform sorting) make a null effect hard to confidently establish.

triplet_key: 9466|allowing and practicing short selling|cross-sectional variation in equity returns
context: This paper analyzes 1990–2001 equity market data from 47 countries worldwide, using firm-week and country-year observations (individual stocks and value‑weighted market indices) to study how legal and practical short‑selling restrictions and securities‑lending frictions affect price discovery, return synchronicity (MYY measures and R2), return skewness, volatility, and frequency of extreme negative returns. The empirical setting exploits cross‑country variation and several regulatory changes (e.g., Hong Kong, Sweden, Norway, Malaysia, Thailand) and controls for GDP per capita, industry concentration, governance, and year and country fixed effects.

difficulty.justification: Identifying that short‑selling increases cross‑sectional return variation requires solid asset‑pricing and market‑microstructure knowledge; the effect depends strongly on institutional features (liquidity, enforcement) and there are plausible opposing channels (price correction reducing dispersion). The empirical design (cross‑country variation and regulatory changes with fixed effects) helps identification, but remaining endogeneity, concurrent reforms, and multiple mediating channels keep the problem moderately difficult.

context: This paper studies the rise of “zombie credit” and its effect on inflation using firm-level (≈1.17 million firms) and product‑level CPI data across 65 industries in 12 European countries (Austria, Belgium, Germany, Denmark, Spain, Finland, France, Italy, Poland, Portugal, Sweden, Slovakia) roughly over 2009–2016 with emphasis on the post‑2012 period. The setting is post‑global financial and sovereign‑debt crises under ultra‑accommodative ECB policies (negative rates, asset purchases), where weakly‑capitalized banks and search‑for‑yield behavior provided cheap credit to impaired firms; units are firms and industry‑country “markets.”

difficulty.justification: Multiple countervailing channels exist (zombies can depress prices via excess supply/competition or raise them via misallocation, lower productivity and higher costs), and identification is plagued by strong endogeneity (bank lending responses to demand shocks), market-structure heterogeneity and general-equilibrium feedbacks; the rich panel/product data help but specialized identification strategies are needed and the opposite sign remains plausible without strong quasi-experiments.

context: This paper uses U.S. state-level panels (and household survey microdata) to study the effects of the 1960s–1980s staggered adoption of unilateral (“no-fault”) divorce laws across states. Outcomes cover roughly 1964–1996 for suicide, 1968–1994 for homicide, and 1976 and 1985 household surveys for domestic violence. The units are state-year aggregates (suicide and homicide) and households/couples (domestic violence). The institutional change—allowing a spouse to obtain divorce without partner consent (sometimes after separation)—is exploited as a policy shock to marital bargaining and exit threats.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a negative effect requires intermediate institutional knowledge of divorce law and family bargaining, but is sensitive to state-specific norms, enforcement, and contemporaneous trends; multiple plausible opposing channels (short‑run retaliation, selection, reporting changes) and limited, noisy homicide counts make credible identification moderately hard.

context: Using the 20% MedPAR sample of U.S. Medicare inpatient discharges (1985–1991), this triplet examines how hospital ownership affected upcoding after HCFA’s 1988 elimination of the “age over 69” DRG qualifier. Upcoding is measured as the share of DRG-pair admissions assigned to the higher-paying “with complications” code, computed separately for young and old patients, and the analysis compares time-series trends and regression estimates across ownership types (for-profit, not-for-profit, government), finding larger increases among for-profit hospitals.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate knowledge of DRG incentives and ownership incentives; the 1988 policy change provides a useful natural experiment but differential trends, selection, and coding/auditing practices across ownership types make unambiguous identification challenging.

triplet_key: 15323|teacher performance pay|teaching effort conditional on presence
context: This paper reports a randomized evaluation (2005–07) of teacher performance pay in rural Andhra Pradesh, India. The authors randomized 500 sampled government primary schools (focus on 300 schools: 100 group-incentive, 100 individual-incentive, 100 control) across 50 mandals, following baseline tests in June–July 2005 and endline tests in March–April 2006 and 2007. Units are pupils (grades 1–5), teachers, and schools. The intervention paid teacher bonuses (≈3% of annual pay) based on average student test-score improvements, in a setting with low learning, high teacher absence, and civil-service pay rules.

difficulty.justification: A randomized evaluation provides fairly strong identification of incentive effects on effort conditional on presence, so moderate domain knowledge suffices, but institutional details (small bonus size, civil-service rules, test-based pay) make the sign context-dependent and allow plausible alternative channels (teaching-to-test, gaming). Measurement and channel heterogeneity lower difficulty but do not eliminate ambiguity about true effort versus test-specific behaviors.

context: The paper studies Oregon University System employees participating in the Optional Retirement Plan (ORP) from October 1996 through December 2009, exploiting a November 2007 regime change that removed a broker-sold provider (HIGH). Using matched administrative and account-level data on individual ORP participants (contributions, holdings, demographics) across providers (HIGH with brokers, LOW, NEW with target-date funds), the authors analyze how access to broker recommendations (limited to asset allocation and fund selection) affects fees, risk exposures, and after-fee returns.

difficulty.justification: Understanding whether broker recommendations reduce after-fee returns requires intermediate asset-management knowledge but is highly context-dependent (institutional rules, provider set) and subject to plausible opposite explanations (advice could pick higher gross-return funds). The Nov 2007 removal gives a strong quasi-experiment, reducing evidence gaps, but selection, composition, fee versus risk channels, and generalizability make correct sign identification nontrivial.

triplet_key: 25749|stopping receipt of ui and sp benefits|long-run consumption
treatment: Stopping receipt of UI and SP benefits
context: This paper studies consumption responses to job displacement in the state of São Paulo, Brazil using matched administrative employment (RAIS), UI registry, and de-identified high-frequency VAT receipt data for roughly 400,000 users over 2010–2014 (primary layoff window 2011–2013). Units are individual formally employed workers experiencing involuntary separations; institutional features include Brazil’s Unemployment Insurance (monthly UI), Severance Pay and FGTS lump-sum accounts, a 30-day UI waiting period, and a high informal sector (~35%). The analysis links timing of layoffs, UI payments, and UI exhaustion to household expenditures.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a negative long-run effect requires substantive institutional knowledge (UI timing, FGTS lump sums, informal work) and dynamic identification to separate benefit-exhaustion effects from persistent unemployment/selection. The matched administrative + VAT data give strong leverage, but unobserved household resources, informal income and heterogeneous re-employment paths leave nontrivial identification challenges.

context: This paper analyzes monthly and daily prices for Dow-listed stocks dual-traded on the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE) and the Consolidated Stock Exchange (CSE) in New York City over 1886–1925. The NYSE introduced staged multilateral netting in May 1892 (and later mutualization in 1920) while the CSE had a clearinghouse since 1886. Units are security-months/days; the institutional setting features broker overnight call loans, high leverage, frequent banking panics, and broker defaults, allowing comparison of identical securities across rival exchanges to identify clearing effects.

difficulty.justification: Identifying that a clearinghouse reduces return volatility requires intermediate market-structure knowledge but not exotic theory; the sign is moderately context-dependent and several plausible offsetting channels (changes in leverage, liquidity, selection, and contagion) make the opposite sign plausible. The historical dual-listing, staggered adoption, and within-security comparisons provide reasonably good identification, but contemporaneous shocks and microstructure differences leave nontrivial ambiguity.

context: Using confidential loan-level data for 2007–2013 (focus on the 2009 stimulus), the paper analyzes firm-quarter outcomes in China: newly issued long-term loans from 17 large commercial banks to over 160,000 firms (distinguishing SOEs and non-SOEs). For the private-investment result, the authors merge these bank loans with CSMAR listed-firm data to estimate the elasticity of capital investment to bank credit, then use a back-of-envelope calculation combining that elasticity with the estimated elasticity of bank credit to the 2009 monetary stimulus. "Private investment" refers to non-SOEs; the setting features the PBC's large M2 expansion and the 2008–09 fiscal infrastructure package.

difficulty.justification: Assessing a negative effect of monetary stimulus on private (non-SOE) investment in 2008–09 China requires specialized knowledge of bank-firm politics and sectoral transmission; the sign is highly context-dependent and confoundable (fiscal package, SOE favoritism, selection into loans), involves multiple indirect/GE channels, and — despite rich loan-level data — the back-of-envelope multiplication and sample limitations leave substantial identification uncertainty.

context: Structural life‑cycle analysis of US male workers (ages 22–61) using the 1993 SIPP panel for wage/job transitions and PSID 1988–1996 for employment/unemployment durations. The authors stratify by education (high school or less vs some college+), model quarterly consumption, labor supply and job mobility, and embed US institutions (Unemployment Insurance, Disability Insurance, Food Stamps, Social Security) to estimate wage processes with match effects and run calibrated counterfactual welfare simulations.

difficulty.justification: Higher job destruction plausibly lowers employment and output, but offsetting reallocation gains, match-specific productivity, life‑cycle heterogeneity, and institutional responses (UI, DI, etc.) can change the magnitude or even sign; the structural, calibrated approach aids identification but depends on strong assumptions and limited panel evidence.

context: Using U.S. firm-level Compustat data (1980–2014) on publicly listed firms and bankruptcy events, the paper studies how U.S. bankruptcy law (Chapter 7 liquidation vs Chapter 11 reorganization) affects corporate capital structure, investment, entry/exit, and aggregate allocative efficiency. It estimates a dynamic general-equilibrium structural model (endogenous entry and exit) calibrated by simulated method of moments and evaluates a “fresh start” policy counterfactual proposed by the American Bankruptcy Institute.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether a 'fresh start' reform reduces corporate bankruptcy rates requires deep, specialized knowledge of bankruptcy law and corporate finance and hinges on institutional details (Chapter 7 vs 11), selection and credit‐supply channels, and general equilibrium feedbacks; public-firm Compustat data plus a calibrated dynamic GE model helps but cannot remove substantial ambiguity and identification sensitivity, so the sign is highly uncertain without very strong structural assumptions.

triplet_key: 28197|household credit boom|real gdp per capita recovery
context: This paper exploits a newly assembled long-run panel of business (corporate) credit and macroeconomic data for 17 advanced economies from the late 19th century through 2019, analyzing country-level business-cycle peaks, recessions and recoveries (outcomes include GDP per capita, investment, consumption, credit and asset prices). It contrasts corporate versus household credit booms and, for 1978–2019, studies how insolvency/restructuring frictions (creditor rights, recovery rates) affect outcomes, using legal-origin variation as an instrument for institutional differences.

difficulty.justification: Distinguishing household-credit-driven effects on post‑recession per‑capita GDP requires substantial macro‑finance and institutional knowledge; the sign is highly context‑dependent and confounded by selection, balance‑sheet, and general equilibrium channels, and although long‑run panel data and legal‑origin IVs help, identification and measurement challenges remain substantial.

context: Using Danish matched employer–employee administrative data over 2003–2008, the paper studies firms and individual employees around a legal threshold (firms with ≥35 employees) after Denmark’s 2006 Act No. 562 requiring firms above the cutoff to produce gender-disaggregated wage statistics (effective 2007). The analysis focuses on firms with 20–50 employees (treated: 35–50; controls: 20–34) to estimate how mandated pay-transparency affected wages, hiring, promotions, productivity, and profits in the private sector.

difficulty.justification: A researcher needs intermediate labor-economics and policy knowledge, but the institutional RDD around the 35-employee cutoff and rich administrative data provide fairly strong identification; nonetheless multiple channels (wage, promotion, sorting) and possible firm responses/manipulation make the sign moderately ambiguous and causally complex.

triplet_key: 22589|cps race-neutral tier-based admission policy|efficiency at increasing minority representation
context: Using 2013–14 Chicago Public Schools applicant data (≈16,818 applicants), this paper evaluates CPS’s 2010–2012 shift from race-based racial quotas to a race-neutral, place-based tier admissions system for selective exam high schools (notably Walter Payton and Northside). The analysis is set against the 1980s consent decree and post-2007 Supreme Court limits on race-based affirmative action. The authors simulate counterfactual admissions under alternative race-neutral and race-conscious rules to quantify efficiency and diversity trade-offs across students as the unit of observation.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate policy and market knowledge about school admissions and affirmative‑action law; the sign depends heavily on CPS institutional details and spatial demographics, multiple mediating channels (assignment rules, applicant sorting, seat constraints) create plausible alternative signs, and single‑year simulated counterfactuals leave important behavioral and general equilibrium gaps.

triplet_key: 20889|nih funding|patents with different primary disease affiliation
outcome: Patents with different primary disease affiliation
context: This paper studies the causal effect of U.S. NIH public R&D on private-sector patenting in the biopharmaceutical industry, linking NIH grants (1980–2005) to patents through 2012. The unit of analysis is a disease–science–year (DST) pairing and private-sector patents. Identification exploits NIH institutional features—chartered study sections, within-study “science” ranks, and Institute-level “rank-of-ranks” payline funding rules—across NIH Institutes and study sections in the U.S.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a positive causal effect of NIH funding on private-sector patents across disease affiliations requires specialized knowledge of NIH institutional rules and patent–disease mapping, is highly context dependent (disease, time, industry structure), and is subject to plausible alternative mechanisms (selection into funding, crowding-out, or cross-disease complementarities). The institutional quasi‑experimental variation described helps identification, but noisy mapping and multiple mediating channels make causal attribution moderately difficult.

triplet_key: 25245|initial allowance of an ex-ante valuable patent|worker compensation
treatment: Initial allowance of an ex-ante valuable patent
context: This paper studies US firms and their workers using linked USPTO patent applications (published 2001–2011, analysis up to 2010 filings) and US Treasury business and W2/1099 tax records (1997–2014). The unit of analysis is patent-applying firms (EINs), typically small newly-innovative firms (median ~17 employees), and their employees. It exploits USPTO initial allowance versus rejection decisions (within art-unit/year) and predicted ex-ante patent value to identify how patent-induced productivity and rents affect firm outcomes, wages, hiring, and retention in the U.S. patent institutional setting.

difficulty.justification: Understanding this link requires specialized knowledge of patent examination, firm-level rent-sharing and labor-market channels; effects vary by firm, industry, and labor-market conditions, and alternative channels (owners capture rents, sorting, selection) are plausible, though the within-art-unit/year allowance design and rich admin data provide reasonably strong but not definitive evidence.

context: This study analyzes U.S. publicly listed firms over 1994–2015 (66,341 firm-year observations) to test whether greater stock price informativeness (measured by PIN, PSI and variants) causally increases firm-level total factor productivity (TFP). The unit is the publicly traded firm-year, drawing on Compustat, CRSP, TAQ, IBES and governance/competition datasets. Identification leverages within-firm panel variation and plausibly exogenous shocks—including brokerage research closures (circa 2000–2007) and S&P 500 additions—along with IV and matched DiD comparisons.

difficulty.justification: Economic theory gives clear monotone channels (better information → improved resource allocation/monitoring → higher TFP) but also requires intermediate finance/organization knowledge. Reverse causation, omitted confounders and dynamic feedbacks make the sign plausible but nontrivial to pin down despite the study's strong panel and shock-based identification.

triplet_key: 17347|trade negotiations starting at nash tariffs|government welfare
treatment: Trade negotiations starting at Nash tariffs
context: This paper uses 2007 industry-level data covering seven regions (Brazil, China, EU, India, Japan, US, Rest of World) and 33 agriculture/manufacturing sectors, estimating CES demand elasticities from UN trade data (1994–2008) and calibrating to GTAP8 and tariff sources (Market Access Map, TRAINS). Units are bilateral industry-region trade flows, prices, and tariffs. These observables are embedded in a multi-country, multi-industry general-equilibrium model with iceberg trade costs, monopolistic competition, and political-economy weights to simulate optimal, Nash, and cooperative tariff and welfare counterfactuals.

difficulty.justification: Establishing that negotiations starting from Nash tariffs increase government welfare requires specialized multi-country GE and political-economy knowledge; the sign is highly context-dependent with multiple opposing channels (terms-of-trade, tariff revenue, redistributive weights) and calibration-based evidence helps but cannot fully eliminate competing plausibilities.

triplet_key: 16213|one additional year of celera's ip|subsequent scientific research
treatment: One additional year of Celera's IP
context: This empirical study examines how Celera’s short-term intellectual property on human genes affected subsequent scientific research and product development. It uses gene-level observations (≈27,882 genes; ≈1,682 initially held by Celera) linked to publications, genotype–phenotype links, and diagnostic-test availability. Identification exploits timing variation in sequencing and public re-sequencing around 1999–2003 (outcomes observed through 2009) in the Human Genome Project vs. Celera institutional setting, where Celera used contract-based IP that lapsed once public resequencing occurred.

difficulty.justification: Evaluating whether one additional year of Celera’s IP reduced follow-on research requires specialized knowledge of sequencing, IP institutions, and measurement of scientific outputs; the sign depends on institutional and technological context and is confounded by selection (which genes Celera sequenced first) and pre-existing scientific interest. Multiple mediating channels (private incentives, access delays, public resequencing) make causal reasoning nontrivial, though the gene-level timing variation provides reasonably informative but not definitive evidence.

context: This estimate assesses the effect of Boston Public Schools K1 preschool attendance for first-time four-year-old applicants (cohorts 1997–2003) on grade repetition outcomes. Identification uses lottery-based assignment with a 2SLS (IV) approach. Grade-repetition measures (including "starting 1st grade on time" and a repeated-grade indicator defined as appearing in the same grade in more than one year) are drawn from Massachusetts DESE administrative records and are restricted to students who are observed in Massachusetts public schools.

difficulty.justification: Lottery-based assignment and administrative outcomes give strong identification, but noncompliance/LATE, sample restriction to MA public schools (attrition/migration), possible low power and cohort/institutional specificity make a null sign plausible but not trivial to accept.

context: This paper studies the causal effects of foreign supermarket entry in Mexico from January 2002 to March 2014, exploiting variation in the timing of first foreign-store openings across primarily urban municipalities. Units include households, barcode-level transactions, individual stores and store profits, workers, and municipalities. The setting follows Mexico’s 1993 retail FDI liberalization and a major expansion of global chains (notably Walmart), using administrative and survey microdata to assess impacts on prices, household welfare (cost-of-living), employment and retail profits.

difficulty.justification: Multiple offsetting channels (lower consumer prices and welfare vs local retail displacement, wage and employment effects), endogenous entry into growing municipalities, and heterogeneous household exposure make the sign highly contestable; however, rich microdata and timing variation provide a credible but not definitive basis for identification.

triplet_key: 8711|larger private benefits of control|external market capitalization/gnp
context: Using 412 firm-level control transactions from 1990–2000 across 39 countries, this paper estimates the value of private benefits of control (block-premia) and links those country-level measures to financial development outcomes. Units of observation include transaction-level blocks and firms, with country-level aggregates (ownership concentration, privatization methods, market capitalization/GDP). The institutional background exploits cross-country variation in shareholder legal rights (anti-director index), law enforcement, press diffusion, tax compliance, product-market competition, and privatization regimes to explain and instrument private benefits.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a negative effect of private control benefits on market capitalization requires intermediate corporate-governance knowledge but is sensitive to institutional context, reverse causality, and omitted-country factors; measurement (block-premia → country aggregates) and instrument validity create substantial identification challenges despite plausible instruments and a multi-country sample.

context: This triplet refers to the paper's application of its theoretical welfare framework to the U.S. disability insurance system. Instead of the Austrian administrative quasi-experiments, the authors apply their sufficient-statistics formulas to existing U.S. empirical estimates and conclude that loosening DI eligibility rules (and increasing benefits) would be welfare-increasing in the U.S. context.

difficulty.justification: Establishing that loosening DI eligibility increases welfare requires specialized knowledge of insurance versus incentive tradeoffs, selection and heterogeneity, and fiscal feedbacks; empirical estimates are context-specific, confounded by selection and behavioral responses, and thus provide weak and ambiguous guidance on the sign in the U.S. setting.

context: Using the DTCC Trade Information Warehouse snapshot of single-name CDS as of December 30, 2011 (184 reference entities, 885 anonymized counterparties and 14 dealers), the paper simulates counterfactual clearing and margin arrangements and finds that client clearing reduces system-wide collateral demand only conditional on dealers being able to re-use a sufficiently large share of collateral received from clients (rehypothecation) and when investors’ portfolios are large enough to realize netting and diversification benefits.

difficulty.justification: Requires specialized knowledge of clearing, margining and rehypothecation mechanics; the sign is highly conditional on institutional details (rehypothecation rates, portfolio concentration and netting) and alternative mechanisms could easily reverse it, so identification is complex and data-sensitive.

context: This paper studies long-term causal effects of Boston Public Schools' universal K1 preschool on educational and behavioral outcomes for first-time preschool applicants (four-year-olds) who applied between 1997 and 2003 in Boston, MA. Using randomized school-assignment tie-break lotteries within BPS, linked to Massachusetts DESE administrative records and National Student Clearinghouse data with ~20 years of follow-up, the study evaluates impacts on college enrollment, SAT-taking, MCAS scores, high-school graduation, and disciplinary outcomes for a largely low-income, nonwhite urban cohort.

difficulty.justification: Randomized school-assignment tie-break lotteries plus ~20 years of linked administrative outcomes provide strong identification and ample evidence to detect a positive effect; modest domain knowledge about early-childhood mechanisms and program quality/context is useful but confounding and data gaps are minimal.

context: This paper studies firm bidding in Texas’s restructured wholesale electricity balancing (day-of) market in ERCOT during the early market years (sample spanning the first half of year 2: Aug 2002–Jan 2003). The unit of observation is hourly balancing auctions and firm-level bids (12 focal generating firms, covering ~66% capacity) with observed plant-level marginal costs. The institutional setting is a multi-unit, uniform-price balancing auction following the 2001 market restructuring; the study exploits a mid-sample two-month nuclear outage as a demand/cost shock and uses structural behavioral models to analyze efficiency and merger counterfactuals.

difficulty.justification: Requires specialized auction and electricity-market knowledge; the sign depends critically on institutional details (uniform-price multi-unit auction, concentration, outages) and opposite effects (improved dispatch vs. increased market power) are both plausible, so strong structural identification and assumptions are needed despite rich data and a natural shock.

triplet_key: 23112|snap benefits (vs cash)|respect for fungibility of money
context: Using transaction-level retailer panel data from February 2006–December 2012 together with Rhode Island and national SNAP administrative records, the paper analyzes how monthly SNAP EBT benefits affect household grocery and nonfood spending. The setting is a large U.S. grocery chain’s loyalty-card customers (≈486,570 households across several states) and administrative SNAP data for Rhode Island. The analyses exploit institutional features of SNAP: monthly electronic benefit disbursements, certification/recertification cycles (many spells divisible by six months), and legislated benefit increases (2008 Farm Bill and April 2009 ARRA).

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate policy and consumer-finance knowledge (SNAP rules, EBT timing, mental accounting). Sign depends on institutional/timing and household heterogeneity; alternative explanations (liquidity, selection, stigma, eligibility rules) are plausible, though the administrative/timing variation improves identification but does not eliminate complex mediating channels and remaining confounds.

triplet_key: 5650|flexible exchange regime|ease of return to equilibrium
context: This paper analyzes monthly multilateral real exchange rates for up to 93 countries from 1960–1994, using country-month observations constructed from WPI/CPI and trade weights. Units are countries; background includes exchange-rate arrangements (pegs, floats, dual rates) and macro fundamentals (terms of trade, government spending, openness). The authors identify real appreciation episodes relative to PPP and study their dynamics and correction mechanisms, estimating equilibrium RERs via trend and fundamentals regressions and assessing whether reversions occur through nominal devaluations or inflation differentials.

difficulty.justification: Determining that flexible regimes speed real reversion requires specialized knowledge of exchange-rate dynamics and identification methods; the sign is highly context-dependent and confounded by regime endogeneity, measurement of fundamentals, and multiple interacting channels (nominal moves, inflation inertia, policy responses), so empirical evidence must overcome substantial identification challenges.

triplet_key: 10572|2003 dividend tax cut|probability of increasing regular dividends
context: Using quarterly CRSP data from 1980–2004 Q1 on U.S. publicly traded firms (excluding utilities and financials), the paper examines how the 2003 dividend tax cut (Jobs and Growth Tax Relief Reconciliation Act of 2003, which reduced top individual dividend tax rates to 15% and applied retroactively to 2003) affected corporate payout behavior — regular vs. special dividends, initiations, terminations, and payment amounts — comparing pre- and post-2003 firm-level outcomes and contrasting responses with the 1986 tax reform.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate tax and corporate‑finance knowledge; the positive sign is plausible but depends on firm heterogeneity, substitution to repurchases, anticipation/retroactivity and macro timing, and while quarterly CRSP plus the 1986 contrast aids identification it does not fully eliminate alternative explanations.

context: This analysis focuses on U.S. publicly traded bank holding companies (171 banks) over 1987–2015 (with emphasis on the pre‑crisis window 1987–2006), using FRY‑9C and CRSP/Compustat data to examine how bank asset size (measured in book total assets, with a piecewise specification at the $50 billion 2010‑dollar Dodd‑Frank threshold) relates to leverage (book equity-to-assets). The paper contrasts cross-sectional (year fixed effects) and within-bank (year and bank fixed effects) panel regressions and compares pre‑ and post‑GFC regulatory periods.

difficulty.justification: Multiple competing mechanisms (TBTF/moral hazard and cheaper debt for large banks versus regulatory pressure and internal capital accumulation), strong time- and regime-dependence (pre/post‑GFC, Dodd‑Frank threshold), and confounders (selection, reverse causality, business model, mergers) make the sign hard to pin down without careful identification.

triplet_key: 29905|20% decrease in social security benefits|total pre-tax labor income
context: This paper uses U.S. microdata (SIPP panels 1984–2008 and CPS panels 1979–2018) to estimate a structural life-cycle model of consumption, human-capital investment, labor supply and Social Security claiming. Focusing on male high-school graduates (individual-level units, estimation from ages ~22 up to 80), it models U.S. Social Security rules (claiming from age 62, normal retirement 65, using 2004 rules) and simulates counterfactual benefit and tax changes. Estimation employs indirect inference to match life-cycle employment and measured-wage profiles; extensions include health shocks and part-time work.

difficulty.justification: Multiple interacting channels (substitution vs income effects, claiming/retirement timing, human-capital investment, health and selection) and institution-specific rules make the sign context-dependent and sensitive to structural assumptions, so reasonably hard to identify though a well-specified lifecycle model and rich microdata help.

triplet_key: 20269|patent invalidation|subsequent citations to the focal patent
outcome: subsequent citations to the focal patent
context: The paper studies 1,357 U.S. patent validity appeals (1,258 distinct patents) decided by the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit from 1982 through 2008. The unit of observation is litigated patents/cases and subsequent citing patents (five-year windows) in USPTO data. It exploits random assignment of three-judge panels—court invalidation removes patent rights—to estimate effects of invalidation on follow-on innovation (patent citations) across technology fields (computers, electronics, medical instruments, drugs, chemicals, mechanical). Appealed patents are typically high-value, privately important inventions.

difficulty.justification: Interpreting how invalidation affects subsequent citations requires intermediate specialized knowledge of patent law and innovation dynamics and is quite context-dependent across fields and institutional settings; multiple opposing mechanisms (weakened signaling vs. freer follow-on use) make the sign ambiguous, but the paper's quasi-random panel assignment gives reasonably strong identification within the litigated-patent sample.

context: This paper exploits a 1994 German reform that abolished the one‑third worker‑representative mandate on supervisory boards for stock corporations incorporated on/after August 10, 1994 (grandfathering older cohorts). Using matched IAB‑Orbis employer–employee data, firm financials, and incorporation cohorts (units: firms and workers) for 1989–1999 entrants, it estimates effects on wages, firm pay premia (AKM), productivity, capital and the labor share within Germany’s two‑tier board and collective‑bargaining institutional context, noting a 500‑employee exemption.

difficulty.justification: Determining the sign requires specialized institutional knowledge of German codetermination, two‑tier boards, collective bargaining and the 500‑employee exemption; multiple firm‑level channels (wages, productivity, investment) and selection/heterogeneity issues mean the effect could plausibly go either way. The 1994 grandfathering provides strong quasi‑experimental leverage and rich matched data improve identification, but heterogeneous compliance, local external validity, and GE/selection effects leave nontrivial uncertainty.

context: This paper studies the causal effects of the UK Regional Selective Assistance (RSA) investment-subsidy program on manufacturing plants and firms in Britain over 1986–2004. Using administrative panel data on plants, firms and program applicants (SAMIS, IDBR, ARD), and area aggregates at ward and Travel-to-Work levels, it exploits exogenous EU-driven changes to area eligibility (maps in 1993 and 2000) and Net Grant Equivalent rates to estimate impacts on employment, investment, productivity, firm entry/exit, and local unemployment.

difficulty.justification: Administrative panel data and exogenous EU-driven eligibility changes give good leverage, so moderate domain knowledge suffices, but identification is complicated by spillovers, displacement (crowding out), firm entry/exit and local labour market adjustments; these make opposite or zero effects plausible without careful design and supplementary tests.

context: Between January 2005 and August 2006 Money Management International randomized 79,739 prospective clients across U.S. states into debt-management-plan (DMP) offers. Units are distressed credit‑card borrowers; treated individuals received larger immediate minimum‑payment reductions and delayed debt write‑downs negotiated with eleven card issuers, while controls received status‑quo DMP terms. The study links counselor records to PACER bankruptcy data, TransUnion credit reports, and SSA W‑2 tax records to measure repayment, bankruptcy, credit, employment, and 401(k) outcomes over roughly five years.

difficulty.justification: Randomized assignment and rich administrative outcomes give strong identification within this study, so domain knowledge need not be highly specialized; however multiple mediating channels (stress, credit access, bankruptcy avoidance, job search) and program feature heterogeneity make the sign context-dependent and not obvious without empirical analysis.

context: This paper exploits a 1994 German reform that abolished the one‑third worker‑representative mandate on supervisory boards for stock corporations incorporated on/after August 10, 1994 (grandfathering older cohorts). Using matched IAB‑Orbis employer–employee data, firm financials, and incorporation cohorts (units: firms and workers) for 1989–1999 entrants, it estimates effects on wages, firm pay premia (AKM), productivity, capital and the labor share within Germany’s two‑tier board and collective‑bargaining institutional context, noting a 500‑employee exemption.

difficulty.justification: Estimating the sign of codetermination on revenue requires specialized knowledge of German two‑tier boards, bargaining and size exemptions and is highly context dependent; multiple opposing channels (wage, productivity, investment, selection) make the sign ambiguous, though the 1994 grandfathering reform provides a credible quasi-experiment that renders inference feasible but still nontrivial.

triplet_key: 21562|casino payments (per-capita tribal cash transfers)|parent 2 drug or alcohol use
context: This study uses the Great Smoky Mountains Study (annual waves from 1993) of children recruited in 11 counties in western North Carolina (oversampling Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians) to study effects of exogenous per‑capita casino transfers (~$4,000/year, disbursed mid‑1990s to enrolled adult tribal members). For this triplet the unit is parents: the paper estimates effects of the transfers on parental drug/alcohol use (Parent 2), measured via parent‑ and child‑reports, using individual fixed‑effects linear probability panel regressions that exploit cohort and timing variation.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate policy and behavioral knowledge about income, addiction, and family dynamics; institutional and community context (tribal enrollment, local labor market, norms) strongly shape the sign. The cohort/timing fixed‑effects design helps identification, but plausible opposing mechanisms (stress reduction vs. more cash for substances), reporting/measurement issues, and community‑level channels leave nontrivial uncertainty.

context: This paper studies the effects of the 1970 and 1977 U.S. Clean Air Act Amendments on manufacturing using plant-level Census of Manufactures data from 1967–1987. It exploits county-level, pollutant-specific attainment/nonattainment designations for four criteria pollutants across 3,070 counties and 1.75 million plant observations; polluting plants in nonattainment counties faced stricter plant-specific regulation. The unit of analysis is individual manufacturing plants; identification leverages cross-sectional and temporal variation in county nonattainment status and industry emitting status to estimate regulation impacts on employment, capital, and shipments.

difficulty.justification: Establishing a null effect is challenging because nonattainment designations trigger multiple competing channels (compliance costs, investment, plant exit and reallocation) and correlate with local industrial composition and trends; however, pollutant- and industry-specific, staggered county variation and rich plant-level data substantially aid identification, making the task moderately difficult but feasible with careful designs.

context: This estimate examines the AP microfinance shock’s effect on household total investment using the NSS Round 70 “Debt and Investment” module (mid‑2012/2013). It links pre‑2010 district‑level MFI exposure from for‑profit MFIs to representative rural household reports of investment and business expenditures in 2012, using cross‑sectional regressions that control for pre‑crisis district covariates. The broader setting remains Indian rural districts affected indirectly by the October 2010 Andhra Pradesh ordinance and ensuing MFI balance‑sheet spillovers.

difficulty.justification: Establishing that a reduction in microcredit causally lowers household investment requires intermediate knowledge of microfinance markets and the Andhra Pradesh institutional shock, and is sensitive to local institutions, household heterogeneity, and substitution to other lenders. The cross‑sectional linking of pre‑2010 MFI exposure to 2012 investment is highly confoundable and lacks strong identification (pre‑trends, selection, GE spillovers), making a definitive sign hard to credibly pin down without stronger quasi‑experimental strategies.

triplet_key: 20640|targeted outreach to unlikely applicants|charter expansion productivity (achievement)
context: This result is based on model-based counterfactual simulations for Boston charter middle schools using the same administrative sample (BPS students in fourth grade 2006–2009 followed to eighth grade) at the individual-student level. The setting is Boston’s decentralized charter application system with school-specific randomized admission lotteries, distance measures, and a charter-cap; the targeted-outreach effect is simulated within the estimated generalized Roy model (a 20-school expansion counterfactual that alters application costs and preference distributions).

difficulty.justification: Identifying a positive effect requires specialized knowledge of market design and structural models (4) and is highly sensitive to Boston-specific institutions (5). Multiple selection, composition, and GE channels make the sign plausibly reversed under alternative assumptions, and model-based extrapolation to a 20‑school expansion rests on strong identifying assumptions so evidence is weak (all driving a very high overall difficulty).

context: This paper studies how declining trade costs since the early 1970s through 2007 affected GDP volatility across a multi-country sample (24 core countries plus a rest-of-world aggregate). The authors combine sector-level data (24 sectors), bilateral trade flows, and country-year observations with a calibrated multi-sector stochastic trade model that features country- and sector-specific productivity shocks and ex-ante labor allocation. Using the model and data, they back out trade costs and shocks and run counterfactuals to quantify trade’s causal impact on aggregate volatility.

difficulty.justification: Establishing that trade-driven diversification reduces GDP volatility requires specialized multi-sector trade theory and careful GE reasoning because sectoral reallocation, terms-of-trade and shock correlation can easily reverse the sign; rich data and a calibrated model help identification but results remain sensitive to modeling assumptions and shock-decomposition.

triplet_key: 11551|increased aid flows to developing countries|developing countries' incomes
treatment: Increased aid flows to developing countries
context: Cross-country analysis using a cross-section of about 53 countries circa 1996–2000 (Penn World Tables 6.1 for GDP/capital; World Bank wealth data ~2000; Bernanke & Gurkaynak capital shares). Unit of observation is the country. The authors assume competitive domestic goods and factor markets and Cobb–Douglas production, and they adjust for reproducible vs. natural capital and for domestic relative prices of investment goods. They perform structural counterfactual reallocations of the world reproducible-capital stock to assess deadweight losses from non‑equalized returns.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a true zero effect of aid on country incomes requires strong structural assumptions (Cobb–Douglas, competitive markets), careful handling of endogeneity/selection of aid, dynamics, measurement of natural vs reproducible capital, and heterogeneity in institutions/absorptive capacity; given cross‑sectional data and these confounders, the sign is highly context‑dependent and difficult to establish.

triplet_key: 25187|growing, fluctuating credit access|newborn welfare (lifetime consumption preference)
context: This triplet refers to model-based welfare for newborn agents in the calibrated general-equilibrium search-and-matching model (with defaultable debt) comparing U.S. economies from 1977 to 2010: one with stochastically growing and procyclical credit matching efficiency (expanding to 2010 levels) versus fixed 1977 levels. Welfare is measured as the percent of ex-ante lifetime consumption newborns would forgo; newborns are willing to give up about 0.12% of lifetime consumption to be born in the economy with growing, fluctuating credit access.

difficulty.justification: Determining this sign relies on specialized macro and search-and-matching knowledge (default dynamics, calibration) and involves strong general-equilibrium feedbacks and selection effects; the positive effect found is sensitive to institutional/calibration details and plausible alternative channels (volatility, redistribution, default) could attenuate or reverse it, while evidence is largely model-based rather than experimentally identified.

triplet_key: 9607|marginal income tax cuts|upward trend in top income shares
outcome: Upward trend in top income shares
context: The paper uses Canadian individual income tax returns for adults (1920–2000) but, for this causal claim, focuses on the period since the late 1970s and a cross-country comparison with the United States. It notes that Canada experienced much more modest reductions in marginal income tax rates than the U.S., and uses the parallel rise in top income shares to argue tax-rate cuts are unlikely to explain the upward trend in Canadian top income shares. Units are individual adults; the analysis is framed by income-tax schedules and cross-border labor mobility.

difficulty.justification: Assessing whether marginal tax cuts caused changes in top income shares requires specialized tax‑policy and income‑distribution knowledge, is highly sensitive to institutional setting (mobility, avoidance, composition), and faces many plausible alternative explanations (technology, globalization, measurement/avoidance), so strong identification strategies are needed and the available evidence is suggestive but not definitive.

triplet_key: 10121|jobs first time limit|hazard rate of leaving welfare (month 22)
context: This triplet uses MDRC’s randomized Jobs First waiver experiment in Connecticut (random assignment Apr 1996–Feb 1997; follow‑up through Dec 2000) among 4,803 welfare cases (women) in New Haven and Manchester. The estimated outcome is the monthly hazard rate of leaving welfare measured among women who remain in their first welfare participation spell; the treatment is Jobs First’s 21‑month time limit (which first binds at month 22). Administrative UI and welfare records are the primary data.

difficulty.justification: Randomized assignment in the MDRC experiment gives strong internal identification of a time‑limit effect, so basic policy knowledge suffices; however, interpreting the hazard at month 22 requires dynamic reasoning about anticipation, selection among survivors of the first spell, and local institutional/labor‑market context.

context: This paper analyzes payday loan outcomes using CFPB administrative loan-level data covering July 2011–June 2012 (six months before and after Jan 1, 2012) to evaluate a Texas statewide behaviorally‑motivated disclosure mandate and concurrent Austin and Dallas municipal loan‑size and amortization ordinances. The setting compares loans, stores, and borrowers in Texas to lenders in other states and across Texas cities, exploiting policy timing and enforcement start‑dates (Austin May 1, Dallas June 17, 2012) to study effects on loan volume, renewals, pricing, and compliance.

difficulty.justification: Interpreting how statewide behavioral disclosures affect payday loan volume requires intermediate-to-advanced knowledge of payday markets and disclosure effects. Concurrent municipal ordinances, lender adaptation, timing/spillover issues and selection make the sign context-dependent and confoundable, and although rich administrative data and staggered enforcement help identification, disentangling overlapping policies remains challenging.

treatment: American Rescue Plan Act fiscal stimulus
context: This triplet concerns the U.S. effect of the $1.9 trillion American Rescue Plan Act (ARPA) enacted in early 2021. Using their estimated two‑agent New Keynesian model, the authors assign part of the Q1 2021 increase in spending to unfunded transfers and construct conditional, out‑of‑sample forecasts (including real‑time data available in 2021:Q2) to assess ARPA’s inflationary impact, finding a higher peak inflation (6.3% vs 3.7%).

difficulty.justification: Assessing whether ARPA raised U.S. inflation requires specialized macro/fiscal identification and modeling knowledge, and the sign depends heavily on context (slack, reopening, supply bottlenecks, monetary response). Multiple plausible confounders and general‑equilibrium channels make the causal link complex and model/data dependent, so credible inference is moderately-to-highly difficult.

triplet_key: 4594|continuation of coverage mandates|likelihood of being insured after retirement
outcome: Likelihood of being insured after retirement
context: This U.S. study uses nationally representative CPS (MORG) and SIPP data from the 1980s (panels spanning mid-1980s through 1989 and surrounding years) to analyze individuals (primarily men aged 55–64) across states. It exploits variation in state “continuation of coverage” mandates and the phased-in federal COBRA implementation (1985–87) to study how the availability of continued employer health insurance after job separation affects early retirement decisions and post-retirement insurance coverage.

difficulty.justification: Interpreting whether continuation mandates raise post-retirement insurance requires intermediate policy knowledge (COBRA and state rules) and is sensitive to institutional details (era, employer size, take-up). The state-mandate + phased COBRA variation gives plausible quasi-experimental leverage, but selection into retirement, employer responses, awareness/take-up, and measurement issues make opposite or attenuated effects reasonably plausible without careful identification.

context: Using U.S. firm-level Compustat data (1980–2014) on publicly listed firms and bankruptcy events, the paper studies how U.S. bankruptcy law (Chapter 7 liquidation vs Chapter 11 reorganization) affects corporate capital structure, investment, entry/exit, and aggregate allocative efficiency. It estimates a dynamic general-equilibrium structural model (endogenous entry and exit) calibrated by simulated method of moments and evaluates a “fresh start” policy counterfactual proposed by the American Bankruptcy Institute.

difficulty.justification: Answering whether a fresh-start bankruptcy reform causally lowers borrowing costs requires advanced corporate‑finance and macro/IO knowledge, is highly context- and institution-dependent, and faces plausible opposing mechanisms (creditor pricing vs. reallocation benefits). Identification relies on a complex dynamic GE structural model and strong calibration/assumption choices while using only publicly listed firms, so the sign is difficult to pin down robustly.

context: Using transaction-level retailer panel data from February 2006–December 2012 together with Rhode Island and national SNAP administrative records, the paper analyzes how monthly SNAP EBT benefits affect household grocery and nonfood spending. The setting is a large U.S. grocery chain’s loyalty-card customers (≈486,570 households across several states) and administrative SNAP data for Rhode Island. The analyses exploit institutional features of SNAP: monthly electronic benefit disbursements, certification/recertification cycles (many spells divisible by six months), and legislated benefit increases (2008 Farm Bill and April 2009 ARRA).

difficulty.justification: Institutional timing and legislated benefit changes provide plausibly exogenous variation that helps identification, but multiple behavioral channels (liquidity, intra-month smoothing, substitution across stores/products), selection into SNAP and into loyalty-card data, and heterogeneity across places make the sign context-dependent and alternative signs reasonably plausible without careful design.

context: This study estimates long-run effects of Head Start’s county-level rollout in the United States (1965–1980) on cohorts born 1950–1980, using linked 2000 Census and 2001–2018 ACS data merged to the SSA Numident. The unit is individuals (adults observed at ages 25–54), and identification exploits county Head Start launch dates combined with school-entry age eligibility (ages 3–5 vs age 6+), controlling for county fixed effects and state-by-birth-year trends. The setting features a rapid, idiosyncratic OEO grant roll-out and phased program implementation.

difficulty.justification: The county-rollout × school-entry eligibility design gives plausibly quasi-experimental variation, but assessing long-run effects on high-school completion requires specialized knowledge of Head Start implementation and careful handling of threats (selective placement, migration, changing program quality, spillovers, measurement error), so sign inference is credible but nontrivial.

triplet_key: 4934|identifying and closing insolvent banks|depositor information problems
context: This paper examines bank failures during the Great Depression, focusing on the June 1932 Chicago banking panic. Using individual Chicago bank-level observations (stock prices, balance sheets, income statements, liability composition, and failure dates) from 1931–1932, the authors compare banks that failed during the panic, banks that failed outside the panic window, and survivors. Institutional context includes absence of deposit insurance, clearing-house and correspondent arrangements, Reconstruction Finance Corporation interventions, municipal fiscal stress and major corporate failures (Insull), and episodes of fraud (Bain chain).

difficulty.justification: Determining whether closing insolvent banks reduces depositor information problems in 1932 Chicago requires specialized knowledge of banking panics, clearinghouse behavior, and policy responses; the effect is highly context-dependent and plausibly reversed by signaling/contagion, involves selection and general-equilibrium feedbacks, and the historical data are unlikely to provide clear exogenous variation to pin down the sign.

triplet_key: 18674|large non-cerp reconstruction spending|insurgent attacks per capita
context: Using panel data from Feb 2004–Dec 2008/Feb 2009 on Iraq’s reconstruction, this paper studies effects of U.S. development spending across districts (N≈103) observed in half‑year intervals. It links IRMS project‑level spending (CERP, USAID CAP/CSP, large vs. small projects), troop strength (maneuver battalions), and Provincial Reconstruction Team presence to SIGACT insurgent‑incident rates, exploiting variation in timing, project size, local security, and implementation modality to evaluate when aid reduced violence during the Iraq war and U.S. counterinsurgency operations.

difficulty.justification: Identifying a null effect of large non‑CERP reconstruction on insurgent attacks requires specialized counterinsurgency and program‑implementation knowledge, and the sign is highly context‑dependent (security, timing, modality). Endogeneity, selection into projects, spillovers, and interacting military presence make the opposite or heterogeneous effects very plausible and demand strong identification strategies despite a rich panel dataset.

context: The paper studies Oregon University System employees participating in the Optional Retirement Plan (ORP) from October 1996 through December 2009, exploiting a November 2007 regime change that removed a broker-sold provider (HIGH). Using matched administrative and account-level data on individual ORP participants (contributions, holdings, demographics) across providers (HIGH with brokers, LOW, NEW with target-date funds), the authors analyze how access to broker recommendations (limited to asset allocation and fund selection) affects fees, risk exposures, and after-fee returns.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether broker recommendations reduce Sharpe ratios requires intermediate market and retirement-plan knowledge and is sensitive to institutional details (plan rules, participant selection). Brokers can plausibly both raise or lower risk-adjusted returns through advice, fees, and risk-taking, so untangling selection, fee effects, reallocation and behavioral responses—even with a regime change—poses nontrivial identification challenges.

context: This empirical study (management survey conducted in 2006) examines public acute hospitals (hospital trusts) in England—100 hospitals, 161 interviews in cardiology and orthopaedics—and links management scores to clinical and financial outcomes (AMI mortality, MRSA, waiting lists) using 2005–2006 outcomes. Identification exploits England’s centralized NHS and political incentives: the share of marginal Labour constituencies (1997) within 30km is used as an instrument for the number of nearby public hospitals during a mid-1990s–early-2000s consolidation period when closures were centrally decided by the Department of Health.

difficulty.justification: Understanding this link requires intermediate health‑economics and hospital policy knowledge rather than pure common sense; the sign depends strongly on NHS institutional details (access/travel times, consolidation, ambulance systems) and could flip with different contexts. Multiple plausible channels (volume–outcome, access delays, specialization, resource reallocation) and potential confounders (politically driven closures correlated with local health trends) make identification nontrivial despite the clever instrument, so evidence is informative but not definitive.

triplet_key: 24006|unconditional subsidy to households with young children|household welfare (relative policy)
treatment: Unconditional subsidy to households with young children
context: This paper studies married U.S. households using PSID (1999–2015), ATUS (2003–2015) and CEX data to estimate a life-cycle structural model of consumption, spouses' wages and time allocation to work, leisure and childcare. Units are married couples (wives aged 25–65), distinguishing families with young children (≤10). The setting incorporates permanent and transitory wage shocks, progressive taxation, childcare production technology, liquidity constraints and childcare costs to analyze how families self-insure consumption via labor supply and time reallocation.

difficulty.justification: Multiple interacting channels (labor supply, time allocation to childcare, wages, liquidity constraints and tax financing) make the sign plausible but model-dependent; rich life‑cycle structural estimation helps identification but relies on substantive parameter and institutional assumptions so inference is moderately difficult.

context: This empirical study uses the Great Smoky Mountains Study (annual waves from 1993) of 1,420 children in 11 counties in western North Carolina (oversampling Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians) to estimate effects of exogenous per‑capita casino transfers (~$4,000/year, disbursed mid‑1990s to enrolled adult tribal members) on adolescent personality, emotional, and behavioral outcomes. It compares younger American Indian cohorts exposed to payments before age 16 with older cohorts and non‑Indian families, exploiting panel data on children, parents, household characteristics, and census‑tract location.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate knowledge of income‑shock effects on child development and personality; sign depends on institutional/cultural setting and timing; plausible alternative mechanisms and confounders exist; multiple mediating channels and community feedbacks matter; the quasi‑experimental design helps but residual threats remain.

context: This paper studies how national property-right assignments and financial development affect fishing effort across global marine stocks from 1960–2012. Using a panel of 4,692 fish stocks (772 species) in 130 countries, units are stock-country-year observations; the key institutional shock is staggered implementation of 200‑nautical-mile Exclusive Economic Zones (EEZs) under UNCLOS, with species-specific mobility determining local property-right security. Credit market development is measured by private-sector credit and lending rates, with governance and vessel-level data used to examine mechanisms.

difficulty.justification: Understanding the sign requires intermediate knowledge of fisheries bioeconomics, property-rights institutions, and credit effects; species mobility, enforcement, fleet responses, and cross-border spillovers make the sign context-dependent and plausibly confounded, though the staggered EEZ shock and rich panel data make credible identification feasible but nontrivial.

triplet_key: 11983|increasing depth of mortgage secondary market|impact of local funding shocks on credit supply
treatment: Increasing depth of mortgage secondary market
outcome: Impact of local funding shocks on credit supply
context: This empirical study analyzes U.S. mortgage applications merged with bank Call Report data from 1992–2003. The units are individual mortgage applications and bank-year observations in the U.S. residential mortgage market, where GSEs (Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac) purchase only non-jumbo loans, creating a discrete, annually indexed jumbo-loan cutoff that sharply reduces liquidity. The paper examines how banks’ funding costs, balance-sheet liquidity, and broader securitization depth affect acceptance rates for jumbo versus non-jumbo mortgages.

difficulty.justification: Identifying that deeper mortgage secondary markets mitigate the effect of local funding shocks requires substantial mortgage/securitization knowledge and depends on institutional details (GSE jumbo cutoff, time period), multiple mediating channels and plausible opposing mechanisms (selection, risk‑shifting), but the described rich data and discrete cutoff provide fairly good identification leverage so inference is challenging but feasible.

context: This result comes from the paper’s calibrated structural incomplete-markets overlapping-generations model of U.S. households over the 1997–2015 housing boom and bust. The paper evaluates a counterfactual principal-reduction (debt forgiveness) program implemented two years into the bust and covering over one-quarter of homeowners with mortgages. The unit of analysis is households interacting with competitive rental, construction, and mortgage intermediation sectors; the outcome studied is foreclosures.

difficulty.justification: Assessing whether principal reduction reduces foreclosures requires specialized modeling/calibration and depends heavily on timing, coverage, and institutional details; lender responses, strategic default, and general-equilibrium housing/credit feedbacks make the sign plausibly ambiguous without careful structural identification and strong parameter evidence.

context: Compares regulatory enforcement and stock market development in Poland and the Czech Republic during the 1990s post-communist transition. Units include national securities regulators, firms and stock markets; the analysis focuses on periods when judicial systems remained underdeveloped and different regulatory approaches—Poland’s active Securities and Exchange Commission enforcing securities law versus the Czech Republic’s hands-off stance—affected market listings, investor protection, and market functioning.

difficulty.justification: Plausible negative effect (lax rules reduce investor protection and thus equity supply) but establishing the sign requires specialized institutional knowledge and addressing strong confounders, policy endogeneity, and context-specific channels in the 1990s Poland–Czech setting.

context: This study analyzes the effect of state-level wrongful discharge laws (public-policy, implied-contract, and especially the good-faith exception) on firm innovation and entrepreneurship in the U.S. using firm-year data from 1971–1999. Exploiting staggered, precedent-driven adoption of these common-law exceptions across states, the authors link USPTO patents (counts and citations) matched to Compustat firms, patents per employee/R&D, and Business Dynamics Statistics on new establishments and job creation to identify changes in innovative effort and start-up formation.

difficulty.justification: Determining the sign requires specialized knowledge of wrongful‑discharge doctrines and labor/innovation channels, plus careful empirical identification; effects vary by state/institution, many plausible opposing mechanisms (employee protection vs. firm flexibility), and adoption timing/endogeneity and GE/selection issues make convincing inference moderately hard.

context: This empirical study (management survey conducted in 2006) examines public acute hospitals (hospital trusts) in England—100 hospitals, 161 interviews in cardiology and orthopaedics—and links management scores to clinical and financial outcomes (AMI mortality, MRSA, waiting lists) using 2005–2006 outcomes. Identification exploits England’s centralized NHS and political incentives: the share of marginal Labour constituencies (1997) within 30km is used as an instrument for the number of nearby public hospitals during a mid-1990s–early-2000s consolidation period when closures were centrally decided by the Department of Health.

difficulty.justification: Establishing that more nearby competing public hospitals causally raises management quality in the NHS setting requires specialized institutional and political knowledge (centralized closure process, local politics) and faces substantial context dependence and plausible alternative mechanisms (selection of closures, scale economies, local demand, funding). The identification hinges on an instrument (share of marginal Labour constituencies) that is clever but susceptible to exclusion and omitted-variable concerns, plus complex selection/composition and general equilibrium channels, making correct sign identification difficult.

context: Using the 2012 Beginning Postsecondary Students (BPS) cohort in the United States, followed to 2017, the paper studies first-time college enrollees’ baseline subjective expectations and realized outcomes (degree completion, employment, earnings, loan delinquency). In the institutional setting of federally-backed, largely non-dischargeable student loans and limited private income-share agreements, the authors link survey elicitations, FAFSA and administrative loan records to quantify private information, adverse selection in hypothetical equity and state-contingent contracts, and welfare gains from government subsidies.

difficulty.justification: Needs intermediate policy/market knowledge and is sensitive to US institutional features (federal lending, non-dischargeability, limited ISAs). Rich survey and administrative data help, but screening/pricing responses, moral hazard, selection into enrollment, and equilibrium supply-side adjustments make attributing the negative effect empirically challenging.

context: This paper analyzes California school districts using bond referenda from 1987–2006 (with housing transactions through 1988–2005 and multi-year fiscal and test-score panels) to estimate causal effects. The unit is the school district; the institutional setting is California’s voter-approved general obligation school bonds (initially requiring a 2/3 supermajority, later sometimes 55%), whose revenues are legally restricted to capital projects. The design exploits close elections and repeated proposals to identify impacts on district finances, housing prices, and student achievement.

difficulty.justification: Although basic public‑finance knowledge helps (bonds are capital‑restricted), the net effect on current expenditures depends on context-specific channels (maintenance and operating costs of new facilities, substitution of operating for capital spending, tax/aid interactions) and multiple mediating mechanisms; the close‑election/repeated‑proposal design provides reasonably strong identification but timing, accounting, and heterogeneous institutional responses keep the problem fairly challenging.

context: The paper studies Oregon University System employees participating in the Optional Retirement Plan (ORP) from October 1996 through December 2009, exploiting a November 2007 regime change that removed a broker-sold provider (HIGH). Using matched administrative and account-level data on individual ORP participants (contributions, holdings, demographics) across providers (HIGH with brokers, LOW, NEW with target-date funds), the authors analyze how access to broker recommendations (limited to asset allocation and fund selection) affects fees, risk exposures, and after-fee returns.

difficulty.justification: Broker recommendations can push portfolios in either direction depending on broker incentives, participant selection, and product menus; concurrent market events (2007–09) and dynamic rebalancing create confounding and mediation, but the 2007 provider-removal and linked administrative/account data provide a reasonably credible quasi-experiment if exclusion restrictions and no other concurrent shocks hold.

triplet_key: 25245|initial allowance of a high-value patent|average tax revenue per worker
treatment: Initial allowance of a high-value patent
context: This paper studies US firms and their workers using linked USPTO patent applications (published 2001–2011, analysis up to 2010 filings) and US Treasury business and W2/1099 tax records (1997–2014). The unit of analysis is patent-applying firms (EINs), typically small newly-innovative firms (median ~17 employees), and their employees. It exploits USPTO initial allowance versus rejection decisions (within art-unit/year) and predicted ex-ante patent value to identify how patent-induced productivity and rents affect firm outcomes, wages, hiring, and retention in the U.S. patent institutional setting.

difficulty.justification: Multiple plausible channels produce opposite effects (wage rises, rent capture, hiring/compositional dilution, changes in compensation form or exit/acquisition taxed differently), and the sign depends on institutional details of firm type and tax treatment; linked USPTO–Treasury data are rich but still leave important behavioral and tax-treatment margins that make clean inference challenging.

triplet_key: 17347|50 percentage point tariff increase in chemicals|government welfare
treatment: 50 percentage point tariff increase in chemicals
context: This triplet refers to a counterfactual 50 percentage-point increase in the US import tariff on chemicals simulated in the paper's calibrated 2007 multi-country, multi-industry general-equilibrium model (regions: Brazil, China, EU, India, Japan, US, Rest of World; 33 sectors). The model uses CES demand elasticities estimated from UN trade data (1994–2008) and GTAP8/Market Access Map/TRAINS tariff data; the unit of observation is country-industry bilateral trade flows and the outcome is US government welfare (real-income measure) implied by the GE counterfactual.

difficulty.justification: Determining whether a 50‑point chemicals tariff raises US government welfare requires specialized trade/G E knowledge because input‑output linkages, terms‑of‑trade effects, tariff revenue, and retaliation can all push the sign either way; the calibrated multi-country GE model provides structure but results are sensitive to elasticity estimates and model assumptions.

context: The paper analyzes the effects of state malpractice liability reforms (direct versus indirect) on medical expenditures using data on all elderly Medicare beneficiaries treated for serious heart disease in U.S. states in 1984, 1987, and 1990, comparing expenditure changes in reform states to nonreform states.

difficulty.justification: Requires intermediate policy/healthcare knowledge (malpractice law, physician incentives, Medicare). Effects vary substantially by reform type and state context, are subject to plausible confounders (policy endogeneity, concurrent reforms, coding/selection), and operate through multiple channels (defensive medicine, treatment intensity, composition), so diff‑in‑diff helps but identification risks remain.
